For a B.Tech student entering the auto industry, understanding OM is essential for roles in:
- Production engineering and shop floor management
- Supply chain and logistics planning
- Quality assurance and Six Sigma deployment
- Plant layout and facilities design
- ERP system implementation (SAP, Oracle)
- EV manufacturing and battery production operations
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Impact Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Importance of OM vs. Business Outcomes

The following analytical matrix demonstrates the **cause-effect relationship** between operations management initiatives and measurable business outcomes. This matrix is a high-value framework for KTU 14-mark essay-type questions.

$$
\text{Business Impact} = f(\text{OM Strategy}, \text{Implementation Quality}, \text{Technology Integration})
$$

| **OM Initiative** | **Cost Impact** | **Quality Impact** | **Time Impact** | **Strategic Value** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lean Manufacturing | $15\text{-}25\%$ cost reduction | $\downarrow 40\%$ defects | $50\%$ lead time cut | High |
| Six Sigma | $10\text{-}20\%$ cost savings | $\leq 3.4$ DPMO | $30\%$ cycle time reduction | Very High |
| JIT Inventory | $20\text{-}30\%$ inventory cost cut | $\uparrow$ freshness of components | Eliminates storage delays | High |
| TQM | $5\text{-}10\%$ long-term cost savings | $\uparrow$ customer satisfaction | $\downarrow$ rework time | Very High |
| ERP Integration | $10\%$ administrative cost cut | $\uparrow$ data accuracy | Real-time decision making | Very High |
| Predictive Maintenance | $25\text{-}30\%$ maintenance cost cut | $\uparrow$ equipment reliability | $\downarrow$ unplanned downtime | High |
| Industry 4.0 / Smart Factory | $20\text{-}30\%$ productivity gain | $\uparrow$ defect detection | $40\%$ faster changeovers | Transformative |

### 3.2 Quantitative Derivation: Cost Impact of Effective Operations Management

Let us derive the **Total Cost of Poor Operations Management (TCPOM)** to demonstrate the financial importance of OM:

**Step 1: Identify Cost Categories**

The cost of poor operations management is composed of:

$$
C_{TCPOM} = C_{scrap} + C_{rework} + C_{warranty} + C_{downtime} + C_{excess\,inventory} + C_{late\,delivery}
$$

Where each term represents:
- $C_{scrap}$ = Cost of materials wasted due to defects
- $C_{rework}$ = Labor and material cost to fix defects
- $C_{warranty}$ = Claims paid for vehicle failures
- $C_{downtime}$ = Lost production due to equipment failure
- $C_{excess\,inventory}$ = Holding costs of unnecessary stock
- $C_{late\,delivery}$ = Penalties and lost sales from delays

**Step 2: Express Each Component in Expanded Form**

$$
C_{scrap} = D_{rate} \times U_{produced} \times C_{unit}
$$

Here, $D_{rate}$ is the defect rate, $U_{produced}$ is the number of units produced, and $C_{unit}$ is the unit cost.

$$
C_{downtime} = H_{down} \times R_{hourly}
$$

Here, $H_{down}$ is the total downtime hours and $R_{hourly}$ is the revenue per production hour.

$$
C_{excess\,inventory} = I_{avg} \times H_{cost\,pct}
$$

Here, $I_{avg}$ is the average inventory value and $H_{cost\,pct}$ is the holding cost percentage (typically **20-30%** of inventory value annually).

**Step 3: Worked Numerical Example**

Consider an automotive plant producing **100,000 vehicles/year** with the following baseline data:
- Defect rate: $D_{rate} = 5\% = 0.05$
- Unit cost: $C_{unit} = \text{₹}8{,}00{,}000$
- Downtime: $H_{down} = 200$ hours/year
- Revenue per hour: $R_{hourly} = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000$
- Average inventory: $I_{avg} = \text{₹}200$ crore $= \text{₹}2{,}00{,}00{,}00{,}000$
- Holding cost percentage: $H_{cost\,pct} = 25\% = 0.25$

**Calculating each cost component:**

$$
C_{scrap} = 0.05 \times 100{,}000 \times \text{₹}8{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{scrap} = 5{,}000 \times \text{₹}8{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}400 \text{ crore}
$$

$$
C_{downtime} = 200 \times \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}100 \text{ crore}
$$

$$
C_{excess\,inventory} = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = \text{₹}50 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 4: Assumed values for remaining components** (based on industry averages):
- $C_{rework} = \text{₹}150 \text{ crore}$
- $C_{warranty} = \text{₹}300 \text{ crore}$
- $C_{late\,delivery} = \text{₹}100 \text{ crore}$

**Step 5: Total Cost of Poor OM**

$$
C_{TCPOM} = 400 + 100 + 50 + 150 + 300 + 100 = \text{₹}1{,}100 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 6: Impact of Effective OM**

If effective operations management reduces these costs by **30%**:

$$
\text{Savings} = 0.30 \times \text{₹}1{,}100 \text{ crore} = \text{₹}330 \text{ crore/year}
$$

$$
\text{Percentage of Revenue Saved} = \frac{330}{1{,}100 \text{ crore (baseline revenue estimate)}} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

**Step 7: Interpretation**

$$
\boxed{\text{Effective OM saves ₹330 crore annually, equivalent to a 4-5% improvement in net profit margin.}}
$$

This quantitative analysis clearly demonstrates that **operations management is not a support function but a primary value-creation engine** in the automotive industry.

### 3.3 Strategic Importance Matrix: Porter's Perspective Applied to Auto OM

Michael Porter's competitive strategy framework can be adapted to show how operations management enables each competitive advantage:

$$
\text{Competitive Advantage} = f(\text{Cost Leadership}, \text{Differentiation}, \text{Focus})
$$

| **Porter's Strategy** | **OM Contribution** | **Auto Industry Example** |
|---|---|---|
| Cost Leadership | Process optimization, scale economies, lean operations | Maruti Suzuki's low-cost production model |
| Differentiation | Superior quality, custom features, premium experience | BMW's flexible manufacturing for variants |
| Focus (Niche) | Specialized production for segment needs | Ferrari's hand-crafted low-volume production |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Exam Answers:** When asked to "Discuss the importance of operations management," structure your answer using the **three-tier framework**: (1) Strategic importance, (2) Operational importance, (3) Tactical importance. This layered approach is the gold standard expected by KTU board examiners.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Framework: Pillars of OM Importance in Auto Industry

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the seven interconnected pillars that establish the importance of operations management in the automotive sector, mapped to their respective business outcomes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B1 --> C1[Business Outcome: 15-25% Cost Reduction]
```

This diagram maps the importance of operations management across three hierarchical levels, showing how strategic decisions cascade into operational and tactical outcomes.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

Define Operations Management. Why is it considered the backbone of the automotive industry?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Operations Management is the administration of business practices to create the highest level of efficiency possible within an organization, specifically focused on converting materials and labor into goods and services as effectively as possible in the context of the automotive industry.

**Why Backbone (1.5 Marks):** Operations Management is the backbone of the automotive industry because it integrates all functions — from procurement of raw materials to final delivery of vehicles — ensuring cost efficiency, quality control, supply chain coordination, and timely production. Without effective OM, an automaker cannot achieve the scale, speed, and quality demanded by global markets.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

List any **six key performance indicators (KPIs)** used to measure the effectiveness of operations management in an automotive manufacturing plant.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 marks each):**

1. **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** — measures availability, performance, and quality combined.
2. **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — percentage of products meeting quality standards without rework.
3. **Inventory Turnover Ratio** — frequency of inventory replenishment per year.
4. **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** — percentage of orders delivered by committed date.
5. **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — defect rate at Six Sigma level.
6. **Cycle Time** — time required to complete one unit of production from start to finish.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **strategic importance of operations management** in the automotive industry. Explain with suitable examples how it contributes to competitive advantage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of operations management in supply chain integration and quality assurance** for an automotive company. How does effective OM help in reducing the Total Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Strategic Importance (7 Marks):**

The strategic importance of operations management in the automotive industry can be understood through the following dimensions:

**(1) Enabling Cost Leadership Strategy (2 Marks):**
Operations management enables automakers to achieve cost leadership through lean manufacturing, economies of scale, and process optimization. For example, Maruti Suzuki's operations strategy has consistently delivered the lowest production costs in India, allowing it to price vehicles competitively while maintaining healthy margins. Toyota Production System (TPS) eliminates seven types of waste (muda) to achieve world-class cost efficiency.

**(2) Supporting Product Differentiation (2 Marks):**
Through flexible manufacturing systems and modular platform architectures, operations management enables automakers to offer diverse product variants from common production lines. BMW, for instance, produces multiple vehicle models on a single assembly line, allowing premium differentiation without sacrificing efficiency.

**(3) Accelerating Time-to-Market (1.5 Marks):**
In the rapidly evolving EV segment, companies with superior operations management can bring new models to market in 24-30 months compared to the traditional 48-60 month cycle. Tesla's gigafactory operations exemplify this advantage.

**(4) Building Organizational Resilience (1.5 Marks):**
Strategic OM decisions about plant locations, supplier diversification, and inventory buffers determine an automaker's ability to withstand disruptions like the 2021-2023 semiconductor crisis.

**Part (b) — OM in Supply Chain and Quality (7 Marks):**

**(1) Supply Chain Integration (3 Marks):**
A modern vehicle comprises 30,000+ parts from 1,500-2,000 suppliers globally. Operations management coordinates this complex network through:
- **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)** systems
- **Just-in-Time (JIT)** delivery schedules
- **Electronic Data Interchange (EDI)** for real-time communication
- **Tier-1 and Tier-2 supplier synchronization**

For example, Toyota's JIT system delivers components to the assembly line within hours of need, eliminating warehousing costs while maintaining production continuity.

**(2) Quality Assurance (2 Marks):**
Operations management deploys quality tools such as:
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** for real-time monitoring
- **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** for risk prevention
- **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing) devices
- **IATF 16949** quality management system compliance

**(3) Reducing Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) (2 Marks):**
COPQ includes prevention costs, appraisal costs, internal failure costs (scrap, rework), and external failure costs (warranty, recalls). Effective OM reduces COPQ by:
- Investing in prevention (training, process design) to reduce failures
- Automating inspection to lower appraisal costs
- Implementing Six Sigma to eliminate root causes of defects
- Establishing feedback loops for continuous improvement

The economic principle states: **every rupee spent on prevention saves ₹10-100 in failure costs.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven pillars of importance** of operations management in the automotive industry. Discuss how each pillar contributes to manufacturing excellence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable numerical example, demonstrate how **effective operations management can generate significant cost savings** in an automotive manufacturing plant. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Seven Pillars (7 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**

1. **Cost Competitiveness:** OM reduces production costs by 15-25% through waste elimination and process optimization, critical when margins are thin (5-10%).

2. **Quality Assurance:** OM ensures deployment of IATF 16949, Six Sigma, and SPC, preventing costly recalls that average $500 million per incident.

3. **Supply Chain Integration:** OM coordinates 1,500-2,000 suppliers globally, enabling JIT delivery and resilience against disruptions.

4. **Time-to-Market Acceleration:** OM compresses development cycles from 48-60 months to 24-30 months, capturing first-mover advantage in segments like EVs.

5. **Innovation Enablement:** OM bridges R&D and production, integrating Industry 4.0 technologies like IoT, AI, digital twins, and additive manufacturing.

6. **Sustainability Compliance:** OM ensures adherence to BS-VI emission norms, EU carbon targets (95g CO₂/km), and circular economy principles for End-of-Life Vehicles.

7. **Workforce Productivity and Safety:** OM optimizes human-machine interaction, preventing safety incidents that can halt production costing $1-5 million per day.

**Part (b) — Numerical Example (7 Marks):**

Consider an automotive plant with the following data:
- Annual production: **50,000 vehicles**
- Defect rate: **4%**
- Unit cost: **₹10,00,000**
- Annual downtime: **150 hours**
- Revenue per production hour: **₹40,00,000**
- Average inventory value: **₹100 crore**
- Holding cost rate: **25%**

**Step 1: Scrap Cost (1 Mark)**

$$
C_{scrap} = 0.04 \times 50{,}000 \times \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}200 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 2: Downtime Cost (1 Mark)**

$$
C_{downtime} = 150 \times \text{₹}40{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}60 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 3: Excess Inventory Cost (1 Mark)**

$$
C_{inventory} = \text{₹}100 \text{ crore} \times 0.25 = \text{₹}25 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 4: Other Costs (Rework + Warranty + Late Delivery) (1 Mark)**

$$
C_{other} = \text{₹}100 + \text{₹}80 + \text{₹}60 = \text{₹}240 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 5: Total Cost of Poor OM (1 Mark)**

$$
C_{TCPOM} = 200 + 60 + 25 + 240 = \text{₹}525 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 6: Savings with Effective OM at 35% Reduction (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{Annual Savings} = 0.35 \times \text{₹}525 \text{ crore} = \text{₹}183.75 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 7: Impact on Profit Margin (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{Profit Margin Improvement} = \frac{183.75}{5{,}000 \text{ crore (assumed revenue)}} \times 100 = 3.68\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A 35% improvement in operations management effectiveness generates ₹183.75 crore in annual savings, boosting profit margins by approximately 3.7 percentage points.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Vague Definitions:** Students often write generic definitions of "management" or "production" instead of the specific automotive operations context. Always contextualize your answer to the **automotive industry specifically**.
> 
> 2. **Missing Quantitative Support:** Merely listing qualitative points (cost, quality, time) without numerical backing (percentages, benchmarks, formulas) fetches only **60-70% marks**. Always include **at least 2-3 numerical examples or industry benchmarks**.
> 
> 3. **No Real-World Examples:** Answers that do not reference **any real company** (Toyota, Tesla, Tata, Maruti, Ford, BMW) are penalized. KTU examiners explicitly look for application-level evidence.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring Sustainability:** Modern KTU question papers (2024 scheme) increasingly emphasize **green operations, EV manufacturing, and sustainability**. Do not omit these aspects.
> 
> 5. **Confusing OM with General Management:** Operations Management is specifically about **processes, production, and supply chain** — not HR, finance, or marketing. Stay focused.
> 
> 6. **Formula Presentation Errors:** When using formulas, always define every variable. An undefined variable in a derivation loses 0.5-1 mark per occurrence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Examination:**

- ☐ **Definition:** OM in auto industry = systematic conversion of inputs into vehicles with maximum efficiency, quality, and minimal cost.
- ☐ **Seven Pillars of Importance:** Cost, Quality, Supply Chain, Time, Innovation, Sustainability, Workforce.
- ☐ **Key Metrics to Memorize:** OEE ≥ 85%, FPY ≥ 99%, ITR = 8-12, DPMO ≤ 3.4, OTD ≥ 98%, MCE ≥ 25%.
- ☐ **Important Formulas:** OEE formula, FPY formula, ITR formula, Capacity Utilization, Productivity.
- ☐ **Real-World Examples:** Toyota TPS, Maruti's cost leadership, Tesla's gigafactories, BMW's flexible manufacturing, Tata's EV transition.
- ☐ **Industry Benchmarks:** 5-10% profit margins, 30,000+ parts per vehicle, 1,500-2,000 suppliers, 48-60 month development cycle, $500M average recall cost.
- ☐ **Modern Trends:** Industry 4.0, EV manufacturing, BS-VI compliance, circular economy, digital twins, predictive maintenance.
- ☐ **Three Strategic Levels:** Strategic (long-term), Operational (mid-term), Tactical (short-term/day-to-day).
- ☐ **Porter's Strategies:** Cost leadership, Differentiation, Focus — all enabled by OM.
- ☐ **TCPOM Framework:** Scrap + Rework + Warranty + Downtime + Inventory + Late Delivery = Total cost of poor OM.
- ☐ **Golden Rule for KTU Answers:** Always structure as Definition → Pillars → Examples → Quantitative Support → Conclusion.
- ☐ **Avoid:** Generic management concepts, HR/Finance topics, unsubstantiated claims, missing numerical data.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques of Work Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Techniques of Work Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Measurement (BSI / British Standards Institution Definition):**
> *"Work Measurement is the application of techniques designed to establish the time for a qualified worker to carry out a specified job at a defined level of performance."*

In the context of the **Automotive Industry**, work measurement is the systematic process of determining the **standard time** required by an average-skilled operator, working at a normal pace, to perform a specific automotive operation (e.g., tightening a bolt on a chassis, mounting a dashboard, painting a panel) under stipulated working conditions.

### Fundamental Goal
The ultimate objective is to convert human effort into **standard minutes (SM)** or **standard hours (SH)** so that every station on the assembly line, every work cell in a press shop, and every operation in a machine shop can be planned, balanced, costed, and incentivised.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pit Stop" Mental Model

Imagine a **Formula 1 pit crew**. Before the race, engineers don't just say "the tyre change will take *some* time." They:
- **Break it down** into micro-actions (wheel-gun, jack, wheel off, wheel on, jack down).
- **Time** each micro-action across dozens of practice stops.
- **Rate** the crew's speed against a benchmark.
- **Add recovery time** (allowances) for the unexpected.
- **Publish a standard**, e.g., 2.3 seconds.

That published number is the **Standard Time** — a contractual benchmark for performance.
Work measurement in the auto industry is **exactly this**, but applied to thousands of operations per car, per shift, per factory.

---

## 1.3 Why Work Measurement Matters in the Auto Industry

| Driver | KTU High-Yield Point |
|---|---|
| **Line Balancing** | Standard times are required to distribute work evenly across stations. |
| **Takt Time Alignment** | Cycle time of each station must match the **Takt Time = Available Time / Customer Demand**. |
| **Costing & Pricing** | Labour cost per vehicle = Σ (Standard Time × Labour Rate). |
| **Incentive Wage Schemes** | Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, and Emerson plans are all anchored on standard time. |
| **Lean / Six Sigma** | Forms the basis for *Kaizen*, *Poka-Yoke*, and cycle-time reduction projects. |
| **Capacity Planning** | Determines the number of machines and workers required. |

---

## 1.4 Master Classification of Work Measurement Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus categorises work measurement techniques into **three broad families**. This taxonomy must be memorised verbatim.

### A. Direct Methods
Techniques where the worker's actual time is observed and measured directly.
- **Time Study (Stopwatch Method)** — the most important
- **Continuous Timing Method**
- **Repetitive Timing Method (Fly-back / Snap-back)**
- **Multiple Activity Chart (Man/Machine Chart)**

### B. Indirect Methods (Predetermined)
Techniques that use pre-established time standards for human motions.
- **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** — e.g., **MTM-1, MTM-2, MTM-UAS, MOST**
- **Standard Data Systems** (Synthesis)
- **Analytical Estimating**
- **Reference Data**

### C. Statistical / Sampling Methods
- **Work Sampling (Ratio Delay Study)**
- **Activity Sampling**
- **PTS Sampling**

> **Standard International Benchmarks Used in Auto Plants:** **MTM (Methods Time Measurement)** in European OEMs; **Most Work Measurement System (MOST)** in US OEMs.

---

## 1.5 Quick-Reference Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Observed Time (OT)** | Time recorded directly by the stopwatch for an element. |
| **Performance Rating (R)** | Worker's pace expressed as a percentage of standard (e.g., 110 = 10% faster). |
| **Normal Time (NT)** | OT × R — time for a qualified worker at normal pace. |
| **Allowance (A)** | Time added to NT for rest, personal needs, contingencies. |
| **Standard Time (ST)** | The final time used for planning, costing, and paying incentives. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Equation — Chain of Standard Time

Every work measurement technique, regardless of classification, ultimately feeds into one master pipeline:

$$
\text{Observed Time (OT)} \xrightarrow{\times R} \text{Normal Time (NT)} \xrightarrow{\div (1-A)} \text{Standard Time (ST)}
$$

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Pipeline

1. **Capture raw observed time** of the operation by stopwatch or PMTS lookup.
2. **Apply Performance Rating (R)** to normalise the worker's pace to the standard pace (defined as 100 on the rating scale — equivalent to walking 3 mph or a BSI standard pace).
3. **Compute Normal Time (NT):** the time a *qualified*, *trained* worker would take at *normal* speed.
4. **Add Allowances (A):** to compensate for non-productive but necessary activities (rest, personal needs, contingencies, process delays).
5. **Compute Standard Time (ST):** the planned, contractual time used downstream.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Treatment of Each Technique

### 2.2.1 Time Study (Stopwatch Method) — *The Workhorse of Auto Plants*

**Procedure (Standard 8-Step British Standards / KTU Recommended Sequence):**

1. **Define the job** to be studied (Operation Sheet, Method Study output).
2. **Select the operator** — average-skilled, trained on the method.
3. **Record data** — job description, conditions, equipment, materials.
4. **Break the job into elements** (each element = a logical work-unit).
5. **Time each element** with a stopwatch to the nearest **0.01 minute** (or 0.0001 hour).
6. **Rate the worker's performance** element-by-element.
7. **Compute the Average Elemental Time** after discarding abnormal readings.
8. **Apply the rating factor and allowances** to obtain the Standard Time.

**Elemental Time vs. Cycle Time:**
- A job is divided into 5–15 elements.
- Each element's NT is computed: $\text{NT}_i = \text{OT}_i \times R_i$.
- Total NT = $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{NT}_i$.
- Total ST = $\dfrac{\sum \text{NT}_i}{1 - A}$.

**Performance Rating Scales Used in Industry:**

| Scale | Range | Used By |
|---|---|---|
| **100 (BSI)** | 0–150 | European OEMs (BMW, Mercedes, Tata Motors) |
| **75 (Westinghouse)** | 0–100 | Older US plants |
| **133 (Bedaux)** | 0–133 | Bedaux incentive plans |
| **60 (MTM pace)** | 0–120 | Pure MTM systems |

---

### 2.2.2 Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)

**Core Principle:** Time values for *fundamental human motions* (reach, grasp, move, position, release) are pre-determined in laboratory conditions. Standard time is **synthesised** without ever using a stopwatch.

**Famous PMTS in the Auto Industry:**

| System | Developer | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **MTM-1** | H.C. Maynard (1948) | 1 TMU = 0.00001 hr; ~100 basic motions |
| **MTM-2** | MTM Association | Simplified; ~40 motions |
| **MTM-UAS** | Universal Analysis System | High-level; 48 cards |
| **MOST** | H.B. Maynard (1967) | Sequence Model — used in GM, Ford |
| **MTM-MEK** | German Auto Federation | Specifically for car assembly |

**Formula:** $\text{ST} = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{TM}_i\right) \times (1 + A)$, where $\text{TM}_i$ is the predetermined time of motion $i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auto Industry Application:** Before launching a new car model, Ford, Toyota, and Maruti use **MTM-UAS or MOST** to pre-time the assembly line *offline* — even before the line physically exists.

---

### 2.2.3 Work Sampling (Ratio Delay Study)

**Concept:** Instead of timing every cycle, the observer makes **random instantaneous observations** of a worker or machine and notes whether the activity is *productive* or *idle*. From the *proportion* of idle observations, the *fraction of time* spent idle is statistically inferred.

**Distribution Used:** **Binomial Distribution** (or Normal approximation for large $n$).

**Sample Size Formula (for a given accuracy L at confidence level Z):**

$$
n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot q}{L^2}
$$

where $p$ = proportion of idle time (from pilot study), $q = 1 - p$, $L$ = absolute error tolerance, $Z$ = standard normal value (1.96 for 95%, 2.58 for 99%).

**Confidence Limits for the True Proportion:**

$$
p \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p \cdot q}{n}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Auto Industry Use Case:** Estimating machine utilisation in a press shop, proportion of delay in a paint shop, or % of time a forklift remains idle.

---

### 2.2.4 Synthesis from Standard Data

Standard times for elements (e.g., "tighten M10 bolt") are stored in a database. For a new model, ST is built up:

$$
\text{ST}_{\text{new job}} = \sum_{i} \text{ST}_{\text{standard element}, i} + \text{Job-specific allowances}
$$

Used by Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai, and Tata for variant engineering.

---

### 2.2.5 Analytical Estimating

The estimator mentally breaks the job into broad elements and assigns a time based on experience and historical data. Fastest but least accurate. Used in *budget proposals* and *make-or-buy* decisions.

---

## 2.3 The Allowance Question — Hidden but Critical

Allowances compensate for **non-productive but legitimate** time. They are **added after rating**, never before.

### Standard Allowance Structure (KTU Convention)

| Allowance Type | Typical % | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Personal (P)** | **5%** | Drinking water, toilets |
| **Rest / Fatigue (R)** | **10–12%** | Recovery from physical/mental effort |
| **Contingency (C)** | **3–5%** | Minor delays, supervisor talk |
| **Process (Pr)** | **2–5%** | Waiting for crane, coolant |
| **Total (A)** | **≈ 20%** | Sum of above |

**Special Allowances** (added separately, not in $A$):
- *Interference allowance* — for group work.
- *Abnormal allowance* — for unusually harsh conditions.
- *Policy allowance* — for management-controlled rest.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal Time | $NT = OT \times R$ | $R$ as decimal; e.g., 110% = 1.10 |
| 2 | Standard Time (additive) | $ST = NT + (NT \times A)$ | $A$ as decimal of NT |
| 3 | Standard Time (multiplicative) | $ST = \dfrac{NT}{1 - A}$ | $A$ as fraction of ST |
| 4 | Total Normal Time | $NT_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} OT_i \times R_i$ | Element-wise summation |
| 5 | Work Sampling — Sample Size | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot q}{L^2}$ | Binomial large-sample |
| 6 | Work Sampling — Precision | $L = Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{pq}{n}}$ | Absolute error |
| 7 | 95% Confidence Interval for $p$ | $p \pm 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{pq}{n}}$ | $Z = 1.96$ |
| 8 | PMTS Standard Time | $ST = \left(\sum TM_i\right) \cdot (1 + A)$ | $TM$ in TMU or seconds |
| 9 | MTM Conversion | $1 \text{ TMU} = 0.00001 \text{ hr} = 0.0006 \text{ min} = 0.036 \text{ s}$ | KTU-standard |
| 10 | MOST Conversion | $1 \text{ MOST Unit} = 0.00036 \text{ hr} = 0.0216 \text{ min} = 1.296 \text{ s}$ | Index sequence |
| 11 | Performance Rating Index | $R = \dfrac{\text{BSI Normal Pace}}{\text{Observed Pace}}$ | BSI = 100 |
| 12 | Takt Time | $T_t = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | Drives ST targets |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **fully expanded, line-by-line**. No algebraic step is omitted.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Observed Time to Standard Time

**Premise:** A worker performs an element in 1.20 min, observed pace judged at 110 on the BSI-100 scale, and the operation carries a 15% allowance.

**Step 1 — Convert Rating to a Multiplier**

$$
R = \dfrac{110}{100} = 1.10
$$

*Logic:* A rating of 110 means the worker is 10% faster than the BSI normal pace (100). To bring it to *normal* pace, we *expand* the observed time by 10%.

**Step 2 — Compute Normal Time (NT)**

$$
NT = OT \times R = 1.20 \times 1.10 = 1.32 \text{ min}
$$

*Logic:* If a 110% worker took 1.20 min, a 100% worker would have taken 10% longer, i.e., 1.32 min.

**Step 3 — Convert Allowance to a Fraction**

$$
A_{\text{frac}} = \dfrac{15}{100} = 0.15
$$

*Logic:* Allowance is 15% of the Standard Time (industry convention).

**Step 4 — Compute Standard Time (ST) — Method A (Multiplicative)**

$$
ST = \dfrac{NT}{1 - A_{\text{frac}}} = \dfrac{1.32}{1 - 0.15} = \dfrac{1.32}{0.85}
$$

$$
\boxed{ST \approx 1.5529 \text{ min per piece}}
$$

*Logic:* Allowance is 15% of ST, so NT must be 85% of ST. Hence $ST = NT / 0.85$.

**Step 5 — Cross-Check (Method B — Additive)**

$$
ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 1.32 \times 1.15
$$

$$
ST_{\text{additive}} = 1.518 \text{ min per piece}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The two methods give slightly different answers** (1.553 vs 1.518). KTU Board Examiners accept **either**, but in Part B (14-mark) problems, students are expected to **state both** and clarify the convention used. Method A ($ST = NT/(1-A)$) is the **preferred KTU convention** because allowance is conventionally defined as a percentage of *Standard Time*, not of *Normal Time*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multi-Element Time Study (Full Elemental Computation)

**Given (Auto Body Welding Cell — Spot-Welding Job):**

| Element | Observed Time (min) | Performance Rating |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Pick & place part | 0.45 | 100% |
| 2. Position in jig | 1.20 | 90% |
| 3. Weld (machine auto-cycle) | 0.80 | 110% |
| 4. Inspect & unload | 0.30 | 100% |

Total Allowance = 20%.

**Step 1 — Element-wise Normal Time**

$$
NT_1 = 0.45 \times 1.00 = 0.4500 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_2 = 1.20 \times 0.90 = 1.0800 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_3 = 0.80 \times 1.10 = 0.8800 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_4 = 0.30 \times 1.00 = 0.3000 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum Total Normal Time**

$$
NT_{\text{total}} = 0.4500 + 1.0800 + 0.8800 + 0.3000
$$

$$
NT_{\text{total}} = 2.7100 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Allowance (20%)**

$$
ST = \dfrac{NT_{\text{total}}}{1 - 0.20} = \dfrac{2.71}{0.80}
$$

$$
\boxed{ST = 3.3875 \text{ min per job}}
$$

*Engineering Interpretation:* The auto body spot-welding station can produce approximately $\frac{60}{3.3875} \approx 17.71$ jobs per hour, which means for a daily demand of 1,200 units, the plant needs $\lceil 1200 / (17.71 \times 8) \rceil = \lceil 8.47 \rceil = 9$ such stations (in 2 shifts, demand halved to 600 units, needs ~5 stations).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Work Sampling — Required Number of Observations

**Problem Statement:**
A supervisor of an automotive **press shop** wants to estimate the *proportion of machine idle time*. A pilot study of 100 observations revealed that machines were idle in 35 observations. Required accuracy: $\pm 5\%$, Confidence level: 95%.

**Step 1 — Extract Pilot Proportions**

$$
p = \dfrac{35}{100} = 0.35, \quad q = 1 - p = 0.65
$$

**Step 2 — Select Z-Value (95% Confidence)**

$$
Z = 1.96
$$

*Logic:* From standard normal tables, 95% of the area lies within 1.96 standard deviations of the mean.

**Step 3 — Specify Accuracy**

$$
L = \dfrac{5}{100} = 0.05
$$

*Logic:* The supervisor is willing to accept an error of ±5 percentage points around the true proportion.

**Step 4 — Apply the Sample Size Formula**

$$
n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot q}{L^2}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.35 \times 0.65}{(0.05)^2}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{3.8416 \times 0.2275}{0.0025}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{0.8740}{0.0025}
$$

$$
n = 349.60
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 350 \text{ observations (rounded up)}}
$$

*Engineering Interpretation:* The supervisor (or a team of observers) must make **350 random observations** of the press machines — distributed over several days and shifts — to estimate idle time with the desired accuracy. If $L$ is relaxed to ±10%, the sample size drops to $n = 350 / 4 = 88$ observations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Confidence Limits for True Proportion

**Given:** 400 random observations of welding robots; 120 were observed *idle*. Find 95% confidence limits for the true idle proportion.

**Step 1 — Sample Proportion**

$$
\hat{p} = \dfrac{120}{400} = 0.30, \quad \hat{q} = 0.70
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Error**

$$
SE = \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p} \cdot \hat{q}}{n}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.30 \times 0.70}{400}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.21}{400}} = \sqrt{0.000525}
$$

$$
SE = 0.022913
$$

**Step 3 — Margin of Error (95% Confidence)**

$$
ME = Z \times SE = 1.96 \times 0.022913 = 0.044910
$$

**Step 4 — Confidence Interval**

$$
p = \hat{p} \pm ME = 0.30 \pm 0.0449
$$

$$
p_{\text{lower}} = 0.30 - 0.0449 = 0.2551 \quad (\text{i.e., } 25.51\%)
$$

$$
p_{\text{upper}} = 0.30 + 0.0449 = 0.3449 \quad (\text{i.e., } 34.49\%)
$$

$$
\boxed{25.51\% \leq p_{\text{idle}} \leq 34.49\% \text{ (95\% confidence)}}
$$

*Engineering Interpretation:* The plant manager can state with 95% confidence that the *true* robot idle proportion lies between 25.51% and 34.49% — a critical input for capacity-planning decisions.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — PMTS (MTM) Standard Time for an Auto Assembly Operation

**Operation:** Mount a dashboard in a car cockpit. The MTM analysis yields the following motion sequence:

| Motion Code | Description | TMU |
|---|---|---|
| R20B | Reach for dashboard | 20 |
| G1A | Grasp (loose) | 10 |
| M20B | Move to cockpit | 20 |
| P1SE | Position (symmetric) | 20 |
| RL1 | Release | 10 |
| **Total TMU** |  | **80 TMU** |

Allowance = 25%.

**Step 1 — Convert TMU to Hours**

$$
\text{Total TMU} = 80 \text{ TMU}
$$

$$
\text{Time in hours} = 80 \times 0.00001 = 0.0008 \text{ hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Minutes**

$$
\text{Time in minutes} = 0.0008 \times 60 = 0.048 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Add Allowance**

$$
ST = 0.048 \times (1 + 0.25) = 0.048 \times 1.25
$$

$$
\boxed{ST = 0.0600 \text{ min} = 3.6 \text{ seconds}}
$$

*Engineering Interpretation:* Each station on the dashboard-assembly line can be allocated 3.6 s. For a Takt Time of 60 s, the dashboard mounting can be combined with several other small motions to fill the station's cycle.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions are aligned with the KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, Bloom's Taxonomy Level distribution, and CO-mapping for **PEAUT524 — Operations Management in Auto Industry**.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Answer any two out of three, typically 2 × 3 = 6 marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**"Define Work Measurement. List any four techniques of work measurement used in the automotive industry."** `[3 Marks]`

#### Model Answer:
> **Work Measurement** is the application of techniques designed to establish the time required for a qualified worker to perform a specified job at a defined level of performance (BSI definition).
> 
> **Four techniques used in the automotive industry:**
> 1. **Time Study (Stopwatch Method)** — for repetitive assembly operations.
> 2. **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (MTM/MOST)** — for new model pre-planning.
> 3. **Work Sampling (Ratio Delay Study)** — for long, irregular tasks like press-shop monitoring.
> 4. **Synthesis from Standard Data** — for variant engineering and model derivatives.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**"What is the difference between Normal Time and Standard Time?"** `[3 Marks]`

#### Model Answer:
| Aspect | Normal Time (NT) | Standard Time (ST) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Time a qualified worker takes at *normal* (100%) pace. | NT plus allowances for rest, personal needs, contingencies. |
| **Formula** | $NT = OT \times R$ | $ST = NT / (1 - A)$ |
| **Includes Allowances?** | No | Yes |
| **Use** | Performance benchmarking. | Planning, costing, line balancing, incentives. |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Answer any one full question — ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1 Choice Pattern]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Explain any **five** techniques of work measurement with their relative merits and demerits. Indicate where each is best applied in an automotive plant."** `[7 Marks, Bloom Level: Understand / Apply]`

#### Model Answer (Tabulated, 7-Mark Format):

| # | Technique | Working Principle | Merits | Demerits | Auto Industry Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time Study** | Stopwatch-based direct measurement of a worker's actual time, then rating & allowance. | Accurate for short repetitive jobs; widely understood; permits performance rating. | Stopwatch is intrusive; rating is subjective; needs trained observer. | Spot-welding, paint shop, machining cells. |
| 2 | **PMTS (MTM/MOST)** | Synthesises time from pre-determined basic-motion time tables; no stopwatch. | Objective; offline-capable (used before the line exists); consistent across plants. | Time-consuming to learn; less accurate for non-standard motions. | New car model pre-engineering (Ford, Toyota, Maruti). |
| 3 | **Work Sampling** | Statistical inference from random instantaneous observations. | Non-intrusive; can study many machines/operators at once; cost-effective for long tasks. | Less precise; requires large sample; statistical knowledge required. | Press-shop machine utilisation, paint-shop delay analysis. |
| 4 | **Standard Data / Synthesis** | Builds up ST from stored elemental standard times. | Fast for variant jobs; consistent across variants. | Database must be maintained; errors propagate. | Variant engineering of a common platform (e.g., Tiago, Nexon, Punch variants). |
| 5 | **Analytical Estimating** | Experienced estimator's mental breakdown of a job. | Fastest, cheapest; useful for budgeting. | Lowest accuracy; depends on estimator's skill. | Make-or-buy decisions, supplier quotation benchmarking. |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"In an automotive sheet-metal press shop, a time study was carried out on a power-press operation. The data recorded for the elements is as follows. Compute the Standard Time per piece if the allowances are: Personal 6%, Rest 10%, Contingency 4%."** `[7 Marks, Bloom Level: Apply / Compute]`

**Given Data:**

| Element | Observed Time (min) | Performance Rating |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Pick blank | 0.30 | 100 |
| 2. Position in die | 0.50 | 90 |
| 3. Press (auto) | 0.40 | 110 |
| 4. Unload & stack | 0.25 | 100 |
| 5. Inspect | 0.20 | 95 |

**Step 1 — Element-wise Normal Time (NT = OT × R/100)**

$$
NT_1 = 0.30 \times \dfrac{100}{100} = 0.3000 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_2 = 0.50 \times \dfrac{90}{100} = 0.4500 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_3 = 0.40 \times \dfrac{110}{100} = 0.4400 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_4 = 0.25 \times \dfrac{100}{100} = 0.2500 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_5 = 0.20 \times \dfrac{95}{100} = 0.1900 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Normal Time**

$$
NT_{\text{total}} = 0.30 + 0.45 + 0.44 + 0.25 + 0.19
$$

$$
NT_{\text{total}} = 1.63 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Allowance**

$$
A = 6\% + 10\% + 4\% = 20\% = 0.20
$$

**Step 4 — Standard Time (Preferred KTU Convention)**

$$
ST = \dfrac{NT}{1 - A} = \dfrac{1.63}{1 - 0.20} = \dfrac{1.63}{0.80}
$$

$$
\boxed{ST = 2.0375 \text{ min per piece}}
$$

**Step 5 — Production Rate**

$$
\text{Pieces per hour} = \dfrac{60}{2.0375} \approx 29.45 \text{ pieces/hr}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rating % to decimal** before multiplication.
> 2. **Using the wrong ST formula** — $NT \times (1+A)$ vs $NT/(1-A)$. The KTU answer key uses the multiplicative form.
> 3. **Omitting the unit "minutes"** in the final answer.
> 4. **Not summing allowances** (giving only one allowance, ignoring the rest).
> 5. **Skipping the cross-check** step — students are expected to verify the answer logically.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[Alternative Choice — ESE 2024 Pattern]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Define Time Study. With a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step procedure of conducting a time study in an automotive assembly line. What are the various methods of rating the worker's performance?"** `[7 Marks, Bloom Level: Remember / Understand]`

#### Model Answer:

**Definition (1 mark):**
> Time Study is a work measurement technique in which a trained observer uses a stopwatch (or electronic timing device) to record the time taken by a qualified worker to perform each element of a specified job at a defined level of performance, after which the observed times are normalised by a performance rating and increased by relaxation allowances to obtain the Standard Time.

**Procedure (4 marks — Flowchart Style):**

1. **Job selection & definition** — select operation; describe the method on a time-study observation sheet.
2. **Operator selection** — pick an average-skilled, trained worker.
3. **Elemental breakdown** — divide the job into 5–15 logical elements (each element = a clear work-unit).
4. **Recording observed times** — time each element with a stopwatch to 0.01 min.
5. **Rating performance** — judge the worker's pace against BSI normal (100) for each element.
6. **Compute Normal Time** — $NT = OT \times R$ for each element.
7. **Apply Allowances** — add rest, personal, contingency, process allowances.
8. **Compute Standard Time** — $ST = NT / (1 - A)$.
9. **Publish & use** — for line balancing, costing, and incentive wage payment.

**Performance Rating Methods (2 marks):**

| Method | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **BSI (0–150)** | Pace rating; 100 = BSI normal | UK, Europe, India (BSI/IS) |
| **Westinghouse (4-Factor)** | Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency each rated on 6-point scale | USA |
| **Synthetic Rating** | Rating computed by comparison with predetermined time | PMTS-based plants |
| **Objective Rating** | Pace + Skill factor | Modern best practice |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"In a work sampling study of an automotive engine assembly conveyor, 500 random observations were taken. The conveyor was found stopped (idle) in 150 observations. Determine (i) the 95% confidence limits for the true proportion of idle time, and (ii) the number of additional observations required to achieve an accuracy of ±3% at 95% confidence."** `[7 Marks, Bloom Level: Apply / Compute]`

**Given:** $n_1 = 500$, $x = 150$, $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence), target $L_2 = 0.03$.

#### Part (i) — Confidence Limits for True Proportion

**Step 1 — Sample Proportion**

$$
\hat{p} = \dfrac{150}{500} = 0.30, \quad \hat{q} = 0.70
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Error**

$$
SE = \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p} \hat{q}}{n_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.30 \times 0.70}{500}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.21}{500}} = \sqrt{0.00042}
$$

$$
SE = 0.020494
$$

**Step 3 — Margin of Error**

$$
ME = 1.96 \times 0.020494 = 0.040168
$$

**Step 4 — 95% Confidence Interval**

$$
p = 0.30 \pm 0.0402
$$

$$
\boxed{0.2598 \leq p \leq 0.3402 \quad \text{i.e., } 25.98\% \text{ to } 34.02\%}
$$

#### Part (ii) — Additional Observations Needed for $\pm 3\%$ Accuracy

**Step 1 — Apply Sample-Size Formula (using the latest estimate of $p$)**

$$
n_2 = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot q}{L^2} = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.30 \times 0.70}{(0.03)^2}
$$

$$
n_2 = \dfrac{3.8416 \times 0.21}{0.0009} = \dfrac{0.8067}{0.0009} = 896.36
$$

$$
n_2 = 897 \text{ observations (rounded up)}
$$

**Step 2 — Additional Observations**

$$
\boxed{n_{\text{additional}} = n_2 - n_1 = 897 - 500 = 397 \text{ additional observations}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Frequent Mark Loss in Work-Sampling Questions:**
> 1. **Using $Z = 2$** instead of $Z = 1.96$ (loses ½ mark on a 7-mark question).
> 2. **Forgetting to square $L$** in the denominator ($L^2$, not $L$).
> 3. **Mixing up $p$ and $q$** ($p$ = idle, $q$ = working; both must be between 0 and 1).
> 4. **Not rounding up** $n$ (KTU mandates rounding up — never down).
> 5. **Confusing "additional" with "total"** observations in part (ii).

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE Module 1**

### A. Definitional Anchors
- **Work Measurement** = time determination for a qualified worker at a defined level of performance.
- **Time Study** = stopwatch-based direct measurement.
- **PMTS (MTM / MOST)** = predetermined, offline, motion-based.
- **Work Sampling** = statistical, random observations, binomial-based.
- **Standard Data** = pre-stored elemental standards.
- **Analytical Estimating** = judgment-based, fastest, least accurate.

### B. The Master Equation (Must Memorise)
- $NT = OT \times R$
- $ST = \dfrac{NT}{1 - A}$ ← KTU-preferred
- $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ ← Alternative convention
- $n_{\text{work sampling}} = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot q}{L^2}$
- $p \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{pq}{n}}$ ← 95% CI uses $Z = 1.96$

### C. Key Numerical Conversions
- $1 \text{ TMU} = 0.00001 \text{ hr} = 0.0006 \text{ min} = 0.036 \text{ s}$
- $1 \text{ MOST Unit} = 0.00036 \text{ hr} = 0.0216 \text{ min} = 1.296 \text{ s}$
- $1 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ s} = 1/60 \text{ hr}$
- $1 \text{ hr} = 60 \text{ min} = 3600 \text{ s}$

### D. Allowance Composition
- Personal = 5%
- Rest = 10–12%
- Contingency = 3–5%
- Process = 2–5%
- **Total typical = ~20%**

### E. Selection Rule of Thumb
| Job Type | Use |
|---|---|
| Repetitive, short cycle | Time Study |
| New model, not yet built | PMTS (MTM/MOST) |
| Long, irregular, multi-machine | Work Sampling |
| Variant of an existing model | Standard Data (Synthesis) |
| Quick budget estimate | Analytical Estimating |

### F. Common KTU-Exam Mistakes
- ❌ Rating as a percentage value (e.g., 110) but applying directly without dividing by 100.
- ❌ Using $L$ (not $L^2$) in the work-sampling formula.
- ❌ Using $Z = 1.645$ (90% CI) instead of 1.96 (95% CI) unless stated.
- ❌ Stopping at $NT$ — forgetting allowances → no Standard Time → 2-mark penalty.
- ❌ Not stating the units (min vs hr) in the final answer.
- ❌ Confusing **observed time** with **normal time**.

### G. Auto-Industry Special Mentions (Valued by KTU Examiners)
- Mention **Takt Time** alignment when answering line-balancing questions.
- Reference **MTM-UAS** or **MOST** specifically when answering PMTS questions.
- Reference **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** when discussing work sampling of machines.
- Reference **Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, Ashok Leyland** in any application-oriented answer.
- Mention **Industry 4.0 / IoT-based automatic time study** as a contemporary trend.

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 1 / TOPIC: TECHNIQUES OF WORK MEASUREMENT**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure of Conducting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Procedure of Conducting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Procedure of Conducting (Operations Study Procedure)** is the systematic, step-by-step methodology employed by industrial engineers and operations managers to analyze, record, examine, and improve work methods and process efficiencies in manufacturing and service environments. In the automotive industry, it represents a standardized framework for executing Method Study, Work Measurement, Process Analysis, and Productivity Improvement initiatives.

The procedure of conducting operations studies is a structured investigation methodology governed by internationally recognized standards (such as those from the **International Labour Organization (ILO)**) and forms the backbone of **Industrial Engineering (IE)** practice. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT524)** context, this procedure is taught as the foundation for analyzing assembly line operations, engine manufacturing processes, and body shop workflows within automotive Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) and their Tier-1/Tier-2 suppliers.

The procedure is broadly classified into **two interlocking pillars**:

| Pillar | Core Focus | Primary Output |
|--------|------------|----------------|
| **Method Study** | "Doing things better" - improving the method | Standardized work instructions |
| **Work Measurement** | "Doing it faster" - measuring work content | Standard times and labour standards |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Doctor's Diagnostic" Analogy** — Imagine a patient (the manufacturing process) visits a doctor (the Industrial Engineer). The doctor does NOT immediately prescribe medicine. Instead, the doctor follows a strict procedure: **(1)** Listen to symptoms (Identify the problem), **(2)** Order blood tests and X-rays (Record the current state), **(3)** Compare results with normal values (Examine critically), **(4)** Identify the disease root cause (Develop the improved method), **(5)** Prescribe a treatment plan (Install the new method), and **(6)** Schedule a follow-up (Maintain the standard). This six-step diagnostic procedure is EXACTLY the procedure of conducting an operations study.

In an **automotive plant**, when the production rate of a **crankshaft machining line** drops or when **rework percentages** increase on a **paint shop conveyor**, the operations manager does not "guess" a fix. Instead, they invoke the formal **Procedure of Conducting** — a disciplined, evidence-based investigation sequence that ensures every improvement is justified by data, not assumption.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics in Operations Studies

The procedure of conducting operations management activities relies on the following standard metrics (all **must be memorized** for KTU examinations):

- **Standard Time (ST)** — measured in **minutes** or **seconds**
- **Observed Time (OT)** — measured in **minutes** or **seconds**
- **Normal Time (NT)** — measured in **minutes** or **seconds**
- **Basic Time (BT)** — measured in **minutes** or **seconds**
- **Rating Factor (RF)** — dimensionless ratio
- **Allowances (A)** — expressed as a **percentage (%)**
- **Performance Rating (PR)** — dimensionless ratio
- **Frequency of Occurrence (F)** — number of times per cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Procedure of Conducting forms **Module 1 foundational knowledge** because every subsequent module — Production Planning, Quality Management, Supply Chain, Lean Manufacturing — is built upon the analytical discipline that this procedure instills.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Six-Step Procedure of Conducting (ILO Framework)

The procedure of conducting an operations study in the automotive industry follows a **rigorous six-step universal framework** codified by the **International Labour Organization (ILO)**. Each step has a specific objective, specific tools, and specific deliverables.

### Step 1: SELECT (Define the Problem & Scope)
- **Objective:** Identify the specific work area, operation, or process to be studied.
- **Tools Used:** Pareto Analysis, Brainstorming, Gemba Walks, Production Reports, Customer Complaints database.
- **Automotive Industry Application:** Selecting a bottleneck station on a Toyota Camry final assembly line (e.g., dashboard mating station) where takt time violations occur.
- **Deliverable:** A **Project Charter** defining scope, boundaries, expected benefits, and timeline.

### Step 2: RECORD (Document the Current Method)
- **Objective:** Capture every micro-detail of the existing work method using objective observation tools.
- **Tools Used:**
  - **Process Charts** (Operation, Inspection, Transport, Delay, Storage symbols)
  - **Flow Process Charts** (Man, Material, Equipment)
  - **Two-Handed Process Charts** (for repetitive manual tasks)
  - **Multiple Activity Charts** (for multi-machine/multi-operator scenarios)
  - **String Diagrams** (for movement patterns over floor layouts)
  - **Cycle Graphs & Chronocyclegraphs** (using motion picture cameras)
  - **Video Recordings** (modern automotive plants use 4K high-speed cameras)
- **Automotive Industry Application:** Recording the 47 micro-motions a welder performs when joining a floor pan to the side rail using a **Yaskawa robotic welder** in a body shop.
- **Deliverable:** Comprehensive **Process Charts, Charts & Diagrams** in standard BS ISO 9001:2015 documentation format.

### Step 3: EXAMINE (Critical Analysis of the Recorded Data)
- **Objective:** Subject the recorded method to systematic questioning using the **5W1H + 2W** questioning technique.
- **The 5W1H + 2W Technique:**
  - **WHAT** is being done? → Purpose Question
  - **WHY** is it being done? → Necessity Question
  - **WHERE** is it being done? → Place Question
  - **WHEN** is it being done? → Sequence Question
  - **WHO** is doing it? → Person Question
  - **HOW** is it being done? → Means Question
  - **WITH WHAT** is it being done? → Equipment/Tool Question
  - **AT WHAT COST** is it being done? → Economic Question
- **Tools Used:** The questioning technique paired with **Principles of Motion Economy** (Therbligs - 18 fundamental motions identified by Frank Gilbreth).
- **Automotive Industry Application:** Examining a **riveting operation** on a BMW i3 carbon fiber body frame to identify non-value-added motions.

### Step 4: DEVELOP (Design the Improved Method)
- **Objective:** Create a superior, more efficient, safer, and ergonomically sound method.
- **Design Constraints:** Must satisfy the **7 Principles of Motion Economy**, the **Principles of Work Design** (job satisfaction, skill utilization, autonomy), and all regulatory safety standards (OSHA, ISO 12100).
- **Tools Used:** Mock-ups, simulations (Siemens Plant Simulation, DELMIA, Tecnomatix), 3D digital twins.
- **Automotive Industry Application:** Designing an improved dashboard assembly method using **collaborative robots (cobots)** to reduce the RULA (Rapid Upper Limb Assessment) score.

### Step 5: INSTALL (Implement the New Method)
- **Objective:** Put the developed method into actual production practice.
- **Sub-Steps:** 
  1. Gain acceptance from workers, supervisors, and union representatives
  2. Train the workforce thoroughly
  3. Phase out old equipment/jigs as needed
  4. Establish the new standard operating procedure (SOP)
- **Automotive Industry Application:** Installing a new **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle)** routing algorithm in a Mercedes-Benz S-Class body-in-white (BIW) shop.

### Step 6: MAINTAIN (Sustain the New Standard)
- **Objective:** Ensure the new method does not regress to old habits.
- **Mechanisms:** Periodic audits, control charts, standard work audits, kaizen workshops.
- **Automotive Industry Application:** Maintaining the new SOP through **layered process audits (LPA)** and **andon system** monitoring.

## 2.2 The Procedure of Conducting Work Measurement

Work measurement is the second pillar and follows its own distinct procedure:

> [!NOTE]
> **Work Measurement Procedure (Sequential Steps):**
> 1. **Establish the standard method** (Method study must be completed first)
> 2. **Break the work into elements** (Elemental breakdown)
> 3. **Time each element** using stopwatch, PMTS, or predetermined time systems
> 4. **Rate the operator's performance** (Rating Factor)
> 5. **Compute the Normal Time (NT)**
> 6. **Apply relaxation and contingency allowances**
> 7. **Compute the Standard Time (ST)**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains **all** high-yield formulas mandatory for KTU Board Examinations on this topic:

| Formula Symbol | Formula Expression | Definition of Variables | Units |
|----------------|--------------------|--------------------------|-------|
| $OT$ | $OT = \text{Stopwatch Reading} \times \text{Corrections}$ | $OT$ = Observed Time (actual time measured) | seconds |
| $NT$ | $NT = OT \times RF$ | $NT$ = Normal Time, $OT$ = Observed Time, $RF$ = Rating Factor | seconds |
| $ST$ | $ST = NT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A}{100}\right)$ | $ST$ = Standard Time, $A$ = Total Allowance Percentage | seconds |
| $ST$ (Alternative) | $ST = NT + \text{Absolute Allowance}$ | When allowance is in absolute time | seconds |
| $RF$ | $RF = \dfrac{\text{Observed Performance Speed}}{\text{Normal Performance Speed}}$ | $RF > 1$ faster than normal, $RF < 1$ slower | dimensionless |
| $BT$ | $BT = \text{Predetermined Time from PMTS}$ | $BT$ = Basic Time from Methods Time Measurement | TMU or seconds |
| $NT$ (PMTS) | $NT = BT \times RF$ | PMTS-based Normal Time calculation | seconds |
| $Cycles\_to\_Record$ | $n = \left(\dfrac{40 \sqrt{N \cdot \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}}{\sum x_i}\right)^2$ | $n$ = sample size, $N$ = preliminary trials, $x_i$ = observed time | dimensionless |
| $Line\_Efficiency$ | $LE = \dfrac{\sum CT_i}{n \times \text{Bottleneck CT}} \times 100$ | $LE$ = Line Efficiency, $CT$ = Cycle Time | percent |
| $Standard\_Output$ | $SO = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{ST}$ | Production capacity per period | units/period |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern automotive operations, the procedure of conducting operations studies is utilized for:

- **Capacity Planning:** Determining the throughput of an engine assembly line (e.g., Maruti Suzuki's Manesar plant producing 1.5 million engines annually)
- **Cost Estimation:** Computing the labour cost per vehicle for bidding on new contracts
- **Lean Transformation:** Supporting **VSM (Value Stream Mapping)** initiatives
- **Ergonomics:** Reducing **MSD (Musculoskeletal Disorders)** through motion study
- **Industry 4.0 Integration:** Providing baseline data for **digital twin** simulations and AI-based process optimization
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Standard Time Formula

The **Standard Time (ST)** is the **single most important formula** in the KTU 2024 Scheme Operations Management syllabus. The complete derivation proceeds as follows:

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** A KTU 2024 examiner often asks: *"Derive the Standard Time formula starting from the definition of Observed Time."* The following 7-step derivation is the canonical KTU Board answer.

### Step 1: Define the Observed Time (OT)
The Observed Time is the **raw time recorded by the observer** (Industrial Engineer) using a stopwatch, video timestamp, or digital counter. Mathematically:

$$OT = T_{recorded}$$

where $T_{recorded}$ is the stopwatch reading for a single work element. No correction has been applied at this stage.

### Step 2: Apply the Performance Rating
A skilled observer recognizes that no operator works at exactly the **"Normal Pace"** (defined by ILO as 100 on the 0–100 Westinghouse scale, or 1.0 on the decimal rating scale). The observer multiplies the observed time by a **Rating Factor (RF)** to convert it to a "what it would have taken a normal operator."

$$\text{Adjusted Time} = OT \times RF$$

### Step 3: Define the Normal Time (NT)
The product of Observed Time and Rating Factor is defined as the **Normal Time (NT)**:

$$NT = OT \times RF$$

This represents the time a **qualified, trained worker** would take working at a **normal pace** to perform the element under standard conditions.

### Step 4: Introduce the Concept of Allowances
A worker cannot sustain effort for the entire shift without breaks, personal needs, or unavoidable delays. **Allowances (A)** are added to Normal Time to account for:
- **Relaxation Allowance (RA):** Fatigue recovery
- **Personal Needs Allowance (PNA):** Drinking water, restroom
- **Contingency Allowance (CA):** Minor breakdowns, tool changes
- **Special Allowance (SA):** Process-specific (e.g., waiting for paint to cure)

The total allowance as a percentage is:

$$A_{\text{total}} = RA + PNA + CA + SA$$

with typical industrial values in the range of $A_{\text{total}} \in [10\%, 25\%]$ of Normal Time.

### Step 5: Convert Percentage Allowance to Multiplier
The allowance is expressed as a percentage of Normal Time. To convert to a multiplier:

$$\text{Allowance Multiplier} = \left(1 + \dfrac{A_{\text{total}}}{100}\right)$$

### Step 6: Compute the Standard Time
The Standard Time is obtained by multiplying the Normal Time by the allowance multiplier:

$$ST = NT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A_{\text{total}}}{100}\right)$$

### Step 7: Substitute NT = OT × RF (Final Canonical Form)
Substituting the Normal Time expression from Step 3 yields the **complete KTU Board formula**:

$$\boxed{ST = OT \times RF \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A_{\text{total}}}{100}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** This boxed equation is the **highest-weightage formula** in the entire Module 1. Memorize it letter-perfect, including the parenthesis placement. The KTU Board Examiner will NOT award full marks if the parentheses are mis-placed.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem Statement:** In an automotive plant, an Industrial Engineer conducts a time study on a **brake disc mounting operation** on a Maruti Suzuki Baleno assembly line. The following data was collected:

| Element | Observed Time (sec) | Rating Factor (%) | Allowance (%) |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Pick up brake disc from conveyor | 8 | 110 | 15 |
| Align holes with caliper bracket | 12 | 95 | 15 |
| Insert 4 mounting bolts | 20 | 105 | 15 |
| Torque bolts to 90 Nm | 15 | 100 | 15 |
| Place wrench back on rack | 5 | 90 | 15 |

**Required:** Calculate the **Standard Time** for the complete brake disc mounting operation.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute Normal Time for each element using $NT_i = OT_i \times \dfrac{RF_i}{100}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_1 &= 8 \times \dfrac{110}{100} = 8.800 \text{ sec} \\[6pt]
NT_2 &= 12 \times \dfrac{95}{100} = 11.400 \text{ sec} \\[6pt]
NT_3 &= 20 \times \dfrac{105}{100} = 21.000 \text{ sec} \\[6pt]
NT_4 &= 15 \times \dfrac{100}{100} = 15.000 \text{ sec} \\[6pt]
NT_5 &= 5 \times \dfrac{90}{100} = 4.500 \text{ sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum the Normal Times to obtain the total Normal Time:

$$
NT_{total} = 8.800 + 11.400 + 21.000 + 15.000 + 4.500 = 60.700 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the allowance multiplier (15%):

$$
ST = 60.700 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{15}{100}\right) = 60.700 \times 1.15
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final Standard Time:

$$
\boxed{ST = 69.805 \text{ seconds per operation}}
$$

**Step 5 (Optional KTU Bonus):** Determine the **Standard Output per hour** assuming the operator works continuously:

$$
SO = \dfrac{3600 \text{ sec/hr}}{69.805 \text{ sec/op}} = 51.58 \text{ operations per hour}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The plant can produce approximately **51 brake disc mountings per hour** per operator under the standard method. This number directly informs **line balancing decisions** and **takt time calculations** in subsequent modules.

## 3.3 Sample Size Determination Derivation

The KTU Board often asks for the **number of cycles to be observed** in a time study to achieve a specified **accuracy and confidence level**. The derivation uses the **statistical normal distribution** principle:

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{Z \cdot s}{E \cdot \bar{x}}\right)^2
$$

where $Z$ = standard normal variate (1.96 for 95% confidence), $s$ = sample standard deviation, $\bar{x}$ = sample mean, $E$ = acceptable relative error (typically $\pm 5\%$). This formula is then re-expressed in the KTU-specific form using the **40√N** shortcut:

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{40 \cdot \sqrt{N \cdot \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}}{\sum x_i}\right)^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students often confuse the **absolute error** with the **relative error**. KTU Board expects **relative error** unless the question explicitly states "absolute."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Six-Step Procedure Flow (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **complete six-step ILO Procedure of Conducting** as a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow. This diagram is the **standard KTU Board expected schematic** when questions ask for a "flowchart of the method study procedure."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Step 0: Trigger Event - Productivity Loss Detected]) --> Step1

    Step1["Step 1: SELECT - Define the Problem and Scope using Pareto Analysis and Gemba Walk"]:::processNode
    Step2["Step 2: RECORD - Document Current Method using Process Charts, String Diagrams, and Two-Handed Charts"]:::processNode
    Step3["Step 3: EXAMINE - Critical Analysis using 5W1H Plus 2W Questioning Technique and Therbligs"]:::processNode
    Step4["Step 4: DEVELOP - Design Improved Method using Motion Economy Principles and Digital Twin Simulation"]:::processNode
    Step5["Step 5: INSTALL - Implement New Method with Worker Training and SOP Documentation"]:::processNode
    Step6["Step 6: MAINTAIN - Sustain Standard via Layered Process Audits and Kaizen"]:::processNode

    Feedback["Continuous Feedback Loop - Data Driven Iteration"]:::feedbackNode
    Output(["Deliverable: Improved Productivity and Reduced Standard Time"]):::outputNode

    Step1 ==> Step2
    Step2 ==> Step3
    Step3 ==> Step4
    Step4 ==> Step5
    Step5 ==> Step6
    Step6 ==> Output
    Step6 -.-> Feedback
    Feedback -.-> Step1

    classDef processNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef feedbackNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C
    classDef outputNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:3px,color:#1B5E20
```

## 4.2 Work Measurement Sub-Procedure Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Standardized Method from Method Study] --> B[Step A: Break Work into Elements]
    B --> C[Step B: Select Time Study Technique]
    C --> D{Technique Type}
    D -->|Stopwatch| E[Continuous or Snap-Back Timing]
    D -->|PMTS| F[Methods Time Measurement or Maynard Operation Sequence]
    D -->|Standard Data| G[Historical Database Lookup]
    E --> H[Step C: Record Observed Times]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step D: Apply Performance Rating]
    I --> J[Step E: Compute Normal Time]
    J --> K[Step F: Add Allowances]
    K --> L[Output: Standard Time per Unit]:::outputNode

    classDef outputNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:3px,color:#1B5E20
```

## 4.3 Elemental Time Computation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Raw["Raw Stopwatch Reading for Element i"]:::inputNode
    RFInp["Rating Factor RF for Element i"]:::inputNode
    AllowInp["Total Allowance Percentage A"]:::inputNode

    Raw --> Mult1
    RFInp --> Mult1
    Mult1["Multiplication: OT times RF divided by 100"]:::computeNode
    Mult1 --> NTCalc

    NTCalc["NT equals OT times RF per 100 - Normal Time for Element i"]:::computeNode
    NTCalc --> SumNode

    AllowInp --> Mult2
    NTCalc --> Mult2
    Mult2["Add Allowance Multiplier: 1 plus A divided by 100"]:::computeNode
    Mult2 --> FinalOut

    SumNode["Sum of all Element NT values - Total Normal Time"]:::computeNode
    SumNode --> FinalOut

    FinalOut["ST equals NT total times 1 plus A per 100 - Final Standard Time"]:::finalNode:::outputNode

    classDef inputNode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B
    classDef computeNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#F57F17
    classDef outputNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef finalNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#1B5E20
```

## 4.4 The 5W1H + 2W Questioning Matrix Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Step 3: EXAMINE Stage"]):::inputNode

    Q1["WHAT is being done? - Identify the Activity"]
    Q2["WHY is it being done? - Justify Necessity"]
    Q3["WHERE is it being done? - Evaluate Location"]
    Q4["WHEN is it being done? - Check Sequence"]
    Q5["WHO is doing it? - Assess Skill Match"]
    Q6["HOW is it being done? - Critique Method"]
    Q7["WITH WHAT is it being done? - Tool or Equipment"]
    Q8["AT WHAT COST is it being done? - Economic Viability"]

    Decision{Is the Question Valid?}

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 --> Decision
    Decision -->|Yes Eliminate or Combine| Q2
    Q2 --> Decision
    Decision -->|Yes Change Location| Q3
    Q3 --> Decision
    Decision -->|Yes Rearrange Sequence| Q4
    Q4 --> Decision
    Decision -->|Yes Reassign Operator| Q5
    Q5 --> Decision
    Decision -->|Yes Simplify Method| Q6
    Q6 --> Decision
    Decision -->|Yes Upgrade Tool| Q7
    Q7 --> Decision
    Decision -->|Yes Reduce Cost| Q8
    Q8 --> EndNode(["Output: List of Improvement Opportunities"]):::outputNode

    classDef inputNode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B
    classDef outputNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#1B5E20
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each diamond in the 5W1H+2W flowchart represents a **decision gate** where the Industrial Engineer asks whether the current state of the operation under that question category is optimal. If not, a **creative alternative** is generated and recorded for inclusion in the DEVELOP stage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part A questions test **Remember and Understand** levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be **precise, definition-based, and limited to 50-80 words.**

### Part A — Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q1.** *Define Method Study. List the six universal steps of conducting a Method Study as per the ILO framework.* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Method Study** is the systematic recording, examination, and critical analysis of existing and proposed ways of doing work, with the objective of developing easier and more effective methods and thereby reducing costs.

The **six universal steps** as codified by the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** are:
1. **SELECT** — Define the problem and scope
2. **RECORD** — Document the current method using process charts
3. **EXAMINE** — Subject the recorded data to critical analysis (5W1H+2W)
4. **DEVELOP** — Design the improved method using motion economy principles
5. **INSTALL** — Implement the new method with worker training
6. **MAINTAIN** — Sustain the new standard through audits and kaizen

---

### Part A — Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q2.** *What is Standard Time? State the formula relating Standard Time, Normal Time, and Allowance.* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Standard Time (ST)** is the amount of time that a qualified, trained worker, working at a normal pace, would take to perform a specific task or operation, including provision for **personal needs, relaxation, and contingencies**.

The formula is:

$$ST = NT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A}{100}\right)$$

where $NT$ is the **Normal Time** and $A$ is the **total allowance percentage** (sum of relaxation, personal, contingency, and special allowances). Standard Time forms the basis for **production planning, scheduling, costing, and incentive wage systems** in the automotive industry.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Part B questions test **Apply, Analyze, and Evaluate** levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. The question paper provides internal choice — a student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q.A.** *(a)* Explain in detail the **procedure of conducting a Method Study** in an automotive manufacturing unit. Mention the tools used at each stage with an automotive industry example. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* A time study was conducted on a **piston ring assembly operation** at a Tata Motors engine plant. The data recorded is as follows:

| Element | Observed Time (sec) | Performance Rating (%) | Allowance (%) |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Fetch piston rings from tray | 6 | 90 | 12 |
| Position compression ring 1 | 10 | 110 | 12 |
| Position compression ring 2 | 9 | 105 | 12 |
| Position oil control ring | 8 | 100 | 12 |
| Inspect gap with feeler gauge | 7 | 95 | 12 |

Calculate the **Standard Time per assembly** and the **Standard Output per 8-hour shift**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The procedure of conducting Method Study follows the **ILO six-step universal framework**:

**Step 1 — SELECT (1.5 Marks):** The Industrial Engineer defines the problem and scope using **Pareto Analysis** (80/20 rule) to identify the vital few operations causing the majority of waste. **Example:** Selecting a **crankshaft grinding station** in a Tata Motors Pune plant where cycle time exceeds the takt time by 23 seconds.

**Step 2 — RECORD (1.5 Marks):** The current method is documented using **Process Charts, Flow Process Charts (Man/Material/Equipment), Two-Handed Process Charts, String Diagrams, and Multiple Activity Charts.** Modern plants supplement with **4K video recording.** **Example:** Recording the **welder's 23-element hand motion sequence** during front floor pan welding.

**Step 3 — EXAMINE (1.5 Marks):** Critical analysis is performed using the **5W1H + 2W questioning technique** combined with the **17 (or 18) Therbligs** and the **Principles of Motion Economy** by Frank Gilbreth. **Example:** Examining a **seat assembly line operation** at Maruti Suzuki Gurugram plant to question whether the seat-bolt tightening sequence is optimal.

**Step 4 — DEVELOP (1 Mark):** A new method is designed using **digital twin simulation, mock-up trials, and ergonomic assessments (REBA, RULA, NIOSH Lifting Equation).** **Example:** Designing a **cobot-assisted dashboard mating operation** to reduce RULA scores from 5 to 2.

**Step 5 — INSTALL (0.75 Mark):** Implementation requires **worker training, supervisor buy-in, and SOP documentation** in compliance with **ISO 9001:2015.** **Example:** Installing a new **AGV routing system** in a Mahindra SUV body shop.

**Step 6 — MAINTAIN (0.75 Mark):** Sustainment is achieved through **Layered Process Audits (LPA), Standardized Work audits, and continuous Kaizen.** **Example:** Monthly kaizen workshops on the **Hyundai Creta paint shop conveyor** to prevent regression.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Normal Time for each element** using $NT_i = OT_i \times \dfrac{RF_i}{100}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_1 &= 6 \times \dfrac{90}{100} = 5.400 \text{ sec} \\[4pt]
NT_2 &= 10 \times \dfrac{110}{100} = 11.000 \text{ sec} \\[4pt]
NT_3 &= 9 \times \dfrac{105}{100} = 9.450 \text{ sec} \\[4pt]
NT_4 &= 8 \times \dfrac{100}{100} = 8.000 \text{ sec} \\[4pt]
NT_5 &= 7 \times \dfrac{95}{100} = 6.650 \text{ sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the Normal Times:**

$$
NT_{total} = 5.400 + 11.000 + 9.450 + 8.000 + 6.650 = 40.500 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 12% allowance:**

$$
ST = 40.500 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{12}{100}\right) = 40.500 \times 1.12
$$

$$
\boxed{ST = 45.360 \text{ seconds per assembly}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Standard Output per 8-hour shift:**

$$
\text{Available Time} = 8 \times 3600 = 28{,}800 \text{ sec/shift}
$$

$$
SO = \dfrac{28{,}800}{45.360} = 634.92 \text{ assemblies per shift}
$$

$$
\boxed{SO \approx 634 \text{ assemblies per 8-hour shift}}
$$

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q.B.** *(a)* With a neat flowchart, explain the **procedure of conducting Work Measurement** in an automotive operations management context. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* The following data was obtained during a time study on a **clutch plate fitting operation** in a Hyundai plant. Calculate the **Standard Time** and the **number of cycles to be timed** for 95% confidence and ±5% accuracy using the **40√N shortcut formula.** The preliminary 10 readings are: 1.20, 1.25, 1.18, 1.22, 1.27, 1.15, 1.30, 1.24, 1.21, 1.19 minutes. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The procedure of conducting Work Measurement is illustrated in the **flowchart** below (text-rendered for the written exam):

**Stage 1 (1.5 Marks):** **Establish the standard method** — Method study must be completed first; otherwise, time study is conducted on an inferior method.

**Stage 2 (1 Mark):** **Break the work into elements** — Using the **elemental breakdown technique** with consistent start/end points (preferably the "ping" or audible signal method).

**Stage 3 (1.5 Marks):** **Select the time-study technique** — Choose between **Stopwatch Time Study, Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS) [MTM, MOST, MTM-1, MTM-2, MTM-V], Standard Data Systems, or Computer-Aided Time Study.**

**Stage 4 (1 Mark):** **Time each element** — Using continuous timing, snap-back timing, or repetitive timing based on the operation type.

**Stage 5 (1 Mark):** **Rate the operator's performance** — Using the **Westinghouse System** (Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency) or the **Synthetic Rating** method.

**Stage 6 (0.5 Mark):** **Compute Normal Time (NT = OT × RF).**

**Stage 7 (0.5 Mark):** **Apply Allowances** and compute Standard Time.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given data:** Ten preliminary readings (in minutes): 1.20, 1.25, 1.18, 1.22, 1.27, 1.15, 1.30, 1.24, 1.21, 1.19. Let Rating Factor $RF = 100\% = 1.0$ (assumed normal pace) and Allowance $A = 15\%$.

**Step 1 — Compute Mean Observed Time:**

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{1.20+1.25+1.18+1.22+1.27+1.15+1.30+1.24+1.21+1.19}{10} = \dfrac{12.21}{10} = 1.221 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sum x_i^2$:**

$$
