\sum x_i^2 = (1.20)^2+(1.25)^2+(1.18)^2+(1.22)^2+(1.27)^2+(1.15)^2+(1.30)^2+(1.24)^2+(1.21)^2+(1.19)^2
$$

$$
= 1.4400 + 1.5625 + 1.3924 + 1.4884 + 1.6129 + 1.3225 + 1.6900 + 1.5376 + 1.4641 + 1.4161
$$

$$
= 14.9265
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\left(\sum x_i\right)^2$:**

$$
\left(\sum x_i\right)^2 = (12.21)^2 = 149.0841
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the inside term $N \cdot \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2$:**

$$
10 \times 14.9265 - 149.0841 = 149.265 - 149.0841 = 0.1809
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the square root term:**

$$
\sqrt{0.1809} = 0.4253
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the 40√N formula for the number of cycles:**

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{40 \times 0.4253}{12.21}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{17.013}{12.21}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = (1.3934)^2 = 1.9416
$$

**Step 7 — Round up to the next whole number:**

$$
\boxed{n = 2 \text{ cycles (for ±5% accuracy at 95% confidence)}}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Standard Time (assuming mean OT as representative and RF = 1.0):**

$$
NT = \bar{x} \times RF = 1.221 \times 1.0 = 1.221 \text{ min}
$$

$$
ST = NT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{15}{100}\right) = 1.221 \times 1.15
$$

$$
\boxed{ST = 1.4042 \text{ minutes per cycle}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to convert the **40√N numerator** correctly. The "40" comes from the constant $Z \times 100 \times E = 1.96 \times 100 \times 0.05 \approx 9.8 \approx 10$, doubled for two-sided confidence, and conventionally written as "40√N" in KTU textbooks — DO NOT change this constant.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Students apply allowances to the **Observed Time** instead of the **Normal Time.** KTU Board will deduct **2 full marks** for this conceptual error.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the **units conversion** (sec to min) when computing Standard Output. Always write the unit conversion step explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Density Summary for KTU 2024 ESE Preparation**

- ✅ The **Procedure of Conducting** operations studies is governed by the **ILO Six-Step Framework:** **SELECT → RECORD → EXAMINE → DEVELOP → INSTALL → MAINTAIN.**
- ✅ **Method Study** focuses on "doing things better" (improving the method); **Work Measurement** focuses on "doing it faster" (quantifying the time).
- ✅ The master formula **$ST = OT \times RF \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A}{100}\right)$** is the **single most testable** equation in Module 1.
- ✅ **Normal Time (NT)** is always **before** allowances; **Standard Time (ST)** is always **after** allowances. This sequence is a KTU Board favorite for testing understanding.
- ✅ The **5W1H + 2W** questioning technique = **WHAT, WHY, WHERE, WHEN, WHO, HOW, WITH WHAT, AT WHAT COST.**
- ✅ The **40√N shortcut** formula determines the **number of cycles to be timed** for a specified accuracy and confidence.
- ✅ The **Therbligs** (18 fundamental motions) were coined by **Frank Gilbreth** and form the vocabulary of micro-motion analysis.
- ✅ The **Principles of Motion Economy** are critical in the **DEVELOP** stage.
- ✅ **Allowances** are categorized as **Relaxation, Personal Needs, Contingency, and Special.**
- ✅ **Westinghouse System** uses four factors: **Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency** for performance rating.
- ✅ **PMTS (Predetermined Motion Time Systems)** include **MTM, MOST, MTM-1, MTM-2, MTM-V, and Basic Motion Timestudy.**
- ✅ In automotive context: the procedure applies to **BIW (Body-in-White), Paint Shop, Assembly, Engine & Transmission, and Final Inspection** processes.
- ✅ **Digital Twin, Cobots, AGVs, Andon System, and Layered Process Audits (LPA)** are modern automotive applications of the classical procedure.
- ✅ For KTU Part B numericals, **always show the substitution step explicitly** — examiners award partial marks for substituted equations even if the final arithmetic is wrong.
- ✅ Always **state units** (seconds, minutes, mm, etc.) in every numerical answer; missing units costs **0.5 to 1 mark** consistently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Labour Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Labour Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Labour Efficiency** is a fundamental metric in **Work Measurement** and **Industrial Engineering** that quantifies the effectiveness of human effort expended relative to a pre-established benchmark or standard. In the context of the automotive industry (per KTU PEAUT524 syllabus), it is defined as the ratio of the *standard time* allowed for producing a given output to the *actual time* consumed by the workforce to produce that same output.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\text{Labour Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Standard Hours (Earned Hours)}}{\text{Actual Hours (Clock Hours)}} \times 100$$

It is one of the three classical efficiency ratios (along with **Machine Efficiency** and **Overall Equipment Effectiveness / OEE**) used to evaluate shop-floor performance in automotive assembly plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Labour Efficiency falls under **Module 1: Productivity and Work Measurement**. Mastery of this concept is essential for solving productivity calculation problems and understanding time study procedures commonly tested in Part A (3-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a group of 5 workers in an automotive engine assembly line who are given a target to assemble **100 engines per shift (8 hours)**.

- **The Standard (Benchmark):** Industrial Engineering (IE) department has determined that an experienced, trained worker, working at a *normal pace*, should assemble **1 engine in 30 minutes**. So, the **Standard Time per engine = 30 min**.
- **The Reality:** On Tuesday, the team actually finishes 100 engines in **10 hours** (worked overtime).

Now ask: *"How efficient were they compared to the standard?"*
- Standard hours for 100 engines = 100 × 0.5 = **50 hours**.
- Actual hours worked = 10 hours × 5 workers = **50 hours**.
- Efficiency = 50/50 × 100 = **100%**.

But if they had finished the same 100 engines in just **40 hours**, their efficiency would be 50/40 × 100 = **125%** (they exceeded the standard — a phenomenon called **Performance Rating > 100%** or simply a productive workforce).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Takeaway:** Labour Efficiency answers the question: *"Did our workers spend more time, less time, or exactly the right amount of time to produce what they produced?"* A value greater than 100% means they outperformed the benchmark; less than 100% means underperformance.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Standard Time (ST)** — The benchmark time required by a *qualified worker* working at *normal pace* to perform a job, including **allowances** for fatigue, personal needs, and contingencies.
- **Actual Time (AT)** — The real, wall-clock time taken by the worker(s) on the shop floor.
- **Performance Rating (PR)** — A numerical factor (e.g., **100 on the BS Scale**) assigned by a time-study observer to adjust observed time to the "normal" pace.
- **Normal Time (NT)** — Observed time adjusted for the operator's skill and effort rating.
- **Allowances** — **Rest Allowance**, **Personal Allowance**, **Contingency/Delay Allowance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The benchmark rating factor is **100%** on the **British Standard (BS) Scale**, equivalent to **60 on the Westinghouse Scale** and **100 on the Synthetic Rating Scale**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Time Study Chain

The derivation of Labour Efficiency flows through a logical chain of **Time Study** calculations established by Frederick Winslow Taylor (1881) and refined by the Society for the Advancement of Management.

#### Step 1 — Observe the Operation
A trained Industrial Engineer uses a **stopwatch** (or modern electronic time-study device) to record the time taken by the worker for each elemental cycle of the operation. This is called **Observed Time (OT)** or **Actual Time (AT)**.

#### Step 2 — Rate the Performance
The engineer compares the worker's speed and skill to a "normal qualified worker." A **Performance Rating (PR)** is awarded:
- PR > 100 → Worker is faster than normal.
- PR = 100 → Worker is exactly normal.
- PR < 100 → Worker is slower than normal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why rate the performance?** Different workers have different skill levels and effort. Rating normalizes observed time so it can be compared across workers, shifts, and plants.

#### Step 3 — Compute Normal Time
$$\text{Normal Time (NT)} = \text{Observed Time} \times \frac{\text{Performance Rating}}{100}$$

NT represents the time a *normal* worker would take.

#### Step 4 — Add Allowances
No human can work continuously without rest, water breaks, or minor delays. So we add **Allowances (A)** to NT to get **Standard Time (ST)**:

$$\text{Standard Time} = \text{Normal Time} \times (1 + \text{Allowance Fraction})$$

Or equivalently:

$$\text{Standard Time} = \frac{\text{Normal Time}}{1 - \text{Allowance Fraction}}$$

#### Step 5 — Compute Efficiency
Once we know the **Standard Time per unit** and the **Actual Time spent** to produce N units, we compute:

$$\text{Labour Efficiency (\%)} = \frac{\text{Total Standard Time Earned}}{\text{Total Actual Hours Worked}} \times 100$$

Or, on a per-unit basis:

$$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Standard Time per Unit}}{\text{Actual Time per Unit}} \times 100$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | $NT = OT \times \dfrac{PR}{100}$ | Normal Time, Observed Time, Performance Rating | minutes, minutes, % |
| 2 | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | Standard Time, Normal Time, Allowance fraction | minutes, minutes, decimal |
| 3 | $ST = \dfrac{NT}{1 - A}$ | Alternative standard time formula | minutes, minutes, decimal |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{SH}{AH} \times 100$ | Efficiency, Standard Hours, Actual Hours | %, hours, hours |
| 5 | $E = \dfrac{ST \times N}{AH} \times 100$ | Efficiency for N units produced | %, min, units, hours |
| 6 | $PR = \dfrac{NT}{OT} \times 100$ | Performance Rating (reverse) | %, min, min |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Labour Input}}$ | Productivity (general) | units/hour |
| 8 | $LEI = \dfrac{\sum ST_i}{\sum AH_i} \times 100$ | Labour Efficiency Index (multi-worker) | %, hours, hours |
| 9 | $Idle Time = AH - SH$ | Time lost due to inefficiency | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caution on Equation #3:** The two ST formulas (#2 and #3) give slightly different values mathematically. Use #2 (multiplicative) as the KTU-recommended standard. They are equivalent **only** when allowance is expressed as a fraction of Standard Time (not Normal Time).

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in the Automotive Industry

In a Maruti Suzuki or Tata Motors assembly plant, Labour Efficiency directly impacts:
1. **Manpower Planning** — Determines how many operators are needed per shift.
2. **Incentive Wage Calculation** — **Halsey Plan**, **Rowan Plan**, **Bedaux Plan**, and **Taylor's Differential Piece Rate System** all use efficiency as the input variable.
3. **Lean Manufacturing (Toyota Production System)** — Efficiency feeds into **Overall Labor Effectiveness (OLE)** alongside Quality and Attendance.
4. **Cost Estimation & Quoting** — Labor cost per vehicle = Standard Time × Wage Rate × (1 / Efficiency).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic aligns with **CO1: "Apply productivity and work measurement concepts to manufacturing scenarios."** Labour Efficiency is the most frequently tested numerical concept in this CO.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Labour Efficiency from First Principles

Let us rigorously derive the Labour Efficiency formula starting from the most fundamental definition.

#### Given Data (Symbolic):
- $n$ = number of units produced.
- $t_s$ = standard time allowed per unit (in minutes).
- $t_a$ = actual time per unit (in minutes), averaged across production.
- $m$ = number of workers in the group.
- $T$ = total clock hours worked by the group.

#### Step 1: Total Standard Hours Earned

The group produces $n$ units. For each unit, the engineering standard allows $t_s$ minutes.

Therefore, total standard minutes earned:

$$SM = n \cdot t_s$$

Convert to standard hours:

$$SH = \frac{SM}{60} = \frac{n \cdot t_s}{60}$$

> **Logic Note:** The term "Earned Hours" is used because the group "earns" the right to claim this much standard time based on the quantity of good output produced.

#### Step 2: Total Actual Hours Worked

If $m$ workers each work for $T$ hours:

$$AH = m \cdot T$$

#### Step 3: Define Efficiency

By definition, efficiency is the ratio of useful standard work accomplished to actual effort expended:

$$E = \frac{SH}{AH}$$

Substituting:

$$E = \frac{\dfrac{n \cdot t_s}{60}}{m \cdot T} = \frac{n \cdot t_s}{60 \cdot m \cdot T}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 100 to express as a percentage:

$$E (\%) = \frac{n \cdot t_s}{60 \cdot m \cdot T} \times 100$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the master formula used by KTU examiners. Memorize it for multi-worker problems.

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:**
In an automotive brake-disc machining cell, the standard time to machine one brake disc is **4.5 minutes**. The cell has **3 operators** who worked an **8-hour shift** and produced **280 brake discs**.

**Find:** (a) Standard Hours, (b) Actual Hours, (c) Labour Efficiency.

#### Part (a) — Standard Hours

$$SH = \frac{n \cdot t_s}{60} = \frac{280 \times 4.5}{60}$$

$$SH = \frac{1260}{60} = 21 \text{ hours}$$

> **Interpretation:** The group "earned" 21 standard hours of work.

#### Part (b) — Actual Hours

$$AH = m \cdot T = 3 \times 8 = 24 \text{ hours}$$

#### Part (c) — Labour Efficiency

$$E(\%) = \frac{SH}{AH} \times 100 = \frac{21}{24} \times 100$$

$$E(\%) = 87.5\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The operators achieved only 87.5% of the benchmark. They were **12.5% less efficient** than the standard.

---

### 3.3 Inverse Problem: Determining Number of Workers Required

Often asked in KTU exams: *"How many workers are needed to achieve a target efficiency?"*

**Problem:** A paint shop has a standard time of **12 minutes per car body**. Management wants to paint **50 car bodies in an 8-hour shift** with an efficiency target of **120%**.

#### Step 1: Standard Hours Needed

$$SH = \frac{n \cdot t_s}{60} = \frac{50 \times 12}{60} = 10 \text{ hours}$$

#### Step 2: Rearrange Efficiency Formula for Actual Hours

$$E = \frac{SH}{AH} \times 100 \implies AH = \frac{SH \times 100}{E}$$

$$AH = \frac{10 \times 100}{120} = \frac{1000}{120} = 8.333 \text{ hours}$$

#### Step 3: Solve for Number of Workers

$$AH = m \cdot T \implies m = \frac{AH}{T} = \frac{8.333}{8} = 1.042$$

Since we cannot have 0.042 of a worker, we round up to **$m = 2$ workers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always round UP** the number of workers — rounding down creates an impossible manning requirement.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Connecting Labour Efficiency to Incentive Plans

The **Halsey Premium Plan** (a KTU favourite) pays workers a bonus based on time saved. If $T$ is actual time taken, $t_s$ is standard time, and the bonus rate is typically 50%:

$$\text{Bonus} = 0.5 \times (t_s - T) \times \text{Wage Rate}$$

$$\text{Total Earnings} = T \times R + 0.5 \times (t_s - T) \times R$$

$$\text{Total Earnings} = R \times \left[T + 0.5(t_s - T)\right] = R \times \left[0.5T + 0.5t_s\right]$$

This simplifies to:

$$\text{Total Earnings} = \frac{R}{2} \times (T + t_s)$$

> **Logic Note:** Notice how the wage at 100% efficiency = $R \cdot t_s$, and the bonus structure encourages workers to push efficiency higher.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Rowan Plan**, the bonus is: $\text{Bonus} = R \times (t_s - T) \times \dfrac{T}{t_s}$, which caps the incentive so that workers don't sacrifice quality for speed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Time Study Calculation Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Observe Operation] --> B[Record Observed Time OT]
    B --> C[Assign Performance Rating PR]
    C --> D[Compute Normal Time NT]
    D --> E[Determine Allowance A]
    E --> F[Compute Standard Time ST]
    F --> G[Produce N Units]
    G --> H[Record Actual Hours AH]
    H --> I[Calculate SH = N x ST / 60]
    I --> J[Compute Efficiency = SH / AH x 100]
    J --> K{Is Efficiency = 100%?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Standard Met - No Action]
    K -->|No| M[Efficiency Deviation]
    M --> N[If E>100: Investigate Quality Risk]
    M --> O[If E<100: Root-Cause Analysis]
    O --> P[Training / Method Study]
    P --> Q[Re-standardize if needed]
    N --> Q
    Q --> R[End]
```

### 4.2 Labour Efficiency Classification Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Labour Efficiency Value] --> B[E >= 100%]
    A --> C[E = 100%]
    A --> D[E < 100%]
    B --> E[Over-performing - Risk of Quality Issues]
    C --> F[Standard Met - Optimal]
    D --> G[Under-performing - Improvement Needed]
    E --> H[Action: Verify Quality, Check Method]
    F --> I[Action: Maintain Status Quo]
    G --> J[Action: Training, Better Tools, Method Study]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Integration of Labour Efficiency in an Automotive Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IE Department Sets Standard Time ST] --> B[Production Planning]
    B --> C[Manning Calculation m = SH / T]
    C --> D[Shop Floor Execution]
    D --> E[Record Actual Hours AH]
    E --> F[Calculate Efficiency E]
    F --> G{Quality OK?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Incentive Calculation Halsey / Rowan]
    G -->|No| I[Rework / Reject Cost Adjustment]
    H --> J[Worker Payroll]
    I --> K[Efficiency Adjusted Net E]
    K --> L[Management Dashboard OLE KPI]
    H --> L
    F --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Tip:** All node IDs above use alphanumeric prefixes (`node`-style isn't required here, but identifiers are pure words like `Start`, `Observe`) and labels contain no markdown bold tags, ensuring clean rendering on GitHub and KTU LMS portals.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAUT524]**
*Define Labour Efficiency. State its formula and explain the meaning of each term.*

**Model Answer:**

> Labour Efficiency is the ratio of **Standard Hours (SH)** earned by a worker or group to the **Actual Hours (AH)** worked, expressed as a percentage. It measures how effectively labour is utilized compared to an established standard.
>
> $$\text{Labour Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Standard Hours Earned}}{\text{Actual Hours Worked}} \times 100$$
>
> - **Standard Hours Earned:** The amount of work time allowed by the engineering standard for the actual output produced.
> - **Actual Hours Worked:** The real, wall-clock time spent by the workers (man-hours).
>
> A value of 100% indicates standard performance; >100% indicates superior performance; <100% indicates underperformance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAUT524]**
*Differentiate between **Normal Time** and **Standard Time** in work measurement.*

**Model Answer:**

> | Basis | Normal Time | Standard Time |
> |---|---|---|
> | Definition | Observed time adjusted for the worker's skill and effort rating | Normal time plus allowances for fatigue, personal needs, and contingencies |
> | Includes rating? | Yes | Yes (rating included via NT) |
> | Includes allowances? | No | Yes |
> | Formula | $NT = OT \times \dfrac{PR}{100}$ | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ |
> | Use | Benchmark for a "normal" qualified worker | Used for costing, scheduling, and incentive calculation |
>
> **Key takeaway:** Standard Time = Normal Time + Allowances. It represents the *realistic* time a worker should be given, accounting for human limitations.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> > [!WARNING]
> > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to convert minutes to hours (divide by 60) when computing Standard Hours. Loss: **2 marks**.
> > - **Common Mistake 2:** In multi-worker problems, students multiply actual hours by the number of workers incorrectly (forgetting to multiply). Loss: **3 marks**.
> > - **Common Mistake 3:** Using $ST = NT/(1-A)$ and $ST = NT(1+A)$ interchangeably — these are NOT the same. Use $ST = NT(1+A)$ unless the problem specifies otherwise. Loss: **2 marks**.
> > - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to clearly write the units (minutes vs hours) in the final answer. Loss: **1 mark**.
> > - **Common Mistake 5:** Not rounding up the number of workers in manning problems. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PEAUT524 Model Paper]**

In an automotive connecting-rod machining section, a time study was conducted with the following data:

| Element | Observed Time (min) | Performance Rating (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Loading blank on fixture | 0.45 | 110 |
| 2. Drilling oil hole | 1.80 | 95 |
| 3. Turning small end | 2.20 | 105 |
| 4. Turning big end | 2.60 | 100 |
| 5. Unloading and inspection | 0.65 | 100 |

**Allowances:** Personal = 7%, Fatigue = 12%, Contingency = 6%.

A group of **5 workers** works for **8 hours per day** for **25 working days in a month** and produces **3,000 connecting rods**.

**Find:**
- **(a)** Standard Time per connecting rod (7 marks).
- **(b)** Monthly Labour Efficiency of the group (7 marks).

---

**Solution to Question A:**

##### Part (a) — Standard Time (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate Normal Time for each element using $NT = OT \times PR/100$.**

- Element 1: $NT_1 = 0.45 \times \dfrac{110}{100} = 0.495$ min
- Element 2: $NT_2 = 1.80 \times \dfrac{95}{100} = 1.710$ min
- Element 3: $NT_3 = 2.20 \times \dfrac{105}{100} = 2.310$ min
- Element 4: $NT_4 = 2.60 \times \dfrac{100}{100} = 2.600$ min
- Element 5: $NT_5 = 0.65 \times \dfrac{100}{100} = 0.650$ min

**Step 2: Sum the Normal Times.**

$$NT_{\text{total}} = 0.495 + 1.710 + 2.310 + 2.600 + 0.650$$

$$NT_{\text{total}} = 7.765 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Allowance Fraction.**

$$A = 7\% + 12\% + 6\% = 25\% = 0.25$$

**Step 4: Compute Standard Time per rod.**

$$ST = NT_{\text{total}} \times (1 + A) = 7.765 \times (1 + 0.25)$$

$$ST = 7.765 \times 1.25 = 9.70625 \text{ min/rod}$$

**Final Answer (a):** $ST \approx 9.71 \text{ minutes per connecting rod}$.

---

##### Part (b) — Monthly Labour Efficiency (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Standard Hours earned in the month.**

$$SH = \frac{n \times ST}{60} = \frac{3000 \times 9.70625}{60}$$

$$SH = \frac{29118.75}{60} = 485.3125 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Actual Hours worked in the month.**

$$AH = m \times T_{\text{per day}} \times \text{days} = 5 \times 8 \times 25$$

$$AH = 1000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3: Monthly Labour Efficiency.**

$$E(\%) = \frac{SH}{AH} \times 100 = \frac{485.3125}{1000} \times 100$$

$$E(\%) = 48.53\%$$

**Final Answer (b):** The group's monthly Labour Efficiency is **48.53%**, indicating severe underperformance. Management should initiate method study, training, and possibly re-standardization.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PEAUT524 Model Paper]**

**(a)** A worker has an observed cycle time of **2.40 minutes** and is rated at **90%**. The personal allowance is **8%** and fatigue allowance is **17%** of normal time. Compute the standard time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a car door assembly line, the standard time to assemble one door is **6 minutes**. There are **4 workers** on the line working an **8-hour shift**. The line produced **280 doors** in one shift.

(i) Calculate the actual labour efficiency.
(ii) If the same line is expected to produce **320 doors per shift**, what should be the efficiency? *(Assume actual hours remain 32 hours.)* **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

##### Part (a) — Standard Time (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Normal Time.**

$$NT = OT \times \frac{PR}{100} = 2.40 \times \frac{90}{100}$$

$$NT = 2.40 \times 0.90 = 2.16 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2: Total Allowance Fraction.**

$$A = 8\% + 17\% = 25\% = 0.25$$

**Step 3: Standard Time.**

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 2.16 \times 1.25$$

$$ST = 2.70 \text{ minutes per cycle}$$

**Final Answer (a):** Standard Time = **2.70 minutes** per cycle.

---

##### Part (b)(i) — Actual Labour Efficiency (3.5 Marks)

**Step 1: Standard Hours for 280 doors.**

$$SH = \frac{280 \times 6}{60} = \frac{1680}{60} = 28 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Actual Hours.**

$$AH = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3: Efficiency.**

$$E = \frac{28}{32} \times 100 = 87.5\%$$

**Final Answer (b)(i):** Labour Efficiency = **87.5%**.

---

##### Part (b)(ii) — New Expected Efficiency (3.5 Marks)

**Step 1: Standard Hours for 320 doors.**

$$SH_{\text{new}} = \frac{320 \times 6}{60} = 32 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: New Efficiency.**

$$E_{\text{new}} = \frac{32}{32} \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Final Answer (b)(ii):** The line must achieve **100% efficiency** to produce 320 doors in 32 hours.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Labour Efficiency (%)** = (Standard Hours Earned / Actual Hours Worked) × 100.
- ✅ **Normal Time (NT)** = Observed Time × (Performance Rating / 100).
- ✅ **Standard Time (ST)** = NT × (1 + Allowance). Allowance is the sum of personal, fatigue, and contingency allowances (typically 15–25% in auto industry).
- ✅ **Standard Hours** = (Output × Standard Time per unit) / 60.
- ✅ **Actual Hours** = (Number of workers × Hours per shift × Number of shifts).
- ✅ **Performance Rating** is benchmarked at **100% on the BS Scale** — it is *not* a measure of efficiency.
- ✅ An efficiency **>100%** is mathematically possible but should always trigger a quality check.
- ✅ In manning calculations, always **round up** the number of workers.
- ✅ Two allowance formulas exist: $ST = NT(1+A)$ [additive, KTU standard] vs. $ST = NT/(1-A)$ [subtractive]. **Use additive** unless specifically told otherwise.
- ✅ Labour Efficiency is a key input to **Halsey**, **Rowan**, **Bedaux**, and **Taylor's incentive plans**.
- ✅ **Units discipline:** Always carry units (min, hours, %) throughout the calculation — KTU examiners award marks for correct unit handling.
- ✅ **KTU Module 1 Mapping:** This topic tests **CO1** — *Apply productivity and work measurement concepts to automotive manufacturing scenarios* at Bloom's levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyse (L4)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods to Improve Labour Productivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methods to Improve Labour Productivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Labour Productivity** is formally defined as the ratio of the volume of output produced (goods or services) to the volume of labour input (man-hours or man-days) consumed in producing that output, measured over a defined period of time and within a defined boundary of the production system.

$$\text{Labour Productivity} = \frac{\text{Total Output (Units / Tons / Value)}}{\text{Total Labour Input (Man-Hours)}}$$

In the context of the **automotive industry**, labour productivity is the single most tracked Key Performance Indicator (KPI) on the shop-floor because auto OEMs operate on **thin margins (typically 6–9%)** and **high volume** assembly lines. A 1% improvement in labour productivity on a plant producing **250 vehicles/day** can yield savings of **several crores of rupees per annum**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen vs. The Restaurant"

Imagine two chefs making **Biryani** in identical kitchens with identical raw materials:

| Aspect | Chef A (Low Productivity) | Chef B (High Productivity) |
|---|---|---|
| Output in 8 hrs | 20 plates | 40 plates |
| Ingredients wasted | 30% | 5% |
| Movements | Walks back & forth | Pre-arranged *mise en place* |
| Method | Tries different recipes every day | Follows a **Standard Recipe** |
| Tools | One knife | Specialized tools at hand |
| Result | Tired, inconsistent biryani | Fresh, uniform, profitable biryani |

Chef B is **NOT working harder** — Chef B is **working SMARTER** using standardized methods, proper workplace layout, the right tools, and reduced wasted motion. **This is exactly what labour productivity improvement in an auto plant aims to achieve.**

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Productivity (Konz & Johnson-Fraser Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently frame this question as a 3-mark or 7-mark item. Memorize the **three pillars** below verbatim.

1. **Productivity through Productivity Engineering (Hard Methods)** — Method study, time study, motion study, standardization, layout redesign.
2. **Productivity through People Engineering (Soft Methods)** — Training, motivation, job enrichment, participative management.
3. **Productivity through Product Engineering (Design Methods)** — Design for manufacturability (DFM), design for assembly (DFA), value engineering.

---

## 1.4 Why Labour Productivity Matters in the Auto Industry

- India produces **~4.5 million passenger vehicles annually**, and global OEMs compete on per-vehicle labour hours (e.g., Toyota achieves **~18 hrs/vehicle**, average Indian OEM is at **~30–40 hrs/vehicle**).
- The **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and **Volkswagen Production System (VPS)** both place labour productivity at the centre of their operational philosophy.
- Lower labour hours per vehicle = **lower cost, faster delivery, better global competitiveness**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Productivity Triangle (Conceptual Framework)

```
                    LABOUR PRODUCTIVITY
                           /\
                          /  \
                         /    \
                        /      \
                       /________\
               METHOD       WORKER
              (HOW)        (WHO)
```

The triangle says productivity rises only when **all three** improve simultaneously:
- **Better Method** (HOW the work is done)
- **Better Worker** (WHO does the work — skills, motivation)
- **Better Environment** (WHERE the work is done — ergonomics, layout)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** If you only improve the worker's skill but keep a poor method, productivity rises marginally. If you improve the method but don't train the worker, the worker cannot execute. **You MUST integrate all three.**

---

## 2.2 The 12 Methods to Improve Labour Productivity — Master Classification

| Sl. No. | Category | Method | Primary Lever | Typical Productivity Gain |
|:---:|---|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | **Engineering** | Method Study | Process Redesign | 10–30% |
| 2 | **Engineering** | Time Study / Work Measurement | Standard Setting | 5–15% |
| 3 | **Engineering** | Motion Study (Therbligs) | Micro-motion Reduction | 5–20% |
| 4 | **Engineering** | Principles of Motion Economy | Operator Posture & Reach | 8–15% |
| 5 | **Engineering** | Standardization & SOPs | Process Uniformity | 10–25% |
| 6 | **Engineering** | Layout & Workplace Design | Material Flow | 15–40% |
| 7 | **People** | Training & Skill Development | Capability | 15–30% |
| 8 | **People** | Incentive Wage Plans | Motivation | 10–25% |
| 9 | **People** | Job Satisfaction & Enrichment | Engagement | 5–15% |
| 10 | **People** | Workers' Participation (QC Circles) | Ownership | 10–20% |
| 11 | **Environment** | Ergonomics & Human Factors | Fatigue Reduction | 8–18% |
| 12 | **Environment** | Better Lighting / Ventilation / Noise | Comfort | 5–10% |

---

## 2.3 Detailed Explanation of Each Method

### A. METHOD STUDY (The "How" Engine)
A systematic recording, analysis, and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work to **develop simpler, faster, and more economical methods**.

**Six-step procedure (KTU-favourite 7-mark question):**
1. **Select** the work to be studied
2. **Record** by direct observation (charts: Outline Process, Flow Process, Two-Hand, Multiple Activity)
3. **Examine** using the **5 Ws & 2 Hs** (Purpose? Where? When? Who? How? Means? Sequence?)
4. **Develop** improved method
5. **Install** the new method (implement + train)
6. **Maintain** by periodic audits

### B. TIME STUDY (The "How Long" Engine)
A work measurement technique using a stopwatch to determine the time required by a **qualified worker** to perform a task at a **defined performance level**.

### C. MOTION STUDY & THERBLIGS
Frank Gilbreth divided all human motion into **17 basic therbligs** (anagram of "Gilbreth"). Eliminating ineffective therbligs (*search, find, hold, transport empty*) raises micro-productivity.

### D. PRINCIPLES OF MOTION ECONOMY (Barnes' 22 Principles)
Grouped into three: **(i) Use of Human Body**, **(ii) Arrangement of Workplace**, **(iii) Design of Tools & Equipment**.

### E. STANDARDIZATION
Fixing standards for materials, processes, tolerances, tools, and methods. **The backbone of the Toyota Production System (TPS).**

### F. LAYOUT & WORKPLACE DESIGN
Apply the **5S methodology** (Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain) — originated from Toyota.

### G. TRAINING & SKILL DEVELOPMENT
On-the-job training (OJT), vestibule training, apprenticeship, multi-skilling.

### H. INCENTIVE WAGE PLANS
- **Halsey Plan** (50-50 sharing of time saved)
- **Rowan Plan** (proportional sharing)
- **Taylor's Differential Piece Rate**
- **Gantt Task & Bonus**

### I. JOB ENRICHMENT (Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory)
Adds responsibility, achievement, and growth to the job.

### J. WORKERS' PARTICIPATION & QUALITY CIRCLES
Small groups of 6–12 workers solving problems in their own work area (Kaizen).

### K. ERGONOMICS (Human Factors Engineering)
Designing the job **to fit the worker**, not the other way around.

### L. WORKING CONDITIONS
ISO 45001 compliance, optimum temperature (**18–24°C for assembly**), lighting (**≥ 300 lux**), noise control (**< 85 dB**).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (CRITICAL — Memorize All)

> [!WARNING]
> **The vertical pipe `|` symbol will break the markdown table. We use `\vert` instead. This is the exact KTU-style cheat-sheet.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation (LaTeX-safe) | Typical Application |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Labour Productivity (Basic)** | $\text{LP} = \dfrac{Q}{L}$ | Q = units, L = man-hours |
| 2 | **Productivity Index** | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{\text{Standard Output}}{\text{Actual Output}} \times 100$ | Efficiency in % |
| 3 | **Efficiency Rating** | $\text{Eff} = \dfrac{\text{Standard Time}}{\text{Actual Time}} \times 100$ | Worker efficiency |
| 4 | **Normal Time** | $\text{NT} = \text{Observed Time} \times \text{Rating Factor}$ | Time study |
| 5 | **Standard Time** | $\text{ST} = \text{NT} \times (1 + \text{Allowance})$ | Adds rest, contingency |
| 6 | **Halsey Plan Total Wages** | $E = T \cdot R + \dfrac{S}{2} \cdot R$ | T = time taken, S = time saved |
| 7 | **Rowan Plan Total Wages** | $E = T \cdot R + \dfrac{S}{T_s} \cdot T \cdot R$ | T_s = standard time |
| 8 | **Taylor Diff. Piece Rate** | Below std: $0.8 \cdot P_s \cdot T$ \quad Above std: $1.2 \cdot P_s \cdot T$ | High / Low piece rates |
| 9 | **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Availability × Performance × Quality |
| 10 | **Capacity Utilisation** | $\text{CU} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Max Possible Output}} \times 100$ | Plant load factor |
| 11 | **Absenteeism Cost Index** | $\text{ACI} = \dfrac{\text{Man-hours Lost}}{\text{Scheduled Man-hours}} \times 100$ | Lost productivity |
| 12 | **% Idle Time** | $\%IT = \dfrac{\text{Idle Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100$ | Wasted labour |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Allowances for Auto Industry:** Personal needs = **10%**, Basic fatigue = **10%**, Contingency = **5%**, so total allowance often = **15–25%**. Always use **25%** for heavy assembly if KTU does not specify.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Plant Layout Engineers** use method study to redesign assembly lines for **Takt-time balancing**.
- **Industrial Engineers** use time study to set the **Standard Minute Value (SMV)** for garment & auto workers.
- **Human Factors Engineers** apply motion economy in designing car dashboards and operator cockpits.
- **HR & L&D teams** use training ROI analysis to justify skill-building budgets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Observed Time to Standard Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation chain is a **MANDATORY KTU exam derivation**. Memorize the chain: **OT → NT → ST**.

We begin with the basic observation that any stopwatch reading captured during a time study must be normalised to a standard worker's pace and then relaxed by allowances.

### Step 1 — Capture the Observed Time (OT)
The industrial engineer measures the time taken by the operator using a stopwatch, typically using the **snap-back method** or **continuous timing method**. Let this be denoted $OT_i$ for each cycle $i$.

### Step 2 — Compute the Average Observed Time (AOT)
We sum all the individual observed times and divide by the number of cycles observed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOT &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} OT_i \\
    &= \frac{OT_1 + OT_2 + OT_3 + \dots + OT_n}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Performance Rating Factor (R)
The rating factor converts the operator's actual pace to the "**normal pace**" of a standard qualified worker. A rating of **100 (or 1.0)** indicates standard pace. Indian factory convention typically uses **British Standard Rating (BSR)** or **Pace Rating**.

$$
\text{Normal Time (NT)} = \text{Average Observed Time (AOT)} \times \text{Rating Factor (R)}
$$

### Step 4 — Add Relaxation Allowance (A)
Standard time must include rest for personal needs, basic fatigue, and unforeseen contingencies. Allowance (A) is expressed as a **decimal fraction** of NT.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Standard Time (ST)} &= \text{NT} \times (1 + A) \\
                          &= AOT \times R \times (1 + A)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Master Equation (KTU Final Answer)

$$
\boxed{\text{ST} \;=\; \frac{R \cdot (1 + A)}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} OT_i}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A time study was conducted on a CNC milling operation in an auto-component plant. Five readings were taken: **1.20, 1.30, 1.10, 1.40, 1.25 minutes**. The performance rating was observed to be **110%**. The personal, fatigue, and contingency allowance is **20%**. Determine the standard time per piece.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } & OT_1=1.20,\; OT_2=1.30,\; OT_3=1.10,\; OT_4=1.40,\; OT_5=1.25 \\
& n = 5, \quad R = 1.10, \quad A = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Sum the observations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{5} OT_i &= 1.20 + 1.30 + 1.10 + 1.40 + 1.25 \\
                   &= 6.25 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Average Observed Time (AOT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOT &= \frac{6.25}{5} \\
    &= 1.25 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the rating factor to get Normal Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= AOT \times R \\
   &= 1.25 \times 1.10 \\
   &= 1.375 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add allowance to get Standard Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT \times (1 + A) \\
   &= 1.375 \times (1 + 0.20) \\
   &= 1.375 \times 1.20 \\
   &= 1.65 \text{ minutes per piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer:
$$
\boxed{\text{Standard Time} = 1.65 \text{ min/piece} \;\;(= 99 \text{ seconds/piece})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Halsey Wage Plan (KTU 7-Mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Standard time for a job is **10 hours**. A worker actually completes the job in **8 hours**. His basic hourly rate is **₹50/hour**. Compute his total earnings under the **Halsey Plan** and **Rowan Plan**.

### Step A — Halsey Plan

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time Taken (T)} &= 8 \text{ hrs} \\
\text{Standard Time (T_s)} &= 10 \text{ hrs} \\
\text{Time Saved (S)} &= T_s - T = 10 - 8 = 2 \text{ hrs} \\
\text{Rate (R)} &= \text{₹} 50 / \text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Earnings (E_H)} &= T \cdot R + \frac{S}{2} \cdot R \\
                           &= (8 \times 50) + \left(\frac{2}{2}\right) \times 50 \\
                           &= 400 + 50 \\
                           &= \boxed{\text{₹} 450}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B — Rowan Plan

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Earnings (E_R)} &= T \cdot R + \left(\frac{S}{T_s}\right) \cdot T \cdot R \\
                           &= (8 \times 50) + \left(\frac{2}{10}\right) \times 8 \times 50 \\
                           &= 400 + 80 \\
                           &= \boxed{\text{₹} 480}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Parameter | Halsey Plan | Rowan Plan |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Time Saved | 2 hrs | 2 hrs |
| Bonus Paid | ₹ 50 (50% of saved) | ₹ 80 (proportional) |
| Total Earnings | ₹ 450 | ₹ 480 |
| Worker's Risk | Lower (50% guaranteed share) | Higher (proportional) |
| Employer's Cost | Lower | Higher |
| **When to Use** | When time-saved is large | When time-saved is marginal |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Productivity Improvement Engine

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level architecture diagram maps the **complete cause-effect chain** from measurement to improvement in an automotive plant. Mermaid-syntax safeguards applied (alphanumeric node IDs, plain uppercase labels inside quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([PROBLEM IDENTIFICATION]) --> A2[MEASURE CURRENT LP]
    A2 --> A3{SELECT METHOD}
    A3 -->|Engineering| B1[METHOD STUDY]
    A3 -->|Engineering| B2[TIME STUDY]
    A3 -->|Engineering| B3[MOTION ECONOMY]
    A3 -->|Engineering| B4[STANDARDIZATION 5S]
    A3 -->|People| C1[TRAINING SKILL DEV]
    A3 -->|People| C2[INCENTIVE WAGE PLAN]
    A3 -->|People| C3[QC CIRCLES KAIZEN]
    A3 -->|Environment| D1[ERGONOMICS DESIGN]
    A3 -->|Environment| D2[BETTER LIGHTING NOISE]
    B1 --> E1[IMPLEMENT NEW METHOD]
    B2 --> E1
    B3 --> E1
    B4 --> E1
    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[POST IMPROVEMENT MEASUREMENT]
    F1 --> G1{LP INCREASED BY 10 OR MORE}
    G1 -->|YES| H1[STANDARDIZE AND SUSTAIN]
    G1 -->|NO| A3
    H1 --> I1([HIGHER LABOUR PRODUCTIVITY])
    style A1 fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I1 fill:#8AE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style E1 fill:#8AB8E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 The Six-Step Method Study Procedure (Sub-Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[1 SELECT WORK] --> S2[2 RECORD FACTS]
    S2 --> S3[3 EXAMINE 5W 2H]
    S3 --> S4[4 DEVELOP NEW METHOD]
    S4 --> S5[5 INSTALL IMPLEMENT]
    S5 --> S6[6 MAINTAIN AUDIT]
    S6 -->|Continuous Loop| S1
    style S1 fill:#FFD580
    style S6 fill:#80FF80
```

---

## 4.3 Three-Pillar Productivity Triangle (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[METHOD ENGINEERING] --> R1[HOW]
    P2[PEOPLE ENGINEERING] --> R2[WHO]
    P3[PRODUCT DESIGN ENGINEERING] --> R3[WHAT]
    R1 --> OUT[IMPROVED LABOUR PRODUCTIVITY]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    style OUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

---

## 4.4 Time Study Chain — From Stopwatch to Standard Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[STOPWATCH READING OT] --> T2[SUM DIVIDE BY N]
    T2 --> T3[AVERAGE OBSERVED TIME AOT]
    T3 --> T4[MULTIPLY BY RATING R]
    T4 --> T5[NORMAL TIME NT]
    T5 --> T6[ADD ALLOWANCE A]
    T6 --> T7[STANDARD TIME ST]
    style T7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

---

## 4.5 Incentive Wage Plan Selection Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Worker Skill Level} -->|Skilled| Q2{Time Saved Magnitude}
    Q1 -->|Semi Skilled| Q3[HAWTHORNE PREMIUM]
    Q2 -->|Large| Q4[ROWAN PLAN]
    Q2 -->|Small| Q5[HALSEY PLAN]
    Q2 -->|Very Large| Q6[TAYLOR DIFFERENTIAL]
    style Q4 fill:#FFD580
    style Q5 fill:#87CEEB
    style Q6 fill:#FFA07A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define **Labour Productivity**. Mention its importance in the automotive industry.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Labour productivity is the ratio of output produced to labour input consumed, expressed as units per man-hour. It is a critical KPI in the automotive sector because auto OEMs operate on high-volume, low-margin production lines. Improving labour productivity directly reduces the per-vehicle cost, enhances global competitiveness against OEMs like Toyota and Volkswagen, and increases plant profitability. A 10% rise in labour productivity on a plant producing 200 vehicles/day can save several crores annually.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** List any **four** methods to improve labour productivity.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Method Study** — eliminates unnecessary operations and rearranges workflow.
> 2. **Time Study / Work Measurement** — sets realistic, fair time standards.
> 3. **Training & Skill Development** — enhances operator capability.
> 4. **Incentive Wage Plans** — motivate workers to exceed standard times.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (READ CAREFULLY):**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing **Method Study** with **Motion Study** — these are different. Method study covers the whole process; motion study covers micro-motions of a single operator.
> - **Mistake 2:** Writing only the **definition** of time study without showing the **derivation chain** OT → NT → ST. **You lose 3–4 marks** if you skip the chain.
> - **Mistake 3:** In Halsey vs Rowan, students **swap the bonus formulas**. Memorize: Halsey = `S/2`, Rowan = `(S/Ts) × T`.
> - **Mistake 4:** Forgetting to add **allowance** in standard time calculation — loses 2 marks immediately.

---

### 🅰️ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`

**Q:** With a neat flowchart, explain the **six-step procedure of Method Study**. How does it contribute to labour productivity improvement in an automotive assembly plant? *(14 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Six-Step Procedure of Method Study *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Step 1 — SELECT** the work to be studied based on the **ABC analysis** (high cost, high volume, repetitive work). In auto assembly, typically door-fitting or dashboard installation is selected.

2. **Step 2 — RECORD** all relevant facts by direct observation using charts:
   - **Outline Process Chart** — sequence of operations & inspections.
   - **Flow Process Chart** — includes material handling and delays.
   - **Two-Hand Process Chart** — left and right hand motions.
   - **Multiple Activity Chart** — man-machine interaction.

3. **Step 3 — EXAMINE** critically using the **5 Ws & 2 Hs**:
   - **WHAT** is done? Is it necessary?
   - **WHERE** is it done? Can the workplace be relocated?
   - **WHEN** is it done? Could it be combined with another operation?
   - **WHO** does it? Could a cheaper-skilled worker do it?
   - **HOW** is it done? Is there a better method?
   - **MEANS** — are tools adequate?
   - **SEQUENCE** — can the order be changed?

4. **Step 4 — DEVELOP** the improved method by combining the best answers from Step 3.

5. **Step 5 — INSTALL** the new method by gaining supervisor & worker acceptance, training, and phased implementation.

6. **Step 6 — MAINTAIN** by periodic audits, SOP updates, and feedback loops.

#### Part (b) — Contribution to Labour Productivity *(7 marks)*

Method study directly improves productivity by:
- **Eliminating unnecessary operations** (e.g., removing a redundant inspection station saves 30 seconds per vehicle).
- **Combining operations** — e.g., merging drilling and tapping into a single CNC operation.
- **Rearranging sequence** to reduce material handling (every handling ≈ 5% labour waste).
- **Reorganizing workplace** using 5S, reducing operator's reach distance.
- In an auto plant producing **250 vehicles/day**, even a **5-second saving per station across 50 stations** saves **4.3 hours of labour per day**, equivalent to **₹2,500+ daily** at ₹500/hr.

---

### 🅱️ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]` **(Alternative Choice)**

**Q:** A time study was conducted on a CNC turning operation in an auto component shop. The observations recorded for **6 cycles** were: **2.10, 1.95, 2.20, 2.05, 1.90, 2.15 minutes**. The operator was rated at **95%**. The personal and fatigue allowance is **15%**, and contingency allowance is **5%**. Determine:
> **(a)** Average observed time, Normal time, and Standard time per piece. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** If the operator produces **250 pieces per 8-hour shift**, calculate the operator's efficiency and suggest **two methods** to improve labour productivity on this operation. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Time Study Calculations *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Average Observed Time (AOT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum OT &= 2.10 + 1.95 + 2.20 + 2.05 + 1.90 + 2.15 \\
       &= 12.35 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOT &= \frac{12.35}{6} \\
    &= 2.0583 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Normal Time (NT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= AOT \times R \\
   &= 2.0583 \times 0.95 \\
   &= 1.9554 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Time (ST):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Allowance} &= 15\% + 5\% = 20\% = 0.20 \\
ST &= NT \times (1 + A) \\
   &= 1.9554 \times 1.20 \\
   &= \boxed{2.3465 \text{ min per piece}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Efficiency & Improvement Suggestions *(7 marks)*

**Step 4 — Operator Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Standard Production in 8 hrs} &= \frac{8 \times 60}{2.3465} \\
                                   &= \frac{480}{2.3465} \\
                                   &= 204.57 \text{ pieces}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Efficiency} &= \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Standard Output}} \times 100 \\
                  &= \frac{250}{204.57} \times 100 \\
                  &= \boxed{122.2\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Operator is producing **22.2% above standard** — possibly due to under-rated timing, high skill, or relaxed standards.

**Two Methods to Improve Labour Productivity:**

1. **Method Study & Standardization:** Redesign the CNC loading/unloading sequence using a **two-handed process chart** and introduce a **robotic loader** to free the operator for machine monitoring on multiple machines.

2. **Incentive Wage Plan:** Introduce a **Halsey Plan** (or Rowan Plan) to reward the operator for exceeding standard. This sustains high output and improves morale.

3. **(Bonus Point)** 5S workplace redesign — reduce tool-search time using shadow boards and color-coded fixtures.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 1**

- [x] **Definition:** Labour Productivity = Output / Labour Input (man-hours).
- [x] **Three Pillars:** Method, People, Product — all three MUST be tackled.
- [x] **Method Study = 6 Steps:** Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Install → Maintain.
- [x] **Motion Study = 17 Therbligs** (Gilbreth). Effective ones: *grasp, transport loaded, assemble, use, disassemble, release*.
- [x] **Time Study Chain:** OT → AOT → NT (= AOT × R) → ST (= NT × (1 + A)).
- [x] **Standard Allowance** for Indian auto industry ≈ **20–25%**.
- [x] **Halsey Bonus** = `S/2 × R`. **Rowan Bonus** = `(S/Ts) × T × R`.
- [x] **Halsey** favours worker less when time saved is large; **Rowan** tapers off naturally.
- [x] **5S** (Sort, Set-in-Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain) is the foundation of all layout & workplace improvements.
- [x] **Ergonomics** reduces fatigue → reduces allowance → improves productivity.
- [x] **QC Circles / Kaizen** are *soft* productivity methods from Japan.
- [x] **Taylor's Differential Piece Rate** uses **120% rate above standard, 80% below standard** (or 1.2 × Ps / 0.8 × Ps).
- [x] **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality** — world-class benchmark is **≥ 85%**.
- [x] Always write **units** in numerical answers (min/piece, ₹/hr, %).
- [x] Always end with an **interpretation sentence** in long answers — examiners love it.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Methods to Improve Labour Productivity** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manpower Planning - Factors Affecting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manpower Planning - Factors Affecting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Manpower Planning (MP)**, also referred to as **Human Resource Planning (HRP)**, is the systematic and continuous process of analyzing, forecasting, and determining the future manpower requirements of an organization in terms of **quantity** (number of employees), **quality** (skills, competencies, experience), **timing** (when they are needed), and **location** (where they are needed), followed by the formulation of action plans to bridge the gap between projected demand and anticipated supply.

According to **Decenzo and Robbins**, *“Human Resource Planning is the process of anticipating and providing for the movement of people into, within, and out of an organization.”*

In the context of the **automotive industry**, manpower planning holds critical importance because production is highly capital-intensive, demand is cyclical, and the workforce must possess a blend of **technical (mechatronics, robotics, quality control)** and **managerial competencies**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Manpower Planning is treated as a *strategic sub-system* of Operations Management in the auto industry. Students are expected to know the **definition, objectives, process, and the internal & external factors** affecting manpower planning in automotive manufacturing set-ups.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **head chef of a large restaurant** preparing for the upcoming wedding season. You know you will serve **500 guests per day** for the next two months. You cannot cook and serve by yourself; you need the right number of *chefs, sous-chefs, servers, cleaners, and cashiers* — each with specific skills and arriving at specific times. If you **hire too many**, you waste salary money. If you **hire too few**, customers wait and complain. If you **hire the wrong skills** (e.g., a baker when you need a griller), your kitchen fails.

**Manpower Planning is exactly this logic — but for a factory.**
In an automotive plant, the "wedding season" could be a new SUV launch, a festive demand surge, or a stricter emission norm requiring retooling. The "chefs" are the assembly-line workers, robotics technicians, quality engineers, and supervisors. **MP ensures the plant has the right people, with the right skills, at the right time, and at the right cost.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in Auto Industry:** The industry benchmark is the **Manpower-to-Vehicle Ratio (MVR)**, typically **12–18 man-hours per vehicle** in modern automated plants, and a **Direct-to-Indirect Labor Ratio** maintained near **70:30** in mass production.

## 1.3 Objectives of Manpower Planning (Quick Snapshot)

1. **Ensure timely availability** of right-skilled manpower.
2. **Optimize labor costs** and prevent over-staffing or under-staffing.
3. **Forecast future HR needs** aligned with production volume.
4. **Facilitate talent pipeline** through training and succession planning.
5. **Support organizational growth, expansion, and diversification.**
6. **Maintain industrial harmony** by balancing employee welfare and productivity.
7. **Comply with labor laws** (Factories Act, Apprentices Act, Industrial Disputes Act in the Indian context).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Process of Manpower Planning (Step-by-Step)

The manpower planning process in an automotive plant is executed as a **closed-loop cycle**, typically reviewed **quarterly or semi-annually** in alignment with the production plan.

1. **Step 1 — Analyze Organizational Objectives:**
   The plant's strategic plan (e.g., *"increase SUV production by 25% in FY26"*) is the **starting input**. Manpower needs are *derived* from production targets.

2. **Step 2 — Demand Forecasting (How many people do we NEED?):**
   - **Top-Down Approach:** Estimated by management based on budget and growth.
   - **Bottom-Up Approach:** Calculated per workstation using standard time studies and line balancing.
   - **Workload Method:** $D = \dfrac{\text{Total Standard Hours Required}}{\text{Average Productive Hours per Worker per Year}}$

3. **Step 3 — Supply Forecasting (How many people WILL we HAVE?):**
   - Internal supply: Existing workforce + new hires in pipeline − attrition − retirements − transfers.

4. **Step 4 — Gap Analysis (Demand − Supply):**
   - If **Positive Gap (Surplus):** Redeployment, VRS (Voluntary Retirement Scheme), attrition management.
   - If **Negative Gap (Deficit):** Recruitment, overtime, outsourcing, training.

5. **Step 5 — Action Plan & Control:**
   - Implement recruitment drives, training programs, contract labor, automation.
   - Monitor with KPIs: Absenteeism, Attrition Rate, Overtime Hours, Skill Compliance.

6. **Step 6 — Review & Feedback:**
   - Compare actual vs. forecast; refine the model.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Manpower Planning

Manpower planning is influenced by a complex web of **External (uncontrollable)** and **Internal (controllable)** factors. For an automotive company like Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, or Hyundai, these factors interact dynamically.

### A. EXTERNAL FACTORS (PESTEL Framework)

| # | Factor | Impact on MP in Auto Industry |
|---|--------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Economic Conditions** | Recession reduces demand → lay-offs; boom expands demand → mass hiring. Inflation raises wage bills. |
| 2 | **Government Policies & Labor Laws** | Factories Act 1948, Industrial Disputes Act, Apprentices Act, Code on Wages 2019 dictate hiring norms, working hours, contract labor limits. |
| 3 | **Technological Change** | EV shift, Industry 4.0, robotics reduce direct labor demand but increase need for *skilled mechatronics engineers* and *data analysts*. |
| 4 | **Demographic Changes** | Aging workforce in Japan/Europe vs. young workforce in India → training intensity differs. |
| 5 | **Competition & Market Dynamics** | New entrants (e.g., Tesla) force incumbents to launch new models faster, requiring flexible manpower. |
| 6 | **Social & Cultural Factors** | Diversity policies, women-in-shop-floor initiatives, work-life balance expectations. |
| 7 | **Political Stability & Globalization** | FDI, free-trade agreements, import duties on components affect plant location and thus manpower. |
| 8 | **Education & Skill Ecosystem** | Availability of ITI, diploma, and engineering graduates from local institutions. |

### B. INTERNAL FACTORS (Organizational)

| # | Factor | Impact on MP in Auto Industry |
|---|--------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Organizational Goals & Strategy** | Expansion, new product launch, exports, diversification. |
| 2 | **Organizational Structure** | Centralized vs. decentralized; flat vs. tall hierarchy. |
| 3 | **Production Schedule & Volume** | Lean production (Toyota) vs. mass production (old GM). Daily/Shift output decides headcount. |
| 4 | **Technology & Automation Level** | Higher automation → fewer direct workers, more indirect (maintenance, programming). |
| 5 | **Recruitment & Selection Policy** | In-house vs. outsourcing; campus hiring vs. lateral hiring. |
| 6 | **Training & Development** | Existing staff upskilling reduces external hiring need. |
| 7 | **Employee Turnover & Absenteeism** | High attrition forces continuous hiring buffer. |
| 8 | **Financial Resources / Budget** | Salary budget ceilings restrict hiring. |
| 9 | **Job Design & Work Measurement** | Standard time per operation directly determines headcount. |
| 10 | **Industrial Relations Climate** | Union strength, strikes, lockouts affect manpower availability. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are routinely tested in KTU Numerical/Analytical Part-B questions.

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Unit | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Manpower Demand (Workload Method)** | $D = \dfrac{W}{H \times P}$ | Workers | Where $W$ = total standard work-hours, $H$ = hours/worker/year, $P$ = performance rating factor |
| 2 | **Turnover Rate** | $T = \dfrac{\text{Separations}}{\text{Average Workforce}} \times 100$ | % | Forecasts replacement needs |
| 3 | **Absenteeism Rate** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Man-days lost}}{\text{Total Man-days scheduled}} \times 100$ | % | Adjusts effective workforce |
| 4 | **Direct-to-Indirect Labor Ratio** | $R_{D/I} = \dfrac{\text{Direct Workers}}{\text{Indirect Workers}}$ | Ratio | Indicates automation level; auto industry standard ≈ 70:30 |
| 5 | **Manpower-to-Vehicle Ratio (MVR)** | $MVR = \dfrac{\text{Total Workers}}{\text{Vehicles Produced per Year}}$ | Workers/vehicle | Plant productivity benchmark |
| 6 | **Net Manpower Requirement (NMR)** | $NMR = D + R - S_i$ | Workers | Where $D$ = Demand, $R$ = Replacement (attrition), $S_i$ = Internal Supply |
| 7 | **Productivity Index** | $PI = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Man-hours Input}}$ | Units/hour | Workforce efficiency check |
| 8 | **Training Need Ratio** | $TNR = \dfrac{\text{Untrained Staff}}{\text{Total Staff}} \times 100$ | % | Identifies skill gap |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, writing the *unit* of the final answer (e.g., *workers*, *%*, *vehicles*) carries **½ mark**. Always append the unit.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in the Auto Industry

- **Toyota Production System (TPS):** Uses manpower planning to maintain *Just-in-Time* (JIT) workforce — no excess workers on the line.
- **Tata Motors — Pune Plant:** Uses *Workload Method* to size workforce for the Nexon EV assembly line.
- **Maruti Suzuki — Manesar:** Maintains a *70:30 direct-to-indirect ratio* with extensive *multi-skilling* (one operator can handle 3–4 stations), reducing total headcount by ~15%.
- **Hyundai Motor India:** Recalibrates manpower every 6 months based on export demand forecasts to European markets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Manpower Demand Using the Workload Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU 2024 ESE for Operations Management papers.

### Problem Setup

An automotive plant plans to produce **24,000 passenger cars** in the upcoming financial year. The standard time to assemble one car is **40 hours**. Each worker works **8 hours/day**, **250 days/year**, and operates at a **performance rating of 0.90** (i.e., 90% efficiency). The company also plans for a **15% allowance** for absenteeism, fatigue, and personal time. Find the **number of direct workers required** on the assembly line.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data (clearly tabulated for KTU valuation):**
- Annual production target: $N = 24{,}000$ cars
- Standard time per car: $S_t = 40$ hours
- Working hours per day: $H_d = 8$ hours
- Working days per year: $D_y = 250$ days
- Performance rating: $P_r = 0.90$
- Allowance factor: $A_f = 15\% = 0.15$

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Standard Workload (in hours):**

$$W = N \times S_t$$

$$W = 24{,}000 \times 40 = 960{,}000 \text{ hours/year}$$

*Logic:* Every car requires 40 standard hours, so 24,000 cars require $24{,}000 \times 40$ hours of work.

**Step 2 — Calculate Effective Productive Hours per Worker per Year:**

First, find **basic hours per worker per year**:

$$H_b = H_d \times D_y = 8 \times 250 = 2{,}000 \text{ hours/year}$$

*Logic:* A worker is available 8 hours/day × 250 days.

Now, adjust for **Performance Rating (P_r)** and **Allowance Factor (A_f)**:

$$H_{eff} = H_b \times P_r \times (1 - A_f)$$

$$H_{eff} = 2{,}000 \times 0.90 \times (1 - 0.15)$$

$$H_{eff} = 2{,}000 \times 0.90 \times 0.85$$

$$H_{eff} = 1{,}530 \text{ hours/worker/year}$$

*Logic:* A worker is only 90% efficient (P_r) and is absent/relaxed for 15% of time (Allowance). So in 2,000 scheduled hours, only 1,530 hours are *actually productive*.

**Step 3 — Calculate Number of Direct Workers Required:**

$$D = \dfrac{W}{H_{eff}}$$

$$D = \dfrac{960{,}000}{1{,}530}$$

$$D \approx 627.45$$

*Logic:* Total workload divided by workload handled by one worker.

**Step 4 — Round Up (Always Round UP for Manpower!):**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always round UP, never down. You cannot have "0.45 of a worker". A fractional answer implies shortage, so the next whole integer is taken.

$$D_{final} = \lceil 627.45 \rceil = 628 \text{ workers}$$

**Final Answer:** The plant requires **628 direct workers** on the assembly line to meet the production target of 24,000 cars/year.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Net Manpower Requirement (NMR) with Attrition

### Problem Setup

A two-wheeler manufacturing unit currently has **500 workers**. Demand forecast indicates a need for **620 workers** by year-end. The company has 50 internal candidates undergoing upskilling who will be deployable by year-end. The historical **attrition rate is 12%**. Calculate the **NMR (Net Manpower to be Recruited Externally)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Current workforce: $W_c = 500$
- Demand (end of year): $D = 620$
- Internal upskilling pipeline: $S_i = 50$
- Attrition rate: $T = 12\% = 0.12$

**Step 1 — Estimate Replacement Need Due to Attrition:**

$$R = W_c \times T$$

$$R = 500 \times 0.12 = 60 \text{ workers}$$

*Logic:* 12% of the current 500 are expected to leave (resign, retire, terminate). They must be replaced to keep the headcount stable.

**Step 2 — Calculate Total Gross Demand:**

$$D_{gross} = D + R$$

$$D_{gross} = 620 + 60 = 680 \text{ workers}$$

*Logic:* To *end up* with 620 workers, the company needs to start with 620 + 60 (replacements) = 680 *total* workforce positions.

**Step 3 — Calculate Net External Recruitment (NMR):**

$$NMR = D_{gross} - S_i$$

$$NMR = 680 - 50 = 630 \text{ workers}$$

Wait — this exceeds the gross demand. The internal upskilling pipeline (50) only *partially* covers the 60 attrition replacements, so external hiring is needed.

Let us reformulate more carefully:

$$NMR = (D - W_c) + R - S_i$$

$$NMR = (620 - 500) + 60 - 50$$

$$NMR = 120 + 60 - 50 = 130 \text{ workers}$$

**Final Answer:** The company must **recruit 130 workers externally** to meet the year-end demand of 620, after accounting for 60 attrition replacements and 50 internal deployable candidates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Verification:** Starting workforce 500 + 130 new = 630. After 12% attrition (76 workers, roughly 60 on current base, 16 on new), the surviving workforce ≈ 630 − 76 ≈ 554… *Correction:* Attrition applies to current + new. The 50 internal upskilling does not replace *new* workers; they fill the *demand gap*. So $500 + 130 - 60 = 570$, plus 50 from pipeline = 620. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Factors vs. Manpower Planning Decisions

| Factor | Effect on Demand for Manpower | Effect on Supply of Manpower | Mitigation Strategy in Auto Industry |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EV Transition** | ↑ Demand for battery & software engineers; ↓ Demand for engine/transmission workers | ↓ Supply of ICE-skilled workers; ↑ Supply of EV-skilled workers | Massive re-skilling drives; partner with NSDC, ASDC |
| **Recession / Demand Drop** | ↓ Demand for assembly workers | ↑ Supply (no exits, hiring freeze) | Lay-off, sabbaticals, VRS, reduced shifts |
| **New Government Safety Norms (BS-VII, AIS)** | ↑ Demand for testing & compliance engineers | Stable | Hire specialists; outsource testing |
| **High Attrition (e.g., 25%)** | ↑ Replacement demand | ↓ Effective supply | Improve wages, ESOPs, grievance redressal |
| **Robotics & Automation** | ↓ Direct demand; ↑ Indirect (maintenance, programming) | Need new skill profile | Apprentice + Diploma in Mechatronics hiring |
| **Plant Expansion (New Line)** | ↑ Demand across categories | Need local talent | Pre-recruitment 6 months before launch |
| **Festival / Seasonal Demand Spike** | Short-term ↑ demand | Use overtime & contractors | Temporary staffing agencies |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Manpower Planning Process Cycle (Auto Industry)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Organizational Objectives and Production Plan"] --> B["Demand Forecasting - How many do we need"]
    B --> C["Supply Forecasting - How many will we have"]
    C --> D{"Gap Analysis - Demand minus Supply"}
    D -- "Surplus Gap" --> E1["Redeployment"]
    D -- "Surplus Gap" --> E2["Voluntary Retirement Scheme VRS"]
    D -- "Surplus Gap" --> E3["Attrition Management - No Replacement"]
    D -- "Deficit Gap" --> F1["External Recruitment"]
    D -- "Deficit Gap" --> F2["Overtime and Shift Extension"]
    D -- "Deficit Gap" --> F3["Contract Labour and Outsourcing"]
    D -- "Deficit Gap" --> F4["Training and Multi-Skilling"]
    E1 --> G["Action Plan Implementation"]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H["Monitoring with KPIs - Attrition, Absenteeism, Productivity"]
    H --> I["Review and Feedback Loop"]
    I --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam:** Trace the closed loop. Begin at *Organizational Objectives*, go through forecasting, identify the gap, choose a remedy from the four surplus options or four deficit options, implement, monitor, and feed back. This is the *full MP cycle* that KTU expects you to draw/describe.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Factors Affecting Manpower Planning (Fishbone / Ishikawa-Style Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROOT["Manpower Planning Effectiveness"]:::root

    EXT["External Factors PESTEL"]:::ext
    ECON["Economic Conditions"]
    POL["Government Policies and Labor Laws"]
    TECH["Technology and Automation"]
    DEMO["Demographics and Workforce Age"]
    COMP["Competition and Market Dynamics"]
    SOC["Social and Cultural Factors"]
    EDU["Education and Skill Ecosystem"]

    INT["Internal Factors Organizational"]:::int
    GOAL["Goals and Production Strategy"]
    STRUCT["Organizational Structure"]
    RECR["Recruitment Policy"]
    TRAIN["Training and Development"]
    TURNOVER["Attrition and Retention"]
    BUDGET["Financial Budget"]
    IR["Industrial Relations"]

    ROOT --> EXT
    ROOT --> INT
    EXT --> ECON
    EXT --> POL
    EXT --> TECH
    EXT --> DEMO
    EXT --> COMP
    EXT --> SOC
    EXT --> EDU
    INT --> GOAL
    INT --> STRUCT
    INT --> RECR
    INT --> TRAIN
    INT --> TURNOVER
    INT --> BUDGET
    INT --> IR

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ext fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000
    classDef int fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#b45f06,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Demand-Supply Gap Decision Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN["Inputs: Production Plan, Sales Forecast, Strategy"] --> CALC["MP Calculation Engine"]
    CALC --> D["Demand Forecast D"]
    CALC --> S["Supply Forecast S"]
    D --> SUB["Subtract D minus S"]
    S --> SUB
    SUB --> OUT1["Output: Surplus - S greater than D"]
    SUB --> OUT2["Output: Deficit - D greater than S"]
    SUB --> OUT3["Output: Balanced - D equals S"]
    OUT1 --> A1["Options: VRS, Redeployment, Hiring Freeze"]
    OUT2 --> A2["Options: Recruit, Overtime, Contract, Train"]
    OUT3 --> A3["Options: Maintain, Monitor KPIs"]
    A1 --> CTRL["HR Control Room - Monthly Review"]
    A2 --> CTRL
    A3 --> CTRL
    CTRL --> FEED["Feedback to Strategic Plan"]
    FEED --> IN
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level architecture maps the **decision logic** an automotive HR team follows. In the exam, even a simple labelled flowchart (5–6 boxes) earns full marks if labelled correctly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Manpower Planning. State any four external factors affecting it.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Manpower Planning (MP) is the ongoing process of forecasting an organization's future human resource needs in terms of *quantity, quality, timing, and location*, and developing action plans to bridge the gap between *demand* and *supply* of personnel. *(2 marks)*

**Four External Factors Affecting MP:** *(1 mark for naming, 0.25 each explanation is optional)*

1. **Economic Conditions:** Recession reduces demand, leading to surplus manpower; growth periods cause demand spikes and talent shortages.
2. **Government Policies & Labor Laws:** The Factories Act 1948, Industrial Disputes Act, and Apprentices Act dictate hiring ratios, working hours, and contract labor limits.
3. **Technological Change:** Adoption of EV technology, robotics, and Industry 4.0 changes the skill mix demanded.
4. **Demographic Changes:** An aging workforce in some regions and a young workforce in others alters training intensity and retirement planning.

---

### **Question 2: List any six internal factors that affect manpower planning in an automotive company.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (6 factors, 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Organizational Goals and Production Targets** (e.g., EV launch, export volume).
2. **Organizational Structure** (centralized vs. decentralized hierarchy).
3. **Recruitment and Selection Policy** (in-house vs. outsourcing).
4. **Training and Development Programs** (multi-skilling, apprenticeships).
5. **Employee Turnover and Absenteeism Rates** (determines replacement buffer).
6. **Financial Budget Constraints** (salary ceiling, capex allocation).
7. *(Bonus, if asked)* **Industrial Relations Climate** (union dynamics, strikes).
8. *(Bonus)* **Job Design and Work Measurement Standards.**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE full 14-mark question** per module, with a choice between two alternatives. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehension + Numerical*

> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **process of manpower planning** in an automotive industry with the help of a neat flowchart. Discuss in detail the **external factors** affecting manpower planning.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* An automotive component manufacturing unit plans to produce **36,000 gearboxes** in a year. The **standard time per gearbox is 25 hours**. Each worker works **8 hours/day for 300 days/year** at a **performance rating of 0.80**. The company provides a **20% allowance** for rest and personal needs. Calculate:
(i) Total workload in hours/year
(ii) Effective productive hours per worker per year
(iii) Number of direct workers required (round up to next integer).

---

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

**Manpower Planning Process (Flowchart Description — 3 marks):**
*(Refer to Diagram 4.1 above. Student must draw and explain 6 stages:)*

1. **Analyzing Organizational Objectives:** The MP process begins with the plant's strategic plan — annual production target, new model launches, capacity expansion plans. For example, a target of 1.5 lakh vehicles/year for FY26.
2. **Demand Forecasting:** Using the *Workload Method* or *Trend Method*, the total number of workers required is computed from the production plan.
3. **Supply Forecasting:** The HR department estimates internal supply — current workforce minus attrition minus retirements plus internal candidates under training.
4. **Gap Analysis:** Demand is compared with supply. A *surplus* (Supply > Demand) or *deficit* (Demand > Supply) is identified.
5. **Action Plan Formulation:** Surplus → VRS, redeployment, hiring freeze. Deficit → recruitment, overtime, training, outsourcing.
6. **Monitoring, Review, and Control:** Monthly KPIs (attrition, absenteeism, productivity) are tracked, and the plan is revised.

**External Factors (Detailed — 4 marks, 1 mark each for any 4 well-explained):**

- **Economic Conditions:** GDP growth, inflation, interest rates. Recession → demand drop → surplus workers; Boom → expansion → hiring surge. In the auto industry, demand is income-elastic.
- **Government Policies & Labor Laws:** The Factories Act mandates maximum working hours, safety standards, welfare measures. The Industrial Disputes Act governs lay-offs and retrenchment procedures. EV-focused FAME-II subsidies shift manpower needs.
- **Technological Change:** Adoption of robots, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), and IoT reduces demand for direct labor but increases demand for *maintenance technicians, robotics programmers, and data analysts*.
- **Demographic Factors:** An aging workforce (Japan, Germany) creates succession pressure; India's young demographic dividend provides abundant labor but with skill gaps.
- **Competition:** Entry of new EV players (Tesla, BYD) forces traditional OEMs to launch new models faster, increasing manpower needs for R&D and retooling.
- **Social & Cultural Factors:** Increasing participation of women on the shop floor, demands for flexible work, diversity and inclusion policies.

---

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Production target: $N = 36{,}000$ gearboxes
- Standard time per unit: $S_t = 25$ hours
- Hours per day: $H_d = 8$ hours
- Working days per year: $D_y = 300$ days
- Performance rating: $P_r = 0.80$
- Allowance: $A_f = 20\% = 0.20$

**(i) Total Workload (2 marks):**

$$W = N \times S_t$$

$$W = 36{,}000 \times 25 = 900{,}000 \text{ hours/year}$$

**(ii) Effective Productive Hours per Worker (3 marks):**

$$H_b = H_d \times D_y = 8 \times 300 = 2{,}400 \text{ hours/year}$$

$$H_{eff} = H_b \times P_r \times (1 - A_f)$$

$$H_{eff} = 2{,}400 \times 0.80 \times (1 - 0.20)$$

$$H_{eff} = 2{,}400 \times 0.80 \times 0.80$$

$$H_{eff} = 1{,}536 \text{ hours/worker/year}$$

**(iii) Number of Direct Workers Required (2 marks):**

$$D = \dfrac{W}{H_{eff}} = \dfrac{900{,}000}{1{,}536} \approx 585.94$$

Since we cannot have a fraction of a worker, **round up**:

$$D_{final} = \lceil 585.94 \rceil = 586 \text{ workers}$$
