# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**Q: Define Product Data Management (PDM). List any four core functions of a PDM system.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Product Data Management (PDM) is a software-based system used to centrally capture, organize, manage, control, and disseminate all product-related data and associated processes throughout the product lifecycle.

**Four core functions:**

1. **Data Vaulting and Document Management** — Secure, centralized storage of CAD files, drawings, and documents with metadata.
2. **Revision and Version Control** — Tracking of every change to a document with full audit trail.
3. **Bill of Materials (BOM) Management** — Multi-level structuring and management of EBOM, MBOM, and SBOM.
4. **Workflow and Change Management** — Automated routing of documents for review, approval, and release (ECR/ECO).

*(Any 4 of the 6 pillars from Section 1.6 would be acceptable.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**Q: Differentiate between EBOM and MBOM in the automotive context.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | EBOM (Engineering BOM) | MBOM (Manufacturing BOM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Perspective** | Functional / design view | Process / assembly view |
| **Owner** | Design engineering | Manufacturing engineering |
| **Created at** | End of design phase | Start of production planning |
| **Contents** | Functional parts and sub-assemblies | Includes fasteners, adhesives, lubricants, consumables |
| **Structure** | Logical, what-the-product-IS | Physical, how-the-product-is-BUILT |
| **Example** | Car has an engine, transmission, chassis | Car on assembly line gets 4 bolts + 50 ml grease + 1 gasket per wheel hub |
| **Consumer** | Engineering, costing, service | Shop floor, suppliers, MES |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. You are given an **internal choice** — you must answer either **Question A OR Question B** (full 14 marks).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **n-tier architecture of a Product Data Management (PDM) system** with a neat labeled diagram. Describe the function of each tier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Engineering Change Management (ECM) process** in detail. Explain the stages of an ECR and ECO, with a suitable example from the automotive industry. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Q.A(a) — PDM Architecture (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A modern enterprise PDM system follows an **n-tier architecture** that separates presentation, application logic, business rules, data, and infrastructure into distinct layers. This separation enables scalability, security, and maintainability.

**The 5 Tiers (5 marks):**

1. **Tier 1 — Presentation Layer:** The topmost layer that interacts with the user. It includes thick clients (e.g., CATIA-integrated PDM clients), web browsers, and mobile apps. Its function is to provide the user interface for searching, viewing, editing metadata, and initiating workflows.

2. **Tier 2 — Application Layer:** Houses the **workflow engine, change manager, BOM service, and search service**. This tier processes user requests, executes business operations, and orchestrates the flow of work between users.

3. **Tier 3 — Business Logic Layer:** Enforces the **organizational rules** of the company — for example, *"A drawing cannot be released without sign-off from the Chief Engineer"* or *"All fasteners must comply with AIS-010."* This tier contains the validation engine, approval routing rules, and access control policies.

4. **Tier 4 — Data Management Layer:** The **heart of PDM**. Contains the **Object-Relational Database (ORDB)** for metadata and the **File Vault** for actual CAD files and documents. Every file is assigned a unique object ID, and its metadata (part name, revision, owner, status) is stored in the database.

5. **Tier 5 — Infrastructure Layer:** The physical foundation — application servers, database servers, storage area networks (SAN), network switches, and firewalls. In modern deployments, this tier is increasingly **virtualized** on private or public clouds.

**Diagram (1 mark):** A clear 5-layer block diagram (as shown in Section 4.1) with arrows showing top-down request flow and bottom-up response flow.

---

### **Model Answer for Q.A(b) — Engineering Change Management (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Engineering Change Management (ECM) is the **formal, auditable process** by which modifications to a product are requested, evaluated, approved, implemented, and tracked. In the automotive industry, ECM is critical due to safety, regulatory, and cost implications. The ECM process is implemented within the PDM system through the **ECR → ECO** workflow.

**The 7-Stage Process (5 marks):**

1. **Problem Identification:** A field failure (e.g., brake hose crack in monsoon), a cost-reduction target, or a regulatory change (e.g., new BS-VII norms) triggers the need for change.

2. **Engineering Change Request (ECR):** The requesting engineer logs into PDM and creates an ECR, filling in the problem description, proposed change, affected parts, and change class (A/B/C). The ECR is routed to the **Change Review Board (CRB)**.

3. **Impact Analysis:** The CRB uses the PDM's **where-used reports** to identify all vehicles, sub-assemblies, tools, and suppliers impacted. The **cost and time** of the change are estimated.

4. **CRB Decision:** The CRB either **approves, rejects, or defers** the ECR. If approved, the ECR is converted into an **Engineering Change Order (ECO)**.

5. **Design Modification:** The design engineer modifies the CAD model in CATIA, saves the new file to PDM, and links the modified part to the ECO. The change class determines the urgency.

6. **Multi-Level Approval:** The ECO travels through the workflow engine for sign-offs from **Design Lead, CAE Lead, Manufacturing Lead, Quality Lead, Service Lead, Purchase Lead, and Chief Engineer**. Each sign-off is digitally recorded with timestamp and user ID.

7. **Release and Implementation:** After all approvals, the part is **released** with a new revision letter. The PDM system automatically **notifies ERP, MES, and suppliers**. Production cut-in happens on the specified date or from a specified VIN. The PDM maintains a **complete audit trail** for future reference and regulatory compliance.

**Automotive Example (1 mark):**
In 2018, a leading Indian OEM identified that the **fuel filler neck** in their compact SUV was prone to corrosion in coastal regions. An ECR was raised (Class B — Quality). The CRB found that the part was used in 2.3 lakh vehicles. An ECO was issued to switch from mild steel to **galvannealed steel** with a thicker coating. The change was implemented in production from a specific VIN, and a service campaign was launched for already-sold vehicles. The entire process was managed in the OEM's Teamcenter PDM system with full traceability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a)** What is a **Bill of Materials (BOM)**? Explain the different types of BOMs (EBOM, MBOM, SBOM) used in the automotive industry with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **benefits and challenges of implementing a PDM system** in an automotive company. Illustrate your answer with a **cost-benefit analysis**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Q.B(a) — Bill of Materials (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A Bill of Materials (BOM) is a **structured, hierarchical list of all parts, sub-assemblies, and raw materials** required to manufacture a product. In the automotive industry, multiple BOMs are maintained for the same vehicle to serve different functional needs.

**Types of BOMs (5 marks):**

1. **EBOM (Engineering BOM):** Created by the **design engineering team**, reflects the **functional structure** of the product. It is the *as-designed* view. *Example:* A car's EBOM lists the engine, transmission, chassis, body-in-white, and interior as top-level assemblies.

2. **MBOM (Manufacturing BOM):** Created by the **manufacturing engineering team**, reflects *how* the product is **physically assembled** on the shop floor. It includes all **consumables** that are not in the EBOM — fasteners, adhesives, lubricants, paint, labels, etc. *Example:* Mounting a wheel hub on the assembly line requires 4 bolts, 1 gasket, 50 ml of grease, and 1 wheel — only the wheel is in the EBOM.

3. **SBOM (Service BOM):** Created for the **aftermarket and service network**, shows the **serviceable parts** with their interchangeability codes. *Example:* A service BOM for an engine lists the gasket kit, filter, and spark plug as individual serviceable items, even though they are sub-components of the engine assembly in the EBOM.

4. **PBOM (Purchase BOM):** Used by the **procurement department** to buy parts from suppliers. It includes supplier part numbers, lead times, and pricing.

5. **CBOM (Customer BOM):** The actual configuration of the vehicle sold to a specific customer, linked to the **VIN** (Vehicle Identification Number). *Example:* A customer-ordered car with sunroof + navigation + leather seats has a unique CBOM reflecting those options.

**BOM Explosion Concept (1 mark):**
A **multi-level BOM** is a tree structure (parent → child). A **BOM explosion** flattens this tree to show every individual part needed to build the top-level product. PDM systems support both top-down (where-used) and bottom-up (BOM explosion) queries.

---

### **Model Answer for Q.B(b) — PDM Implementation: Benefits, Challenges, and Cost-Benefit Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Benefits of PDM Implementation (3 marks):**

1. **Single Source of Truth (SSOT):** Eliminates data duplication and version conflicts across teams and locations.
2. **Faster Time-to-Market:** Reduces engineering cycle time by 20–30% through parallel engineering and faster change implementation.
3. **Improved Product Quality:** Catches design errors early through CAE integration and digital mock-up, reducing warranty costs.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** Maintains full traceability for IATF 16949, AIS, ECE, and other regulatory audits.
5. **Cost Reduction:** Enables part reuse across platforms, reduces scrap/rework, and lowers warranty expenses.
6. **Supplier Collaboration:** Secure release of design data to suppliers via web portals, reducing email-based data exchange errors.
7. **Knowledge Preservation:** Retains engineering knowledge even after employees leave the company.

**Challenges of PDM Implementation (2 marks):**

1. **High Initial Cost:** Software licensing, infrastructure, and consulting fees can run into crores.
2. **User Resistance:** Engineers may resist moving from familiar file-based workflows to the structured PDM workflow.
3. **Integration Complexity:** PDM must integrate with multiple CAD, ERP, MES, and SCM systems, which is technically challenging.
4. **Data Migration:** Migrating legacy data from old systems to the new PDM is time-consuming and risky.
5. **Training Costs:** Users need extensive training on the new system.
6. **Customization vs. Standardization:** Balancing company-specific needs with industry best practices.

**Cost-Benefit Analysis (2 marks):**
As demonstrated in Section 3.5, a typical PDM implementation with an initial investment of ₹2 crore and annual maintenance of ₹20 lakh generates annual benefits of ₹1.2 crore (from error reduction and labor savings). This results in a **payback period of 2 years** and a **10-year ROI of 400%**, making PDM a high-value investment for any automotive company.

**Conclusion:**
Despite the challenges, the long-term benefits of PDM far outweigh the costs, and it is now considered a **mandatory infrastructure** for any serious automotive OEM or supplier.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PDM with PLM:** Students often use the two terms interchangeably. **PDM** is a *subset* of **PLM**. If the question asks for PLM, do not answer only about PDM.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "where-used" concept:** Many students answer BOM management but forget to mention the **where-used reverse query**, which is the heart of change impact analysis.
>
> 3. **No diagram = lost marks:** Every 7-mark question in this module *expects* a diagram. Always draw the **ECO workflow**, the **BOM tree**, or the **n-tier architecture**.
>
> 4. **Vague change process answers:** Simply saying "ECR is for requesting, ECO is for approving" is worth only 2–3 marks. You must explain the **7 stages in sequence with an automotive example**.
>
> 5. **Memorizing only definitions:** The KTU 2024 scheme tests **application and analysis** (Bloom's Level 3 and 4), not just recall. Be prepared for scenario-based questions like *"If the brake pedal design is changed, what reports will you generate in PDM?"*
>
> 6. **Ignoring effectivity:** Many students overlook the concept of **date/unit/lot/serial effectivity**, which is a high-weightage topic in configuration management.
>
> 7. **No numerical support in ROI:** If you claim PDM is beneficial, you **must** show the cost-benefit calculation. Hand-waving claims will not earn full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 60-SECOND REVISION — Read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **PDM = Centralized management of all product data** (CAD, drawings, BOMs, changes, documents).
- ✅ **PLM = PDM + business processes + extended enterprise.**
- ✅ **PDM architecture = 5 tiers** (Presentation, Application, Business Logic, Data, Infrastructure).
- ✅ **6 Core capabilities:** Vaulting, Revision Control, Workflow, BOM, Configuration, Integration.
- ✅ **ECM Process = ECR → Impact Analysis → CRB → ECO → Design → Approval → Release → Implementation.**
- ✅ **3 Main BOMs:** EBOM (design), MBOM (manufacturing), SBOM (service).
- ✅ **Where-Used** = upward query (impact analysis); **BOM Explosion** = downward query (MRP/costing).
- ✅ **Effectivity = Date / Unit / Lot / Serial** — defines when a part becomes valid.
- ✅ **RBAC = Role-Based Access Control** — enforces principle of least privilege.
- ✅ **PDM ROI drivers:** Reduced rework, faster time-to-market, lower warranty costs.
- ✅ **Change Classes:** A (Safety — immediate), B (Quality — next model), C (Cost — next design).
- ✅ **Integration:** PDM ↔ CAD (CATIA, NX), ERP (SAP), MES, SCM, CRM.
- ✅ **PDM Lifecycle Support:** Concept → Design → Prototype → Testing → Production → Service → EOL.
- ✅ **Top PDM Vendors:** Siemens Teamcenter, Dassault Enovia/3DEXPERIENCE, PTC Windchill, Aras, SAP PLM.
- ✅ **Exam Mantra:** Always pair your answer with a **diagram** and a **real automotive example**.

---

> **🏁 END OF NOTES — PEAUT524 MODULE 2 — PRODUCT DATA MANAGEMENT (PDM)**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | Aligned with NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education | Bloom's Taxonomy Levels 1–4 covered.*
