The automotive industry uses **multiple specialized BOM types** for different stages and purposes. **Types of BOM — Detailed Explanation (4 Marks):** 1. **Engineering BOM (EBOM):** The as-designed structure originating from CAD. Example: A Maruti Brezza's EBOM contains 1.6L K-Series engine components as designed in CATIA. 2. **Manufacturing BOM (MBOM):** The as-built structure with all process consumables added. Example: The same engine's MBOM includes torque sealants, thread lockers, oil for pre-filling, and shop-floor gaskets. 3. **Planning BOM (PBOM):** A summarized BOM used for long-term material forecasting and capacity planning. Used by Tata Motors for 12-month vendor scheduling. 4. **Service BOM (SBOM):** Lists only parts that can be ordered as spares post-warranty, including service kits (e.g., 30,000 km service kit containing oil filter, air filter, spark plugs). 5. **Configurable BOM (CBOM):** Supports build-to-order variants. Example: Hyundai Creta's CBOM allows 15 engine-transmission combinations × 6 trim levels × 5 color options = 450+ variants. 6. **Modular BOM / Kit BOM:** Group components into functional modules. Example: VW Polo exports CKD kits to South Africa in modular containers — *Front-End Module, Cockpit Module, Powertrain Module*. **Modular BOM in CKD/SKD Operations (2 Marks):** In **CKD (Completely Knocked Down)** and **SKD (Semi-Knocked Down)** operations, the manufacturer in one country (e.g., India) ships the vehicle in modules to assembly plants in other countries (e.g., Africa, Latin America, ASEAN). - A **Modular BOM** groups parts into self-contained kits (e.g., "Engine Bay Kit" with engine, harness, radiator, hoses pre-bundled). - This **reduces kitting time** at the destination plant by 30–40%. - **Maruti Suzuki** ships modular kits to its facility in **Gurugram for export to Nepal and Bhutan** — modular BOMs enable 1-day assembly of an SKD vehicle. - **Quality control** is easier since each module can be inspected as a unit before crating. **Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** Proper BOM type selection is foundational to efficient automotive production, costing, and global supply chain operations. --- ##### Part (b) Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** - Production Plan (Master Schedule) = **200 cars** - Each car has **1 Engine Assembly** - Therefore, **Engine Assembly requirement = 200 units** **Step 1 — Gross Requirement (2 Marks):** $GR_i = \text{Production Plan} \times \text{Qty per Engine}$ | Component | Qty/Engine | **GR** | |-----------|-----------|--------| | Engine Block | 1 | $200 \times 1 = 200$ | | Piston | 4 | $200 \times 4 = 800$ | | Cylinder Head | 1 | $200 \times 1 = 200$ | | Crankshaft | 1 | $200 \times 1 = 200$ | | Connecting Rod | 4 | $200 \times 4 = 800$ | | Valve Set | 8 | $200 \times 8 = 1600$ | | Timing Belt | 1 | $200 \times 1 = 200$ | | Oil Pan | 1 | $200 \times 1 = 200$ | **Step 2 — Available Stock and Net Requirement (3 Marks):** $AS_i = OH_i + SR_i = OH_i + 0$ $NR_i = GR_i - AS_i \quad$ (if positive) | Component | GR | OH | AS | **NR** | |-----------|----|----|----|--------| | Engine Block | 200 | 50 | 50 | **150** | | Piston | 800 | 350 | 350 | **450** | | Cylinder Head | 200 | 80 | 80 | **120** | | Crankshaft | 200 | 90 | 90 | **110** | | Connecting Rod | 800 | 320 | 320 | **480** | | Valve Set | 1600 | 700 | 700 | **900** | | Timing Belt | 200 | 120 | 120 | **80** | | Oil Pan | 200 | 80 | 80 | **120** | **Step 3 — Top 3 Critical Components (1 Mark):** Ranking by **absolute Net Requirement (deficit)**: 1. **Valve Set — NR = 900 units** (longest production lead time, foundry-intensive) 2. **Connecting Rod — NR = 480 units** (forging-dependent, 4-week lead time) 3. **Piston — NR = 450 units** (aluminum casting, 3-week lead time) **Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** Immediate purchase orders must be raised for the above three items, with **Valve Set** being the most time-sensitive due to foundry scheduling and the absence of any alternate qualified vendor. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Discuss the concept of **Low-Level Coding (LLC)** and **Phantom Assembly** in BOM design. How do they prevent errors in MRP execution? *(7 Marks)* **(b)** With a suitable **multi-level BOM diagram**, explain how a **4-wheeler passenger car** is decomposed from the finished good level down to the fastener level. What is the significance of the **BOM Accuracy** metric in real automotive plants? *(7 Marks)* --- #### **Model Solution – Question B** ##### Part (a) Solution (7 Marks) **Low-Level Coding (LLC) — Explanation (3 Marks):** The **Low-Level Code** is the **numerically deepest (highest level number)** at which a particular component appears in *any* BOM in the system. MRP systems use LLC to ensure that **each component is planned exactly once**, at its lowest usage point, to avoid: - Double-counting (same part scheduled twice) - Out-of-sequence orders - Inventory build-up and "MRP nervousness" **Example:** A **M10 Hex Bolt** used in: - Engine Mount (Level 2) → contributes to LLC of 2 - Transmission Housing (Level 2) → contributes to LLC of 2 - Seat Frame (Level 3) → contributes to LLC of 3 Therefore, **LLC of M10 Hex Bolt = 3**. The MRP system will plan this bolt only at Level 3, accumulating *all* requirements (engine + transmission + seat) into a single consolidated order. **Phantom Assembly — Explanation (2.5 Marks):** A **Phantom Assembly** is a *logical* grouping of parts that: - Exists in the BOM structure - Is **never actually built, stocked, or moved as a unit** - Has its components consumed directly by the parent operation - Has **zero lead time** and **zero cost rollup** (for inventory valuation) **Example:** The *Engine Wiring Harness Sub-Assembly* is a phantom — wires, connectors, and clips are kitted and assembled directly onto the engine on the line; the harness is never stored as a discrete item. This is common in: - Paint shop (primer + base coat + clear coat) - Welding jigs and clamps - Trim and final assembly line **How They Prevent MRP Errors (1.5 Marks):** | Issue | Prevention Mechanism | |-------|---------------------| | Double planning of common parts | LLC ensures single planning point | | Inflated WIP inventory | Phantoms prevent fictitious sub-assemblies | | Lead time accumulation errors | Phantoms have zero lead time | | Lot sizing errors | LLC ensures lot-for-lot at the right level | | Order release sequencing | Net requirements are correctly timed | --- ##### Part (b) Solution (7 Marks) **Multi-Level BOM Diagram of a 4-Wheeler Car (5 Marks):** The typical decomposition of a passenger car (e.g., Maruti Swift) is: - **Level 0 — Finished Good:** Maruti Swift Petrol VX - **Level 1 — Major Modules** (~7 modules): - Powertrain Module - Body-in-White (BIW) - Front-End Module - Rear-End Module - Cockpit / Interior Module - Chassis / Suspension Module - Electrical & Electronics Module - **Level 2 — Sub-Assemblies** (~30–50): Engine, Transmission, Dashboard, Front Bumper, Doors, Seats, etc. - **Level 3 — Sub-Sub-Assemblies** (~200+): Piston, Crankshaft, ECU, Window Regulator, Seat Frame, etc. - **Level 4 — Parts** (~2000+): Individual castings, forgings, plastic parts, stampings - **Level 5 — Sub-Parts** (~5000+): O-rings, gaskets, washers, small brackets - **Level 6 — Fasteners** (~10000+): Bolts, nuts, screws, rivets, clips, hose clamps ``` L0: CAR (Finished Good) └── L1: Powertrain Module └── L2: Engine Assembly └── L3: Piston, Cylinder Head, Crankshaft └── L4: Piston Ring, Piston Pin, Valve, Bolt └── L5/L6: Snap Ring, O-Ring, M6 Bolt ``` **Significance of BOM Accuracy (2 Marks):** **BOM Accuracy** = (Number of correct BOM lines) ÷ (Total BOM lines) × **100%** In real automotive plants, BOM accuracy is a **Tier-1 KPI** for the Production Planning department. - **Acceptable:** ≥ 99.0% - **World-Class (Toyota, Honda):** ≥ 99.9% - **Below 95%:** Indicates serious engineering or data integrity issues **Consequences of poor BOM accuracy:** 1. **Line stoppages** — missing parts halt the conveyor 2. **Wrong parts fitment** — quality defects, warranty claims 3. **Inventory inflation** — phantom demand, excess stock 4. **Cost rollup errors** — mispriced vehicles, margin erosion 5. **Recall risks** — incorrect component traceability **Industry example:** In 2014, GM's ignition switch recall (2.6 million cars) was traced partly to a BOM/EBOM discrepancy where the defective switch was not correctly traced in the multi-level structure. This led to **BOM data governance** becoming a regulated requirement under IATF 16949. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > - ✘ **Confusing EBOM with MBOM** — these are *different*; you will lose 1–2 marks if not distinguished clearly. > - ✘ **Forgetting the "+SR" term** in Net Requirement — many students write $NR = GR - OH$ and lose 1 full mark. > - ✘ **Not stating the BOM level** (L0, L1, L2...) in multi-level questions — examiners specifically check for level numbering. > - ✘ **Confusing Phantom with Sub-Assembly** — phantoms are *never built*; sub-assemblies are. Mixing these up costs 2 marks. > - ✘ **Skipping the UOM and Scrap %** when listing BOM fields — these are favorite tick-box items. > - ✘ **Drawing the BOM tree without quantity per** — examiners specifically look for the multiplier on each line. > - ✘ **Ignoring the Low-Level Code** in MRP-related questions — LLC is mandatory whenever common parts appear at multiple levels. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bill of Material (BOM)** **Core Concepts (Must Know):** - ☐ BOM = structured list of all parts to make **one** finished product - ☐ BOM is the **master input** to MRP, MPS, costing, and procurement - ☐ Each line has: **Part Number, Qty/Parent, UOM, Level, ECN, Scrap %** **BOM Types — Memorize the 5×4 Matrix:** - ☐ **EBOM** (Engineering) — designed structure from CAD - ☐ **MBOM** (Manufacturing) — as-built with process consumables - ☐ **PBOM** (Planning) — long-term material forecast - ☐ **SBOM** (Service) — spare parts post-warranty - ☐ **CBOM** (Configurable) — build-to-order variants - ☐ **Modular/Kit BOM** — used in CKD/SKD exports - ☐ **Super/Family BOM** — common parts across variants - ☐ **Single-Level vs Multi-Level** — display format choice - ☐ **Phantom BOM** — logical, never built or stocked **Advanced Concepts:** - ☐ **Low-Level Code (LLC)** = deepest level of any common part - ☐ **Phantom Assembly** = zero lead time, zero stock, direct consumption - ☐ **Modular BOM** = kit of parts pre-assembled off-line - ☐ **Configurable BOM** = rule-based variant generation - ☐ **BOM Explosion** = MRP multiplication of parent qty × child qty - ☐ **BOM Accuracy KPI** ≥ 99.5% in world-class automotive plants **Key Formulas (Recall Instantly):** - ☐ $GR_i = \sum (PQ_j \times Q_{i,j})$ - ☐ $NR_i = GR_i - (OH_i + SR_i)$ - ☐ $TBO = \sum (Q_i \times P_i)$ - ☐ $NR_{adj} = \dfrac{NR}{1 - S}$ (scrap-adjusted) **Real-World Links:** - ☐ SAP PP / Oracle / MES = tools that consume BOM - ☐ IATF 16949 = quality standard requiring BOM governance - ☐ ELV Directive = uses SBOM for end-of-life vehicle dismantling - ☐ Toyota Production System = uses Modular + Phantom BOM heavily - ☐ Tesla Build-to-Order = uses Configurable BOM for millions of variants **Quick Exam Heuristics:** - ☐ Always show **L0 → L1 → L2...** in BOM diagrams - ☐ Always write **qty per assembly** on every connecting line - ☐ In numericals, compute **GR first, then NR** - ☐ Top 3 critical components = **highest NR × longest lead time** - ☐ Mention **IATF 16949 / ISO 9001** linkage for full marks - ☐ Draw **dashed box** for Phantom Assemblies in diagrams
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bill of Material (BOM) — *End of Notes*

topics.title: Objectives
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Objectives

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objectives of Production Planning and Control (PPC) in the Automotive Industry** refer to the well-defined, measurable, and time-bound goals that guide the systematic planning, coordination, routing, scheduling, dispatching, and expediting of all production activities in an automobile manufacturing plant. These objectives ensure that the right **product**, in the **right quantity**, of the **right quality**, is produced at the **right time**, at the **right cost**, using the **right method**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course Code: PEAUT524), the objectives of PPC form the **strategic backbone** of an automobile OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) such as Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, Hyundai, or Toyota, where the entire production ecosystem — from raw steel sheets to the final assembled car — must be choreographed with military precision.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Symphony Orchestra of a Maruti Assembly Line**
>
> Imagine an automotive assembly line as a **Symphony Orchestra** performing Beethoven's 9th Symphony. Each musician (worker/machine) plays a different instrument (engine assembly, painting, welding). The **Conductor** is the **PPC Manager**. The **Sheet Music (Objectives)** tells the conductor:
> - *What tempo to maintain* (Production Rate / Takt Time)
> - *When each section plays* (Scheduling)
> - *How loud each instrument should be* (Quality Standards)
> - *When to pause for tuning* (Maintenance / Changeover)
>
> Without a clear **set of objectives**, the orchestra (assembly line) produces *noise* — cars that are delayed, over-budget, and defective. The **Objectives of PPC are the Sheet Music** that transforms industrial chaos into automotive harmony.

### 1.3 Why "Objectives" Matter in the Auto Industry

The global automotive industry operates on **Just-In-Time (JIT)** and **Lean Manufacturing** principles. Without crystal-clear PPC objectives, a single missing bolt can halt an entire production line costing **₹15-20 lakhs per hour** in lost revenue. Therefore, the objectives are not just academic — they are **profitability survival tools**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in PPC Objectives

The following bolded metrics are the universally accepted Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in any automobile PPC system:

- **Takt Time** = Available Production Time / Customer Demand
- **Cycle Time** = Time required to complete one unit of work at a station
- **Lead Time** = Total time from customer order to delivery
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** = Availability × Performance × Quality
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio** = Cost of Goods Sold / Average Inventory
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** = Good Units Produced / Total Units Started

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The objectives of PPC are directly linked to **Course Outcome CO3** — *"Apply production planning and control techniques in automobile manufacturing scenarios."* Every objective must be measurable, achievable, and traceable to plant-floor KPIs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 10 Primary Objectives of PPC in the Auto Industry

The objectives of Production Planning and Control in an automotive plant are not random — they are derived from the fundamental **5M + 1E framework**: **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Money, and Environment**. Each objective addresses one or more of these resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Insight:** When asked "List the objectives of PPC," examiners expect students to write **at least 6-8 objectives** with **brief one-line explanations**. Writing only the names without explanations fetches only **2 out of 3 marks** in Part A questions.

#### Objective 1: Ensure Timely Delivery of Finished Vehicles
- **Why:** Customer satisfaction in the auto industry hinges on delivery promise dates.
- **How:** Master Production Schedule (MPS) is prepared months in advance, factoring in supplier lead times, model mix, and dealer demand.
- **Industry Practice:** Toyota's *Heijunka* (level scheduling) ensures uniform vehicle delivery across the month.

#### Objective 2: Minimize Production Costs (Cost Optimization)
- **Why:** The automobile industry has razor-thin margins (typically **4-8% net profit margin**).
- **How:** Eliminating waste (Muda), reducing overproduction, optimizing batch sizes, and reducing Work-In-Progress (WIP) inventory.
- **Industry Practice:** Tata Motors' *One Platform, Multiple Models* strategy reduces per-unit cost.

#### Objective 3: Achieve Optimum Utilization of Resources
- **Why:** A robotic welding cell costs **₹5-10 crores**; under-utilization is financial loss.
- **How:** Balancing the line, scheduling maintenance during non-peak hours, multi-skilling workers.
- **Industry Practice:** **OEE target of 85%+** is the global benchmark for world-class auto plants.

#### Objective 4: Maintain and Improve Product Quality (Six Sigma / Zero Defect)
- **Why:** A single defective airbag or brake system can trigger a **mass recall** costing crores.
- **How:** Statistical Process Control (SPC), Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing), and Total Quality Management (TQM).
- **Industry Practice:** **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** = Six Sigma standard followed by Ford, GM, BMW.

#### Objective 5: Minimize Inventory and WIP (Lean Inventory)
- **Why:** Inventory is **frozen capital** that depreciates daily.
- **How:** Just-In-Time (JIT) delivery from suppliers, Kanban pull system, and vendor-managed inventory (VMI).
- **Industry Practice:** Toyota's *Kanban system* maintains **less than 2 hours of WIP** at most stations.

#### Objective 6: Ensure Flexibility in Production (Product Mix Changeover)
- **Why:** Modern auto plants produce **multiple variants** (petrol, diesel, EV, hybrid) on the same line.
- **How:** Single Minute Exchange of Die (SMED) reduces changeover time from hours to minutes.
- **Industry Practice:** Maruti's Manesar plant produces **15+ variants** on a single assembly line.

#### Objective 7: Coordinate All Departments Smoothly
- **Why:** Production cannot function in isolation from sales, purchase, quality, and dispatch.
- **How:** Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems like SAP-PP, weekly coordination meetings, and digital dashboards.
- **Industry Practice:** **SAP S/4HANA** is the industry standard ERP for auto OEMs.

#### Objective 8: Optimize Production Capacity (Capacity Planning)
- **Why:** Underestimating demand leads to lost sales; overestimating leads to idle capacity.
- **How:** Capacity Requirement Planning (CRP), bottleneck analysis, and demand forecasting.
- **Industry Practice:** Hyundai's Sriperumbudur plant runs **3 shifts × 365 days** to meet demand.

#### Objective 9: Maintain Safety and Environmental Compliance
- **Why:** Auto plants handle hazardous chemicals (paints, solvents, battery acid) and heavy machinery.
- **How:** OSHA standards, ISO 14001 (Environmental Management), and regular safety audits.
- **Industry Practice:** **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** is mandatory in paint shops across KTU-affiliated auto plants.

#### Objective 10: Develop a Continuous Improvement Culture (Kaizen)
- **Why:** The auto industry evolves rapidly (EV transition, autonomous driving, BS-VII norms).
- **How:** Kaizen events, Gemba walks, employee suggestion schemes, and PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycles.
- **Industry Practice:** **Toyota's Toyota Production System (TPS)** is built entirely on continuous improvement.

### 2.2 The Hierarchical Pyramid of PPC Objectives

The objectives can be visualized as a **Maslow-style hierarchy** where lower-level objectives must be satisfied before higher-level ones become meaningful:

| **Priority Level** | **Objective**              | **Focus Area**      | **Failure Consequence**          |
|--------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Level 1 (Base)** | Ensure Safety              | Workers + Machines  | Loss of life, legal shutdown     |
| **Level 2**        | Meet Delivery Schedule     | Customer            | Lost market share, penalties     |
| **Level 3**        | Achieve Quality Standards  | Product             | Recalls, warranty claims         |
| **Level 4**        | Optimize Cost              | Finance             | Reduced profitability            |
| **Level 5 (Apex)** | Drive Innovation & Kaizen  | Strategy            | Long-term obsolescence           |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **must-memorize formulas** for KTU University Examinations on PPC Objectives and their quantitative aspects. No vertical pipe `|` is used in any mathematical expression to preserve table formatting.

| **#** | **Formula Name**                | **Equation**                                                            | **Unit**          | **Boundary Condition / Note**                                                              |
|-------|---------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1     | Takt Time                       | $$T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D}$$                                 | seconds/unit      | Demand $D$ must be in same time unit as $T_{available}$                                   |
| 2     | Cycle Time                      | $$T_{cycle} = \dfrac{\text{Total Processing Time}}{\text{No. of Stations}}$$ | seconds/unit  | Must be $\leq T_{takt}$ for line balancing                                                |
| 3     | OEE                             | $$\eta_{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$                                    | dimensionless (%) | World-class benchmark: $\eta_{OEE} \geq 85\%$                                            |
| 4     | Lead Time                       | $$T_{LT} = T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{wait} + T_{transit}$$              | hours / days      | $T_{queue}$ depends on WIP and capacity                                                   |
| 5     | Inventory Turnover             | $$ITR = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg. Inventory}}$$                    | times/year        | Auto industry benchmark: $ITR \geq 12$                                                    |
| 6     | Capacity Utilization            | $$\rho = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Max Capacity}} \times 100$$ | %                 | $0\% \leq \rho \leq 100\%$; optimum $\rho \approx 80\%-85\%$                              |
| 7     | Line Balancing Efficiency       | $$\eta_{LB} = \dfrac{\sum T_{task}}{n \times T_{takt}} \times 100$$     | %                 | $n$ = number of workstations; $\eta_{LB} \geq 90\%$ is ideal                              |
| 8     | First Pass Yield                | $$FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100$$                        | %                 | Six Sigma: $FPY \geq 99.99966\%$                                                          |
| 9     | SMED Changeover Time Reduction  | $$\Delta T_{co} = T_{internal} + T_{external}$$                         | minutes           | $T_{internal}$ must be converted to $T_{external}$ wherever possible                      |
| 10    | Kaizen Cost Savings             | $$\text{Savings} = \dfrac{C_{before} - C_{after}}{C_{before}} \times 100$$ | %              | Cumulative across all Kaizen events in a financial year                                   |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a real automobile plant (e.g., **Mahindra's Chakan plant**), the PPC objectives are implemented through:

- **MES (Manufacturing Execution System):** Real-time production tracking
- **ANDON System:** Visual signal boards for line status (green = running, yellow = caution, red = stop)
- **Jidoka:** Autonomation — machines stop automatically when defects are detected
- **Poka-Yoke:** Mistake-proof fixtures preventing wrong parts installation

These objectives collectively drive the **Industry 4.0 transformation** in Indian automotive manufacturing, where IoT sensors, AI-based demand forecasting, and digital twins are becoming the new operational standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Takt Time as a Foundation of PPC Objectives

The **Takt Time** is the heartbeat of an automotive assembly line. It is the foundation upon which the objective of *"matching production rate to customer demand"* is built. The following exhaustive derivation explains how Takt Time emerges from the primary PPC objectives.

#### Step 1: Define the Problem
A Maruti Suzuki plant operates **2 shifts per day**, each of **8 hours**. Within each shift, there are:
- 1 planned break of 30 minutes
- 2 tea breaks of 10 minutes each
- 1 lunch break (only in the 1st shift) of 30 minutes

The plant must produce **480 cars per day** to fulfill dealer orders.

#### Step 2: Calculate Total Available Production Time per Day

$$
T_{available} = (2 \times 8 \times 60) - (1 \times 30) - (2 \times 10) - (1 \times 30)
$$

#### Step 3: Substitute Values (in minutes)

$$
T_{available} = 960 - 30 - 20 - 30 = 880 \text{ minutes/day}
$$

#### Step 4: Convert to Seconds for Consistency with Industry Standards

$$
T_{available} = 880 \times 60 = 52{,}800 \text{ seconds/day}
$$

#### Step 5: Apply the Takt Time Formula

$$
T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D} = \dfrac{52{,}800 \text{ sec}}{480 \text{ cars}} = 110 \text{ seconds/car}
$$

#### Step 6: Interpret the Result

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The plant must complete **one car every 110 seconds** to meet the daily demand of 480 vehicles. This is the **Takt Time** — the rhythm that synchronizes all subsequent PPC decisions (workstation design, cycle time, line balancing, supplier delivery frequency, etc.).

#### Step 7: Validate Using Line Balancing Constraint

The total task time at a single workstation must not exceed the Takt Time:

$$
\sum T_{task,i} \leq T_{takt} = 110 \text{ seconds}
$$

If a task (e.g., dashboard installation) requires **130 seconds**, the workstation is **overloaded**, and the line cannot meet the PPC objective of timely delivery. The solution is to **split the task** or **add a parallel station**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping PPC Objectives to Auto Industry Subsystems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below connects each PPC objective to a **specific subsystem** of an automobile plant, the **KPI used to measure success**, and the **typical failure mode** if the objective is ignored. This is a **high-yield visual** for KTU ESE preparation.

| **PPC Objective**                     | **Auto Plant Subsystem**            | **KPI Used**              | **Typical Failure Mode**                   | **Industry Example**                          |
|---------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Timely Delivery                       | Final Assembly Line                 | Schedule Adherence (%)     | Customer order delay, dealer stock-out     | Tata Nexon delivery waiting period            |
| Minimize Cost                         | Press Shop + Body Shop              | Cost per unit (₹)         | Erosion of profit margin                   | GST impact on sub-compact segment             |
| Optimum Resource Utilization          | Robotic Welding Cell                | OEE (%)                   | Idle robot arms, energy wastage            | Hyundai's smart factory, Sriperumbudur        |
| Improve Quality                       | Paint Shop                          | FPY, DPMO                 | Orange peel, dust nibs on car body         | BMW Spartanburg paint shop standards          |
| Minimize Inventory                    | Supplier Park + Sub-assembly Line   | Inventory Turnover        | Capital blockage, obsolescence             | Maruti's Manesar vendor park                  |
| Production Flexibility                | General Assembly                    | SMED Time (min)           | Excessive downtime during variant change   | Toyota's SMED implementation                  |
| Inter-departmental Coordination       | ERP / MES (SAP S/4HANA)             | Communication Cycle Time  | Misalignment of MPS with shop floor        | Ford's Global Order Promise System            |
| Capacity Optimization                 | Engine Assembly                     | Capacity Utilization (%)  | Overbooking or underbooking                | Kirloskar Oil Engines capacity ramp-up         |
| Safety & Environmental Compliance     | Paint Shop + Effluent Treatment     | TRIR (Total Recordable Incident Rate) | Worker injury, pollution penalties | Tata Motors Jamshedpur Zero Liquid Discharge   |
| Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)        | Cross-functional                    | Kaizen savings (₹/year)   | Stagnation, loss of competitive edge       | Toyota's 1 million Kaizen/year culture        |

### 3.3 Mathematical Proof: The Link Between OEE and PPC Cost Objective

The objective of **minimizing production cost** is mathematically tied to the **OEE** metric. The relationship is derived as follows.

#### Step 1: Decompose OEE into Three Factors

$$
\eta_{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

Where:
- $A$ = Availability = $\dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$
- $P$ = Performance = $\dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Count}}{\text{Operating Time}}$
- $Q$ = Quality = $\dfrac{\text{Good Count}}{\text{Total Count}}$

#### Step 2: Express the Cost of Poor OEE

If a plant's OEE drops from 85% (world-class) to 60% (typical Indian SME), the **effective production output** drops proportionally:

$$
\text{Output Loss} = (1 - \eta_{OEE}) \times \text{Max Capacity}
$$

For a plant with a max capacity of **1000 cars/day**:

$$
\text{Output Loss} = (0.85 - 0.60) \times 1000 = 250 \text{ cars/day}
$$

#### Step 3: Quantify the Financial Impact

At an average realization of **₹6 lakhs per car**:

$$
\text{Daily Revenue Loss} = 250 \times 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹15 \text{ Crores/day}
$$

#### Step 4: Conclude the Link

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A mere 25% drop in OEE results in a daily revenue loss of **₹15 crores**. This is a direct, quantifiable proof that **OEE optimization (a PPC objective)** is mathematically equivalent to **cost minimization (another PPC objective)**. This is why KTU examiners often ask numerical problems on OEE in 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The PPC Objectives Framework (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Auto Industry PPC System] --> B[Planning Phase]
    A --> C[Control Phase]
    A --> D[Coordination Phase]
    
    B --> B1[Demand Forecasting]
    B --> B2[Master Production Schedule]
    B --> B3[Capacity Planning]
    B --> B4[Material Requirement Planning]
    
    C --> C1[Dispatching]
    C --> C2[Expediting]
    C --> C3[Corrective Action]
    C --> C4[Quality Inspection]
    
    D --> D1[Inter-Department Meetings]
    D --> D2[ERP Integration SAP S4HANA]
    D --> D3[Supplier Coordination]
    D --> D4[Dealer Network Sync]
    
    B1 --> OBJ1[Objective: Timely Delivery]
    B2 --> OBJ1
    B3 --> OBJ2[Objective: Optimum Capacity Utilization]
    B4 --> OBJ3[Objective: Minimize Inventory]
    
    C1 --> OBJ4[Objective: Schedule Adherence]
    C2 --> OBJ4
    C3 --> OBJ5[Objective: Cost Optimization]
    C4 --> OBJ6[Objective: Quality Assurance]
    
    D1 --> OBJ7[Objective: Smooth Coordination]
    D2 --> OBJ7
    D3 --> OBJ8[Objective: JIT Supply Chain]
    D4 --> OBJ1
    
    OBJ1 --> OUTCOME[Plant Profitability and Customer Satisfaction]
    OBJ2 --> OUTCOME
    OBJ3 --> OUTCOME
    OBJ4 --> OUTCOME
    OBJ5 --> OUTCOME
    OBJ6 --> OUTCOME
    OBJ7 --> OUTCOME
    OBJ8 --> OUTCOME
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Relationship of PPC Objectives (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Z[LEVEL 5: STRATEGIC APEX]
        Z1[Kaizen and Continuous Improvement]
    end
    
    subgraph Y[LEVEL 4: FINANCIAL]
        Y1[Minimize Production Cost]
        Y2[Optimize Capacity Utilization]
    end
    
    subgraph X[LEVEL 3: OPERATIONAL]
        X1[Achieve Quality Standards]
        X2[Ensure Timely Delivery]
        X3[Production Flexibility SMED]
    end
    
    subgraph W[LEVEL 2: TACTICAL]
        W1[Minimize Inventory and WIP]
        W2[Inter Departmental Coordination]
    end
    
    subgraph V[LEVEL 1: FOUNDATIONAL]
        V1[Worker and Machine Safety]
        V2[Environmental Compliance]
    end
    
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> W1
    V2 --> W2
    W1 --> X1
    W1 --> X2
    W2 --> X2
    W2 --> X3
    X1 --> Y1
    X2 --> Y1
    X2 --> Y2
    X3 --> Y2
    Y1 --> Z1
    Y2 --> Z1
```

### 4.3 Inputs, Processes, and Outputs of PPC Objectives in an Auto Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Customer Orders] --> P[Production Planning]
    IN2[Forecast Data] --> P
    IN3[Supplier Capacity] --> P
    IN4[Plant Capacity] --> P
    IN5[Bill of Materials BOM] --> P
    
    P --> PROC1[Routing]
    P --> PROC2[Scheduling]
    P --> PROC3[Loading]
    
    PROC1 --> C[Production Control]
    PROC2 --> C
    PROC3 --> C
    
    C --> O1[Dispatching]
    C --> O2[Expediting]
    C --> O3[Inspection]
    C --> O4[Corrective Action]
    
    O1 --> OUT1[Finished Vehicle to Dealer]
    O2 --> OUT1
    O3 --> OUT2[Quality Certified Vehicle]
    O4 --> OUT3[Process Improvement Feedback]
    
    OUT1 --> OBJ[Achieved PPC Objectives]
    OUT2 --> OBJ
    OUT3 --> P
```

### 4.4 Integration of PPC Objectives with Industry 4.0 Technologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AUTO[Auto Plant Floor] --> IOT[IoT Sensors on Machines]
    IOT --> DATA[Big Data Lake]
    DATA --> AI[AI Based Demand Forecasting]
    AI --> MPC[Dynamic Master Production Schedule]
    MPC --> ROBOT[Robotic Execution]
    ROBOT --> CV[Computer Vision Quality Check]
    CV --> DASH[Real Time ANDON Dashboard]
    DASH --> KPI[OEE FPY Cost Tracking]
    KPI --> DEC[Managerial Decision]
    DEC --> AUTO
    
    KPI --> OBJ1[Objective: Zero Defect]
    KPI --> OBJ2[Objective: On Time Delivery]
    KPI --> OBJ3[Objective: Lowest Cost per Unit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** KTU 2024 scheme questions often ask students to *"Draw a block diagram of the production planning and control system."* The above diagrams are directly usable in answer scripts — students can replicate the structure and label the arrows with specific auto-industry examples to score **full 7 marks** in sub-parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"List the primary objectives of Production Planning and Control (PPC) in an automobile manufacturing plant."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The primary objectives of PPC in an automobile plant are:
>
> 1. **Ensuring timely delivery** of finished vehicles to dealers as per the promised schedule.
> 2. **Minimizing production cost** by eliminating waste, reducing WIP, and optimizing resource use.
> 3. **Achieving optimum utilization** of manpower, machinery, and material resources to maximize OEE.
> 4. **Maintaining and improving product quality** through TQM, SPC, and Six Sigma practices.
> 5. **Minimizing inventory** through Just-In-Time (JIT) and Kanban systems.
> 6. **Ensuring production flexibility** to handle multiple variants and quick model changeovers (SMED).
> 7. **Coordinating all departments** — sales, purchase, quality, and dispatch — through ERP integration.
>
> *(Note: Listing 6 objectives with one-line explanations fetches full 3 marks. Writing only names without explanations fetches 1-2 marks.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"Explain the role of the Just-In-Time (JIT) system in achieving the objective of inventory minimization in the auto industry."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Just-In-Time (JIT) system** plays a central role in achieving the PPC objective of inventory minimization in automobile manufacturing by ensuring that **parts and sub-assemblies arrive at the assembly line exactly when needed — neither too early nor too late**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key contributions of JIT:
> - **Eliminates WIP buildup:** Reduces Work-In-Progress inventory to less than 2 hours of stock at most stations.
> - **Reduces storage cost:** Frees up factory floor space, reducing warehousing costs.
> - **Improves cash flow:** Capital is not locked up in idle inventory.
> - **Exposes hidden problems:** A defect or delay becomes immediately visible, forcing quick resolution.
> - **Strengthens supplier linkage:** Vendors must deliver high-quality parts in small, frequent batches (e.g., every 2-4 hours).
>
> **Industry Example:** Toyota's Kanban system, the originator of JIT, maintains some of the lowest inventory levels in the global auto industry while achieving an OEE of 90%+.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **"Discuss in detail the objectives of Production Planning and Control in the automobile industry, with special reference to the following:"**
>
> **(a)** The role of the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** in achieving the objective of timely delivery. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The use of **Takt Time, OEE, and SMED** as quantitative tools to achieve the objectives of cost minimization, resource optimization, and production flexibility. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) Model Solution – Role of MPS in Timely Delivery (7 Marks)**

The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is the central document in any automobile PPC system. It is a **time-phased plan** that specifies *what* finished vehicles (e.g., Swift VXi in red, Baleno Alpha in white) will be produced, *in what quantity*, and *in which week*.

**Step 1: Demand Aggregation (1 Mark)**
The MPS is built upon the **demand forecast** from the dealer network, historical sales data, and macroeconomic indicators. For a Maruti plant, the MPS may project:
- Week 1: 1,200 units of Swift, 800 units of Baleno
- Week 2: 1,000 units of Swift, 1,200 units of Baleno

**Step 2: Capacity Check (1 Mark)**
The proposed production quantities are checked against **plant capacity** (number of shifts, machine availability, workforce strength). If capacity is insufficient, the MPS is adjusted by:
- Adding overtime
- Subcontracting non-critical components
- Adjusting the model mix

**Step 3: Material Availability Check (1 Mark)**
The MPS is exploded into a **Bill of Materials (BOM)** for each model. The MRP (Material Requirement Planning) system checks whether all required parts — engines, transmissions, electronic modules — are available from suppliers in time.

**Step 4: Final MPS Approval and Communication (1 Mark)**
Once approved by the Plant Head, the MPS is disseminated to:
- **Body Shop** (for welding fixtures and robot programs)
- **Paint Shop** (for color mix planning)
- **Assembly Line** (for sequencing)
- **Suppliers** (for delivery call-offs)

**Step 5: Schedule Adherence Tracking (2 Marks)**
During execution, **Schedule Adherence (%)** is calculated:

$$
\text{Schedule Adherence} = \dfrac{\text{Units Produced on Schedule}}{\text{Total Units Planned}} \times 100
$$

A target of **≥ 95% schedule adherence** is the industry benchmark for the "timely delivery" objective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The MPS acts as the **single source of truth** that translates customer demand into shop-floor reality. It is the operational expression of the "timely delivery" PPC objective.

---

#### **Part (b) Model Solution – Takt Time, OEE, SMED (7 Marks)**

**Takt Time and Cost Minimization (3 Marks):**

Takt Time ($T_{takt}$) is calculated as:

$$
T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D}
$$

For example, with **52,800 seconds/day** available and a demand of **480 cars/day**:

$$
T_{takt} = \dfrac{52{,}800}{480} = 110 \text{ seconds/car}
$$

This Takt Time enables **cost minimization** by:
- **Balancing the line:** All workstations operate at the same rhythm, eliminating idle time.
- **Determining optimal workforce size:** Number of workers = Total task time / Takt Time.
- **Justifying automation investment:** If a manual task takes 150 seconds but Takt Time is 110 seconds, automation is economically justified.

**OEE and Resource Optimization (2 Marks):**

OEE is calculated as:

$$
\eta_{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

A world-class target of **$\eta_{OEE} \geq 85\%$** ensures **optimum resource utilization**:
- **Availability (A):** Minimizes unplanned downtime through TPM (Total Productive Maintenance).
- **Performance (P):** Eliminates minor stoppages and reduced speed operations.
- **Quality (Q):** Reduces defect rates through Poka-Yoke and SPC.

**SMED and Production Flexibility (2 Marks):**

**Single Minute Exchange of Die (SMED)** is a methodology to reduce changeover time to **less than 10 minutes**. It is achieved by:
- Converting **internal setup** (done while machine is stopped) to **external setup** (done while machine is running).
- Using **standardized changeover procedures** with visual aids.
- Pre-staging tools and dies on mobile carts.

SMED enables **production flexibility** by allowing quick transitions between vehicle variants (e.g., petrol to diesel) on the same assembly line, a critical objective in modern multi-model auto plants.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]`

> **"Explain the following in the context of PPC objectives in automobile manufacturing:"**
>
> **(a)** How does the **Kaizen philosophy** support the PPC objective of continuous improvement? Discuss with a real-world auto industry example. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **OEE, Schedule Adherence, and Line Balancing Efficiency** for a hypothetical Maruti assembly line using the data provided, and comment on whether the plant meets world-class PPC standards. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) Model Solution – Kaizen and Continuous Improvement (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
**Kaizen** (Japanese: 改 善, "change for the better") is a continuous improvement philosophy that involves **all employees** — from the shop floor worker to the CEO — in identifying and eliminating waste in incremental steps.

**Origin (1 Mark):**
Kaizen was developed by **Toyota** as part of the Toyota Production System (TPS) post-WWII, and is now adopted globally by every major automobile OEM.

**How Kaizen Supports PPC Objectives (3 Marks):**

| **PPC Objective**           | **Kaizen Contribution**                                                       |
|------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Cost minimization            | Small savings from many Kaizens compound into crores annually.              |
| Quality improvement          | Kaizen events often target First Pass Yield (FPY) enhancement.             |
| Safety                       | Safety Kaizens (yellow-tag zones, 5S audits) reduce accident rates.         |
| Inventory reduction          | Kaizens identify over-production and excess motion.                          |
| Delivery                     | Lead time reduction Kaizens directly improve delivery performance.           |

**Real-World Example (2 Marks):**
At **Toyota's Deccan plant (Bangalore)**, a Kaizen event in 2022 identified that workers were walking 25 extra meters per cycle to fetch torque wrenches. The solution — installing a tool shadow board at each station — saved **17,500 man-hours per year** and reduced cycle time by 4 seconds. This single Kaizen contributed to the **"minimize cost"** and **"optimum resource utilization"** objectives simultaneously.

**Conclusion:** Kaizen is not a one-time event but a **culture** that sustains all PPC objectives over decades.

---

#### **Part (b) Model Solution – Numerical Calculations (7 Marks)**

**Given Data for a Hypothetical Maruti Assembly Line:**

| **Parameter**                          | **Value**         |
|----------------------------------------|-------------------|
| Planned Production Time                | 480 min/day       |
| Operating Time (after downtime)        | 432 min/day       |
| Ideal Cycle Time                       | 60 sec/unit       |
| Total Units Produced                   | 380 units         |
| Good Units (passed inspection)         | 360 units         |
| Number of Workstations                 | 8                 |
| Sum of All Task Times                  | 420 sec           |
| Units Planned in MPS                   | 400 units         |
| Units Produced on Schedule             | 372 units         |

**Step 1: Calculate OEE (2 Marks)**

Availability:

$$
A = \dfrac{432}{480} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

Performance:

$$
P = \dfrac{60 \times 380}{432 \times 60} = \dfrac{22{,}800}{25{,}920} = 0.8793 = 87.93\%
$$

Quality:

$$
Q = \dfrac{360}{380} = 0.9474 = 94.74\%
$$

OEE:

$$
\eta_{OEE} = 0.90 \times 0.8793 \times 0.9474 = 0.7496 \approx 74.96\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Schedule Adherence (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{Schedule Adherence} = \dfrac{372}{400} \times 100 = 93\%
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Line Balancing Efficiency (1 Mark)**

$$
\eta_{LB} = \dfrac{420}{8 \times 60} \times 100 = \dfrac{420}{480} \times 100 = 87.5\%
$$

**Step 4: Comment on Whether the Plant Meets World-Class Standards (3 Marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> - **Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds** in the OEE Performance formula.
> - **Calculating OEE in decimal and not converting to percentage.**
> - **Not providing an interpretation** after the numerical calculation — always state whether the value meets the world-class benchmark.
> - **Confusing Schedule Adherence with OEE** — they are entirely different metrics.

**Interpretation:**

| **Metric**                     | **Calculated Value** | **World-Class Benchmark** | **Verdict**            |
|--------------------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| OEE                            | 74.96%               | ≥ 85%                     | **Below benchmark** ❌ |
| Schedule Adherence             | 93%                  | ≥ 95%                     | **Slightly below** ⚠️ |
| Line Balancing Efficiency      | 87.5%                | ≥ 90%                     | **Needs improvement** ⚠️ |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The plant is **not yet at world-class standards**. Recommendations:
- **Improve Availability:** Implement TPM to reduce downtime from 48 min/day to 24 min/day.
- **Improve Performance:** Eliminate minor stoppages by upgrading sensor reliability.
- **Improve Quality:** Introduce Poka-Yoke at the top 3 defect-prone stations.
- **Improve Line Balancing:** Re-distribute tasks to achieve $\eta_{LB} \geq 90\%$.

After these improvements, the plant could realistically achieve $\eta_{OEE} \geq 85\%$, meeting global automotive manufacturing standards (e.g., WCM — World Class Manufacturing).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU ESE):**
>
> - The objectives of PPC form the **strategic compass** of any automobile plant.
> - There are **10 primary objectives** — students must remember at least **6-8** with one-line explanations.
> - The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is the operational tool for the *"timely delivery"* objective.
> - **Takt Time** = $T_{available} / D$ — the heart of line balancing and cost minimization.
> - **OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality — world-class target is **≥ 85%**.
> - **SMED** reduces changeover time to **< 10 minutes**, enabling production flexibility.
> - **Kaizen** is the cultural foundation for continuous improvement and is mandatory for sustaining all PPC objectives.
> - **JIT / Kanban** is the operational tool for inventory minimization.
> - **Safety and environmental compliance** are *foundational* (Level 1) objectives — they precede all other goals.
> - **Industry 4.0** technologies (IoT, AI, Digital Twin) are the modern enablers of all PPC objectives in Industry 4.0-ready plants.
> - The **Maslow-style hierarchy** of PPC objectives must be respected: Safety → Delivery → Quality → Cost → Innovation.
> - In **14-mark questions**, always provide a **numerical example** with full derivation and an **interpretation** linking back to a specific auto industry scenario.
> - In **3-mark questions**, listing objectives with brief explanations is mandatory — bare lists fetch only partial marks.
> - Real-world examples (**Maruti, Tata Motors, Hyundai, Toyota**) carry **bonus marks** and demonstrate conceptual depth to the examiner.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of the Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Elements of the Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Automotive Industry**, a **Supply Chain (SC)** is defined as a *systematic network of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources* involved in moving a vehicle or automotive component from **supplier to manufacturer, manufacturer to distributor, and finally to the end customer**. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** Operations Management framework, the supply chain in the automotive sector is a value-adding pipeline that integrates:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition:** *"The supply chain encompasses every effort involved in producing and delivering a final product or service, from the supplier's supplier to the customer's customer. In the auto industry, this includes raw material vendors, Tier-1, Tier-2, Tier-3 component suppliers, OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers), dealers, service centres, and end consumers."*

The supply chain is governed by **three primary flows**:
1. **Material/Product Flow** — Physical movement of vehicles, parts, and sub-assemblies.
2. **Information Flow** — Demand forecasts, order status, production schedules, and inventory data.
3. **Finance Flow** — Payments, credit terms, invoices, and cost transfers across the chain.

## 1.2 Core Elements of the Supply Chain

The KTU syllabus specifically identifies the following **core elements** of an automotive supply chain:

| # | Element | Automotive Context |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Suppliers (Tier-1, Tier-2, Tier-3)** | Provide raw materials, components, sub-assemblies |
| 2 | **Manufacturer / OEM** | Assembles vehicles (e.g., Maruti, Tata, Hyundai) |
| 3 | **Inventory / Warehousing** | Storage of parts, WIP (Work-In-Progress), finished vehicles |
| 4 | **Transportation / Logistics** | Inbound & outbound movement of parts and vehicles |
| 5 | **Distribution / Dealers** | Showroom network delivering cars to customers |
| 6 | **Customers (End-User)** | Individual buyers, fleet operators, export markets |
| 7 | **Information Systems** | ERP, SAP, MES for tracking and coordination |
| 8 | **Procurement / Sourcing** | Vendor selection, purchase order management |
| 9 | **Production / Operations** | Assembly line manufacturing |
| 10 | **Marketing & Sales** | Promotions, pricing, demand generation |
| 11 | **After-Sales Service** | Spare parts, maintenance, warranty claims |
| 12 | **Returns / Reverse Logistics** | Recalls, end-of-life vehicle disposal, recycling |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In the automotive industry, the supply chain is often called the **"Automotive Value Chain"** because every step adds measurable value (₹) to the final vehicle.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an automotive supply chain as the **human circulatory system of a car company**:

- **Heart (OEM)** — Pumps the product (vehicles) outward.
- **Arteries (Suppliers & Inbound Logistics)** — Carry raw steel, rubber, electronics, and engine parts *into* the OEM.
- **Veins (Distribution & Dealers)** — Carry finished vehicles *out to* customers.
- **Blood (Information & Finance)** — Flows continuously in both directions, feeding decisions everywhere.
- **Capillaries (Service Centres & Reverse Logistics)** — Reach every individual customer and bring back feedback, returns, and used parts.

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Analogy for First-Time Learners:** Imagine ordering a customised coffee. The farmer (raw material supplier) → roaster (Tier-2 supplier) → café chain (Tier-1/OEM) → barista (assembly) → counter (dealer) → YOU (customer). The coffee cup reaches you only when *all these hands cooperate*. **An automotive supply chain is the same — just on a ₹15-lakh scale with thousands of parts!**

## 1.4 The "5 Rights" of a Supply Chain (Automotive Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A well-managed automotive SC ensures the **5 Rights**:
> 1. **Right Product** — Correct part, correct specification.
> 2. **Right Quantity** — Avoid overstock of slow-moving SKUs.
> 3. **Right Place** — Delivered to the assembly line or dealer in time.
> 4. **Right Time** — JIT (Just-In-Time) delivery to minimise inventory.
> 5. **Right Cost** — Optimised landed cost for profitability.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Supply Chain Elements

### **A. Upstream Elements (Supply Side)**

**1. Suppliers — The Tier Hierarchy**
- **Tier-3 Suppliers:** Provide basic raw materials (steel, aluminium, plastic resin, rubber, glass).
- **Tier-2 Suppliers:** Provide sub-components (e.g., brake pads, gaskets, hoses, wiring harnesses).
- **Tier-1 Suppliers:** Provide major assemblies directly to the OEM (e.g., Bosch for ECUs, Continental for infotainment, MRF for tyres, Endurance for shock absorbers).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Fact:** A modern car has roughly **30,000+ parts** sourced from over **1,500 direct suppliers** globally.

**2. Procurement / Sourcing**
The process of identifying, evaluating, and selecting vendors. Common automotive frameworks:
- **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)** — Supplier monitors and refills OEM stock.
- **Long-Term Agreements (LTAs)** — 3–5 year price contracts.
- **Single vs. Multi-Sourcing** — Risk vs. cost trade-off.

**3. Inbound Logistics**
Movement of incoming materials from suppliers to the OEM plant. In the auto industry, this is dominated by **milk runs, cross-docking, and JIT deliveries**.

---

### **B. Internal Elements (OEM Operations)**

**4. Production / Assembly**
- Conversion of raw inputs into finished vehicles.
- Uses **assembly line balancing**, **lean manufacturing**, and **Industry 4.0** automation.

**5. Inventory Management**
Three classical types of automotive inventory:
- **Raw Material Inventory** — Steel coils, plastic granules.
- **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** — Partially assembled cars on the line.
- **Finished Goods Inventory** — Cars ready at plant yard awaiting dispatch.

**6. Quality Assurance & Information Systems**
- ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) systems like **SAP, Oracle, Baan**.
- MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems) for shop-floor control.
- Supplier portals for real-time PO tracking.

---

### **C. Downstream Elements (Demand Side)**

**7. Outbound Logistics & Warehousing**
- Movement of finished cars from plant to regional depots (CKD/CBU yards).
- Uses **car-carriers, railways, and roll-on/roll-off (RORO) vessels** for export.

**8. Distribution (Dealer Network)**
- Authorized dealers, sub-dealers, and online direct-to-customer channels.
- Stock allocation based on **territory potential, historical demand, and demographic data**.

**9. Marketing & Sales**
- Demand generation, branding, advertising, test drives, and digital configurators.

**10. Customers (End-User)**
- B2C (retail buyers), B2B (fleet/rental), Government, Export markets.

**11. After-Sales Service**
- Spare parts supply, scheduled maintenance, warranty servicing.
- Forms a major **revenue stream** (often 20–30% of OEM profits).

**12. Reverse Logistics**
- Product recalls, warranty returns, end-of-life vehicle (ELV) scrappage, and recycling.
- Critical under India's **Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are high-yield for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and Internal Assessment 2 (Module 3).**

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables Explained | Units / Notes |
|--------------|----------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = Annual demand (units), $S$ = Ordering cost (₹/order), $H$ = Holding cost (₹/unit/year) | Optimal order size that minimises total inventory cost |
| **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $\text{ROP} = d \times L + SS$ | $d$ = Average daily demand, $L$ = Lead time (days), $SS$ = Safety stock (units) | Triggers new order |
| **Safety Stock** | $SS = Z \times \sigma_{dL}$ | $Z$ = Service-level z-score, $\sigma_{dL}$ = Std. dev. of demand during lead time | $Z=1.65$ for 95% service |
| **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** | $\text{TIC} = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$ | First term = ordering cost; second term = holding cost | Minimum at EOQ |
| **Pipeline / Transit Inventory** | $\text{Transit Inv.} = d \times L_{t}$ | $L_{t}$ = Transit lead time (days) | Inventory in transit |
| **Supplier On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $\text{OTD \%} = \dfrac{\text{Orders Delivered On Time}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$ | — | KPI for supplier evaluation |
| **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time** | $C2C = DIO + DSO - DPO$ | $DIO$ = Days Inventory Outstanding, $DSO$ = Days Sales Outstanding, $DPO$ = Days Payable Outstanding | Days; lower is better |
| **Total Landed Cost** | $\text{TLC} = P + T + I + C + D$ | $P$=Purchase price, $T$=Transport, $I$=Insurance, $C$=Customs, $D$=Duties | Used in sourcing decisions |
| **Bullwhip Effect (variance amplification)** | $\dfrac{\text{Var(Demand to Supplier)}}{\text{Var(Customer Demand)}} > 1$ | — | Variance amplifies upstream |
| **Service Level (Type-1)** | $\text{SL} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Stockout Units}}{\text{Demand Units}}$ | — | Probability of no stockout |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In all the formulas above, notice the use of `\dfrac` and `\times` instead of the vertical pipe `|`. This ensures the markdown table renders correctly without breaking the row syntax.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in the Automotive Industry

- **EOQ** is used by Maruti Suzuki to decide how many brake discs to order from Bosch.
- **ROP** is critical for air-filter stock at dealer service centres.
- **OTD %** is a Tier-1 supplier rating KPI — Tier-1s scoring below 95% face de-sourcing.
- **Cash-to-Cycle Time** is monitored by CFOs to maintain working-capital efficiency.
- **Total Landed Cost** drives import-vs-local sourcing decisions under **PLI (Production Linked Incentive)** schemes.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

The **EOQ model** (also called the **Wilson-Harris Model**, 1913) is a foundational KTU topic. Below is the **complete step-by-step derivation**.

### Step 1: Define the Variables
Let,
- $D$ = Annual demand of an automotive part (units/year)
- $Q$ = Order quantity per order (units)
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $C$ = Unit purchase cost (₹/unit)

### Step 2: Express Total Cost Function
Total cost of inventory is the sum of **Ordering Cost**, **Holding Cost**, and **Purchasing Cost**.

$$
\text{Total Cost } (TC) = \underbrace{\dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding Cost}} + \underbrace{D \cdot C}_{\text{Purchasing Cost}}
$$

**Explanation:**
- $\dfrac{D}{Q}$ = Number of orders per year.
- $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ = Average inventory level (assumes inventory depletes linearly from $Q$ to $0$).

### Step 3: Simplify for Minimisation
Since $D \cdot C$ is constant (independent of $Q$), we minimise only the *variable* cost:

$$
TC(Q) = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{HQ}{2}
$$

### Step 4: Differentiate with Respect to $Q$
Apply the first derivative test for the minimum:

$$
\dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \dfrac{H}{2}
$$

### Step 5: Set Derivative to Zero

$$
-\dfrac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0
$$

### Step 6: Solve for $Q$

$$
\dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{DS}{Q^{2}}
$$

$$
Q^{2} = \dfrac{2DS}{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}}
$$

This is the **EOQ** — the order quantity that minimises the total variable inventory cost.

### Step 7: Verify with Second Derivative

$$
\dfrac{d^{2}(TC)}{dQ^{2}} = \dfrac{2DS}{Q^{3}} > 0
$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $Q^{*}$ is indeed a **minimum** (not a maximum). ✓

### Step 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **[Automotive Industry Case Study]**
> Maruti Suzuki's Palghar plant consumes **20,000 brake calipers per year**. Each order costs **₹5,000** to place (paperwork, transportation, inspection). Holding cost is **₹200 per caliper per year**. Calculate the **EOQ** and the **minimum total variable cost**.

**Given:**
- $D = 20{,}000$ units/year
- $S = \text{₹}5{,}000$/order
- $H = \text{₹}200$/unit/year

**Step 1: Apply EOQ Formula**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 20{,}000 \times 5{,}000}{200}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Numerator**

$$
2 \times 20{,}000 \times 5{,}000 = 200{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Divide by $H$**

$$
\dfrac{200{,}000{,}000}{200} = 1{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Take Square Root**

$$
\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000} = 1{,}000 \text{ units}}
$$

**Step 5: Number of Orders per Year**

$$
N = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{1{,}000} = 20 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 6: Minimum Total Variable Cost**

$$
TC_{min} = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} \cdot S + \dfrac{Q^{*}}{2} \cdot H
$$

$$
TC_{min} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{1{,}000} \times 5{,}000 + \dfrac{1{,}000}{2} \times 200
$$

$$
TC_{min} = 20 \times 5{,}000 + 500 \times 200
$$

$$
\boxed{TC_{min} = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000 + \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Trick:** At EOQ, the **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost**. In our answer, both equal ₹1,00,000 — confirming the optimum.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Supply Chain Elements

| Element | Primary Function | Typical KPI | Automotive Example |
|---------|------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| Tier-1 Supplier | Delivers major modules | OTD %, PPM defects | Bosch (ECU) |
| Tier-2 Supplier | Sub-components | Cost-down % / year | Endurance (brake hose) |
| Tier-3 Supplier | Raw materials | Price stability | Tata Steel (steel coils) |
| OEM / Manufacturer | Final assembly | Line efficiency, OEE | Tata Motors Pune plant |
| Inventory | Buffer for demand variability | Inventory turnover ratio | Spare parts warehouse |
| Transportation | Move materials/vehicles | Cost per ton-km | Car-carrier trailers |
| Dealer | Customer interface | Customer satisfaction (CSAT) | Maruti Arena outlet |
| After-Sales | Service & parts | First-time-fix (FTF) % | Service centre |
| Reverse Logistics | Recycle / recall | Recovery % | ELV scrappage centre |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Example: Reorder Point Calculation

> **Problem:** A Hyundai service centre uses 80 air filters per day. Lead time from the supplier is 6 days. The manager wants a safety stock of 100 units. Calculate the **Reorder Point (ROP)**.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{ROP} = d \times L + SS
$$

$$
\text{ROP} = 80 \times 6 + 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ROP} = 480 + 100 = 580 \text{ units}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When stock falls to **580 units**, a new order must be placed to avoid a stockout during the 6-day lead time.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Automotive Supply Chain — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete flow of materials, information, and finance** across the automotive supply chain. Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** standard, node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are raw uppercase text to ensure clean rendering.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["T3 Raw Material Supplier"]
    A2["T2 Sub Component Supplier"]
    A3["T1 Module Supplier"]
    B["Inbound Logistics and Warehousing"]
    C["OEM Assembly Plant"]
    D["Quality Inspection and Testing"]
    E["Finished Vehicle Storage Yard"]
    F["Outbound Logistics Distributor"]
    G["Dealer Showroom Network"]
    H["End Customer B2C and B2B"]
    I["After Sales Service Centre"]
    J["Reverse Logistics and Recycling"]
    K["Information System ERP SAP"]
    L["Finance Flow Bank and Payments"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H
    H --> I --> J
    J -.-> A1
    K -.-> A1
    K -.-> A2
    K -.-> A3
    K -.-> C
    K -.-> G
    L -.-> A1
    L -.-> A3
    L -.-> C
    L -.-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows (`-->`) represent **material/product flow**. Dotted arrows (`-.->`) represent **information & finance flows** flowing in both directions.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automotive SC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UpstreamChain["UPSTREAM SUPPLY SIDE"]
        u1["T3 Suppliers Raw Steel Plastic Rubber"]
        u2["T2 Suppliers Hoses Gaskets Cables"]
        u3["T1 Suppliers Bosch Continental MRF"]
    end

    subgraph InternalChain["INTERNAL OEM OPERATIONS"]
        i1["Inbound Logistics JIT Milk Run"]
        i2["Production Assembly Line"]
        i3["Inventory WIP FG Yard"]
        i4["QA and Testing"]
    end

    subgraph DownstreamChain["DOWNSTREAM DEMAND SIDE"]
        d1["Outbound Logistics"]
        d2["Dealer Network"]
        d3["Customer"]
        d4["After Sales Service"]
        d5["Reverse Logistics Recall ELV"]
    end

    subgraph SupportChain["SUPPORTING INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        s1["Information System ERP MES"]
        s2["Finance and Banking"]
        s3["Government PLI Policy"]
    end

    u1 --> u2 --> u3 --> i1 --> i2 --> i3 --> i4 --> d1 --> d2 --> d3 --> d4 --> d5
    d5 -.-> u1
    s1 -.-> i2
    s1 -.-> d2
    s2 -.-> u3
    s3 -.-> i2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When drawing supply chain diagrams, **always label flows** (Material, Information, Finance). Diagrams without flow labels often lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 4.3 Bullwhip Effect — Variance Amplification Flow

The Bullwhip Effect is a critical phenomenon where **small demand fluctuations at the customer end amplify as they travel upstream** to the supplier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1["Customer Demand Std Dev 10"]
    n2["Retailer Dealer Order Std Dev 20"]
    n3["Distributor Order Std Dev 35"]
    n4["OEM Order Std Dev 50"]
    n5["Tier1 Supplier Order Std Dev 75"]
    n6["Tier2 Supplier Order Std Dev 100"]

    n1 --> n2 --> n3 --> n4 --> n5 --> n6
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "**C-R-D-M-T1-T2**" — Customer, Retailer, Distributor, Manufacturer, Tier-1, Tier-2. Variance **grows** at each stage upstream.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1. Define the term 'Supply Chain' in the context of the automotive industry. List any four core elements.**

**Model Answer:**
A supply chain in the automotive industry is a *systematic network of organizations, activities, and resources* involved in moving a vehicle from raw material suppliers to the end customer. It includes sourcing, production, distribution, and after-sales service.

**Four core elements:**
1. **Suppliers** (Tier-1, Tier-2, Tier-3) — providing parts and materials.
2. **OEM/Manufacturer** — assembling the vehicle.
3. **Distribution / Dealers** — delivering the vehicle to customers.
4. **Customers** — end users of the vehicle.

*(Any other valid element like logistics, inventory, information systems may be accepted.)*

---

**Q2. Distinguish between Tier-1 and Tier-2 suppliers with one automotive example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Tier-1 Supplier | Tier-2 Supplier |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Position | Direct supplier to OEM | Supplies components to Tier-1 |
| Function | Delivers complete modules/assemblies | Delivers sub-components |
| Example | **Bosch** (supplies ECU to Maruti) | **Endurance Technologies** (supplies brake hoses to Bosch) |
| Relationship | Direct contract with OEM | Indirect (managed by Tier-1) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **major elements of an automotive supply chain** with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The annual demand for a car battery at a Tata Motors service centre is **4,000 units**. The ordering cost is **₹600 per order**, and the holding cost is **₹30 per unit per year**. Calculate the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**, **number of orders per year**, and the **minimum total variable cost**. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Major Elements (7 marks):**

The automotive supply chain consists of the following major elements:

**1. Suppliers (3 marks)**
- **Tier-3 Suppliers:** Provide raw materials (steel from SAIL, aluminium from Hindalco, rubber from Apollo Tyres raw division, plastic resin).
- **Tier-2 Suppliers:** Provide sub-assemblies (brake pads, gaskets, wiring harnesses, hoses).
- **Tier-1 Suppliers:** Provide major modules directly to OEM (Bosch for ECU, Continental for infotainment, Valeo for lighting, ZF for transmissions).

**2. OEM / Manufacturer (1 mark)**
- The Original Equipment Manufacturer assembles the vehicle. Examples: Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, Mahindra, Hyundai, Kia.
- Activities: Body welding, painting, final assembly, quality testing.

**3. Logistics and Transportation (1 mark)**
- **Inbound logistics** — moving parts from suppliers to plant (JIT milk runs).
- **Outbound logistics** — moving finished vehicles to dealers (car-carriers, RORO ships for export).

**4. Warehousing and Inventory (1 mark)**
- Storage of raw materials, WIP, finished vehicles, and spare parts.

**5. Distribution Network / Dealers (0.5 mark)**
- Showroom network that sells vehicles and provides after-sales service.

**6. Customers and After-Sales Service (0.5 mark)**
- End consumers, B2B clients, export markets.
- Service centres providing maintenance, warranty, and spare parts.

*(A neat block diagram showing all elements must be drawn for full marks.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 4{,}000$ units/year
- $S = \text{₹}600$ per order
- $H = \text{₹}30$ per unit/year

**Step 1: Apply EOQ Formula** *(2 marks)*

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 4{,}000 \times 600}{30}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute** *(2 marks)*

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4{,}800{,}000}{30}} = \sqrt{160{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q^{*} = 400 \text{ units}}
$$

**Step 3: Number of Orders** *(1 mark)*

$$
N = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{400} = 10 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 4: Minimum Total Variable Cost** *(2 marks)*

$$
TC_{min} = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} \cdot S + \dfrac{Q^{*}}{2} \cdot H
$$

$$
TC_{min} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{400} \times 600 + \dfrac{400}{2} \times 30
$$

$$
TC_{min} = 10 \times 600 + 200 \times 30
$$

$$
\boxed{TC_{min} = \text{₹}6{,}000 + \text{₹}6{,}000 = \text{₹}12{,}000 \text{ per year}}
$$

**Verification:** At EOQ, Ordering Cost = Holding Cost = ₹6,000 ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **role of information systems** and **finance flow** in managing an automotive supply chain. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A dealer sells an average of **15 car batteries per day**. The supplier lead time is **5 days**, and the safety stock policy is **50 units**. Calculate the **Reorder Point (ROP)**. If the actual stock is **120 units**, should the dealer place a new order? Justify. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Information & Finance Flow (7 marks):**

**Information Flow (4 marks):**
- Information flow enables *real-time coordination* across the supply chain.
- **Demand Forecasts** from dealers help OEMs plan production.
- **Order Status & Shipment Tracking** via ERP systems (SAP, Oracle) ensures JIT delivery.
- **Production Schedules** (daily/weekly) are shared with Tier-1 suppliers.
- **Inventory Visibility** via Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI) portals.
- Reduces the **Bullwhip Effect** by improving transparency.
- Modern automotive firms use **IoT, AI, and Blockchain** for end-to-end traceability.

**Finance Flow (3 marks):**
- **Payment Terms** — OEMs pay Tier-1 suppliers in 30/60/90 days.
- **Letter of Credit (LC)** used for international procurement.
- **Working Capital Management** — Cash-to-Cash cycle time is a key metric.
- **Cost Transparency** — open-book accounting with strategic suppliers.
- **GST, customs duty, and forex** flows in export-import operations.
- Government incentives like **PLI Scheme** flow from Finance Ministry to OEMs.

**Part (b) — ROP Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $d = 15$ units/day
- $L = 5$ days
- $SS = 50$ units
- Current stock = 120 units

**Step 1: Reorder Point Formula** *(3 marks)*

$$
\text{ROP} = d \times L + SS
$$

$$
\text{ROP} = 15 \times 5 + 50
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ROP} = 75 + 50 = 125 \text{ units}}
$$

**Step 2: Decision** *(2 marks)*

Current stock (120 units) is **less than** ROP (125 units).

> **Decision: YES, the dealer should place a new order immediately**, because stock has already dropped below the safety threshold and a stockout is imminent during the 5-day lead time.

**Step 3: Justification** *(2 marks)*
- The 120 units will last only $120 / 15 = 8$ days, but the lead time is 5 days.
- However, since current stock is **already below** ROP of 125, an order is overdue.
- The safety stock of 50 units is the *minimum buffer* required to absorb demand variability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — Always write "units", "₹/order", "₹/year".
> 2. **Confusing Ordering Cost and Holding Cost** — Remember: ordering cost = per *order*; holding cost = per *unit per year*.
> 3. **Skipping the verification step** in EOQ — At EOQ, OC = HC. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not labelling flows in diagrams** — A supply chain diagram without "Material/Information/Finance" labels loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Confusing Tier-1 with Tier-3 suppliers** — Tier-1 = direct to OEM; Tier-3 = raw material. This is a favourite 2-mark trap question.
> 6. **Not stating the assumption of constant demand** in EOQ derivations — Examiners award marks for stating *"demand is known and constant"*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Elements of the Supply Chain**

- ✅ **Supply Chain** = Supplier → OEM → Dealer → Customer (plus reverse flow).
- ✅ **Three Flows:** Material, Information, Finance.
- ✅ **Tier Hierarchy:** Tier-3 (raw) → Tier-2 (sub-components) → Tier-1 (modules to OEM).
- ✅ **Core Elements:** Suppliers, OEM, Inventory, Transportation, Distribution, Dealers, Customers, After-Sales, Reverse Logistics, Information Systems, Procurement, Production, Marketing.
- ✅ **EOQ Formula:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS / H}$ — minimum inventory cost condition: OC = HC.
- ✅ **ROP Formula:** $\text{ROP} = d \cdot L + SS$ — place order when stock reaches this level.
- ✅ **Safety Stock:** $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{dL}$ — buffer against demand variability.
- ✅ **OTD %:** Key supplier KPI in the auto industry.
- ✅ **Bullwhip Effect:** Variance amplifies as it moves upstream from customer to supplier.
- ✅ **Cash-to-Cash Cycle:** $C2C = DIO + DSO - DPO$ — measures working capital efficiency.
- ✅ **Reverse Logistics:** Recalls, warranty, ELV scrappage under India's Vehicle Scrappage Policy 2021.
- ✅ **5 Rights of SC:** Right Product, Quantity, Place, Time, Cost.
- ✅ **JIT & Lean** are dominant philosophies in automotive SC.
- ✅ Always **state assumptions** before solving EOQ (constant demand, known lead time, independent ordering cost).
- ✅ Always **label the three flows** when drawing supply chain diagrams.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Drivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Operations Management in the Auto Industry**, **Drivers** are defined as the **internal and external forces, factors, or catalysts** that influence, shape, and propel strategic and operational decisions across the automotive value chain. They determine *why* automotive firms transform, *how* operations are structured, and *what* priorities govern production, supply, and distribution systems.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for Module 3, drivers are classified into:

1. **Market Drivers** – Customer expectations, competition, globalization.
2. **Technology Drivers** – Industry 4.0, EVs, autonomous systems, digitalization.
3. **Regulatory Drivers** – Emission norms, safety standards, government policy.
4. **Sustainability & ESG Drivers** – Carbon neutrality, circular economy.
5. **Cost & Efficiency Drivers** – Lean, economies of scale, productivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Drivers):**
> The topic "Drivers" forms the foundation for understanding *why* the automotive industry is undergoing operational transformation. Without a clear grasp of these drivers, students cannot appreciate the rationale behind modern OM tools such as JIT, TQM, Six Sigma, or Agile Manufacturing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an **automobile as a moving vehicle** and the **industry as a road**:
- The **engine** provides the power.
- But what **steers** the vehicle? The **steering wheel**, controlled by the driver.
- In the same way, the **drivers** in auto-industry OM are the "steering forces" that decide the **direction, speed, and destination** of operational strategies.

**Real-World Analogy — The Smartphone Analogy:**
A decade ago, phones were used only for calls. Suddenly, customer demand (a **driver**) for cameras, apps, and internet pushed companies to innovate. Similarly, today, customer demand for **electric, connected, and autonomous (ECA)** vehicles is the biggest driver reshaping the global auto industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Definition (Exam-Ready):**
> "Drivers are the underlying forces — market, technology, regulation, sustainability, and cost — that compel automotive firms to re-engineer their operations and supply chains."
