# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **"Define the Process View of a Supply Chain. List the three macro-processes identified in the cycle view."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Process View** of a supply chain is a framework that analyzes the supply chain as a set of interrelated **processes** that transform inputs (raw materials, information) into outputs (finished products, customer satisfaction) delivered to the end customer. It emphasizes the **flows** of materials, information, and finances across the network.

The three macro-processes in the cycle view are:
1. **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** — Manages interactions with end customers.
2. **Internal Supply Chain Management (ISCM)** — Manages the internal production, warehousing, and distribution.
3. **Supplier Relationship Management (SRM)** — Manages interactions with upstream suppliers and vendors.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming all three, 1 mark for brief description.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **"Distinguish between the Push and Pull views of a supply chain. Where would you place the push-pull boundary in a Make-to-Stock (MTS) auto-component plant?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Push View | Pull View |
|---|---|---|
| Driver | Forecast / long-term plan | Actual customer order |
| Inventory | High (anticipation stock) | Low / minimal |
| Risk | Obsolescence | Stockouts |

In a **Make-to-Stock (MTS)** auto-component plant (e.g., producing standard brake pads), the push-pull boundary is placed **far upstream — at the finished goods inventory itself**. The entire production process is PUSH, and the **issue from finished goods stock to the OEM customer** is the only PULL step.

*(1 mark push definition, 1 mark pull definition, 1 mark for the MTS boundary placement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, you are required to answer **one full 14-mark question**. Internal choice is provided below.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the **eight sub-processes of the Cycle View** of a supply chain with a suitable diagram. Discuss how the cycle view differs from the push-pull view. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An automotive OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) produces **180 cars/day** with a working time of **9 hours/day** (including 1 hour of breaks). Calculate the **Cycle Time (CT)** and interpret its operational significance. If management wants to reduce the CT to **2.5 minutes/car**, what **strategic and tactical actions** would you recommend? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### (a) Eight Sub-Processes of the Cycle View

The **Cycle View**, as formalized by **Chopra & Meindl**, divides the supply chain into **eight cyclical sub-processes** grouped under three macro-processes:

1. **Customer Relationship Management (CRM):** Identifying customer segments, pricing products, generating leads, processing orders, and managing customer feedback.
2. **Order Fulfillment (Demand Planning):** Translating customer orders into production schedules and ensuring timely delivery commitments.
3. **Customer Service Management:** Handling post-sale activities such as warranty claims, spare-parts supply, returns, and repairs.
4. **Demand Management:** Forecasting demand, balancing supply-demand gaps, and updating plans collaboratively with supply chain partners.
5. **Supplier Relationship Management (SRM):** Selecting suppliers, negotiating long-term contracts, and monitoring supplier performance.
6. **Supplier Order Management:** Issuing purchase orders, tracking inbound shipments, and managing supplier lead times.
7. **Manufacturing Flow Management (Internal SCM):** Production scheduling, shop-floor execution, quality control, and capacity planning.
8. **Logistics and Warehousing:** Storage, inventory management, transportation, and distribution of finished products.

**Difference between Cycle View and Push-Pull View:**

| Cycle View | Push-Pull View |
|---|---|
| Focuses on **structural decomposition** of supply chain into processes. | Focuses on **temporal execution** — when processes are triggered. |
| Static framework — answers "**what** processes exist." | Dynamic framework — answers "**when** each process is executed." |
| Independent of demand variability. | Directly tied to demand (push = forecast, pull = order). |

*(7 marks: 4 for listing and explaining all 8 sub-processes with an example, 2 for diagram, 1 for the comparison table.)*

---

### (b) Cycle Time Calculation and Strategic Recommendations

**Step 1: Identify Variables**

- Daily Production, $D = 180$ cars
- Total Working Time, $T_{total} = 9$ hours/day
- Break Time, $T_{break} = 1$ hour
- Net Available Time, $T_{avail} = 9 - 1 = 8$ hours/day

**Step 2: Apply Cycle Time Formula**

$$CT = \frac{T_{avail}}{D}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and Convert**

$$CT = \frac{8 \times 3600 \text{ seconds}}{180 \text{ cars}} = \frac{28800}{180}$$

$$CT = 160 \text{ seconds/car} \approx 2.67 \text{ min/car}$$

**Step 4: Compare with Target**

Target $CT = 2.5 \text{ min/car} = 150 \text{ s/car}$. Current $CT = 160 \text{ s/car}$, so a **6.25% reduction** is required.

**Step 5: Strategic and Tactical Actions to Reduce CT**

| Level | Action |
|---|---|
| **Strategic** | Re-layout the assembly line (cellular manufacturing), invest in automation (Industry 4.0 robotics), and outsource non-core activities. |
| **Tactical** | Implement SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies) to reduce changeover time, deploy Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing), and apply 5S workplace organization. |
| **Operational** | Cross-train workers, implement Andon system for real-time problem signaling, and apply Takt-time synchronization. |

**Final Answer:** Current $CT = 160 \text{ s/car}$. To achieve target of 150 s/car, integrate Lean, Six Sigma, and digital automation.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula + calculation, 2 for interpretation, 3 for strategic actions.)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three macro-processes and their information flows** in the cycle view of an automotive supply chain. Use the example of a Maruti Suzuki OEM supplying to dealerships in Kerala. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Tier-1 auto component manufacturer has the following financial data:
> - Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO) = **50 days**
> - Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) = **70 days**
> - Days Payable Outstanding (DPO) = **35 days**
>
> Calculate the **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time**. If the company wants to reduce it to **60 days**, suggest **three practical strategies** with quantitative reasoning. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### (a) Three Macro-Processes in an Automotive Supply Chain

The three macro-processes in the cycle view are:

1. **Customer Relationship Management (CRM):** At Maruti Suzuki, this involves managing over **2,000 dealerships** across India, capturing bookings, processing finance/insurance, and conducting customer satisfaction surveys. In Kerala, the regional office in Kochi handles dealer relations.

2. **Internal Supply Chain Management (ISCM):** This includes the **Gurgaon and Manesar manufacturing plants** that transform inbound components (engines from Maruti Powertrain, electronics from Bosch) into finished cars. It covers production planning, quality control, and outbound logistics.

3. **Supplier Relationship Management (SRM):** Maruti Suzuki sources from over **1,500 Tier-1 suppliers** including Suzuki Motor Corporation (Japan) for engine technology, and Indian vendors for low-cost components. SRM handles vendor selection, audits, and long-term contracts.

**Information Flows:**
- CRM → ISCM: Customer orders, demand forecasts.
- ISCM → CRM: Vehicle availability, delivery schedules.
- ISCM → SRM: Component requirements, production schedules.
- SRM → ISCM: Delivery confirmations, quality data.

*(7 marks: 2 each for the three macro-processes with Kerala example, 1 for diagram, 2 for information flow description.)*

---

### (b) Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time Reduction

**Step 1: Apply the Cash-to-Cash Formula**

$$CC = DIO + DSO - DPO$$

$$CC = 50 + 70 - 35 = 85 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: Compare with Target**

Target $CC = 60$ days. Reduction required = $85 - 60 = 25$ days.

**Step 3: Three Practical Strategies with Quantitative Impact**

| Strategy | Lever Affected | Estimated Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Implement Just-in-Time (JIT) inventory** | Reduce DIO from 50 to 35 days | Saves **15 days** |
| **2. Tighten credit policy / offer early-payment discounts (e.g., 2/10 net 30)** | Reduce DSO from 70 to 60 days | Saves **10 days** |
| **3. Negotiate longer credit terms with suppliers (e.g., extend from 35 to 45 days)** | Increase DPO from 35 to 45 days | Saves **10 days** |

**Step 4: Verify Total Reduction**

$$\Delta CC = 15 + 10 - 10 = 15 \text{ days}$$

A combined approach easily achieves the 25-day reduction target while improving supplier relationships and customer satisfaction.

**Final Answer:** Current $CC = 85$ days. Target $CC = 60$ days. Implement JIT, strict credit policy, and supplier payment-term renegotiation.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula + calculation, 2 for strategies, 2 for quantitative impact, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students write the Cycle Time formula as $CT = D / T_{avail}$ (inverted). **Always** $CT = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D}$.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to **subtract break time** from total working time before computing cycle time. KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Listing fewer than **eight sub-processes** in the cycle view. Always enumerate all eight — partial enumeration loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **Lead Time** with **Cycle Time**. Lead time spans the **entire order-to-delivery** window; cycle time is **per-unit processing time**.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In numerical questions, omitting **units** (seconds, days, etc.) — KTU follows strict unit-marking policy.
> 6. **Common Mistake #6:** Writing push-pull view explanations without **diagrams**. The KTU 2024 scheme gives **1–2 marks specifically for diagrams** in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ✅ The **Process View** (Cycle View) decomposes the supply chain into **3 macro-processes** and **8 sub-processes**.
- ✅ The **three macro-processes** are: **CRM, ISCM, SRM**.
- ✅ The **eight sub-processes** are: CRM, Order Fulfillment, Customer Service, Demand Management, SRM, Supplier Order Management, Manufacturing Flow Management, Logistics & Warehousing.
- ✅ **Cycle Time** formula: $CT = T_{avail} / D$ — always in **time per unit**.
- ✅ **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time** formula: $CC = DIO + DSO - DPO$ — measured in **days**.
- ✅ **Bullwhip Effect** = demand variability amplification as orders move upstream — measured by $A = \text{Var(Order)} / \text{Var(Demand)}$.
- ✅ **Push-Pull Boundary** = the decoupling point between forecast-driven and demand-driven processes.
- ✅ **Push** = high inventory, low risk of stockout, forecast-driven.
- ✅ **Pull** = low inventory, customized, order-driven.
- ✅ **Make-to-Stock (MTS)** → Push-Pull boundary at finished goods.
- ✅ **Make-to-Order (MTO)** → Push-Pull boundary at raw materials.
- ✅ **Assemble-to-Order (ATO)** → Push-Pull boundary at sub-assembly stage (most common in auto industry).
- ✅ **Lead Time ≠ Cycle Time** — lead time is end-to-end; cycle time is per-process.
- ✅ Always **subtract break/changeover time** from total working time before cycle time calculations.
- ✅ KTU examiners value **diagrams** — always include a Mermaid/hand-drawn flow diagram in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Real-world auto industry examples (Tata, Maruti, Hyundai, Bosch) earn **higher marks** than generic examples.
- ✅ The process view is the **foundation** for Lean, JIT, TQM, and Six Sigma in automotive operations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flows in SCM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Flows in SCM # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Supply Chain Management (SCM)** within the automotive industry, **Flows** refer to the directional movement of resources, data, capital, and products across the interconnected network of suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, retailers, and end customers. According to KTU 2024 Scheme SCM frameworks, a supply chain is not merely a sequence of organizations but a dynamic system where **three primary flows** — Material, Information, and Financial — interconnect to deliver value from raw material extraction to the final consumer, and increasingly include **reverse flows** for sustainability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (CO3-Aligned):** *Supply Chain Flows are the structured, directional movements of physical goods, informational data, and financial capital that occur between upstream partners (suppliers) and downstream partners (customers) to synchronize demand, supply, and value creation across the automotive value chain.* ## 1.2 Types of Flows in SCM (Taxonomy) The KTU 2024 scheme recognizes the following canonical flow categories: | **Flow Type** | **Direction** | **Primary Carrier** | |---|---|---| | Material / Product Flow | Upstream ↔ Downstream | Physical goods, components, assemblies | | Information Flow | Bidirectional | Data, demand signals, forecasts, orders | | Financial / Cash Flow | Bidirectional | Payments, credit, invoices, returns | | Reverse Flow (Logistics) | Downstream → Upstream | Returns, recycling, warranty parts | | Service Flow | Bidirectional | After-sales support, warranty servicing | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The River System > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box: "The Supply Chain is a River"** > Imagine an automotive supply chain as a large river system: > - The **Material Flow** is the *water itself* — actual physical liquid (parts) flowing from the mountain source (Tier-3 raw material supplier) down to the ocean (end customer). > - The **Information Flow** is the *weather report* and *hydrological data* — messages going both up and down the river, telling dams when to release water and farmers when rain is coming. > - The **Financial Flow** is the *currency exchanged* — money flowing upstream to pay for dams, electricity, and water rights, while bills flow downstream. > - The **Reverse Flow** is the *evaporation and rainfall cycle* — used water returning as clouds, then rain, replenishing the source. > > Just as a river without proper flow management causes floods (overstocking) or droughts (stockouts), an automotive supply chain without synchronized flows experiences the **Bullwhip Effect** (demand variability amplification). ## 1.4 Why Flows Matter in the Auto Industry The automotive sector is uniquely sensitive to flow management because: - A modern car contains **~30,000+ parts** sourced globally. - **Just-In-Time (JIT)** production requires near-perfect flow synchronization. - **Lead times** for critical components can exceed **120 days**. - A disruption in any single flow cascade-fails the entire assembly line. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of Each Flow — The "Why" and "How" ### 2.1.1 Material Flow (Physical Flow) - **What it is:** The unidirectional movement of raw materials, sub-assemblies, finished vehicles, and spare parts from suppliers → OEM → dealers → customers. - **Why it matters:** It represents the *physical value* of the supply chain. In the auto industry, this includes **JIT deliveries**, **milk-run logistics**, and **cross-docking**. - **How it operates:** 1. Tier-3 suppliers (e.g., steel mills, polymer producers) ship raw inputs. 2. Tier-2 suppliers (e.g., Bosch, Continental) manufacture subsystems. 3. Tier-1 suppliers deliver modules directly to OEM assembly lines. 4. OEMs assemble vehicles and dispatch via FVL (Finished Vehicle Logistics). 5. Dealers and end customers receive the final product. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Upstream vs. Downstream (KTU Frequently Tested Distinction):** > - **Upstream Flow:** Supplier → OEM (movement toward the source). > - **Downstream Flow:** OEM → Customer (movement toward the market). ### 2.1.2 Information Flow - **What it is:** The bidirectional exchange of data — forecasts, orders, inventory levels, shipment tracking, and demand signals. - **Why it matters:** Information is the *nerve system* of the supply chain. Poor information flow causes the **Bullwhip Effect**. - **How it operates:** - **Downstream → Upstream:** Point-of-Sale (POS) data, dealer orders, demand forecasts. - **Upstream → Downstream:** Order confirmations, shipping notices (ASN), inventory availability, production schedules. ### 2.1.3 Financial Flow - **What it is:** The movement of money, credit terms, payment schedules, and invoices between supply chain partners. - **Why it matters:** Without synchronized financial flow, suppliers face cash crunches, leading to delivery delays. - **How it operates:** - Customer → Dealer → OEM → Tier-1 → Tier-2 → Tier-3 (payment terms typically **Net 30, Net 60, or Net 90**). - Reverse direction: Credit notes, refunds, warranty claim settlements. ### 2.1.4 Reverse Flow (Closed-Loop SCM) - **What it is:** The backward movement of products for returns, repairs, recycling, remanufacturing, and disposal. - **Why it matters:** Driven by **ELV (End-of-Life Vehicle) Directive**, sustainability mandates, and circular economy principles. - **How it operates:** 1. Customer returns defective part under warranty. 2. Dealer → OEM RMA (Return Material Authorization). 3. OEM → Tier-1 for repair/remanufacture. 4. Scrap materials → recycling partners. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Formula / Metric** | **Expression** | **Meaning** | **Units / Notes** | |---|---|---|---| | Bullwhip Effect Ratio | $BW = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{Orders}}}{\sigma_{\text{Demand}}}$ | Demand variability amplification | Ratio (ideal = **1**) | | Order Lead Time | $OLT = PT + WT + QT$ | Processing + Waiting + Queue Time | Days | | Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time | $CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO$ | Days Inventory + Days Sales Outstanding − Days Payable | Days | | Inventory Turnover | $IT = \dfrac{COGS}{\text{Avg. Inventory}}$ | Stock rotation frequency | Times/Year | | Perfect Order Index | $POI = \prod_{i} (1 - \text{Defect Rate}_i)$ | Composite fulfillment score | 0 to 1 | | Flow Time | $FT = PT + \text{WIP Delay}$ | Total time a unit spends in system | Hours / Days | | Little's Law | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | Inventory = Throughput × Flow Time | Units | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Units:** Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO), Days Sales Outstanding (DSO), and Days Payable Outstanding (DPO) must be computed using **consistent reporting periods** (typically 365 days or 90 days). ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In automotive plants, these flow metrics directly drive: - **Plant capacity planning** (using Little's Law to size buffers). - **Working capital optimization** (Cash-to-Cash Cycle minimization). - **Supplier scorecards** (Perfect Order Index). - **Bullwhip mitigation strategies** (CPFR — Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time (CCC) The Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time measures the duration between paying for raw materials and receiving payment from the end customer. This is a foundational financial flow metric. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO) — how long inventory sits before being sold. $$DIO = \frac{\text{Average Inventory}}{\text{COGS}} \times 365$$ **Step 2:** Define Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) — how long customers take to pay. $$DSO = \frac{\text{Accounts Receivable}}{\text{Total Credit Sales}} \times 365$$ **Step 3:** Define Days Payable Outstanding (DPO) — how long the firm delays paying its own suppliers. $$DPO = \frac{\text{Accounts Payable}}{\text{COGS}} \times 365$$ **Step 4:** Combine into the Cash-to-Cash Cycle formula. $$ \begin{aligned} CCC &= DIO + DSO - DPO \\[6pt] &= \left(\frac{\text{Avg. Inventory}}{\text{COGS}} \times 365\right) + \left(\frac{\text{AR}}{\text{Sales}} \times 365\right) - \left(\frac{\text{AP}}{\text{COGS}} \times 365\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** $DIO$ adds days (we pay early, sell later); $DSO$ adds days (customers pay us late); $DPO$ subtracts days (we pay suppliers late). The net is how long our cash is "tied up" in operations. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style **Problem:** An auto component manufacturer has the following data for FY 2024-25: - Average Inventory = ₹ 50,00,000 - COGS = ₹ 5,00,00,000 - Accounts Receivable = ₹ 80,00,000 - Total Credit Sales = ₹ 8,00,00,000 - Accounts Payable = ₹ 60,00,000 **Solution:** **Step A — Compute DIO:** $$ \begin{aligned} DIO &= \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 365 \\[4pt] &= 0.10 \times 365 \\[4pt] &= 36.5 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step B — Compute DSO:** $$ \begin{aligned} DSO &= \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 365 \\[4pt] &= 0.10 \times 365 \\[4pt] &= 36.5 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step C — Compute DPO:** $$ \begin{aligned} DPO &= \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 365 \\[4pt] &= 0.12 \times 365 \\[4pt] &= 43.8 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step D — Compute CCC:** $$ \begin{aligned} CCC &= 36.5 + 36.5 - 43.8 \\[4pt] &= 29.2 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The firm's cash is locked in operations for **29.2 days**. To improve, the firm should either **reduce inventory** (lower DIO) or **negotiate longer payment terms with suppliers** (increase DPO). ## 3.3 Derivation: Little's Law Applied to Flow Little's Law is the most powerful relationship in flow analysis. **Step 1:** Define the long-term average number of entities $L$ in the system. **Step 2:** Define the long-term average arrival rate $\lambda$ (throughput). **Step 3:** Define the average time $W$ an entity spends in the system (flow time). **Step 4:** State Little's Law: $$L = \lambda \cdot W$$ **Step 5 — Application in Auto Plant:** If an assembly line produces vehicles at $\lambda = 60$ vehicles/day and average flow time is $W = 4$ days, then the number of vehicles in the system is: $$ \begin{aligned} L &= 60 \times 4 \\[4pt] &= 240 \text{ vehicles (WIP + finished inventory buffer)} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** If management wants to halve WIP inventory, they must halve flow time OR halve throughput — a direct consequence of Little's Law. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Flow Failure Modes in Auto SCM | **Flow Type** | **Failure Mode** | **Symptom** | **Mitigation** | |---|---|---|---| | Material | Line stoppage | Missing Tier-1 module | Dual sourcing, safety stock | | Information | Bullwhip Effect | Forecast variance | CPFR, VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory) | | Financial | Supplier bankruptcy | Supply disruption | Credit risk scoring, early payment programs | | Reverse | ELV non-compliance | Regulatory penalty | Take-back schemes, remanufacturing | | Service | Warranty backlog | Customer churn | 3PL service network, predictive maintenance | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Three Primary Flows — Bidirectional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB T3["Tier 3 Supplier
Raw Materials"] T2["Tier 2 Supplier
Sub Assemblies"] T1["Tier 1 Supplier
Modules and Systems"] OEM["OEM Assembly Plant
Vehicle Manufacturing"] DEAL["Dealer Network
Sales and Service"] CUST["End Customer
Vehicle Owner"] BANK["Financial Institution
Banking and Credit"] RM["Recycling Partner
ELV and Remanufacturing"] T3 -- Material Flow Downstream --> T2 T2 -- Material Flow Downstream --> T1 T1 -- Material Flow Downstream --> OEM OEM -- Material Flow Downstream --> DEAL DEAL -- Material Flow Downstream --> CUST CUST -- Demand Data Upstream --> DEAL DEAL -- Order Data Upstream --> OEM OEM -- Forecast Upstream --> T1 T1 -- Schedule Upstream --> T2 T2 -- Capacity Data Upstream --> T3 CUST -- Payment Downstream --> DEAL DEAL -- Payment Downstream --> OEM OEM -- Payment Downstream --> T1 T1 -- Payment Downstream --> T2 T2 -- Payment Downstream --> T3 BANK -. Credit Lines and Loans .-> OEM OEM -. Invoicing and Settlements .-> BANK CUST -- Return and Warranty --> DEAL DEAL -- Reverse Logistics --> OEM OEM -- Reverse Logistics --> RM classDef sup fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef oem fill:#add8e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cust fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fin fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class T3,T2,T1 sup class OEM,DEAL oem class CUST,RM cust class BANK fin ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Information Flow & CPFR Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR POS["Point of Sale Data
Dealer Terminal"] FCST["Demand Forecast
OEM Planning Dept"] ORDER["Purchase Order
ERP System"] ASN["Advance Ship Notice
Supplier Portal"] INV["Inventory Status
WMS"] PROD["Production Schedule
MES System"] DEL["Delivery Confirmation
Logistics"] POS --> FCST FCST --> ORDER ORDER --> ASN ASN --> INV INV --> PROD PROD --> DEL DEL -. Feedback Loop .-> FCST classDef data fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class POS,FCST,ORDER,ASN,INV,PROD,DEL data ``` ## 4.3 Reverse Flow Architecture — Closed-Loop SCM ```mermaid flowchart TB USE["Vehicle in Use
Customer Phase"] RET["Return Trigger
Warranty or End of Life"] COLL["Collection Point
Dealer or Authorized Center"] SORT["Sorting and Triage
Refurbish or Recycle"] REFURB["Refurbish and Remanufacture
OEM Reman Plant"] RECYCLE["Material Recycling
Tier 3 Recovery"] RESTOCK["Restock to Spare Parts Channel
Aftermarket"] USE -- Trigger Event --> RET RET --> COLL COLL --> SORT SORT -- Reusable --> REFURB SORT -- End of Life --> RECYCLE REFURB --> RESTOCK RECYCLE -- Raw Material Reentry --> SORT classDef loop fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class USE,RET,COLL,SORT,REFURB,RECYCLE,RESTOCK loop ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The diagrams above use clean, raw uppercase alphanumeric labels inside node boxes (per KTU diagram safeguards) to render correctly across Mermaid compilers. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q1. Define the three primary flows in a Supply Chain Management system.** **Model Answer:** The three primary flows in SCM are: 1. **Material Flow:** The physical movement of goods — raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, and finished products — from upstream suppliers through the OEM to downstream customers. 2. **Information Flow:** The bidirectional exchange of data such as demand forecasts, orders, inventory levels, and shipment statuses across the supply chain partners. 3. **Financial Flow:** The movement of money, credit, invoices, and payment terms between trading partners to settle transactions. > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2. Distinguish between Upstream and Downstream flows with an automotive example.** **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Upstream Flow** | **Downstream Flow** | |---|---|---| | Direction | Supplier → OEM → Raw material source | OEM → Dealer → Customer | | Purpose | Procurement, raw material inflow | Distribution, finished goods delivery | | Auto Example | Bosch (Tier-1) delivering ECUs to Maruti Suzuki plant | Maruti Suzuki dispatching cars to Arena showrooms | | Information Carrier | Forecasts, capacity plans | Orders, POS data | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Q (a)** Explain in detail the different types of flows in a Supply Chain with neat diagrams. Highlight the role of each flow in the automotive industry. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The modern automotive supply chain operates through **five major types of flows** that interconnect multiple tiers of partners. **(i) Material Flow (Physical Flow):** This is the unidirectional downstream movement of physical goods. It begins with Tier-3 raw material suppliers (steel mills, aluminium smelters), proceeds to Tier-2 subsystem makers (gaskets, fasteners), then to Tier-1 module suppliers (Bosch for engine control units, Continental for braking systems), to the OEM assembly plant, and finally to dealers and customers. In the automotive industry, material flow is governed by **JIT (Just-In-Time)** principles to minimize inventory holding costs. **(ii) Information Flow:** A bidirectional flow critical for coordination. Downstream information includes dealer orders, POS data, and customer preferences (e.g., demand for SUVs vs. sedans). Upstream information includes production schedules, shipping notices, and forecasts. Tools like **EDI (Electronic Data Interchange)**, **CPFR**, and **VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory)** facilitate this flow. Poor information flow causes the **Bullwhip Effect**, where demand variability amplifies upstream. **(iii) Financial Flow:** Money flows upstream from customers to raw material suppliers. Typical payment terms are **Net 30 to Net 90 days**. Reverse direction carries credit notes, refunds, and warranty settlements. Financial flow efficiency is measured by **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time (CCC)**. **(iv) Reverse Flow (Closed-Loop SCM):** Returns of defective parts, end-of-life vehicles (ELV), and recycling materials flow back upstream. Driven by EU's **ELV Directive (2000/53/EC)** mandating 95% recyclability, this flow is increasingly important for sustainable automotive operations. **(v) Service Flow:** After-sales support including warranty repairs, maintenance, and spare parts provisioning. This flow sustains customer loyalty post-sale. *(Diagrams: Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)* --- **Q (b)** A car manufacturer has the following financial data. Calculate the **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time** and interpret the result. **(7 Marks)** | **Parameter** | **Value (₹ in Lakhs)** | |---|---| | Average Inventory | 80 | | Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | 600 | | Accounts Receivable | 120 | | Total Credit Sales | 900 | | Accounts Payable | 100 | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute DIO:** $$DIO = \frac{80}{600} \times 365 = 48.67 \text{ days}$$ **Step 2 — Compute DSO:** $$DSO = \frac{120}{900} \times 365 = 48.67 \text{ days}$$ **Step 3 — Compute DPO:** $$DPO = \frac{100}{600} \times 365 = 60.83 \text{ days}$$ **Step 4 — Compute CCC:** $$ \begin{aligned} CCC &= DIO + DSO - DPO \\[4pt] &= 48.67 + 48.67 - 60.83 \\[4pt] &= 36.51 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The manufacturer's cash remains tied up in operations for **36.51 days**. This is moderate. To improve, the firm can either reduce inventory (lean initiatives) or extend payable terms with suppliers. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice Option **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Q (a)** Discuss the **Bullwhip Effect** in the context of information flow in automotive SCM. Explain any four causes and three mitigation strategies. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **Bullwhip Effect** refers to the amplification of demand variability as it propagates upstream in the supply chain — small fluctuations in customer demand become massive swings in factory orders. **Four Major Causes:** 1. **Demand Forecasting Errors:** Each tier uses historical orders (not actual demand) for forecasting, compounding errors. 2. **Order Batching:** Suppliers receive sporadic large orders due to bulk shipping economics, distorting true demand. 3. **Price Fluctuations:** Promotional pricing (e.g., festival discounts on cars) triggers artificial demand spikes. 4. **Rationing and Shortage Gaming:** When supply is constrained, customers over-order to secure allocation. **Three Mitigation Strategies:** 1. **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment):** Sharing POS data across the chain to align forecasts. 2. **VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory):** Letting suppliers monitor and replenish stock directly, removing order batching. 3. **Lead Time Reduction:** Faster deliveries reduce the need for safety stock and dampen the bullwhip. --- **Q (b)** An auto component plant produces **120 units/day** with an average **flow time of 3 days**. Using Little's Law, determine the **Work-In-Process (WIP) inventory**. If the plant wants to reduce WIP to **200 units** while maintaining the same throughput, what should be the new flow time? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply Little's Law:** $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \lambda \cdot W \\[4pt] L &= 120 \times 3 \\[4pt] L &= 360 \text{ units (current WIP)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for new flow time with $L = 200$, $\lambda = 120$:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{new}} &= \frac{L}{\lambda} \\[4pt] W_{\text{new}} &= \frac{200}{120} \\[4pt] W_{\text{new}} &= 1.67 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The plant must compress its flow time from **3 days to 1.67 days** — a **44.3% reduction** — to halve-ish the WIP. This requires process re-engineering, smaller batch sizes, and elimination of non-value-adding delays. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Flows in SCM" Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Upstream with Downstream:** Examiners specifically test this. Remember — *Upstream = toward raw material source*; *Downstream = toward end customer*. Reversing this loses 2-3 marks instantly. > > 2. **Forgetting Reverse Flow:** Many students list only 3 flows (Material, Information, Financial) and miss **Reverse Flow** and **Service Flow** — losing 2 marks on a 7-mark sub-question. > > 3. **Skipping Units in Numerical Problems:** Writing "CCC = 36" without the unit "days" is flagged as incomplete; always specify units. > > 4. **Misapplying Little's Law:** Writing $W = \dfrac{L}{\lambda}$ as $W = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ (inverted) is a critical error. > > 5. **No Diagram in 7-Mark Theory Questions:** A neat labeled diagram of the three flows carries **1.5 marks** by itself — never skip it. > > 6. **Mixing Bullwhip with Lean:** Bullwhip is a *symptom of poor information flow*; Lean/JIT is a *philosophy to mitigate it*. Do not use them interchangeably. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Flows in SCM"** - ☐ SCM has **3 primary flows**: Material (downstream), Information (bidirectional), Financial (upstream). - ☐ **Two additional flows**: Reverse (closed-loop) and Service. - ☐ **Upstream = Supplier-side; Downstream = Customer-side.** - ☐ **Bullwhip Effect** = demand variability amplification upstream, caused by forecast errors, batching, price fluctuations, and shortage gaming. - ☐ **Cash-to-Cash Cycle** = $DIO + DSO - DPO$ — measures working capital locked in operations. - ☐ **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda \cdot W$ — fundamental to flow analysis; WIP = Throughput × Flow Time. - ☐ **JIT (Just-In-Time)** = material flow philosophy minimizing inventory. - ☐ **CPFR, VMI, EDI** = information flow enablers. - ☐ **ELV Directive** = drives reverse flow in automotive. - ☐ **Net 30 / Net 60 / Net 90** = standard financial flow payment terms. - ☐ Always **label diagrams** and **state units** in numerical answers. - ☐ For 14-mark questions, expect a **theory (7M) + numerical (7M)** split. - ☐ The **Perfect Order Index** and **Inventory Turnover** are common follow-up metrics tested.

topics.title: Factors to be Considered for Developing a Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors to be Considered for Developing a Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **PEAUT524 – Operations Management in Auto Industry** syllabus, a **Supply Chain (SC)** is defined as the integrated network of organizations, resources, activities, and information flows involved in the transformation of raw materials into finished automotive products, and their subsequent delivery to the end customer, with the explicit goal of maximizing **customer value** while minimizing **total landed cost (TLC)** and **bullwhip variance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"A supply chain in the automotive industry is a strategically aligned, multi-echelon network comprising Tier-1, Tier-2, and Tier-3 suppliers, Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs), assembly plants, distribution hubs, 3PL/4PL logistics partners, and reverse-logistics agencies, all synchronized through an Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) backbone to deliver the right part, in the right quantity, to the right place, at the right time, in the right condition, at the right cost (the 7 R's of Logistics)."*

The **factors to be considered for developing a supply chain** are, therefore, the **strategic, tactical, and operational decision variables** that an automotive operations manager must jointly optimize when architecting this multi-echelon network. They span across product, process, demand, supplier, location, logistics, IT, financial, regulatory, and risk dimensions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🛣️ The Highway Convoy Analogy**
> Imagine you are the **convoy commander** of a fleet of trucks delivering Maruti Suzuki parts from Gurugram to a dealer in Kochi. You cannot just line up the trucks and start driving. Before you move a single wheel, you must answer a hundred questions:
>
> - **What** is being delivered? (a Maruti Baleno bumper or a Tata Nexon battery? — they have different weight, fragility, and value)
> - **How many** are needed? (daily demand vs. seasonal spike)
> - **Where** are the suppliers? (Pan-India, ASEAN, or local?)
> - **Which route** — NH44 through Tamil Nadu or the Western Coast via Mangalore?
> - **How much** fuel, driver, and toll cost can we absorb?
> - **What if** there is a cyclone warning, a strike, or a port delay?
> - **Can we track** each truck in real time with GPS and IoT?
>
> Every single one of these questions maps to a **factor** that defines your supply chain. The supply chain is **not built first and optimized later**; it is **designed around these factors from Day Zero**.

## 1.3 KTU Categorization of Supply Chain Development Factors (10 Pillars)

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to identify and discuss the following **ten primary factors** that shape any automotive supply chain:

| # | Factor Category | KTU Keyword |
|---|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Customer / Demand Characteristics** | Order winning criteria |
| 2 | **Product Characteristics** | SKU complexity, lifecycle |
| 3 | **Supplier Network & Relationships** | Tier-n mapping |
| 4 | **Facility Location & Network Design** | Plant & DC location |
| 5 | **Transportation & Logistics Infrastructure** | Mode selection |
| 6 | **Inventory Strategy** | Push–Pull boundary |
| 7 | **Information Technology & Integration** | ERP, IoT, Blockchain |
| 8 | **Cost Structure & Financial Factors** | Total Landed Cost |
| 9 | **Regulatory & Environmental Compliance** | BS-VII, AIS, ESG |
| 10 | **Risk Management & Resilience** | Redundancy, dual sourcing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology Anchors (Frequently asked in KTU ESE):**
> - **Echelon** → Each level in a supply chain (Supplier → Plant → DC → Dealer → Customer)
> - **Lead Time (LT)** → Time between order placement and receipt
> - **OTIF** → On-Time In-Full delivery metric (target ≥ 95%)
> - **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** → Purchase price + inbound freight + duties + inventory carrying + obsolescence
> - **Tier-n Supplier** → n = 1 (direct to OEM), n = 2 (to Tier-1), etc.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 10 Factors — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### Factor 1: Customer / Demand Characteristics

The supply chain is a **demand-driven** system in modern automotive practice. Engineers must analyze:
- **Demand Volume (D)**: Annual volume in units, e.g., India's passenger vehicle industry produced **~4.3 million units in FY 2023-24**.
- **Demand Variability (CV)**: Coefficient of Variation = σ/μ. Auto components typically exhibit CV between 0.1 (stable) and 0.8 (highly seasonal).
- **Service Level (β)**: The probability of not stocking out. KTU benchmark: **95% for spares, 99.5% for safety-critical parts** (brakes, airbags).
- **Lot Size Behavior**: Automotive OEMs favor small, frequent shipments (JIT/Lean) while dealers prefer larger, less frequent deliveries (Economic Order Quantity).
- **Visibility of Demand**: B2C retail visibility (Tesla online booking) vs. B2B forecast-driven (Bosch supplying Maruti).

### Factor 2: Product Characteristics

- **Product Life Cycle (PLC)**: Short PLC (smartphone-class car infotainment) requires agile SC; long PLC (commercial trucks) allows efficient SC.
- **Customization Index**: Made-to-Stock (MTS) → Assemble-to-Order (ATO) → Make-to-Order (MTO) → Engineer-to-Order (ETO). Higher customization pushes the **decoupling point (customer order decoupling point – CODP)** downstream.
- **Value Density**: (Product Value) / (Product Volume). Low value density (mattress-class) → locate plant near market; high value density (ECU/ECU chip) → centralize.
- **Substitutability & Commonality**: Use common platforms (e.g., Maruti's Heartect platform shared across Swift, Baleno, Dzire) to reduce SKUs.
- **Quality & Reliability Standards**: ISO/TS 16949 (now IATF 16949:2016), Six Sigma targets at **3.4 DPMO**.

### Factor 3: Supplier Network & Relationships

- **Tier Mapping**: Tier-1 (direct OEM suppliers like Motherson, Bharat Forge), Tier-2 (sub-suppliers), Tier-3 (raw material).
- **Supplier Selection Criteria**: Cost, Quality, Delivery reliability (CQD), Technical capability, Financial health, ESG score.
- **Strategic Relationship Models**:
  - *Arm's Length* (transactional)
  - *Partnership* (long-term contracts)
  - *Strategic Alliance / Keiretsu* (Toyota model)
  - *Vertical Integration* (e.g., Tata Steel → Tata Motors)
- **Supplier Base Rationalization**: Reducing from 1,200 to 800 suppliers gives **5–15% cost reduction** (Bain & Co. automotive benchmark).
- **Localization ("Make in India")**: Higher local content reduces FX risk and lead time; India aims for **100% local EV component manufacturing by 2030**.

### Factor 4: Facility Location & Network Design

- **Plant Location Decisions**: Proximity to market, supplier base, port, labor pool, and tax incentives.
- **Network Optimization Models**:
  - Center-of-Gravity Method
  - Linear Programming (Transportation Problem)
  - Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP) for capacitated facility location
- **Greenfield vs. Brownfield**: New plant (₹4,000 Cr) vs. acquired plant (₹1,500 Cr).
- **Hub-and-Spoke vs. Decentralized**: Hub suits high-volume low-variety; Decentralized suits customized fast-moving goods.

### Factor 5: Transportation & Logistics Infrastructure

- **Mode Selection**: Road (flexible, ~60% of Indian auto freight), Rail (cost-efficient for bulk), Air (high-value, urgent), Sea (export/import), Inland waterways (coastal parts).
- **Cost vs. Speed Trade-off**: Air freight is **4–8× more expensive** than road but **5–10× faster**.
- **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)**: Optimization of delivery routes.
- **3PL/4PL Partners**: DHL, Blue Dart, TVS SCS — automotive companies outsource ~40% of logistics in India.
- **Reverse Logistics**: Spare parts returns, end-of-life vehicle (ELV) recycling per **AIS-010/2017**.

### Factor 6: Inventory Strategy

- **Push–Pull Boundary**: Position where forecast-driven (push) ends and order-driven (pull) begins.
- **Inventory Components**: Cycle stock + Safety stock + Pipeline stock + Anticipation stock.
- **JIT/Lean Principles**: Toyota Production System (TPS) — Zero inventory, Zero defects, Zero breakdowns.
- **Inventory Carrying Cost (ICC)**: Typically **18–25% of inventory value/year** in Indian auto industry.
- **Bullwhip Effect**: Demand variance amplification as we move upstream; SC must dampen it through VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory) and CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment).

### Factor 7: Information Technology & Integration

- **ERP Systems**: SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Cloud SCM.
- **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)**: Shop-floor data.
- **IoT & Telematics**: Real-time fleet tracking.
- **Blockchain**: Provenance and anti-counterfeit parts.
- **AI/ML Forecasting**: Reduces MAPE from 25% to 8–12%.
- **Digital Twin of Supply Chain**: Virtual replica for simulation.

### Factor 8: Cost Structure & Financial Factors

- **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** = Purchase Price + Inbound Freight + Duties + Inventory Carrying + Obsolescence.
- **Working Capital**: Auto component firms operate on **60–90 day receivables**.
- **Currency Risk**: INR/USD volatility affects import-heavy chains.
- **Tariff Structure**: Customs duty (currently 15–25% on CBU imports).

### Factor 9: Regulatory & Environmental Compliance

- **Emission Norms**: BS-VI (current), BS-VII (proposed 2027).
- **AIS Standards**: Automotive Industry Standards (AIS-001 to AIS-200+).
- **GST Input Tax Credit** on intra-state auto transactions.
- **ESG Reporting**: SEBI's BRSR (Business Responsibility & Sustainability Report).
- **End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) Rules 2025**: 85% recyclability target.

### Factor 10: Risk Management & Resilience

- **Disruption Sources**: Pandemic (COVID-19), Geopolitical (Russia-Ukraine), Climate (Fukushima-like), Cyber (NotPetya), Labor (strikes).
- **Resilience Strategies**: Dual sourcing, Safety stock buffer, Nearshoring, N+1 redundancy, Digital twin simulation.
- **SCOR (Supply Chain Operations Reference) Model**: Plan, Source, Make, Deliver, Return, Enable.
- **Risk Score = Probability × Impact** (1–5 Likert scale).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM READY FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for Part B (14 marks) questions**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Unit | Typical Auto Value |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|------|-------------------|
| 1 | EOQ (Economic Order Quantity) | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | D=Annual demand, S=Order cost, H=Holding cost/unit/yr | units | ~ 400–2000 units |
| 2 | Reorder Point (ROP) | $ROP = d \cdot LT + z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | d=daily demand, LT=Lead time, z=Service level z-score, σ=Std dev | units | — |
| 3 | Safety Stock (SS) | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{LT}$ | z=service level factor, σ_d=std dev of daily demand | units | 1–2 weeks of demand |
| 4 | Total Landed Cost | $TLC = P + F + T + (H \cdot \bar{I}) + O$ | P=Price, F=Freight, T=Tariff, H=Holding rate, Ī=avg inventory, O=Obsolescence | ₹/unit | — |
| 5 | Inventory Turnover | $IT = \dfrac{COGS}{Avg\ Inventory}$ | — | times/yr | 8–12 (auto) |
| 6 | OTIF | $OTIF = \dfrac{Orders\ On-Time\ \&\ In-Full}{Total\ Orders} \times 100$ | — | % | ≥ 95% |
| 7 | Capacity Utilization | $CU = \dfrac{Actual\ Output}{Maximum\ Capacity} \times 100$ | — | % | 75–85% |
| 8 | Pipeline / Transit Stock | $PS = d \cdot LT_{transit}$ | d=daily demand, LT_transit=transit lead time | units | — |
| 9 | Supplier Defect Rate (PPM) | $PPM = \dfrac{Defective\ Units}{Total\ Units} \times 10^{6}$ | — | PPM | < 50 |
| 10 | Bullwhip Effect Ratio | $BWR = \dfrac{Var(Q_{order})}{Var(Q_{demand})}$ | — | ratio | >1 (bad) |
| 11 | Center of Gravity (X) | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum x_{i} \cdot w_{i}}{\sum w_{i}}$ | x_i = location, w_i = weight/volume | km | — |
| 12 | Service Level (β) | $P(X \le SS) = 1 - \beta$ | — | probability | 0.95, 0.975, 0.99 |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Reminder:** In all rendered tables, the absolute value / magnitude is written as `\vert x \vert` — never as `|x|` — to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **automotive industry**, the above factors collectively decide:
- Whether Maruti's Manesar plant uses a **lean (JIT) line** or a **batch line**.
- Whether Tata Motors adopts a **hub-and-spoke DC network** for its Nexon EV spares across India.
- Whether Bosch India sources semiconductor chips from Taiwan directly (Air) or routes them via Singapore bonded warehouse (Sea + Road).
- Whether Ashok Leyland's defense vehicles use **redundant dual-sourcing** for safety-critical electronics.
- The **Total Landed Cost** of a single ₹1,200 bumper — which determines whether it is manufactured in-house (Pune) or imported (Thailand).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)

The **EOQ model** (Ford W. Harris, 1913; extended by Wilson, 1934) is the foundational inventory formula KTU expects students to derive in Part B.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the total annual inventory cost function (excluding the constant purchase cost $D \cdot P$).

$$
TC(Q) = \underbrace{\dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding Cost}}
$$

**Textual logic for Step 1:**
- We order $D/Q$ times per year, each costing $S$ → total ordering cost = $(D/Q) \cdot S$.
- Average inventory is $Q/2$ (assuming linear depletion) → total holding cost = $(Q/2) \cdot H$.

**Step 2:** Take the first derivative of TC with respect to Q and set to zero (FOC for minima).

$$
\dfrac{d\,TC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Solve algebraically for Q*.

$$
\dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{DS}{Q^{2}}
$$

$$
Q^{*2} = \dfrac{2DS}{H}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the *minimum* total cost by substituting Q* back.

$$
TC_{\min} = \dfrac{D}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} \cdot S + \dfrac{\sqrt{2DS/H}}{2} \cdot H
$$

$$
TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the number of optimal orders per year.

$$
N^{*} = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DH}{2S}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** The optimal order quantity occurs **precisely when ordering cost = holding cost**. This is a frequently asked 2-mark conceptual question.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

A Maruti spare parts warehouse has the following parameters:
- $D = 50{,}000$ brake pads/year
- $S = ₹200$ per order
- $H = ₹50$ per pad per year

**Step 1:** Calculate EOQ.

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 50{,}000 \cdot 200}{50}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} = 632.46\ \text{pads}
$$

**Step 2:** Round to 632 pads (or 633).

**Step 3:** Number of orders/year.

$$
N^{*} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{632.46} = 79.06 \approx 79\ \text{orders/year}
$$

**Step 4:** Time between orders (Cycle Time).

$$
T_{cycle} = \dfrac{365}{79} = 4.62\ \text{days}
$$

**Step 5:** Total minimum cost.

$$
TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 50{,}000 \cdot 200 \cdot 50} = \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000{,}000} = ₹31{,}623\ \text{per year}
$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Safety Stock (σ-method)

**Given:** A Tata Motors dealer wants **95% service level** (z = 1.65). Daily demand mean = 100 ECUs, σ = 20 ECUs, supplier LT = 7 days.

**Step 1:** Standardize the demand during lead time.

$$
Z = \dfrac{SS - \mu_{LT}}{\sigma_{LT}}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for Safety Stock.

$$
SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT}
$$

Where:
$$
\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{LT}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
\sigma_{LT} = 20 \cdot \sqrt{7} = 20 \cdot 2.6458 = 52.92
$$

**Step 4:** Compute SS.

$$
SS = 1.65 \cdot 52.92 = 87.31 \approx 88\ \text{ECUs}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute Reorder Point (ROP).

$$
ROP = (\text{Mean demand} \cdot LT) + SS = (100 \cdot 7) + 88 = 788\ \text{ECUs}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dealer must place a replenishment order when stock drops to **788 ECUs**, ensuring that during the 7-day lead time, stock-out probability stays at **5%**.
