# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (Module 3 – Internal Choice Style)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any THREE factors to be considered for developing a supply chain in the automotive industry.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks = 3 key points):**

1. **Customer/Demand Characteristics:** The supply chain must be designed to meet the expected service level, demand volume, and variability. For example, commercial vehicles have stable demand (lean SC) while passenger cars have seasonal variation (agile SC).
2. **Product Characteristics:** The product's life cycle, customization level, and value density dictate the choice between MTS, ATO, and MTO strategies. A common platform (e.g., Maruti Heartect) reduces SKU count and simplifies the SC.
3. **Supplier Network:** The depth (Tier-1 to Tier-n), the relationship model (arm's-length, partnership, keiretsu), and the geographic spread of suppliers determine cost, lead time, and resilience. Rationalization from 1,200 to 800 suppliers typically yields a **5–15% cost reduction**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write only *one-word* answers like "Cost" or "Quality". For 3 marks, the KTU valuation key demands **factor name + one-sentence explanation + auto industry example**. Skipping the example costs 1 mark.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Explain the role of the 'Customer Order Decoupling Point (CODP)' in supply chain design.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Customer Order Decoupling Point (CODP)** is the strategic position in the supply chain where the **push (forecast-driven) process ends and the pull (order-driven) process begins**. In the automotive industry:
- If CODP is placed **upstream** (e.g., at the Tier-1 supplier), the firm operates in a **Make-to-Order (MTO)** mode — high customization, high inventory cost, low obsolescence. Example: Bentley custom cars.
- If CODP is placed **downstream** (e.g., at the dealer), the firm operates in a **Make-to-Stock (MTS)** mode — low customization, low lead time to customer, high inventory. Example: Hyundai Santro standard trims.
- The CODP directly determines **lead time to customer**, **inventory carrying cost**, and **responsiveness**. KTU expects students to draw a simple push–pull boundary diagram for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**"With reference to the Indian automotive industry, discuss in detail the various factors to be considered for developing an effective supply chain. Illustrate your answer with suitable examples."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Strategic & Demand-Side Factors

**Model Answer:**

The development of an effective automotive supply chain is governed by ten inter-related factors. The first four are **strategic** in nature.

**(i) Customer/Demand Characteristics (2 marks):** Volume, variety, and variability of demand dictate whether the SC should be **lean or agile**. In India, the FY 2023-24 passenger vehicle production was approximately **4.3 million units**, with peak festival-season demand in October–November. The Coefficient of Variation (CV) of monthly demand typically lies between 0.15 and 0.4. Service level expectations differ by segment: **95% for spares, 99.5% for safety-critical parts** (brakes, airbags, seatbelts).

**(ii) Product Characteristics (1.5 marks):** A modern Indian car contains **~30,000 components**. Their life cycle (5–7 years for ICE, 3–5 years for EV software), value density (₹5 plastic clip vs. ₹50,000 ECU), and customization index (variant count: Swift has 12 variants, Thar has 20+) determine the manufacturing strategy. The **decoupling point** in such a context typically lies at the body shop (ATO mode) for OEMs.

**(iii) Supplier Network (1.5 marks):** A typical OEM like Tata Motors works with **~600 Tier-1 suppliers** and several thousand Tier-2/3 suppliers. Supplier rationalization, local content norms (under the Indian PLI scheme), and **keiretsu-style partnerships** (as practiced by Toyota Kirloskar) are key. Localization of EV components is targeted to reach **100% by 2030**.

**(iv) Facility Location (2 marks):** Indian auto clusters are at **Pune, Chennai (Sriperumbudur), Gurugram, Sanand, and Hosur**. Each cluster has port connectivity, supplier density, and state-level incentives (Tamil Nadu's EV policy, Gujarat's PLI benefits). Network optimization techniques — center-of-gravity and linear programming — are used to balance inbound and outbound freight.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Tactical, Operational, Compliance & Risk Factors

**(v) Transportation & Logistics (1.5 marks):** The modal split in Indian auto is approximately **60% road, 30% rail, 10% sea/air**. Road provides flexibility; rail is cost-efficient for bulk (e.g., Maruti's dedicated rakes); sea handles export/import through Mundra and Chennai ports. **3PL penetration is ~40%**. Vehicle routing and reverse logistics for ELVs (per **AIS-010/2017**) are critical.

**(vi) Inventory Strategy (1.5 marks):** Indian auto follows a **hybrid push–pull** approach. Cycle stock is governed by **EOQ** principles. Safety stock is set per **ROP = d·LT + z·σ·√LT**. Inventory Turnover targets are **8–12 times/year**. The bullwhip effect is mitigated through **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)** at Tier-1 suppliers and **CPFR** between OEM and Tier-1.

**(vii) Information Technology (1 mark):** SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Cloud SCM, IoT-based fleet tracking, and **digital twins** are standard. AI/ML forecasting has reduced MAPE from ~25% (manual) to 8–12%. **Blockchain** pilots are underway for spare-parts provenance.

**(viii) Cost Structure (1 mark):** **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** is the only true cost of procurement. It includes purchase price, inbound freight, customs duty, inventory carrying, and obsolescence. Working capital in the Indian auto component sector is typically 60–90 days.

**(ix) Regulatory & Environmental Compliance (1 mark):** Compliance with **BS-VI (BS-VII from 2027)**, **AIS standards**, **GST input tax credit**, and the upcoming **ELV recycling rules (85% recyclability by 2025)** is non-negotiable. SEBI's **BRSR** mandates ESG disclosure.

**(x) Risk & Resilience (1 mark):** Post-COVID, dual sourcing, 30-day safety stock buffers, and **SCOR model**-based risk mapping (Plan, Source, Make, Deliver, Return, Enable) have become standard. The **Risk Score = Probability × Impact** is computed on a 1–5 scale.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B):**
> 1. Do **not** write all 10 factors in 2-line bullets. The valuation key awards marks for **automotive-specific examples** (BS-VI, EOQ formula, PLI scheme, Indian cluster names). Generic answers score 8/14 max.
> 2. Always **underline or bold the factor name** before the explanation — examiners scan for keywords.
> 3. **Diagrams fetch 2 bonus marks**: a labeled push–pull CODP diagram in part (a) and a multi-echelon Tier-1 to Tier-3 diagram in part (b) can lift your score by 1–2 marks even if the text is average.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**"Explain the role of (a) Inventory strategy and (b) Risk management as critical factors in developing an automotive supply chain. Use Indian examples in your answer."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Inventory Strategy

**Model Answer:**

Inventory is the **single largest working-capital line item** in the automotive sector, and its strategy is therefore a cornerstone of supply chain design. The key components are:

1. **Inventory components** (2 marks): Cycle stock (governed by EOQ), safety stock (governed by service level z), pipeline stock (governed by transit lead time), and anticipation stock (for known demand spikes like Diwali). The optimal order quantity is given by $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$, where the minimum total cost is $\sqrt{2DSH}$.

2. **Push–Pull Boundary (CODP)** (2 marks): In an Indian passenger-car plant like **Maruti Suzuki's Manesar facility**, the body shop operates in a **forecast-driven push mode** (because steel panels are common across variants), while the final assembly operates in an **order-driven pull mode** (because the variant, color, and accessories are customer-specific). The boundary between these two is the CODP.

3. **JIT/Lean and VMI (2 marks):** Toyota Kirloskar's supplier park adjacent to the Bidadi plant is a textbook example of **just-in-time delivery** — Tier-1 suppliers deliver modules every few hours, eliminating warehouse buffer. **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)** at Bosch India ensures that the OEM's line-side stock is autonomously replenished by the supplier based on shared ERP data.

4. **Inventory Turnover** (1 mark): Best-in-class Indian auto component firms achieve **12 turns/year**, translating to **~30 days of inventory**. Lagging firms operate at 4–6 turns, locking up excessive working capital.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Risk Management

**Model Answer:**

Risk management has become the **most important supply chain factor post-2020**. The SCOR (Supply Chain Operations Reference) model provides the framework.

1. **Risk Identification (1.5 marks):** The major risk classes in Indian automotive SC are:
   - *Demand risk* — sudden drop in sales (e.g., the 2018 NBFC liquidity crisis).
   - *Supply risk* — single-source dependency (e.g., semiconductor shortage of 2021–22).
   - *Logistics risk* — port congestion, highway blockades, natural calamities.
   - *Cyber risk* — ransomware attacks (e.g., the A.P. Moller-Maersk NotPetya incident of 2017).
   - *Geopolitical risk* — Russia-Ukraine impact on palladium and neon gas supply for catalytic converters and semiconductors.

2. **Risk Assessment** (1.5 marks): Risk Score = **Probability × Impact** (1–5 Likert scale). High-risk items (Probability 4, Impact 5 → Score 20) trigger immediate mitigation.

3. **Mitigation Strategies** (2 marks):
   - **Dual sourcing** for safety-critical and high-tech parts (e.g., Maruti sources ECUs from both Bosch and Denso).
   - **N+1 redundancy** in supplier selection (always have one backup).
   - **Safety stock buffer** of 15–30 days for critical SKUs post-COVID.
   - **Nearshoring / regionalization** — Tata Motors' acquisition of Ford's Sanand plant in 2023 is a nearshoring example.
   - **Digital twin simulation** to test disruption scenarios before they occur.

4. **Resilience KPIs (1 mark):** Mean Time To Recover (MTTR), OTIF stability, and **SCOR Resilience Index** are tracked. The target MTTR for Tier-1 disruptions is **<72 hours**.

5. **Business Continuity Planning (BCP)** (1 mark): Indian OEMs now conduct **quarterly BCP drills**, an outcome of the COVID-19 lockdown experience that halted production lines for weeks in 2020.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B):**
> 1. **Do not write risk management as a generic "fire-fighting" essay.** Tie every concept to an Indian company and a numeric example.
> 2. **The Risk Score = P × I formula is a guaranteed 2-mark fetch.** Memorize it.
> 3. **Pictorial representation of the SCOR model (Plan-Source-Make-Deliver-Return-Enable)** can earn 1 visualization mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 EXAM-DAY RAPID REVISION — Read twice before entering the exam hall**

- [x] **Supply Chain** = Tier-1 → Tier-2 → Tier-3 → OEM → DC → Dealer → Customer (memorize the 6-echelon chain).
- [x] **10 Factors**: Demand, Product, Supplier, Location, Transport, Inventory, IT, Cost, Regulation, Risk.
- [x] **EOQ Formula**: $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — and remember the optimal condition: **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost**.
- [x] **ROP Formula**: $ROP = d \cdot LT + z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{LT}$.
- [x] **OTIF** is the single most-tracked KPI in the auto industry — target ≥ 95%.
- [x] **Push–Pull CODP** is the heart of supply chain design; placing it downstream reduces inventory but increases lead time.
- [x] **Bullwhip Effect** variance amplification: VMI and CPFR are the dampeners.
- [x] **Inventory Carrying Cost (ICC)** in Indian auto = **18–25% of inventory value/year**.
- [x] **TLC** = Price + Freight + Tariff + Holding + Obsolescence — the only true cost.
- [x] **Service level (β)** = 0.95 → z = 1.65; β = 0.975 → z = 1.96; β = 0.99 → z = 2.33.
- [x] **SCOR Model**: Plan, Source, Make, Deliver, Return, Enable — write in this order.
- [x] **Indian auto clusters**: Pune, Chennai, Gurugram, Sanand, Hosur — use at least 2 names in any 14-mark answer.
- [x] **BS-VI is current; BS-VII is expected 2027**; **AIS-010** governs ELV recycling.
- [x] **Risk Score = Probability × Impact** (1–5 scale); high risk ≥ 15 triggers mitigation.
- [x] **EOQ and ROP are safe 14-mark derivations**; at least one will appear in the ESE.
- [x] **Always draw at least ONE diagram** (push–pull CODP, multi-echelon SC, or SCOR model) in any 14-mark question on this topic — it earns 1–2 visualization marks.

> **Final Tip:** The KTU valuation key rewards **automotive-specific Indian examples** over generic textbook language. Mention *Maruti, Tata, Bosch, Motherson, Hyundai, Toyota Kirloskar, Ashok Leyland, Mahindra* wherever relevant. Names + numbers + formulas = full marks. ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Requirements for Successful SCM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Requirements for Successful SCM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supply Chain Management (SCM)** in the automotive industry is the **systematic, strategic coordination and integration** of all business activities and processes across suppliers, manufacturers, logistics providers, distributors, and end customers to **produce and deliver** vehicles, components, and services that **maximize customer value** and achieve a **sustainable competitive advantage**.

According to the **Council of Supply Chain Management Professionals (CSCMP)**, SCM encompasses the planning and management of all activities involved in **sourcing, procurement, conversion, and logistics management**. In an automotive context, it specifically addresses the flow of **raw materials, parts, sub-assemblies, and finished vehicles** from Tier-3/Tier-2/Tier-1 suppliers through OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers) to dealers and ultimately to the customer.

**Basic Requirements for Successful SCM** refer to the **fundamental pillars, enablers, and competencies** without which an automotive supply chain cannot achieve operational excellence, cost efficiency, quality delivery, and customer satisfaction. These form the **strategic foundation** upon which all SCM activities are built.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Circulatory System as an Automotive Supply Chain**

Think of an automobile company (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors) as a **living human body**:
- The **heart** = The OEM assembly plant (pumps out finished cars)
- The **arteries** = Inbound logistics bringing steel, rubber, electronics, glass
- The **veins** = Outbound logistics delivering cars to dealers
- The **blood** = Materials, components, and information
- The **lungs** = Suppliers (oxygenate the entire system with components)
- The **nervous system** = Information technology & ERP systems (senses, signals, and control)
- The **brain** = Top management & strategic planners

Now, what does a body need to **survive and thrive**? It needs:
1. **A strong heart** (efficient production)
2. **Clean arteries** (smooth logistics)
3. **Healthy lungs** (reliable suppliers)
4. **A working nervous system** (IT and communication)
5. **Proper circulation** (integration)
6. **Immunity** (risk management)
7. **Right diet and exercise** (continuous improvement and lean practices)

**If any of these fails, the body (company) collapses.** Similarly, automotive SCM requires these "vital organs" to function in harmony. A **Toyota Production System** doesn't work if suppliers don't deliver **Just-in-Time (JIT)**; and JIT doesn't work if **information systems** aren't real-time.

## 1.3 Core Definition Callouts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definitions (KTU 2024 Scheme High-Yield Terms)**

| **Term** | **Definition** | **Automotive Industry Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Supply Chain** | The network of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources involved in supplying a product to a consumer | From steel mill → Tier-3 → Tier-2 → Tier-1 → OEM → Dealer → Customer |
| **Tier-1 Supplier** | Direct supplier to the OEM | Bosch supplies ABS systems directly to Maruti |
| **Tier-2 Supplier** | Supplier to the Tier-1 | A casting unit supplies brake-caliper parts to Bosch |
| **Tier-3 Supplier** | Supplier to the Tier-2 | Pig iron smelter supplies to the casting unit |
| **JIT (Just-in-Time)** | Production strategy that aligns raw-material orders with production schedules | Toyota receives seats an hour before car assembly |
| **VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory)** | Supplier monitors and replenishes buyer's stock | A tyre company manages stock at OEM plant |
| **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** | Sum of all costs over the lifecycle of a product/service | Procurement + logistics + warranty + disposal |
| **SCOR Model** | Supply Chain Operations Reference model – process framework | Plan → Source → Make → Deliver → Return → Enable |
| **EOL (End-of-Life) Vehicles** | Vehicles that have reached the end of their useful life | Recycling of batteries, tyres, and metals |
| **Lead Time** | Time between initiating a process and its completion | 6 weeks from order to delivery of a clutch plate |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the Automotive Industry

The **automotive industry** is one of the **most complex supply chains in the world**. A single modern car contains **~30,000+ parts**, sourced from **thousands of suppliers across multiple continents**. Therefore, **basic requirements for successful SCM** are not optional — they are **survival essentials**. Failures like the **2021 global semiconductor shortage** (which forced automakers like Ford, Toyota, and Maruti to slash production by millions of vehicles) demonstrate what happens when these basic requirements are not met.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8 Basic Requirements for Successful SCM

Based on KTU 2024 Scheme module outcomes, the basic requirements for a successful automotive supply chain are:

### **Requirement 1: Strategic Alignment & Top Management Commitment**

- **Definition:** SCM objectives must be **aligned with the overall business strategy** of the automotive firm.
- **Why needed:** Without top-down alignment, departments (procurement, production, sales) work in silos.
- **How to achieve:**
  - Develop a **Supply Chain Strategy Document** with 3–5 year goals
  - Appoint a **Chief Supply Chain Officer (CSCO)** at board level
  - Align KPIs across procurement, production, logistics, and sales
  - Conduct **monthly S&OP (Sales and Operations Planning)** meetings
- **Real-world example:** Tesla's vertical integration strategy aligns SCM with its mission of sustainable transport.

### **Requirement 2: Supply Chain Integration & Coordination**

- **Definition:** Seamless **flow of materials, information, and finances** across all tiers and functions.
- **Types of Integration:**
  - **Internal Integration:** Between procurement, production, marketing, finance
  - **External Integration:** With suppliers, logistics partners, dealers
  - **Vertical Integration:** Owning suppliers/distributors (e.g., Tata's ownership of Jaguar Land Rover)
  - **Horizontal Integration:** Partnerships with peer firms
- **Tools:** EDI (Electronic Data Interchange), Vendor Portals, CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment)

### **Requirement 3: Information Technology & Digital Systems**

- **Definition:** Use of **digital platforms** to enable visibility, traceability, and real-time decision making.
- **Key Systems:**
  - **ERP** (Enterprise Resource Planning) – e.g., SAP, Oracle
  - **MES** (Manufacturing Execution System) – e.g., Siemens Opcenter
  - **WMS** (Warehouse Management System) – e.g., Manhattan Associates
  - **TMS** (Transportation Management System) – e.g., Blue Yonder
  - **SRM** (Supplier Relationship Management) – e.g., SAP Ariba
  - **IoT & RFID** for real-time tracking
  - **AI/ML for demand forecasting**
  - **Blockchain** for traceability in EV battery supply chains

### **Requirement 4: Strong Supplier Relationships & Partnerships**

- **Definition:** Building **long-term, collaborative, win-win relationships** with key suppliers rather than transactional arm's-length dealings.
- **Practices:**
  - **Supplier development programs** (training, audits, Kaizen)
  - **Long-term contracts** (3–5 years)
  - **Single sourcing vs. dual sourcing** strategies
  - **Supplier scorecards** (quality, delivery, cost, innovation)
  - **Co-location of supplier plants** near OEM (e.g., Maruti's Manesar supplier park)
  - **Risk-sharing and gain-sharing** agreements
- **Framework:** **Kraljic Matrix** to classify suppliers (strategic, leverage, bottleneck, routine)

### **Requirement 5: Demand Management & Forecasting Accuracy**

- **Definition:** The ability to **anticipate customer demand** accurately and align supply accordingly.
- **Why critical:** In automotive, demand is **volatile** (festive seasons, new launches, recalls, economic cycles).
- **Techniques:**
  - **Quantitative forecasting:** Time-series, ARIMA, exponential smoothing
  - **Qualitative forecasting:** Delphi method, market research
  - **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, Replenishment)**
  - **Build-to-Order (BTO)** vs. **Build-to-Stock (BTS)** models

### **Requirement 6: Efficient Logistics & Distribution Network**

- **Definition:** Optimized movement of **inbound materials** and **outbound finished vehicles** at minimum cost and maximum speed.
- **Components:**
  - **Inbound logistics:** Milk runs, cross-docking, inbound consolidation centres
  - **Outbound logistics:** Vehicle transport by road (car carriers), rail (auto wagons), and ships (RoRo vessels)
  - **Warehousing:** Centralized vs. decentralized
  - **3PL and 4PL providers**
- **Mode selection criteria:** Cost, speed, reliability, volume, distance, perishability

### **Requirement 7: Performance Measurement & Continuous Improvement**

- **Definition:** Establishing **KPIs and metrics** to monitor SCM performance and drive improvement.
- **Common KPIs:**
  - **Perfect Order Fulfillment Rate** (target > 95%)
  - **Order Cycle Time**
  - **Inventory Turnover Ratio**
  - **Supplier On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)**
  - **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time**
  - **Supply Chain Cost as % of Revenue**
- **Frameworks:** **SCOR Model**, **Balanced Scorecard**

### **Requirement 8: Risk Management & Sustainability**

- **Definition:** Identifying, assessing, mitigating, and monitoring **supply chain risks** while ensuring **environmental and social sustainability**.
- **Risk types:**
  - **Supply risks:** Single-source failure, quality issues
  - **Demand risks:** Forecast errors, market shifts
  - **Operational risks:** Equipment failure, labor strikes
  - **Environmental risks:** Natural disasters, pandemics
  - **Geopolitical risks:** Trade wars, sanctions
  - **Cybersecurity risks**
- **Sustainability aspects:** Carbon footprint, circular economy, ethical sourcing, ESG compliance

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarizes all critical formulas required for numerical/analytical questions on this topic in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| **Formula / Concept** | **Equation / Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Application in Automotive SCM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventory Turnover Ratio** | $$\text{ITR} = \frac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}}$$ | COGS in ₹/year; Inventory in ₹ | Measures how often inventory is sold/replaced per year |
| **Days of Inventory on Hand (DOH)** | $$\text{DOH} = \frac{365}{\text{ITR}}$$ | Days; dimensionless ratio | Days of stock cover; lower = leaner supply chain |
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ | D = Annual demand (units); S = Ordering cost per order (₹); H = Holding cost per unit per year (₹) | Optimal order size to minimize total inventory cost |
| **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $$\text{ROP} = (d \times L) + SS$$ | d = average daily demand; L = lead time (days); SS = safety stock (units) | Trigger point to place a new purchase order |
| **Safety Stock (SS)** | $$\text{SS} = Z \times \sigma_L \times \sqrt{L}$$ | Z = service-level z-score; σ_L = std. dev. of lead-time demand; L = lead time | Buffer stock to absorb demand/supply variability |
| **Total Logistics Cost (TLC)** | $$\text{TLC} = T_c + T_i + T_w + T_l$$ | T_c = transportation cost; T_i = inventory carrying cost; T_w = warehousing cost; T_l = loss/damage cost | Total cost of moving and storing goods |
| **Supplier OTIF (On-Time-In-Full)** | $$\text{OTIF \%} = \left(\frac{\text{Deliveries On-Time and In-Full}}{\text{Total Deliveries}}\right) \times 100$$ | Percentage; dimensionless | Measures supplier delivery performance |
| **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time** | $$\text{CCC} = DIO + DSO - DPO$$ | DIO = Days Inventory Outstanding; DSO = Days Sales Outstanding; DPO = Days Payable Outstanding | Time between paying suppliers and receiving customer payment |
| **Perfect Order Fulfillment Rate** | $$\text{POFR} = \prod_{i} (1 - f_i)$$ | f_i = failure rate of each i-th criterion (on-time, complete, undamaged, accurate docs) | Joint probability of all fulfillment criteria being met |
| **Freight Cost per Unit** | $$\text{FCPU} = \frac{\text{Total Freight Cost}}{\text{Number of Units Shipped}}$$ | ₹/unit | Logistics cost benchmarking |
| **Supplier Defect Rate (DPMO)** | $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Opportunities per Unit} \times \text{Units}}$$ | Defects per Million Opportunities | Six Sigma quality measurement in supplier quality management |
| **Order Cycle Time** | $$\text{OCT} = T_o + T_p + T_s + T_t$$ | T_o = order processing; T_p = production; T_s = shipping prep; T_t = transit | End-to-end time from order placement to delivery |
| **Warehouse Utilization** | $$\text{WU \%} = \frac{\text{Used Storage Volume}}{\text{Total Storage Volume}} \times 100$$ | Percentage | Space efficiency in warehouses |
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $$\text{TCO} = C_p + C_o + C_u + C_m + C_d$$ | C_p = purchase; C_o = operating; C_u = usage; C_m = maintenance; C_d = disposal | True cost of a purchased item over its lifecycle |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These requirements and formulas are used by automotive OEMs and Tier-1 suppliers in:
- **Plant location decisions** (minimizing inbound + outbound logistics cost)
- **Inventory policy design** (JIT vs. JIC vs. EOQ)
- **Supplier evaluation and selection** (weighted scorecards)
- **Network optimization** (number of warehouses, dealers)
- **Sustainability reporting** (Scope 1, 2, 3 emissions)
- **Digital twin simulation** of the supply chain

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** KTU values **applied answers**. If a question asks about SCM requirements, always **tie your answer to an automotive example** (e.g., Maruti's Manesar plant, Tata's Pune plant, Toyota's Bidadi plant, Hyundai's Sriperumbudur plant). This shows contextual understanding and fetches higher marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — Wilson's Formula

**Problem Setup:** An automotive component manufacturer (e.g., a spark plug maker) has:
- Annual demand: $D$ units
- Ordering cost per order: $S$ (₹/order)
- Holding cost per unit per year: $H$ (₹/unit/year)
- Order quantity: $Q$ (units per order)

**Total Annual Cost (TAC)** has two components: ordering cost and holding cost.

**Step 1:** Number of orders placed per year
$$\text{Number of orders} = \frac{D}{Q}$$

**Step 2:** Annual ordering cost
$$C_{\text{order}} = \left(\frac{D}{Q}\right) \times S$$

**Step 3:** Average inventory level (assuming instantaneous replenishment, inventory drops from $Q$ to 0 linearly)
$$I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{Q}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Annual holding cost
$$C_{\text{hold}} = \left(\frac{Q}{2}\right) \times H$$

**Step 5:** Total Annual Cost
$$\text{TAC}(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}$$

**Step 6:** To find the optimal $Q$, differentiate TAC with respect to $Q$ and set to zero.
$$\frac{d(\text{TAC})}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $Q$
$$\frac{DS}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$

**Step 8 (Validation):** Second derivative test for minimum
$$\frac{d^2(\text{TAC})}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{(positive, confirms minimum)}$$

**Real-world numerical example:**
A Tier-1 supplier makes brake pads. Annual demand $D$ = 50,000 units; Ordering cost $S$ = ₹2,000/order; Holding cost $H$ = ₹50/unit/year.

$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50000 \times 2000}{50}} = \sqrt{4{,}000{,}000} = 2000 \text{ units}$$

Optimal order quantity = **2,000 units per order**; 25 orders per year.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Safety Stock for Variable Demand and Lead Time

**Problem Setup:** Daily demand is normally distributed with mean $\bar{d}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_d$. Lead time $L$ is constant.

**Step 1:** Lead-time demand $D_L$ has:
- Mean: $\bar{d} \times L$
- Standard deviation: $\sigma_d \times \sqrt{L}$ (because variance adds over independent days)

**Step 2:** For a desired service level, find the z-score $Z$ from the standard normal table.
Common service levels: 90% → Z = 1.28; 95% → Z = 1.65; 99% → Z = 2.33; 99.9% → Z = 3.09

**Step 3:** Safety stock is the buffer above expected lead-time demand
$$\boxed{\text{SS} = Z \times \sigma_d \times \sqrt{L}}$$

**Step 4:** Reorder Point
$$\boxed{\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \times L + Z \times \sigma_d \times \sqrt{L}}$$

**Numerical Example:**
A car factory uses 500 tyres/day (mean), with $\sigma_d$ = 50 tyres/day. Lead time = 4 days. Service level = 95% (Z = 1.65).

$$\text{SS} = 1.65 \times 50 \times \sqrt{4} = 1.65 \times 50 \times 2 = 165 \text{ tyres}$$

$$\text{ROP} = 500 \times 4 + 165 = 2165 \text{ tyres}$$

The factory should place a fresh order when stock falls to **2,165 tyres**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for an Automotive Component

**Step 1:** Define each cost component
- $C_p$ = Purchase price (₹/unit)
- $C_o$ = Ordering cost (₹/order) × (D/Q) orders/year
- $C_i$ = Inventory carrying cost = (Q/2) × H
- $C_q$ = Quality cost = (Defect rate) × (Rework cost) + Warranty cost
- $C_l$ = Logistics cost (inbound) = (Freight per unit) × D
- $C_u$ = Usage/operating cost (e.g., energy)
- $C_m$ = Maintenance cost over lifetime
- $C_d$ = Disposal/end-of-life cost

**Step 2:** Sum the annualized costs
$$\boxed{\text{TCO} = C_p + C_o + C_i + C_q + C_l + C_u + C_m + C_d}$$

**Step 3:** Numerical example
A gearbox sourced from a Tier-1 supplier at ₹15,000/unit. Annual volume = 10,000 units.

| Cost Component | Value (₹/unit/year) |
|---|---|
| Purchase ($C_p$) | 15,000 |
| Ordering ($C_o$) | 80 |
| Inventory ($C_i$) | 750 |
| Quality/rework ($C_q$) | 200 |
| Logistics ($C_l$) | 120 |
| Warranty reserve ($C_u$) | 350 |
| Disposal ($C_d$) | 50 |
| **Total TCO per unit** | **16,550** |

**Insight:** The "purchase price" is only ~91% of TCO. The other 9% is "hidden" supply chain costs — this is why **procurement decisions** should never be based on quoted price alone.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Requirements to SCOR Model

| **Basic Requirement** | **PLAN** | **SOURCE** | **MAKE** | **DELIVER** | **RETURN** | **ENABLE** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic Alignment | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Integration | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| IT Systems | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Supplier Relationships | | ✔ | | | ✔ | ✔ |
| Demand Management | ✔ | | ✔ | ✔ | | |
| Logistics & Distribution | | | | ✔ | ✔ | |
| Performance Measurement | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Risk Management | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |

## 3.5 Decision-Making Matrix: Selecting a Supplier (Weighted Scorecard)

A Tier-2 brake-disc supplier evaluation (weight × score, 1–5 scale):

| **Criterion** | **Weight** | **Supplier A Score** | **Supplier B Score** | **Supplier A Weighted** | **Supplier B Weighted** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality (PPM defects) | 0.30 | 4 | 5 | 1.20 | 1.50 |
| On-Time Delivery | 0.25 | 5 | 3 | 1.25 | 0.75 |
| Price competitiveness | 0.20 | 3 | 4 | 0.60 | 0.80 |
| Technical capability | 0.15 | 4 | 4 | 0.60 | 0.60 |
| Financial stability | 0.10 | 5 | 3 | 0.50 | 0.30 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | | | **4.15** | **3.95** |

**Decision:** Choose **Supplier A** despite higher price, due to reliability and quality.

## 3.6 Sensitivity Analysis for EOQ

| **Parameter Change** | **EOQ Effect** | **TAC Effect** |
|---|---|---|
| Demand $D$ ↑ 50% | EOQ ↑ 22% | TAC ↑ 22% |
| Ordering cost $S$ ↑ 100% | EOQ ↑ 41% | TAC ↑ 29% |
| Holding cost $H$ ↑ 100% | EOQ ↓ 29% | TAC ↓ 15% |

**Insight:** EOQ scales with the **square root** of parameter changes, making it robust to estimation errors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The 8 Pillars of Successful Automotive SCM (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[PILLAR 1 Strategic Alignment]:::pillar
    P2[PILLAR 2 SC Integration]:::pillar
    P3[PILLAR 3 IT and Digital Systems]:::pillar
    P4[PILLAR 4 Supplier Relationships]:::pillar
    P5[PILLAR 5 Demand Management]:::pillar
    P6[PILLAR 6 Logistics and Distribution]:::pillar
    P7[PILLAR 7 Performance Measurement]:::pillar
    P8[PILLAR 8 Risk and Sustainability]:::pillar
    OUT[Successful Automotive Supply Chain]:::outcome

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> OUT

    P1 -.feedback.- P7
    P3 -.feedback.- P5
    P4 -.feedback.- P6

    classDef pillar fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef outcome fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Automotive Supply Chain Tier Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T3A[Raw Material Supplier\nSteel, Aluminium, Rubber, Plastic]:::tier3
    T3B[Sub-component Maker\nCasting, Forging, Moulding]:::tier3
    T2[Tier 2 Supplier\nSub-assemblies like Brakes, Gears]:::tier2
    T1[Tier 1 Supplier\nModules like Engine, Transmission]:::tier1
    OEM[OEM Assembly Plant\nMaruti Tata Toyota Hyundai]:::oem
    DC[Distribution Centre\nVehicle Storage Yard]:::dc
    DL[Dealer Network\nShowroom and Service]:::dealer
    CUST[End Customer\nVehicle Buyer]:::customer

    T3A --> T3B
    T3B --> T2
    T2 --> T1
    T1 --> OEM
    OEM --> DC
    DC --> DL
    DL --> CUST

    CUST -.order info.-> DL
    DL -.forecast.-> OEM
    OEM -.PO.-> T1
    T1 -.forecast.-> T2
    T2 -.PO.-> T3B
    T3B -.PO.-> T3A

    classDef tier3 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
    classDef tier2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000
    classDef tier1 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000
    classDef oem fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef dc fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    classDef dealer fill:#b4a7d6,stroke:#000
    classDef customer fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: SCOR Model Process Flow for Automotive SCM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PLAN[PLAN\nDemand and Supply Planning]:::process
    SOURCE[SOURCE\nProcurement and Supplier Mgmt]:::process
    MAKE[MAKE\nProduction and Assembly]:::process
    DELIVER[DELIVER\nWarehousing and Transportation]:::process
    RETURN[RETURN\nReverse Logistics and Warranty]:::process
    ENABLE[ENABLE\nIT HR Finance Compliance]:::support

    PLAN --> SOURCE
    SOURCE --> MAKE
    MAKE --> DELIVER
    DELIVER --> RETURN
    RETURN --> PLAN
    ENABLE -.supports.-> PLAN
    ENABLE -.supports.-> SOURCE
    ENABLE -.supports.-> MAKE
    ENABLE -.supports.-> DELIVER
    ENABLE -.supports.-> RETURN

    classDef process fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef support fill:#7f6000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain any five basic requirements for a successful supply chain management (SCM) system in the automotive industry.**

**Model Answer:**

The five basic requirements for a successful SCM system in the automotive industry are:

1. **Strategic Alignment:** SCM strategy must be aligned with the overall business strategy and supported by top management. For example, Maruti Suzuki's strategy of "producing what the market wants" requires tight alignment between sales forecasting and production planning.

2. **Supply Chain Integration:** Seamless flow of materials, information, and finance across internal departments (procurement, production, marketing) and external partners (suppliers, dealers). Integration reduces **bullwhip effect** and improves responsiveness.

3. **Information Technology:** Adoption of ERP, MES, WMS, TMS, and IoT systems to enable real-time visibility, traceability, and decision-making. Modern automotive plants use **digital twins** of their supply chain for simulation.

4. **Strong Supplier Relationships:** Collaborative, long-term partnerships with key suppliers including supplier development, co-location, and risk-sharing. Toyota's **keiretsu** system is a classic example.

5. **Demand Management:** Accurate forecasting using statistical models, market intelligence, and CPFR to align production with volatile automotive demand.

*(Examiner's note: Other valid requirements include logistics efficiency, performance measurement, and risk management — students can pick any five.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Explain the concept of 'Supplier Relationship Management (SRM)' in the context of automotive supply chains. Mention any two practices followed in the industry.**

**Model Answer:**

**Supplier Relationship Management (SRM)** is a systematic approach to **evaluating, managing, and strengthening** the relationship between an automotive OEM and its suppliers to **maximize value, minimize risk, and drive innovation** across the supply chain.

**Two key SRM practices in the automotive industry:**

1. **Supplier Co-location / Supplier Parks:** Major OEMs (e.g., Maruti at Manesar, Hyundai at Sriperumbudur) establish dedicated industrial parks adjacent to assembly plants where Tier-1 suppliers set up units. This enables **JIT/JIS (Just-in-Sequence) delivery**, reduces logistics cost, and fosters collaborative engineering.

2. **Supplier Development Programs:** OEMs invest in training suppliers in quality tools (5S, Kaizen, Six Sigma), provide technical mentorship, and share best practices. For instance, Tata Motors' **ASPIRE** initiative helps small and medium enterprises improve their manufacturing capabilities.

*(Other practices: long-term contracts, gain-sharing, supplier scorecards, single/dual sourcing strategy.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the eight basic requirements for a successful supply chain management (SCM) system in the automotive industry. (7 Marks)**

**(b) With a suitable example, explain the Kraljic Matrix used for supplier segmentation in the automotive industry. (7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Part (a)**

The eight basic requirements for a successful SCM system in the automotive industry are:

**1. Strategic Alignment:**
SCM must be aligned with the corporate strategy. Top management commitment is essential. Example: Tesla's vertical integration strategy aligns SCM with its mission of accelerating the world's transition to sustainable energy.

**2. Supply Chain Integration:**
Integration of material, information, and financial flows across all tiers. This includes internal integration (between procurement, production, sales) and external integration (with suppliers, dealers). Without integration, the **bullwhip effect** amplifies demand variability upstream.

**3. Information Technology:**
Digital systems such as **ERP, MES, WMS, TMS, IoT, AI, and blockchain** enable real-time tracking, predictive analytics, and end-to-end visibility. Modern OEMs use **digital twins** to simulate supply chain scenarios.

**4. Strong Supplier Relationships:**
Long-term, collaborative relationships with Tier-1, Tier-2, and Tier-3 suppliers based on trust, transparency, and mutual benefit. Practices include co-location, supplier development, gain-sharing, and VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory).

**5. Demand Management:**
Accurate demand forecasting using statistical and AI-based models. Practices include **S&OP (Sales and Operations Planning)**, **CPFR**, and **build-to-order** strategies. Reduces inventory and improves service level.

**6. Efficient Logistics and Distribution:**
Optimized inbound and outbound logistics. Inbound uses **milk runs** and **cross-docking**; outbound uses **road car-carriers, rail auto-wagons, and RoRo vessels**. Network design balances **cost vs. service level**.

**7. Performance Measurement:**
KPIs like **OTIF, Perfect Order Fulfillment Rate, Inventory Turnover, Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time** are tracked. Frameworks like the **SCOR Model** and **Balanced Scorecard** are widely used.

**8. Risk Management and Sustainability:**
Identification, assessment, mitigation, and monitoring of risks (supply, demand, operational, geopolitical, environmental). Sustainability includes **circular economy, ESG compliance, ethical sourcing, and carbon footprint reduction** (Scope 1, 2, 3 emissions).

---

### **Model Answer — Part (b)**

**Kraljic Matrix** is a **2×2 strategic sourcing tool** developed by **Peter Kraljic (1983)** that classifies purchased items/suppliers into four categories based on two dimensions:

**Dimension 1:** Supply risk (complexity of supply market, number of suppliers, switching costs)
**Dimension 2:** Profit impact (volume purchased, value added, impact on final product quality)

**The Four Quadrants:**

| **Quadrant** | **Supply Risk** | **Profit Impact** | **Strategy** | **Automotive Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic** | High | High | Long-term partnerships, joint development, deep integration | ECU (Engine Control Unit) chips, rare-earth magnets for EV motors |
| **Leverage** | Low | High | Aggressive negotiation, competitive bidding, multiple suppliers | Steel sheets, tyres, standard fasteners |
| **Bottleneck** | High | Low | Secure supply, dual sourcing, safety stock, redesign | Specialized paints, certain OEM-specific tooling |
| **Routine** | Low | Low | Automate procurement, e-auctions, minimize transaction cost | Office supplies, standard packaging, MRO items |

**Detailed Application in Automotive Industry:**

- **Strategic items (high risk + high impact):** Critical components like **powertrain ECUs, semiconductor chips, battery cells for EVs**. The **2021 chip shortage** demonstrated the consequence of not treating these as strategic. OEMs forged long-term agreements with chip makers (e.g., Ford with GlobalFoundries, GM with Wolfspeed) to secure supply.

- **Leverage items (low risk + high impact):** High-volume, commoditized items like **steel, aluminium, glass, tyres**. Multiple qualified suppliers exist, so the OEM can use **competitive bidding, reverse auctions, and volume discounts** to drive down price. Maruti, being India's largest carmaker, has enormous leverage in negotiating steel prices with SAIL, Tata Steel, and JSW.

- **Bottleneck items (high risk + low impact):** Items with few suppliers but low individual value, e.g., **specialty adhesives, custom-pigmented paints, niche sensor components**. Strategy: ensure supply continuity through **long-term contracts, dual sourcing, or in-house manufacturing**. Risk of line stoppage is high despite low spend.

- **Routine items (low risk + low impact):** Items like **MRO (maintenance, repair, operations) supplies, packaging material, stationery**. Strategy: **automate procurement, use e-catalogues, consolidate vendors, minimize cost per transaction**. Often handled by **P-cards (purchasing cards)** and **VMI**.

**Benefits of Kraljic Matrix in Automotive SCM:**
- Different **sourcing strategies** for different items
- Optimal allocation of **procurement resources**
- Better **risk management** by recognizing bottlenecks
- Stronger **supplier collaboration** for strategic items
- Lower **total cost of ownership**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the importance of logistics and distribution in automotive supply chains. Discuss the various modes of outbound vehicle transportation with their advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A Tier-1 supplier of instrument clusters has an annual demand (D) of 20,000 units. The ordering cost (S) per order is ₹1,500, and the holding cost (H) per unit per year is ₹30. Calculate the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), number of orders per year, total ordering cost, total holding cost, and total inventory cost. (7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Part (a)**

**Importance of Logistics and Distribution in Automotive SCM:**

Logistics and distribution is the **backbone** of automotive supply chain management because:
1. It **bridges** the production plant to the customer
2. It accounts for **5–10% of the vehicle's selling price** (significant cost)
3. It directly impacts **delivery time, vehicle condition, and customer satisfaction**
4. It involves complex **multi-modal transportation** and **warehousing** decisions
5. A single logistics disruption (e.g., port strike) can halt production lines

**Modes of Outbound Vehicle Transportation:**

**1. Road Transport (Car Carriers / Truck Trailers):**
- *Advantages:* Door-to-door delivery, flexible routes, fast for short distances (typically < 500 km), no need for transshipment
- *Limitations:* Limited capacity per trip (8–10 cars), high per-unit cost for long distances, subject to traffic and road conditions
- *Example:* Tata Motors dispatches vehicles from Pune plant to dealers in Maharashtra by road

**2. Rail Transport (Auto Rack Wagons):**
- *Advantages:* Economical for long distances (> 500 km), high capacity (up to 250–300 cars per rake), energy-efficient, lower carbon footprint
- *Limitations:* Requires last-mile road transport from railhead to dealer, fixed routes, slower than road
- *Example:* Maruti dispatches cars from Gurgaon to distant locations via dedicated auto-wagon rakes

**3. Sea Transport (Roll-on/Roll-off or RoRo Vessels):**
- *Advantages:* Most economical for international shipments, handles thousands of vehicles per voyage
- *Limitations:* Slow (weeks of transit), requires port infrastructure, weather-sensitive
- *Example:* Hyundai exports cars from Chennai port to Africa, Latin America, and Europe via RoRo ships

**4. Inland Waterways (in select geographies):**
- *Advantages:* Low cost, low emissions
- *Limitations:* Limited to certain geographies, slow, infrastructure-dependent
- *Example:* Used in Europe (Rhine, Danube) and parts of Asia

**5. Air Transport:**
- *Advantages:* Fastest, used for **spare parts and emergency shipments**
- *Limitations:* Extremely high cost, limited to small/light items
- *Example:* Maruti's express parts delivery to remote service centers

**Decision Factors:** Cost, distance, volume, delivery urgency, vehicle type, environmental impact, and infrastructure availability.

---

### **Model Answer — Part (b)**

**Given:**
- Annual demand, $D = 20{,}000$ units/year
- Ordering cost, $S = ₹1{,}500$ per order
- Holding cost, $H = ₹30$ per unit per year

**Step 1: Calculate EOQ**

$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 20000 \times 1500}{30}}$$

$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{60{,}000{,}000}{30}}$$

$$\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{2{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EOQ} = 1414.21 \approx 1414 \text{ units per order}}$$

**Step 2: Number of orders per year**

$$N = \frac{D}{\text{EOQ}} = \frac{20000}{1414.21} = 14.14 \approx 14 \text{ orders per year}$$

**Step 3: Total ordering cost per year**

$$C_{\text{order}} = \left(\frac{D}{\text{EOQ}}\right) \times S = 14.14 \times 1500 = ₹21{,}213$$

**Step 4: Total holding cost per year**

$$C_{\text{hold}} = \left(\frac{\text{EOQ}}{2}\right) \times H = \frac{1414.21}{2} \times 30 = 707.11 \times 30 = ₹21{,}213$$

**Step 5: Total inventory cost per year**

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{order}} + C_{\text{hold}} = 21213 + 21213 = ₹42{,}426$$

**Verification:** At EOQ, ordering cost = holding cost (a property of the optimal solution). ✔

**Interpretation:**
- The supplier should order **1,414 units** each time
- This will result in **14 orders per year** (approximately one every 26 days)
- Total annual inventory-related cost is **₹42,426**
- Time between orders (cycle time) = 365 / 14.14 ≈ 25.8 days

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in This Topic (and How to Avoid Them):**

1. **Generic answers without automotive context:** Writing textbook definitions of SCM without referencing the **automotive industry** specifically. **Always tie your answer to an automotive example** (e.g., Maruti, Tata, Toyota, Hyundai, Ford). This is a **2–3 mark differentiator**.

2. **Confusing Tier-1, Tier-2, Tier-3 suppliers:** Students often interchange these. Remember: **Tier-1 supplies directly to OEM; Tier-2 supplies to Tier-1; Tier-3 supplies to Tier-2**.

3. **Mixing up KPIs:** Writing "OTIF" without expansion, or confusing it with "On-Time Delivery Rate". OTIF = On-Time **AND** In-Full — both conditions must be met.

4. **Formula errors in EOQ:** Forgetting to take the square root, or using annual demand in days instead of years. **Always recheck units.**

5. **Ignoring the "why" and "how":** KTU 2024 Scheme uses **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**. Don't just list — explain **why** each requirement is needed and **how** to implement it.

6. **Missing the sustainability angle:** The 2024 syllabus emphasizes **ESG and sustainability**. A question on SCM requirements without mentioning **green logistics, circular economy, or Scope 3 emissions** will lose 1–2 marks.

7. **No diagrams in 14-mark questions:** KTU expects **at least one diagram** (block diagram, flowchart, or SCOR model) in 14-mark answers. Always include one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Basic Requirements for Successful SCM"**

### **Must-Remember Concepts:**
- ☐ Definition of SCM and its scope in the automotive industry
- ☐ 8 basic requirements: Strategic Alignment, Integration, IT, Supplier Relationships, Demand Management, Logistics, Performance Measurement, Risk & Sustainability
- ☐ Tier-1, Tier-2, Tier-3 supplier hierarchy
- ☐ SCOR Model: Plan → Source → Make → Deliver → Return → Enable
- ☐ Kraljic Matrix: Strategic, Leverage, Bottleneck, Routine
- ☐ Key KPIs: OTIF, Perfect Order Rate, ITR, DOH, CCC, OTIF

### **Must-Remember Formulas:**
- ☐ $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$
- ☐ $\text{ROP} = (d \times L) + \text{SS}$
- ☐ $\text{SS} = Z \times \sigma_d \times \sqrt{L}$
- ☐ $\text{ITR} = \text{COGS / Avg. Inventory}$
- ☐ $\text{CCC} = \text{DIO} + \text{DSO} - \text{DPO}$
- ☐ $\text{OTIF \%} = (\text{On-Time-In-Full Deliveries / Total Deliveries}) \times 100$
- ☐ $\text{TCO} = C_p + C_o + C_i + C_q + C_l + C_u + C_m + C_d$

### **Must-Remember Real-World Examples:**
- ☐ **JIT** at Toyota (pull-based production)
- ☐ **Supplier park** at Maruti Manesar
- ☐ **Vertical integration** at Tesla
- ☐ **2021 chip shortage** as SCM risk case study
- ☐ **RoRo vessels** for Hyundai exports
- ☐ **VMI** at Tata Motors for fasteners

### **Must-Remember Frameworks & Models:**
- ☐ **SCOR Model** (Supply Chain Operations Reference)
- ☐ **Kraljic Matrix** for supplier segmentation
- ☐ **CPFR** (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, Replenishment)
- ☐ **S&OP** (Sales and Operations Planning)
- ☐ **VMI** (Vendor Managed Inventory)
- ☐ **JIT / JIS** (Just-in-Time / Just-in-Sequence)
- ☐ **Balanced Scorecard** for SCM performance
- ☐ **Digital Twin** of supply chain

### **Must-Remember Trends (2024 Scheme Emphasis):**
- ☐ **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** transforming supply chains (battery sourcing, lithium, cobalt)
- ☐ **Industry 4.0** — IoT, AI/ML, blockchain in SCM
- ☐ **Circular economy** and **End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV)** recycling
- ☐ **Scope 3 emissions** reporting and **green logistics**
- ☐ **Reshoring / Nearshoring** post-COVID and post-chip-shortage
- ☐ **Geopolitical risks** (US-China trade war, Russia-Ukraine) and supply chain resilience

### **Examiner's Final Mantra:**
> *"In any SCM question, define → list requirements → explain with an automotive example → draw a diagram → mention a numerical/KPI if relevant → close with sustainability/risk angle."* This **6-step structure** will help you secure **full marks** in both Part A and Part B questions in KTU 2024 ESE.

topics.title: Supply Chain Structure in Automotive Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supply Chain Structure in Automotive Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Supply Chain Structure in Automotive Industries

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Formal):**
> The *Automotive Supply Chain* is a **complex, multi-tiered network of organizations, resources, activities, and technologies** that is directly involved in the upstream flow of raw materials (steel, aluminium, plastics, rare-earth magnets, semiconductors), intermediate components (engines, gearboxes, ECUs, seats, tyres), and downstream flow of finished vehicles, spare parts, and after-sales services from the **Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM)** to the **end-customer**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine a famous restaurant chain (say, a biryani outlet):

- The **Head Chef (OEM)** does not grow rice or rear chickens. He only plates the final dish.
- His **Sous-Chefs (Tier-1 Suppliers)** supply pre-marinated meat and pre-cooked masala paste.
- The **Spice Vendors (Tier-2 Suppliers)** supply blended ground spices.
- The **Farmers (Tier-3 Suppliers)** supply raw chillies, coriander, and turmeric.

This is exactly how an automotive firm works. A company like **Maruti Suzuki** does not mine iron ore — it assembles cars. The "restaurant" analogy makes one truth very clear: **the OEM is the integrator; the supply chain is its lifeline**.

### Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Standard Meaning in Auto Industry |
|---|---|
| OEM | Original Equipment Manufacturer (e.g., Tata Motors, Hyundai) |
| Tier-1 Supplier | Direct supplier to OEM (e.g., Bosch, Continental, Magna) |
| Tier-2 Supplier | Supplier to Tier-1 (e.g., a sensor maker) |
| Tier-3 / n-Tier | Raw material and sub-component suppliers |
| JIT | Just-In-Time delivery |
| JIS | Just-In-Sequence delivery |
| VMI | Vendor Managed Inventory |
| CKD / SKD | Completely Knocked Down / Semi Knocked Down kits |
| Lead Time | Time gap between order placement and receipt |
| Bullwhip Effect | Demand variability amplification upstream |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between a **Traditional (Push-Based) Supply Chain** vs a **Lean (Pull-Based) Supply Chain** in the Part B questions. Remember that the modern automotive industry has *migrated* from push to lean.

---

## 2. The Five Pillars of an Automotive Supply Chain

An automotive supply chain is built on **5 inter-locking flows**:

1. **Material Flow** — Steel coils → Press shop → Body shop → Paint shop → Assembly → Customer
2. **Information Flow** — Forecasts, orders, EDI messages, IoT telematics
3. **Financial Flow** — Payments, credit terms, milestone billing
4. **Service Flow** — Warranty claims, reverse logistics, recall management
5. **Reverse/Recovery Flow** — End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) recycling, remanufactured parts

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Industry Benchmark:**
> **Inventory Turns in automotive OEM = 8–12 turns/year** (a Toyota benchmark figure). This means a car sitting on the assembly line has inventory turned roughly every month. This is **bold** for KTU.

---

## 3. Conceptual Visualization (Plain Text Map)

```text
        TIER-3 (Miners, Plastic Resin, Steel Mills)
                |
        TIER-2 (Forgings, Castings, Semiconductors)
                |
        TIER-1 (Bosch, ZF, Magna — Sub-Assemblies)
                |
        OEM (Maruti, Tata, Hyundai — Final Assembly)
                |
        DEALERSHIP NETWORK (Sales + Service)
                |
        CUSTOMER  <---->  RECYCLING / ELV CENTRE
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tiered Structure of the Automotive Supply Chain

The industry follows a **pyramid-shaped, multi-tier architecture**. Each tier has distinct characteristics in terms of bargaining power, technical capability, and proximity to the OEM.

### Tier-3 (Base of the Pyramid)
- Mining of iron ore, bauxite; extraction of crude oil → plastics; silicon wafer fabrication.
- **High fragmentation**: thousands of small firms.
- **Low switching cost** for the OEM (commodity-grade input).

### Tier-2 (Mid-Upper Pyramid)
- Specialised component makers: forged crankshafts, alloy wheels, ECU chips, wiring harnesses.
- **Medium bargaining power**; some consolidation happening (M&A activity).

### Tier-1 (Strategic Partners)
- Global giants: **Robert Bosch GmbH, Continental AG, Denso, ZF Friedrichshafen, Magna International, Aisin Seiki**.
- Co-located R&D centres near OEM headquarters.
- Often involved in **modular sourcing**: delivering an entire axle assembly, not just parts.

### OEM (Apex)
- Final brand owner. Owns the marque, design DNA, and customer interface.
- Examples: Tata Motors, Mahindra, Maruti Suzuki (India); Toyota, VW, GM (global).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Trend:** Modern OEMs are shifting to **"modular outsourcing"** — they outsource not just parts but entire subsystems (e.g., the entire dashboard module including HVAC, infotainment, and airbag system) to Tier-1s. This is a KTU frequently asked concept.

---

## 2.2 Supply Chain Models Used in Auto Industry

### Model A: Traditional (Push) Supply Chain

Production is driven by **demand forecasts**. Components are manufactured and pushed to the next stage regardless of actual consumption.

$$\text{Stock}_{\text{Buffer}} = \text{Forecast Demand} + \text{Safety Stock}$$

- High inventory carrying cost.
- Long lead times.
- Common in pre-1980s US auto industry (GM, Ford pre-Lean era).

### Model B: Lean (Pull / Toyota Production System) Supply Chain

Production is triggered by **actual customer demand**. Famous principle: *"Produce only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the amount needed."*

- Anchored on **Just-In-Time (JIT)** and **Heijunka (level scheduling)**.
- Reduces inventory turns but **increases supply chain risk** (e.g., 2011 Tōhoku tsunami disrupted Tier-2/3 chip supply globally).

### Model C: Agile Supply Chain

Used for **high-variance, high-margin vehicles** (luxury, niche EVs). Uses **postponement strategy** — final differentiation done late in the supply chain.

### Model D: Leagile (Hybrid)

Combines lean upstream + agile downstream. Used by most modern OEMs.

---

## 2.3 Key Operational Levers in Auto SCM

| Lever | Definition | KTU Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| JIT | Delivery of parts at the exact moment needed | Toyota Production System |
| JIS | Parts delivered in the *exact sequence* matching production order | Common in paint & final assembly |
| VMI | Supplier monitors OEM inventory and refills autonomously | Reduces OEM ordering effort |
| Kanban | Visual signal card to trigger replenishment | Toyota innovation, 1953 |
| Cross-Docking | Incoming parts sorted and immediately loaded onto outbound trucks, no storage | Reduces warehousing cost |
| Milk Run | A single truck follows a circular route picking up from multiple Tier-1s | Reduces inbound logistics cost |
| Milk Run Frequency | Typically **2–6 deliveries per day** per OEM plant | Industry benchmark value |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units | Order sizing for bought-out parts |
| Reorder Point (ROP) | $ROP = D_{avg} \cdot L_{T} + SS$ | units | Inventory trigger for spares |
| Safety Stock | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L_{T}}$ | units | Buffer against demand variability |
| Inventory Turnover | $IT = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | turns/year | Measure of supply chain efficiency |
| Bullwhip Ratio | $BWR = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{orders upstream}}}{\sigma_{\text{demand downstream}}}$ | dimensionless | Measures demand amplification |
| Lead Time Variance | $\sigma_{LT}$ | days | Used in safety stock calculation |
| Total Logistics Cost | $TLC = T_{\text{transport}} + T_{\text{warehousing}} + T_{\text{stockout}} + T_{\text{inventory carry}}$ | ₹/vehicle | Strategic KPI for OEM |
| Supplier On-Time-In-Full | $\text{OTIF} = \dfrac{\text{Deliveries on time and complete}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \times 100$ | % | KTU-favourite KPI |
| Dock-to-Stock Time | Time from unloading to put-away | hours | Inbound logistics efficiency |
| Container Fill Ratio | $\text{Fill} = \dfrac{\text{Actual volume}}{\text{Container volume}} \times 100$ | % | Outbound logistics optimisation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note on Pipes:** In every formula above, I have intentionally written $\sigma_{LT}$ (and not $\vert\sigma_{LT}\vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in your KTU answer scripts.

---

## 2.5 The Bullwhip Effect (Detailed)

The **Bullwhip Effect** is the *amplification of demand variability* as one moves upstream in the supply chain.

$$\text{Amplification} \uparrow \text{ with } \uparrow \text{ Lead Time},\ \uparrow \text{ Order Batching},\ \uparrow \text{ Price Fluctuations},\ \downarrow \text{ Information Sharing}$$

**Real-world example (KTU exam classic):**
If a car dealer sees 100 cars sold in a week but expects a 10% rise next week, he orders 110. The distributor, fearing shortage, orders 130 from the OEM. The OEM, anticipating big demand, orders 180 from Tier-1. By the time the order reaches Tier-3, it might be 250 units — for what was originally a 100-unit demand!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remedies taught in KTU syllabus:** (i) Information sharing via EDI / VMI, (ii) Reducing lead time, (iii) Eliminating order batching, (iv) Stabilising pricing.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Industry Utility

- **Supplier Selection:** Tier-1 firms now use *digital twin SCM platforms* to simulate Tier-2/3 disruptions.
- **EV Transition:** Battery supply chains (lithium, cobalt, nickel) are re-engineering the traditional Tier-3 layer.
- **Software-Defined Vehicles:** Semiconductor content per car has risen from ~$300 to ~$1,500 — making chip-Tier-2 a strategic player.
- **Geopolitics:** The Russia-Ukraine war (palladium, neon gas for chips) and Taiwan Strait tensions have made SCM a *national-security* topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example #1 — EOQ for an Auto Ancillary

> **Problem:** An Indian Tier-1 auto component firm (say, a clutch manufacturer) supplies to a nearby OEM plant. Annual demand $D$ = **24,000 units**. Ordering cost per order $S$ = **₹3,000**. Holding cost per unit per year $H$ = **₹60**.
> **Find:** EOQ, number of orders per year, time between orders, total variable cost.

### Step 1 — Identify and substitute the values

We are given:
- $D = 24{,}000\ \text{units/yr}$
- $S = \text{₹}3{,}000\ \text{per order}$
- $H = \text{₹}60\ \text{per unit per year}$

### Step 2 — Apply the EOQ formula

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}$$

### Step 3 — Plug in the numbers

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 3{,}000}{60}}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the numerator

$$2 \times 24{,}000 \times 3{,}000 = 144{,}000{,}000$$

### Step 5 — Divide by H

$$\frac{144{,}000{,}000}{60} = 2{,}400{,}000$$

### Step 6 — Take the square root

$$EOQ = \sqrt{2{,}400{,}000} \approx 1549.19\ \text{units}$$

### Step 7 — Number of orders per year

$$N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{24{,}000}{1549.19} \approx 15.49 \approx 16\ \text{orders per year}$$

### Step 8 — Time between successive orders (Cycle Time)

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{365}{16} \approx 22.81\ \text{days per order}$$

### Step 9 — Total Annual Variable Cost

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = N \cdot S = 16 \times 3{,}000 = \text{₹}48{,}000$$

$$\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{EOQ}{2} \cdot H = \frac{1549.19}{2} \times 60 = \text{₹}46{,}476$$

$$\boxed{TC_{\text{variable}} = 48{,}000 + 46{,}476 = \text{₹}94{,}476}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic Verification:** At EOQ, ordering cost ≈ holding cost. Here ₹48,000 ≈ ₹46,476 — confirms optimality. Minor difference is due to rounding of $N$ to a whole number.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Reorder Point with Safety Stock

> **Problem:** A spare-parts warehouse supplies Maruti service centres. Average daily demand $D_{avg}$ = **40 units**. Lead time $L_T$ = **6 days**. Lead-time standard deviation $\sigma_{LT}$ = **1.5 days**. Desired service level corresponds to $Z$ = **1.65 (95%)**.
> **Find:** ROP and Safety Stock.

### Step 1 — Expected demand during lead time

$$D_{LT} = D_{avg} \cdot L_T = 40 \times 6 = 240\ \text{units}$$

### Step 2 — Safety Stock

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L_T}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$SS = 1.65 \times 1.5 \times \sqrt{6}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify

$$SS = 2.475 \times 2.449 \approx 6.06 \approx 7\ \text{units}$$

### Step 5 — Final ROP

$$ROP = D_{LT} + SS = 240 + 7 = \boxed{247\ \text{units}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #3 — Bullwhip Ratio Computation

> **Problem:** Downstream retailer demand std-dev $\sigma_{\text{retail}}$ = **50 units/week**. Wholesaler orders std-dev $\sigma_{\text{whole}}$ = **120 units/week**. Distributor orders std-dev $\sigma_{\text{dist}}$ = **210 units/week**. OEM production orders std-dev $\sigma_{\text{OEM}}$ = **330 units/week**.
> **Find:** The cumulative bullwhip ratio and interpret.

### Step 1 — Compute BWR at each tier

$$BWR_{\text{retail}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{whole}}}{\sigma_{\text{retail}}} = \frac{120}{50} = 2.4$$

$$BWR_{\text{whole}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{dist}}}{\sigma_{\text{whole}}} = \frac{210}{120} = 1.75$$

$$BWR_{\text{dist}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{OEM}}}{\sigma_{\text{dist}}} = \frac{330}{210} \approx 1.57$$

### Step 2 — Cumulative BWR

$$BWR_{\text{cumulative}} = \frac{330}{50} = 6.6$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Demand variability is amplified 6.6× from retailer to OEM. This is the classic bullwhip pattern.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SCM Model Selection by Vehicle Segment

| Vehicle Segment | Forecast Accuracy | Lead Time Required | Recommended SCM Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mass-Market Sedan / Hatch (e.g., Swift, Alto) | High (≥ 90%) | Short (1–3 days) | **Lean / JIT** | Standardised variants, high volume |
| Premium SUV (e.g., Scorpio-N, Fortuner) | Medium (70–85%) | Medium (5–10 days) | **Leagile Hybrid** | Multiple variants, option-rich |
| Luxury / Bespoke (e.g., Mercedes S-Class) | Low (~50%) | Long (4–8 weeks) | **Agile / Postponement** | Customisation, low volume, high margin |
| EV (e.g., Tata Nexon EV) | Improving (~75%) | Medium | **Leagile + Digital Twin** | Battery constraints, software updates |
| Commercial Trucks (e.g., Tata LPT) | Order-based | Long | **ETO (Engineer-to-Order)** | Each truck is partly customised |
| Spare Parts Aftermarket | Highly variable | Medium | **Agile + Distribution Centre Hub** | 100,000+ SKUs |

---

## 3.5 7-Step Decision Framework for SCM Design (KTU Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 7-step framework is a **board-exam favourite** for KTU 14-mark questions.

1. **Define Customer Requirements** — variety, lead time, cost, quality.
2. **Segment the Product Portfolio** — group by demand pattern.
3. **Map the Supply Chain Stages** — Tier-3 → Tier-2 → Tier-1 → OEM → Dealer.
4. **Identify the Decoupling Point** — where push transitions to pull (typically at the OEM's final assembly).
5. **Select the Appropriate SCM Model** — Lean / Agile / Leagile.
6. **Align the Information Systems** — EDI, ERP, IoT telematics.
7. **Measure, Audit, Improve** — using OTIF, BWR, Inventory Turns.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Automotive Supply Chain Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional architecture** of a typical Indian automotive OEM supply chain, including reverse logistics and digital monitoring layers.

```mermaid
graph LR
    rawM["Tier 3 Raw Material Suppliers"]:::tier3
    compM["Tier 2 Component Makers"]:::tier2
    subA["Tier 1 Sub Assembly Suppliers"]:::tier1
    oem["OEM Final Assembly Plant"]:::oem
    deal["Dealership Network"]:::dealer
    cust["End Customer"]:::customer
    servC["Service Centres and Spare Parts"]:::service
    revL["Reverse Logistics and ELV Recycling"]:::reverse
    cloud["Digital Twin and ERP Cloud"]:::digital

    rawM --> compM
    compM --> subA
    subA --> oem
    oem --> deal
    deal --> cust
    cust --> servC
    servC --> revL
    revL --> rawM

    cloud -. monitors .-> rawM
    cloud -. monitors .-> compM
    cloud -. monitors .-> subA
    cloud -. monitors .-> oem
    cloud -. monitors .-> deal
    cloud -. monitors .-> servC

    classDef tier3 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a33,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tier2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b88,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tier1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#5a7,stroke-width:2px

    classDef reverse fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#936,stroke-width:2px
    classDef digital fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

---

## 4.2 Push vs Pull SCM Comparison Block

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PushBlock["PUSH BASED SUPPLY CHAIN"]
        pushA["Forecast Generated"]:::push --> pushB["Production Begins"]:::push
        pushB --> pushC["Buffer Inventory Built"]:::push
        pushC --> pushD["Shipped to Warehouse"]:::push
        pushD --> pushE["Sold to Customer"]:::push

    subgraph PullBlock["PULL BASED SUPPLY CHAIN"]

    classDef push fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#a33,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pull fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 JIT vs JIS Operational Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
    truck --> dock
    dock --> qc
    qc --> seq

    classDef sup fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b8a000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef trans fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#36c,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Information & Material Flow Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB

        edi["EDI and ERP Systems"]:::info

        ai["AI based Demand Forecasting"]:::info
    end

    subgraph MaterialLayer["MATERIAL LAYER"]
        inbound["Inbound Logistics Milk Run"]:::mat
        storage["Just in Time Storage Buffer"]:::mat
        prod["Production Assembly"]:::mat
        outbound["Outbound Logistics to Dealer"]:::mat
    end

    subgraph FinanceLayer["FINANCIAL LAYER"]
        po["Purchase Orders and Milestone Billing"]:::fin
        inv["Invoicing and GST Compliance"]:::fin

    ioT -.-> storage
    ai -.-> prod
    po -.-> outbound
    inv -.-> outbound

```

---

## 4.5 Decoupling Point Visualisation

```mermaid

    pushStage["Push Region: Upstream Suppliers"]:::push
    decouple["DECOUPLING POINT At OEM Assembly"]:::decoup

    pushStage --> decouple
    decouple --> pullStage
    decouple -.- inventoryBuf
```
