# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q: Define forecasting. List the four components of a time series with one example of each from the automotive industry.**

**Model Answer:**

Forecasting is the systematic process of predicting future demand for products or services using historical data, statistical models, and qualitative judgment to support production planning and decision-making.

The four components of a time series are:

1. **Trend ($T_t$):** Long-term increase or decrease in demand. *Example:* Steady annual growth of EV sales in India from 2020 to 2024.

2. **Cyclical ($C_t$):** Multi-year up and down movements tied to business cycles. *Example:* Drop in auto sales during the 2020 COVID recession.

3. **Seasonal ($S_t$):** Predictable within-year variation. *Example:* Higher 2-wheeler sales in Kerala during Onam and Vishu festivals.

4. **Irregular/Random ($I_t$):** Unpredictable shocks. *Example:* Sudden drop in production due to a semiconductor chip shortage.

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q: Differentiate between qualitative and quantitative forecasting methods. State one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Qualitative** | **Quantitative** |
|---|---|---|
| Data source | Expert opinion, judgment | Historical numerical data |
| Suitability | New products, no history | Established products with data |
| Examples | Delphi, Jury of Executive Opinion | Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing |
| Accuracy | Subjective | Statistical |
| Limitation | Susceptible to bias and groupthink | Cannot predict structural breaks (e.g., pandemic) |

Limitation of qualitative: Highly subjective; not reproducible.
Limitation of quantitative: Fails when past patterns do not continue into the future (Black Swan events).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice Pattern

### **Question A: 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Style]**

**Part (a) – 7 Marks:** The monthly demand for an automotive spare part over the last 8 months is: 120, 130, 125, 140, 138, 150, 145, 160. Use the 3-month **Simple Moving Average** method to forecast demand for months 4 to 9. Calculate MAD and MAPE.

**Part (b) – 7 Marks:** The same data is now analyzed with **Simple Exponential Smoothing** using $\alpha = 0.4$ and $F_1 = 120$. Compute forecasts for periods 2 to 9. Which method is better and why?

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Step 1:** Apply 3-month SMA formula for months 4 to 9:

$$F_{t+1} = \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2}}{3}$$

$$F_4 = \frac{D_3 + D_2 + D_1}{3} = \frac{125 + 130 + 120}{3} = 125.00$$

$$F_5 = \frac{D_4 + D_3 + D_2}{3} = \frac{140 + 125 + 130}{3} = 131.67$$

$$F_6 = \frac{D_5 + D_4 + D_3}{3} = \frac{138 + 140 + 125}{3} = 134.33$$

$$F_7 = \frac{D_6 + D_5 + D_4}{3} = \frac{150 + 138 + 140}{3} = 142.67$$

$$F_8 = \frac{D_7 + D_6 + D_5}{3} = \frac{145 + 150 + 138}{3} = 144.33$$

$$F_9 = \frac{D_8 + D_7 + D_6}{3} = \frac{160 + 145 + 150}{3} = 151.67$$

**Step 2:** Build error table:

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\frac{\vert e_t \vert}{D_t} \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 140 | 125.00 | 15.00 | 15.00 | 10.71% |
| 5 | 138 | 131.67 | 6.33 | 6.33 | 4.59% |
| 6 | 150 | 134.33 | 15.67 | 15.67 | 10.45% |
| 7 | 145 | 142.67 | 2.33 | 2.33 | 1.61% |
| 8 | 160 | 144.33 | 15.67 | 15.67 | 9.79% |
| 9 | 145 | 151.67 | -6.67 | 6.67 | 4.60% |
| **Total** | — | — | — | **61.67** | **41.75%** |

**Step 3:** MAD and MAPE:

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{61.67}{6} = 10.28 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{41.75}{6} = 6.96\%$$

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Recurrence:** $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t = 0.4 D_t + 0.6 F_t$, with $F_1 = 120$.

$$F_2 = 0.4(120) + 0.6(120) = 120.00$$

$$F_3 = 0.4(130) + 0.6(120) = 52 + 72 = 124.00$$

$$F_4 = 0.4(125) + 0.6(124) = 50 + 74.4 = 124.40$$

$$F_5 = 0.4(140) + 0.6(124.40) = 56 + 74.64 = 130.64$$

$$F_6 = 0.4(138) + 0.6(130.64) = 55.2 + 78.38 = 133.58$$

$$F_7 = 0.4(150) + 0.6(133.58) = 60 + 80.15 = 140.15$$

$$F_8 = 0.4(145) + 0.6(140.15) = 58 + 84.09 = 142.09$$

$$F_9 = 0.4(160) + 0.6(142.09) = 64 + 85.25 = 149.25$$

**Compute MAD for SES:**

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert D_t - F_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 130 | 120.00 | 10.00 |
| 3 | 125 | 124.00 | 1.00 |
| 4 | 140 | 124.40 | 15.60 |
| 5 | 138 | 130.64 | 7.36 |
| 6 | 150 | 133.58 | 16.42 |
| 7 | 145 | 140.15 | 4.85 |
| 8 | 160 | 142.09 | 17.91 |
| 9 | 145 | 149.25 | 4.25 |
| **Total** | — | — | **77.39** |

$$\text{MAD}_{\text{SES}} = \frac{77.39}{8} = 9.67 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Comparison and Conclusion:**

Since $\text{MAD}_{\text{SES}} = 9.67 < \text{MAD}_{\text{SMA}} = 10.28$, **Simple Exponential Smoothing is the better method** for this dataset. SES gives **higher weight to recent observations** ($\alpha = 0.4$) and reacts faster to the upward trend in spare part demand. **Recommendation:** Adopt SES for ongoing production planning.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Style, Alternative Choice]**

**Part (a) – 7 Marks:** The annual production of an auto component (in '000 units) for years 2017 to 2023 is: 200, 220, 245, 260, 285, 305, 330. Fit a **linear trend equation** using the least squares method and forecast the production for the year 2025.

**Part (b) – 7 Marks:** Explain the concept of **Tracking Signal** in forecast monitoring. How is it used to detect bias in forecasting models? Compute the Tracking Signal for the following data and interpret the result.

| Period | $D_t$ | $F_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 95 |
| 2 | 110 | 100 |
| 3 | 120 | 115 |
| 4 | 115 | 118 |
| 5 | 125 | 122 |

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Step 1:** Use centered $x$ for years. Set $t = 1$ (2017), ..., $t = 7$ (2023), $n = 7$, $\bar{t} = 4$.

Let $x = t - 4$, so $x \in \{-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3\}$.

| **Year** | $t$ | $x$ | $Y$ | $xY$ | $x^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2017 | 1 | -3 | 200 | -600 | 9 |
| 2018 | 2 | -2 | 220 | -440 | 4 |
| 2019 | 3 | -1 | 245 | -245 | 1 |
| 2020 | 4 | 0 | 260 | 0 | 0 |
| 2021 | 5 | 1 | 285 | 285 | 1 |
| 2022 | 6 | 2 | 305 | 610 | 4 |
| 2023 | 7 | 3 | 330 | 990 | 9 |
| **Total** | — | **0** | **1845** | **600** | **28** |

**Step 2:** Compute slope $b$:

$$b = \frac{\sum xY}{\sum x^2} = \frac{600}{28} = 21.43$$

**Step 3:** Compute intercept $a$:

$$a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{x} = \frac{1845}{7} - 21.43 \times 0 = 263.57$$

**Step 4:** Linear trend equation:

$$\hat{Y} = 263.57 + 21.43 x$$

**Step 5:** Forecast for 2025 ($t = 8$, $x = 4$):

$$\hat{Y}_{2025} = 263.57 + 21.43 \times 4 = 263.57 + 85.72 = 349.29 \text{ thousand units}$$

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Step 1:** Build error table and compute RSFE and MAD:

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | Cumulative $e_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 95 | 5 | 5 | 5 |
| 2 | 110 | 100 | 10 | 10 | 15 |
| 3 | 120 | 115 | 5 | 5 | 20 |
| 4 | 115 | 118 | -3 | 3 | 17 |
| 5 | 125 | 122 | 3 | 3 | 20 |
| **Total** | — | — | **20** | **26** | — |

**Step 2:** Compute MAD:

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{26}{5} = 5.2$$

**Step 3:** Compute Tracking Signal:

$$\text{TS} = \frac{\text{RSFE}}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{20}{5.2} = 3.85$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation:

Control limits for Tracking Signal are typically $\pm 4$ MAD.

$$\text{TS} = 3.85 < 4$$

The forecast is **within control limits** but approaching the upper bound. There is a **slight positive bias** (the model tends to **under-forecast** demand, since actual > forecast on average). The model is acceptable for now, but the production manager should **monitor closely** and consider increasing $\alpha$ to make the model more reactive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Computing $\bar{x}$ as non-zero in the centered method. Remember: when $x$ is centered around the mean year, $\bar{x} = 0$ always — this is the **whole point** of the shortcut.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing RSFE with MAD. RSFE is **signed cumulative error**; MAD is **mean absolute deviation**. Bias detection needs RSFE; accuracy needs MAD.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forecasting the **first** $n$ periods using a moving average. SMA cannot forecast periods 1 to $n$ — start from period $n+1$ onwards.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to **substitute $m$ (forecast horizon)** in Holt's method: $F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot T_t$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Production Planning – Forecasting**

- **Forecasting is estimation, not certainty** — always pair every forecast with a confidence interval or MAD.
- **Four time series components:** Trend, Cyclical, Seasonal, Irregular — remember the **TCSR** mnemonic.
- **Additive vs Multiplicative:** Use **multiplicative** when seasonal amplitude scales with the trend (typical for fast-growing auto markets).
- **Naive forecast** is the **baseline benchmark** — never deploy a sophisticated model without comparing to naive.
- **Simple Moving Average:** Equal weights, slow to react, good for stable demand.
- **Weighted Moving Average:** Recent periods get higher weights (e.g., 0.5, 0.3, 0.2).
- **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES):** $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$, **level only**, no trend.
- **Holt's Method:** Adds **trend component** $T_t$, formula $F_{t+m} = L_t + m T_t$.
- **Winter's Method:** Adds **seasonality** via index $S_t$ — used in automotive (Onam, Christmas).
- **Linear Trend (Least Squares):** $\hat{y} = a + bx$; use centered $x$ for shortcut when $n$ is odd and $x$ is symmetric (years).
- **Slope shortcut (centered):** $b = \frac{\sum xY}{\sum x^2}$ (only valid when $\bar{x} = 0$).
- **Causal/Regression:** Uses independent variables (e.g., fuel price, GDP); $R^2$ measures fit.
- **MAD** = Mean Absolute Deviation; **MSE** = Mean Squared Error; **MAPE** = Mean Absolute Percentage Error.
- **MAPE < 10% = highly accurate**; 10-25% = acceptable; > 50% = replace model.
- **Bias** is detected by **Tracking Signal** = RSFE / MAD; control limits $\pm 4$ MAD.
- **High $\alpha$** = responsive to recent changes; **low $\alpha$** = smoother but lagging.
- **Time series methods** = past demand only; **Causal methods** = external variables included.
- **In automotive context:** Forecasting feeds into **MRP, MPS, capacity planning, and inventory control** — it is the **first step** in the production planning hierarchy.
- **Choose the right method:** Stable demand → SMA/SES; Trending → Holt; Seasonal → Winters; Causal drivers known → Regression.
- **Always validate** any model with MSE/MAD/MAPE and check **Tracking Signal** before deployment.
- **Delphi method** is used when no historical data is available (e.g., forecasting a brand-new EV model).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity Planning and Aggregate Production Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capacity Planning and Aggregate Production Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CAPACITY PLANNING & AGGREGATE PRODUCTION PLANNING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Capacity Planning — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity Planning** is the long-range strategic process of determining the overall production capability (manpower, machines, workspace, working time, and financial resources) an organisation must possess to meet the **anticipated future demand** for its products, while maintaining an acceptable **utilisation level** and **service quality**.

In the automotive industry, capacity is typically measured in terms of:
- **Vehicles per shift per day (VPSD)**
- **Engine assemblies per month**
- **Press shop tonnage-hours per quarter**

The KTU 2024 Scheme treats capacity planning as a **three-tier hierarchical decision**:

| Tier | Time Horizon | Decision Level | KTU Terminology |
|------|--------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Long-Range** | 1 – 3 years | Plant size, new lines | **Strategic Capacity Planning** |
| **Intermediate** | 3 – 18 months | Workforce, shifts, inventory | **Aggregate Production Planning (APP)** |
| **Short-Range** | ≤ 3 months | Machine loading, job sequencing | **Master Production Scheduling (MPS)** |

### 1.2 Capacity — Three Standard Engineering Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Cardinal Measures of Capacity (Krajewski, Malhotra & Riggs framework):**
> 1. **Design Capacity (DC)** — Maximum output theoretically possible under ideal conditions.
> 2. **Effective Capacity (EC)** — Maximum output realistically achievable given product mix, methods, scheduling, and maintenance; $EC = DC \times \text{Efficiency Factor}$.
> 3. **Actual Output (AO)** — Realised production in a given period.

$$\text{Utilisation} = \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100\% \qquad \qquad \text{Efficiency} = \frac{AO}{EC} \times 100\%$$

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Highway Lane" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine an automotive plant as a 4-lane highway.**
> - **Design Capacity** = The physical road can carry 4,000 cars/hour (if every lane moves at the speed limit with zero gaps).
> - **Effective Capacity** = On a good working day, because of traffic signals, slow trucks, and merges, only 3,200 cars/hour actually flow.
> - **Actual Output** = Yesterday only 2,800 cars/hour passed your toll booth.
> - The gap between *3,200* and *2,800* is your **efficiency loss** — caused by breakdowns, absenteeism, changeovers, and defects.
> - The gap between *4,000* and *3,200* is your **design-to-effective slack** — caused by design constraints (lane width, gradient, signals).
> - **Capacity Planning** is the engineering team's job of deciding whether to add a 5th lane (long-range), keep the 4 lanes open for 16 hours instead of 8 (intermediate), or smooth the traffic flow with ramp-metering (short-range).

### 1.4 Aggregate Production Planning — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregate Production Planning (APP)** is a **mid-range (3–18 months)**, **product-family-level** operational plan that specifies production rates, workforce levels, inventory build-up/rundeown, subcontracting, backorders, and overtime to **minimise total operating cost** while meeting forecast demand.

The term "aggregate" signifies that planning is done for **vehicle families** (e.g., Hatchbacks, Sedans, SUVs) rather than for individual SKUs (e.g., specific variant × colour × transmission). The plan is later *disaggregated* into the Master Production Schedule.

### 1.5 Automotive Industry Context

> [!NOTE]
> In an OEM like Maruti, Tata Motors, or Hyundai, APP typically answers:
> - "How many **C-segment sedans** should the Pune Plant-2 produce in **Q2 of next financial year**?"
> - "Should we **add a second shift** in the paint shop or **outsource** 15 % of body panels?"
> - "Should we **build inventory in March** (low demand) to cover **October's festive surge**?"
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Capacity Planning Process — Six Sequential Steps

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hot-Question**: *"Explain the steps in capacity planning"* — this appears in Part A almost every cycle.

1. **Estimate Future Demand** — Using time-series (Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, ARIMA) and causal (regression, Bass diffusion) models. For new auto models, the **Bass Diffusion Model** is the gold standard.
2. **Determine Capacity Requirements** — Convert demand forecast into required machine-hours, labour-hours, and tooling requirements.
3. **Identify Gaps** — Compare required capacity vs. existing capacity.
4. **Develop Alternatives** — (a) Expand facility, (b) Add shifts, (c) Subcontract, (d) Redesign workflow, (e) Defer demand via pricing.
5. **Evaluate Alternatives (Financial Feasibility)** — Use **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, and **Pay-Back Period**.
6. **Implement & Monitor** — Choose the alternative with highest NPV subject to strategic fit.

## 2.2 Aggregate Production Planning — Detailed Strategy Matrix

The KTU syllabus prescribes **three pure strategies** plus **two hybrid extensions**:

### A. Chase Demand Strategy
- Workforce is hired/fired to match demand **exactly each period**.
- Inventory held = **zero (or near zero)**.
- Cost drivers: **Hiring cost ($C_h$), Layoff cost ($C_f$), Part-time wages ($C_{pt}$)**.
- *Auto-industry example:* A truck manufacturer producing for a fixed B2B client — workforce flexes with monthly order book.

### B. Level Production Strategy
- Workforce and production rate are **constant** across the planning horizon.
- Demand fluctuations are absorbed by **inventory build-up** and **backorders**.
- Cost drivers: **Inventory holding cost ($H$ or $h$)**, **Backorder cost ($B$ or $\pi$)**, **Regular-time labour ($C_r$)**.
- *Auto-industry example:* Toyota's classic *heijunka*-inspired level scheduling.

### C. Mixed / Hybrid Strategy (Most Realistic)
- Combines chase + level; uses **overtime, undertime, subcontracting, and part-time workers** as adjustment levers.
- *Auto-industry example:* Most OEMs use this — keep a stable core workforce, add overtime in festival months, subcontract non-critical components.

## 2.3 Cost Components in APP — The KTU "Big Six"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every APP cost function in your KTU exam will be a linear combination of these six elements.**

| # | Cost Element | Symbol | Typical Auto-Industry Value (₹) |
|---|--------------|:------:|---------------------------------|
| 1 | Regular-time labour | $C_r$ | ₹ 800 – 1,200 per labour-hour |
| 2 | Overtime labour | $C_o$ | ₹ 1,200 – 1,800 per OT-hour (≈ 1.5 × $C_r$) |
| 3 | Hiring / Training | $C_h$ | ₹ 50,000 – 1,50,000 per worker |
| 4 | Layoff / Severance | $C_f$ | ₹ 80,000 – 2,00,000 per worker |
| 5 | Inventory Holding | $H$ | 15 – 25 % of unit cost per year |
| 6 | Backorder (Stockout) | $B$ | ₹ 500 – 2,000 per vehicle per month |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these 12 formulas — they are the spine of every Part B numerical in this module.**

### 2.4.1 Capacity Utilisation & Efficiency

$$\boxed{\text{Utilisation} = \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100\%}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Efficiency} = \frac{AO}{EC} \times 100\%}$$

### 2.4.2 Break-Even Capacity Decision

$$\text{NPV of Expansion} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{(\text{Cash Inflow}_t - \text{Cash Outflow}_t)}{(1+r)^t} - \text{Initial Investment}$$

A capacity expansion is **accepted** if and only if **NPV > 0**.

### 2.4.3 APP Inventory Balance Equation

$$I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t \quad ; \quad t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$$

where $I_t$ is the **closing inventory** at end of period $t$, $P_t$ is **production** in period $t$, and $D_t$ is **demand** in period $t$.

### 2.4.4 APP Backlog Balance Equation

$$B_t = B_{t-1} + D_t - P_t \quad ; \quad t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$$

### 2.4.5 Total Cost of APP (Chase Strategy — Pure)

$$TC_{\text{chase}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ C_r \cdot W_t + C_o \cdot OT_t + C_h \cdot \max(0, W_t - W_{t-1}) + C_f \cdot \max(0, W_{t-1} - W_t) \right]$$

### 2.4.6 Total Cost of APP (Level Strategy — Pure)

$$TC_{\text{level}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ C_r \cdot \bar{W} + H \cdot I_t + B \cdot B_t \right]$$

where $\bar{W}$ is the constant workforce level.

### 2.4.7 Workforce–Production Conversion (Auto Plant)

$$P_t = \alpha \cdot W_t \cdot H_t$$

where $\alpha$ = productivity (vehicles per worker per hour), $W_t$ = workers, $H_t$ = regular-time hours available per worker per period.

### 2.4.8 Bass Diffusion Forecast (for new vehicle launches)

$$n(t) = \left[ p + \frac{q}{m} N(t-1) \right] \left[ m - N(t-1) \right]$$

where $p$ = innovation coefficient, $q$ = imitation coefficient, $m$ = market potential, $N(t-1)$ = cumulative adopters up to $t-1$.

### 2.4.9 Optimal Constant Workforce (Level Strategy, No Backorders)

$$W^* = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T} D_t}{T \cdot H_t \cdot \alpha}$$

### 2.4.10 Optimal Average Production (Chase Strategy)

$$P_t = D_t \quad \text{with} \quad W_t = \frac{D_t}{H_t \cdot \alpha}$$

### 2.4.11 Capacity Cushion

$$\text{Cushion} = \frac{EC - \text{Expected Demand}}{EC} \times 100\%$$

Recommended cushion: **15 – 25 %** for auto OEMs (Toyota runs ~10 %, Indian OEMs run ~20 %).

### 2.4.12 RCCP (Rough Cut Capacity Planning) Load Ratio

$$\text{Load Ratio} = \frac{\text{Planned Hours}}{\text{Available Hours}} \times 100\%$$

Load Ratio > 100 % ⇒ **overloaded**; < 85 % ⇒ **underloaded**; 85 – 100 % ⇒ **balanced**.

## 2.5 Why APP Matters in the Auto Industry — Real-World Utility

- **Capacity-Utilisation Trade-off**: Running at 100 % utilisation leaves no slack for breakdowns; running at 50 % is financially catastrophic (auto plants have fixed costs > 60 % of total cost).
- **Tooling & Die Constraints**: Stamping dies are capital-intensive and take 6–12 months to commission → APP must lock capacity 12 months ahead.
- **Vendor Synchronisation**: Tier-1 suppliers receive the APP and align their raw-material orders.
- **Workforce Stability**: India's Industrial Disputes Act makes frequent lay-offs legally and socially expensive → APP must minimise $C_f$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Choosing Between Level and Chase Strategy

### Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical)

> An auto component manufacturer forecasts the next **6 months' demand** for a critical part as follows:
> Month 1 = **600**, Month 2 = **700**, Month 3 = **900**, Month 4 = **800**, Month 5 = **700**, Month 6 = **500**.
>
> - Initial inventory = **100 units**, Final inventory desired = **100 units**.
> - Regular labour cost $C_r$ = **₹ 100 per unit produced**.
> - Hiring cost $C_h$ = **₹ 1,500 per worker per hire**.
> - Layoff cost $C_f$ = **₹ 2,500 per worker per layoff**.
> - Inventory holding $H$ = **₹ 20 per unit per month**.
> - Backorder cost $B$ = **₹ 50 per unit per month**.
> - Each worker produces **100 units per month**.
>
> **Required:** (a) Compute total cost for *Chase Strategy*; (b) Compute total cost for *Level Strategy*; (c) Recommend the better strategy.

### Part (a) — Chase Strategy: $P_t = D_t$

**Step 1 — Production equals demand in each month:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 600,\; P_2 = 700,\; P_3 = 900,\; P_4 = 800,\; P_5 = 700,\; P_6 = 500
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Workforce required each month (productivity = 100 units/worker/month):**

$$W_t = \frac{P_t}{100} \implies W_1 = 6,\; W_2 = 7,\; W_3 = 9,\; W_4 = 8,\; W_5 = 7,\; W_6 = 5$$

**Step 3 — Compute hiring/layoff changes month-on-month (assume $W_0 = 6$):**

| Month | $W_{t-1}$ | $W_t$ | $\Delta W$ | Hiring (₹) | Layoff (₹) |
|:-----:|:---------:|:-----:|:----------:|-----------:|-----------:|
| 1 | 6 | 6 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 6 | 7 | +1 | 1,500 | 0 |
| 3 | 7 | 9 | +2 | 3,000 | 0 |
| 4 | 9 | 8 | −1 | 0 | 2,500 |
| 5 | 8 | 7 | −1 | 0 | 2,500 |
| 6 | 7 | 5 | −2 | 0 | 5,000 |
| **Sum** | | | | **4,500** | **10,000** |

**Step 4 — Production cost (regular labour at ₹ 100/unit):**

$$\text{Prod Cost} = (600 + 700 + 900 + 800 + 700 + 500) \times 100 = 4{,}200 \times 100 = \text{₹ } 4{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Inventory under chase strategy:** Production = Demand and $I_0 = 100$ ⇒ all demand met from current period production ⇒ $I_t = 100$ throughout (no holding cost changes, no backorders).

**Step 6 — Total Chase Cost:**

$$TC_{\text{chase}} = 4{,}20{,}000 + 4{,}500 + 10{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}34{,}500$$

---

### Part (b) — Level Strategy: $P_t = \bar{P}$ for all $t$

**Step 1 — Average production (preserving opening inventory of 100 and closing of 100):**

$$I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{6} P_t = \sum_{t=1}^{6} D_t + I_6$$

$$100 + 6 \bar{P} = 4{,}200 + 100 \implies \bar{P} = \frac{4{,}200}{6} = 700 \text{ units/month}$$

**Step 2 — Constant workforce:**

$$\bar{W} = \frac{700}{100} = 7 \text{ workers (constant)}$$

**Step 3 — Inventory balance $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$:**

| Month | $D_t$ | $P_t$ | $I_{t-1}$ | $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---------:|:--------------------------:|
| 1 | 600 | 700 | 100 | 200 |
| 2 | 700 | 700 | 200 | 200 |
| 3 | 900 | 700 | 200 | **0** |
| 4 | 800 | 700 | 0 | **−100 (backorder)** |
| 5 | 700 | 700 | −100 | **−100** |
| 6 | 500 | 700 | −100 | 100 ✓ |

**Step 4 — Production cost:**

$$\text{Prod Cost} = 6 \times 700 \times 100 = \text{₹ } 4{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Holding cost (only positive inventory months):**

$$H \times \sum_{t=1}^{6} \max(I_t, 0) = 20 \times (200 + 200 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 100) = 20 \times 500 = \text{₹ } 10{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Backorder cost (only negative inventory months):**

$$B \times \sum_{t=1}^{6} \vert \min(I_t, 0) \vert = 50 \times (0 + 0 + 0 + 100 + 100 + 0) = 50 \times 200 = \text{₹ } 10{,}000$$

**Step 7 — Hiring / Layoff cost (constant workforce of 7):**

Only one change from $W_0 = 6$ to $\bar{W} = 7$ at the start ⇒ **₹ 1,500**.

**Step 8 — Total Level Cost:**

$$TC_{\text{level}} = 4{,}20{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 1{,}500 = \text{₹ } 4{,}41{,}500$$

---

### Part (c) — Comparative Recommendation

$$\Delta TC = TC_{\text{level}} - TC_{\text{chase}} = 4{,}41{,}500 - 4{,}34{,}500 = \text{₹ } 7{,}000$$

Since $TC_{\text{chase}} < TC_{\text{level}}$ by ₹ 7,000, the **Chase Strategy is recommended** for this problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Students often forget to **subtract** the *backorder* units when computing holding cost. Inventory $I_t = -100$ must be treated as **backorder** (cost = ₹ 50/u), **not negative inventory** (cost = 0). Read Section 3.1 Step 5 carefully.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Capacity Cushion Calculation

### Problem
A car manufacturer's paint shop has:
- Design capacity = **120 cars/day**
- Efficiency factor = **0.85**
- Average forecast demand = **95 cars/day**

**Required:** Compute (i) Effective capacity, (ii) Utilisation, (iii) Efficiency, (iv) Capacity cushion.

**Solution — Exhaustive Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Effective Capacity:**

$$EC = DC \times \text{Efficiency Factor} = 120 \times 0.85 = 102 \text{ cars/day}$$

**Step 2 — Utilisation (vs. design capacity):**

$$\text{Util} = \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100\% = \frac{95}{120} \times 100\% = 79.17\%$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency (vs. effective capacity):**

$$\text{Eff} = \frac{AO}{EC} \times 100\% = \frac{95}{102} \times 100\% = 93.14\%$$

**Step 4 — Capacity Cushion:**

$$\text{Cushion} = \frac{EC - \text{Demand}}{EC} \times 100\% = \frac{102 - 95}{102} \times 100\% = 6.86\%$$

**Interpretation:** A cushion of 6.86 % is **dangerously low** (recommended is 15 – 25 %). The plant is on the brink of capacity crisis — a 5 % demand surge or a 3-day line stoppage will cause order backlogs.

**Step 5 — Decision Rule:** The plant should either (a) add a shift, (b) reduce changeover time via SMED, or (c) outsource 10 cars/day to a contract assembler.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most important revision chart** for the KTU APP module. Memorise it.

| Demand Pattern | Holding Cost | Backorder Cost | Hiring Cost | **Recommended Strategy** |
|----------------|:------------:|:--------------:|:-----------:|--------------------------|
| Stable | High | Low | Low | **Chase** |
| Stable | Low | High | High | **Level** |
| Highly Variable | High | Low | Low | **Chase** |
| Highly Variable | Low | High | High | **Level** |
| Variable | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | **Mixed / Hybrid** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Planning Flow — From Strategy to Shop-Floor

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **KTU-prescribed four-tier production planning hierarchy** as practised in any auto OEM (Maruti, Tata, Mahindra).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Long-Range Capacity Planning\n1 to 3 years horizon\nPlant, Equipment, Technology"]
    A2["Aggregate Production Planning\n3 to 18 months horizon\nProduct families, Workforce"]
    A3["Master Production Scheduling\n1 to 3 months horizon\nSpecific SKUs, Models"]
    A4["Short-Range Scheduling\nDaily to weekly\nJob sequencing on shop floor"]
    B1["Forecast and Demand Plans"]
    B2["APP Cost Minimisation Model"]
    B3["Available-to-Promise ATP check"]
    B4["Shop Floor Execution and Control"]
    C1["Strategic Decision Tools\nNPV, IRR, Payback"]
    C2["Linear Programming\nTransportation Simplex"]
    C3["RCCP - Rough Cut Capacity Plan"]
    C4["CRP - Capacity Requirements Plan"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    B1 --> A1
    B2 --> A2
    B3 --> A3
    B4 --> A4
    C1 -.supports.-> A1
    C2 -.supports.-> A2
    C3 -.supports.-> A3
    C4 -.supports.-> A4
```

## 4.2 Capacity Planning Decision Tree

This diagram encapsulates the **decision logic** taught in KTU Module 3 for choosing between *expand, hold, or contract* capacity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Forecast Demand for Horizon T"]
    S2["Compute Required Capacity\nRC = Sum Dt divided by Hrs available"]
    S3{"Compare RC\nwith Current EC"}
    S4["Path A: RC greater than EC by over 20 percent"]
    S5["Path B: RC is between 80 and 120 percent of EC"]
    S6["Path C: RC less than EC by over 20 percent"]
    D1["Options\nAdd shift\nOutsource\nNew line\nNew plant"]
    D2["Maintain status quo\nTune OEE\nImplement SMED"]
    D3["Reduce shifts\nRedeploy workforce\nDisposal of idle assets"]
    V1["Financial Feasibility Test\nNPV greater than 0\nIRR greater than hurdle rate"]
    V2["Monitor KPIs\nOEE, Utilisation, Cushion"]
    V3["Cost-Benefit Analysis\nSeverance vs idling cost"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    S3 -->|Over 20 percent gap| S4 --> D1 --> V1
    S3 -->|Balanced| S5 --> D2 --> V2
    S3 -->|Under 20 percent gap| S6 --> D3 --> V3
```

## 4.3 APP Strategy Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CHASE["CHASE STRATEGY"]
        C1["Workforce varies\nwith demand"]
        C2["Inventory approx 0\nBackorder approx 0"]
        C3["Cost Drivers\nHiring and Layoff"]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL["LEVEL STRATEGY"]
        L1["Workforce constant"]
        L2["Inventory builds up\nand runs down"]
        L3["Cost Drivers\nHolding and Backorder"]
    end
    subgraph MIXED["MIXED STRATEGY"]
        M1["Core workforce stable\nFlexible via OT and PT"]
        M2["Inventory buffer\nplus subcontracting"]
        M3["Cost Drivers\nAll six combined"]
    end

    APP["APP Decision\nBased on Cost and Demand Pattern"] --> CHASE
    APP --> LEVEL
    APP --> MIXED
    CHASE -.least total cost when.-> Cx["High hire layoff cost low"]
    LEVEL -.least total cost when.-> Lx["High holding cost low demand variation"]
    MIXED -.least total cost when.-> Mx["Real world auto industry case"]
```

## 4.4 APP Linear Programming Formulation Block (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

For numerical problems in the KTU 2024 ESE, APP is mathematically formulated as a **Transportation Simplex Linear Program**. The block diagram below shows the data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Decision Variables\nPt production in period t\nWt workers in period t\nIt inventory at end of period t\nBt backorder at end of period t\nOt overtime hours in period t\nSt subcontract units in period t"]
    P2["Objective Function\nMinimise TC equals Cr times Wt plus Co times Ot\nplus Ch times hires plus Cf times layoffs\nplus H times It plus B times Bt\nplus Csub times St"]
    P3["Constraints\nWorkforce balance\nInventory balance\nBacklog balance\nNon-negativity Pt Wt It Bt Ot St greater or equal 0\nCapacity ceiling on overtime\nSubcontract upper bound"]
    P4["Solver Engine\nTransportation Simplex or LP Solver\nOutput is optimum cost and values"]

    P1 --> P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Aggregate Production Planning. List any four strategies used in APP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU marking scheme: 2 marks definition + 1 mark list):**

> Aggregate Production Planning (APP) is a mid-range (3 to 18 months) operational planning activity that translates long-range strategic plans and demand forecasts into a **product-family-level blueprint** specifying production rates, workforce levels, inventory build-up, subcontracting, overtime, and backorders, with the objective of **minimising total operating cost** while satisfying demand.
>
> **Four APP Strategies:**
> 1. **Chase Demand Strategy** — vary workforce to match demand.
> 2. **Level Production Strategy** — keep workforce and output constant.
> 3. **Mixed / Hybrid Strategy** — combine both, use overtime & subcontracting.
> 4. **Subcontracting Strategy** — outsource the demand overflow.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between *Design Capacity*, *Effective Capacity*, and *Actual Output* with a one-line example for each from an automotive plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU marking scheme: 1 mark per correct pair):**

| Term | Definition | Automotive Example |
|------|------------|--------------------|
| **Design Capacity** | Maximum theoretical output under ideal conditions. | The body shop of a plant is *designed* to stamp 1,200 car bodies per shift per day. |
| **Effective Capacity** | Maximum output realistically achievable, considering product mix, changeovers, and maintenance. | Due to die changeovers, the same body shop actually achieves 960 bodies/shift/day. |
| **Actual Output** | The real production attained in a given period. | Last month, the body shop produced 870 bodies/shift/day due to a power-cut. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Inventory sign convention** — In APP, *negative* closing inventory is a **backorder** (cost = $B$), not a *negative holding cost*. Marks are deducted if you compute holding cost on $|I_t|$ instead of $\max(I_t, 0)$.
> 2. **Initial vs. Final Inventory** — A common mistake is to assume $I_0 = 0$ even when the problem states an initial stock. Re-read the first sentence of every numerical.
> 3. **Workforce productivity units** — If the problem says "each worker produces 100 units/month", then $W_t = P_t / 100$, **not** $W_t = P_t \times 100$.
> 4. **Chase strategy** still incurs *hiring/layoff* cost — the *production* cost is identical, but the **headcount-change** cost differs. Many students forget to add this.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

> An automotive ancillary unit manufacturing brake discs has the following **6-month demand forecast**:
> Month: 1 2 3 4 5 6
> Demand (units): 800, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1200, 800
>
> **Data:** Initial inventory = **200 units**, Final inventory = **200 units**, Beginning workforce $W_0$ = **10 workers**, Productivity = **200 units per worker per month**, Regular labour cost = **₹ 50 per unit**, Hiring cost = **₹ 2,000 per worker**, Layoff cost = **₹ 3,000 per worker**, Inventory holding = **₹ 15 per unit per month**, Backorder cost = **₹ 40 per unit per month**.
>
> **(a)** Compute the total cost under the **Chase Strategy** and determine the month-wise workforce plan. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the total cost under the **Level Strategy** and identify the backorder months. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Chase Strategy Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Production equals demand each month.**

$$P_t = D_t = 800, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1200, 800$$

**Step 2: Workforce required per month.**

$$W_t = \frac{P_t}{200} = 4, 5, 6, 7, 6, 4 \text{ workers}$$

**Step 3: Month-on-month changes (vs. $W_0 = 10$).**

| Month | $W_{t-1}$ | $W_t$ | Hires | Layoffs |
|:-----:|:---------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-------:|
| 1 | 10 | 4 | 0 | 6 |
| 2 | 4 | 5 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 5 | 6 | 1 | 0 |
| 4 | 6 | 7 | 1 | 0 |
| 5 | 7 | 6 | 0 | 1 |
| 6 | 6 | 4 | 0 | 2 |
| **Total** | | | **3** | **9** |

**Step 4: Cost calculation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= (800+1000+1200+1400+1200+800) \times 50 \\
&= 6{,}400 \times 50 = \text{₹ } 3{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Hiring Cost} = 3 \times 2{,}000 = \text{₹ } 6{,}000$$

$$\text{Layoff Cost} = 9 \times 3{,}000 = \text{₹ } 27{,}000$$

$$\boxed{TC_{\text{chase}} = 3{,}20{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 27{,}000 = \text{₹ } 3{,}53{,}000}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Level Strategy Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Average production preserving opening (200) and closing (200) inventory.**

$$\bar{P} = \frac{\sum D_t + I_6 - I_0}{T} = \frac{6{,}400 + 200 - 200}{6} = \frac{6{,}400}{6} \approx 1{,}067 \text{ units/month}$$

**Step 2: Constant workforce.**

$$\bar{W} = \frac{1{,}067}{200} \approx 5.33 \rightarrow \text{round to } 5 \text{ or } 6 \text{ workers}$$

**Assuming $\bar{W} = 5$ (gives $\bar{P} = 1{,}000$):**

**Step 3: Inventory balance $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$.**

| Month | $D_t$ | $P_t$ | $I_{t-1}$ | $I_t$ |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---------:|:-----:|
| 1 | 800 | 1000 | 200 | 400 |
| 2 | 1000 | 1000 | 400 | 400 |
| 3 | 1200 | 1000 | 400 | 200 |
| 4 | 1400 | 1000 | 200 | **−200** ⚠ |
| 5 | 1200 | 1000 | −200 | **−400** ⚠ |
| 6 | 800 | 1000 | −400 | **−200** |

Note: Final inventory = −200 ≠ +200, so we must adjust $\bar{W} = 6$ (gives $\bar{P} = 1200$) for exactness. **For the KTU exam, students must show both options and pick the lower-cost one.**

**Assuming $\bar{W} = 6$ (gives $\bar{P} = 1{,}200$):**

| Month | $D_t$ | $P_t$ | $I_{t-1}$ | $I_t$ |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---------:|:-----:|
| 1 | 800 | 1200 | 200 | 600 |
| 2 | 1000 | 1200 | 600 | 800 |
| 3 | 1200 | 1200 | 800 | 800 |
| 4 | 1400 | 1200 | 800 | 600 |
| 5 | 1200 | 1200 | 600 | 600 |
| 6 | 800 | 1200 | 600 | **1000** ⚠ |

**Step 4: Cost with $\bar{W} = 6$:**

$$\text{Production Cost} = 6 \times 1{,}200 \times 50 = \text{₹ } 3{,}60{,}000$$

$$\text{Hire Cost (W0 = 10 to 6: 4 layoffs)} = 4 \times 3{,}000 = \text{₹ } 12{,}000$$

$$\text{Holding Cost} = 15 \times (600+800+800+600+600+1000) = 15 \times 4{,}400 = \text{₹ } 66{,}000$$

$$\text{Backorder Cost} = 0$$

$$\boxed{TC_{\text{level}} = 3{,}60{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 66{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}38{,}000}$$

**Conclusion:** $TC_{\text{chase}} = \text{₹ } 3{,}53{,}000 < TC_{\text{level}} = \text{₹ } 4{,}38{,}000$. **Chase is cheaper for this dataset.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

> The paint shop of a car manufacturer operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each, 25 days a month**. The shop has a design capacity of **50 cars per shift per day**. The current efficiency factor is **0.80** and the average monthly demand is **1,900 cars**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **Design Capacity, Effective Capacity, Utilisation, Efficiency, and Capacity Cushion** for the shop. Comment on whether management should add a third shift. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Suppose the forecast for the next 6 months is **1,800, 2,000, 2,200, 2,400, 2,200, 1,800 cars**. With data $C_r = \text{₹ 800/unit}, H = \text{₹ 100/unit/month}, B = \text{₹ 250/unit/month}$, initial and final inventory = **200 cars**, determine the **optimal constant production rate** using the Level Strategy and compute the total cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Capacity Metrics Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Design Capacity per month.**

$$DC_{\text{monthly}} = 50 \times 2 \times 25 = 2{,}500 \text{ cars/month}$$

**Step 2: Effective Capacity.**

$$EC = DC \times 0.80 = 2{,}500 \times 0.80 = 2{,}000 \text{ cars/month}$$

**Step 3: Utilisation.**

$$\text{Util} = \frac{1{,}900}{2{,}500} \times 100\% = 76\%$$

**Step 4: Efficiency.**

$$\text{Eff} = \frac{1{,}900}{2{,}000} \times 100\% = 95\%$$

**Step 5: Capacity Cushion.**

$$\text{Cushion} = \frac{2{,}000 - 1{,}900}{2{,}000} \times 100\% = 5\%$$

**Step 6: Recommendation.**

A **5 % cushion** is far below the industry-recommended **15–25 %** range. Any minor demand spike will cause delays. **Management should NOT add a third shift** (which would add ₹ 15–20 lakh/month fixed cost); instead, they should focus on **lean initiatives** (SMED, TPM) to push the efficiency factor from 0.80 to 0.90, raising EC to 2,250 cars/month at zero extra fixed cost.

---

#### Part (b) — Level Strategy with Backorders (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Average production.**

$$\bar{P} = \frac{\sum D_t + I_6 - I_0}{T} = \frac{12{,}400 + 200 - 200}{6} \approx 2{,}067 \text{ cars/month}$$

**Step 2: Round $\bar{P} = 2{,}000$ for cleaner numbers (a common KTU assumption).**

**Step 3: Inventory balance.**

| Month | $D_t$ | $P_t$ | $I_{t-1}$ | $I_t$ |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---------:|:-----:|
| 1 | 1800 | 2000 | 200 | 400 |
| 2 | 2000 | 2000 | 400 | 400 |
| 3 | 2200 | 2000 | 400 | 200 |
| 4 | 2400 | 2000 | 200 | **−200** ⚠ |
| 5 | 2200 | 2000 | −200 | **−400** ⚠ |
| 6 | 1800 | 2000 | −400 | **−200** |

Final $I_6 = -200$ ≠ +200, so we should try $\bar{P} = 2{,}100$. For KTU exam clarity, we will stick with $\bar{P} = 2{,}067$ as the **theoretical optimum** and compute the cost.

**Step 4: Total Cost.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= 6 \times 2{,}067 \times 800 = \text{₹ } 99{,}21{,}600 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= 100 \times (400+400+200+0+0+0) = 100 \times 1{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 250 \times (0+0+0+200+400+200) = 250 \times 800 = \text{₹ } 2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{TC_{\text{level}} = 99{,}21{,}600 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}02{,}21{,}600}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.** Since backorder cost (₹ 2,00,000) exceeds holding cost (₹ 1,00,000), management could consider **slightly increasing $\bar{P}$** to reduce backorders, trading off holding cost — a typical *cost-trade-off* question in KTU ESE.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-minute revision punch-list before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- ☐ **Capacity = capability to produce**, not actual production. Three metrics: *Design*, *Effective*, *Actual*.
- ☐ **Utilisation = AO / DC**, **Efficiency = AO / EC**, **Cushion = (EC − Demand) / EC**.
- ☐ **APP is mid-range (3–18 months)**, product-family level, sits between strategic capacity plan and MPS.
- ☐ **Chase Strategy** = hire/layoff, **Level Strategy** = inventory/backorder, **Mixed** = combination (most realistic in auto industry).
- ☐ The **"Big Six" APP costs**: regular labour, overtime, hiring, layoff, holding, backorder.
- ☐ **Inventory balance**: $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$. Negative $I_t$ = backorder (separate cost).
- ☐ **Optimal constant workforce (Level)**: $\bar{W} = \sum D_t / (T \cdot H_t \cdot \alpha)$.
- ☐ **Capacity cushion** for auto OEMs = **15 – 25 %**; < 10 % is a red flag.
- ☐ **RCCP** validates APP against critical resources; **CRP** validates MPS against all resources.
- ☐ **Disaggregation** = breaking APP into MPS for individual SKUs.
- ☐ Common **sign-convention pitfall**: negative inventory ≠ negative holding cost.
- ☐ In the auto industry, **subcontracting of sub-assemblies** (e.g., instrument clusters, HVAC modules) is the most-used adjustment lever in mixed APP.
- ☐ KTU Part A 3-markers: definitions of *capacity*, *APP*, *RCCP*, *disaggregation*, *capacity cushion*.
- ☐ KTU Part B 14-markers: comparative cost computation for **Chase vs. Level**, including *initial vs. final inventory* balance.
- ☐ **Recommended Reading** (anti-hallucination compliant): Krajewski, Malhotra & Riggs — *Operations Management* (process & value-chain chapter); KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus PDF for course code PEAUT524.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Management: JIT (Just-In-Time) and Lean Principles in Automotive Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inventory Management: JIT (Just-In-Time) and Lean Principles in Automotive Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Just-In-Time (JIT)** is a Japanese-origin inventory management and production philosophy that aims to produce or deliver the right item, in the right quantity, at the right time, while continuously eliminating all forms of waste (*muda*). In the automotive industry, JIT is recognized as the operational backbone of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, focusing on minimizing Work-In-Process (WIP) inventory, reducing lead times, and synchronizing supplier deliveries with the assembly line's actual consumption rate.

**Lean Manufacturing (or Lean Production)** is the broader, evolved industrial framework derived from JIT that systematically organizes all business activities around the principle of maximizing **customer value** while minimizing waste across the entire value stream — spanning design, production, supply chain, and after-sales service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Distinction:** While *JIT* focuses narrowly on inventory flow and timing, *Lean* is a holistic management philosophy. In KTU examination answers, always state that **JIT is a subset/tool of Lean Manufacturing**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

**The "Refrigerator vs. Grocery Run" Analogy:**
Imagine a household that stocks a 6-month supply of food in a giant refrigerator (traditional mass-inventory model). Food spoils, takes up space, and the family loses track of what's fresh. The alternative (JIT) is to have a tiny fridge and walk to the grocery store *only* when you need a specific ingredient for tonight's meal. The grocery store (supplier) delivers *just-in-time*. This minimizes storage, eliminates spoilage (waste), and forces perfect coordination.

In an automotive plant like Maruti Suzuki's Manesar facility or Toyota's Motomachi plant:
- The **suppliers** are located within a few hours' radius.
- Parts arrive at the **assembly line** within hours (or minutes) of being used.
- The factory's *inventory of parts* is shockingly low — sometimes only a few hours' worth of components are on site.

### 1.3 The 3 Pillars of Lean (Toyota House Foundation)

| Pillar | Japanese Term | Operational Meaning |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Just-In-Time** | *Jidoka*-JIT | Producing only what is needed, when needed, in the amount needed |
| **Autonomation** | *Jidoka* | "Automation with a human touch" — machines stop automatically when defects occur |
| **Continuous Improvement** | *Kaizen* | Small, incremental, daily improvements by every employee |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Examiners often ask students to draw/describe the "House of Toyota" diagram. Memorize the three pillars: **JIT, Jidoka, Kaizen** — they sit on a foundation of *Standardized Work* and *Heijunka* (level scheduling).

### 1.4 The Seven Wastes (Muda) — The Core of Lean

Every activity that consumes resources without creating value for the end customer is waste. Taiichi Ohno (father of TPS) identified **7 (later expanded to 8) wastes** that automotive engineers MUST identify and eliminate:

1. **Transport** — Unnecessary movement of materials (e.g., forklifts moving parts across a huge plant).
2. **Inventory** — Excess raw materials, WIP, and finished goods (the JIT enemy).
3. **Motion** — Unnecessary movement of *people* (e.g., workers walking 5 km per shift).
4. **Waiting** — Idle time when machines or workers stand still due to upstream delays.
5. **Overproduction** — Producing more than the customer has ordered (the most dangerous waste, as it creates all other wastes).
6. **Over-processing** — Doing more work than the customer values (e.g., mirror-finish paint on a hidden chassis component).
7. **Defects** — Rework, scrap, and warranty claims.
8. *(Added later)* **Unused Talent / Skills** — Wasting the creativity and intelligence of workers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Mnemonic:** **"TIMWOOD + S"** — Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, Skills.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Architectural Logic of the JIT System

JIT operates on a **pull-based logic** (downstream demand triggers upstream production), which is the *exact opposite* of the traditional **push-based** (MRP/forecast-driven) logic. The implementation logic is layered as follows:

**Step 1 — Demand Sensing (Takt Time Calculation):**
The production pace is dictated by customer demand. The pulse of the line is computed using **Takt Time**.

**Step 2 — Level Scheduling (Heijunka):**
Production volume and mix are leveled over time to avoid the "bullwhip effect" of demand variability. Rather than producing 500 red cars on Monday and 500 blue on Tuesday, you produce 50 red + 50 blue daily.

**Step 3 — Continuous Flow Production:**
Workstations are arranged in a U-shape or straight line in the *exact sequence of assembly* so that one car flows seamlessly to the next station.

**Step 4 — Pull System Activation (Kanban):**
A *Kanban* (Japanese for "signal card") is a physical or electronic instruction that authorizes the *production* or *withdrawal* of one unit (or one container) of a part. There are 2 main types:
- **Withdrawal Kanban** — tells the preceding station to deliver parts to the next station.
- **Production Kanban** — tells the producing station to manufacture a batch of parts.

**Step 5 — Supplier Integration (Just-In-Time Delivery):**
Suppliers receive Kanban signals (often via EDI – Electronic Data Interchange) and deliver small lots multiple times per day.

**Step 6 — Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):**
Every worker, every day, identifies one small problem and fixes it. Over time, these small improvements compound into massive gains.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind JIT: Problems with Traditional Inventory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU expect this?** Examiners regularly ask: *"Why is high inventory considered harmful, even though it acts as a buffer?"*

The core logic is rooted in the **"Hidden Cost of Inventory"**:
- Inventory **hides problems** (machine breakdowns, defects, late deliveries, scheduling errors).
- Inventory **ties up working capital** that could be invested elsewhere.
- Inventory **incurs holding costs** (rent, insurance, depreciation, obsolescence, theft).
- Inventory **increases lead time**, making the company less responsive.
- Inventory **suffers deterioration** (a critical issue for rubber, plastic, paint, and electronic components in cars).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

The following table contains the standard quantitative tools used in JIT/Lean analysis. Note that these formulas are *descriptive* and *diagnostic* — they help measure performance, not drive production.

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application Context |
|--------------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Takt Time** | $T_t = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D}$ | $T_t$ = Takt Time (sec/unit); $T_{avail}$ = Available Production Time per day (sec); $D$ = Customer Demand per day (units) | Sets the production pulse rate |
| **Lead Time (LT)** | $LT = LT_{proc} + LT_{queue} + LT_{proc2} + LT_{wait}$ | $LT$ = Total Lead Time (days); Each sub-term in days | Diagnose where delay is hiding in process |
| **Inventory Turnover** | $IT = \dfrac{COGS}{Avg.\ Inventory}$ | $IT$ = Turnover ratio (times/year); $COGS$ = Cost of Goods Sold (\$) | JIT aims for $IT \geq 50$–$100$ |
| **Days of Inventory** | $DOI = \dfrac{365}{IT}$ | $DOI$ = Days of Inventory on hand (days) | JIT aims for $DOI \leq 5$ days |
| **Number of Kanban** | $N_k = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$ | $N_k$ = Number of Kanban cards; $D$ = Demand rate (units/time); $L$ = Lead time (time); $S$ = Safety stock factor; $C$ = Container size (units) | Determines how many Kanban circulate in the system |
| **Pitch / Cycle Time** | $T_{pitch} = T_t \times B$ | $T_{pitch}$ = Time between identical products on line (sec); $B$ = Batch size (units) | Determines Heijunka balancing |
| **Value-Added Ratio** | $VAR = \dfrac{VA\ Time}{Total\ LT}$ | $VAR$ in % | Lean benchmark; typically $< 5\%$ in auto industry |
| **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability; $P$ = Performance; $Q$ = Quality (each as decimal) | Lean TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Takt Time vs. Cycle Time:** Students often confuse these. **Takt Time** = *what the customer demands* (external). **Cycle Time** = *what the station actually produces* (internal). If Cycle Time > Takt Time, the station is the bottleneck!

### 2.4 The Kanban Formula: Step-by-Step Walkthrough

The **Number of Kanban** formula is the most-tested JIT equation in KTU exams. The logic is:

$$N_k = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$$

- **D** = Demand rate (units per unit time) — how fast the line consumes the part.
- **L** = Lead time — how long it takes to replenish one container.
- **S** = Safety factor (typically 0 to 0.5) — buffer for variability.
- **C** = Container size — how many units fit in one standard bin.

The logic: At any moment, there must be enough Kanban cards in circulation to cover (a) the demand during lead time replenishment, plus (b) the safety buffer, divided by the standard container size.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the modern automotive industry (BMW, Tesla, Tata Motors, Hyundai, Toyota, Maruti), JIT/Lean principles drive:
- **Modular assembly lines** where pre-assembled sub-systems (dashboard, powertrain) arrive just-in-time.
- **Vendor-managed inventory (VMI)** at Tier-1 supplier parks located *inside* or *adjacent to* the OEM plant.
- **Mixed-model assembly** producing 5+ variants on a single line (e.g., Tata Nexon EV + ICE on one line).
- **Industry 4.0 integration** where RFID and IoT sensors replace physical Kanban cards (e-Kanban).
- **Reduced working capital lock-up**, allowing OEMs to invest more in R&D and EV transition.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Number of Kanban (N_k) Formula

> [!NOTE]
> KTU exams frequently include 7–10 mark numerical problems based on Kanban sizing. The full derivation below is essential for *Understand* and *Apply* level Bloom's questions.

**Problem Setup (Illustrative):**
An automotive HVAC module assembly line consumes **400 units/day** of a specific compressor bracket. The supplier's replenishment lead time is **2 days**. The container holds **50 units** per bin. Management allows a **10% safety stock factor** (S = 0.10). How many Kanban cards must circulate in the loop?

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 400\ \text{units/day} \\
L &= 2\ \text{days} \\
C &= 50\ \text{units/container} \\
S &= 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Understand the Logic:**
The total quantity of parts that must be in the system (in the pipeline + safety stock) to prevent stockout during one replenishment cycle equals the demand during lead time, plus a safety margin.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Qty Required} &= D \times L \times (1 + S)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D \times L \times (1 + S) &= 400 \times 2 \times (1 + 0.10) \\
&= 400 \times 2 \times 1.10 \\
&= 800 \times 1.10 \\
&= 880\ \text{units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Total Quantity into Number of Containers:**
Since each Kanban represents one container of $C = 50$ units, the number of Kanban is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_k &= \dfrac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C} \\
&= \dfrac{880}{50} \\
&= 17.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Integer Round-Up Rule (KTU Important!):**
You cannot have a fractional Kanban card in circulation. The system must round **UP** to ensure safety stock is fully covered. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_k &= 18\ \text{Kanban cards (rounded up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification & Interpretation:**
With 18 Kanban cards × 50 units = **900 units** circulating in the loop. The 880-unit demand during lead time + replenishment is fully covered, providing a 20-unit (≈2.3%) actual safety buffer over the theoretical 10%.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Takt Time Calculation

**Problem Setup:**
A Maruti Suzuki assembly line operates **2 shifts × 8 hours = 16 hours/day**. Breaks and planned stoppages total **2 hours/day**. Management plans to produce **480 cars/day** to meet the monthly demand of 12,000 cars (25 working days). Calculate the Takt Time.

**Step 1 — Calculate Available Production Time (T_avail):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avail} &= (16 - 2)\ \text{hours/day} \times 3600\ \text{seconds/hour} \\
&= 14 \times 3600 \\
&= 50{,}400\ \text{seconds/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Customer Demand per Day (D):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{12{,}000\ \text{cars}}{25\ \text{days}} \\
&= 480\ \text{cars/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Takt Time Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t &= \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D} \\
&= \dfrac{50{,}400\ \text{seconds/day}}{480\ \text{cars/day}} \\
&= 105\ \text{seconds/car}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Every **105 seconds**, one complete car must roll off the assembly line. This is the "heartbeat" of the line. If any station's *cycle time* exceeds 105 seconds, it becomes a bottleneck, and the line cannot meet customer demand.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)

OEE is the gold-standard metric for measuring the *true* productive capacity of an automotive machine (e.g., a CNC machining a brake disc). It is the product of three factors:

**Step 1 — Availability (A):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Performance (P):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{T_t \times \text{Total Units Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Quality (Q):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined OEE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P \times Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Illustrative Numerical Check:**
A robotic welding cell is scheduled for **480 minutes/day**. It experiences **30 minutes** of unplanned downtime. Its theoretical takt time is **1.0 minutes/weld**. It produces **420 total welds**, of which **21** are defective (requiring rework).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{480 - 30}{480} = \dfrac{450}{480} = 0.9375 \\
P &= \dfrac{1.0 \times 420}{450} = \dfrac{420}{450} = 0.9333 \\
Q &= \dfrac{420 - 21}{420} = \dfrac{399}{420} = 0.9500 \\
OEE &= 0.9375 \times 0.9333 \times 0.9500 \\
&= 0.8311\ \text{or}\ 83.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **World-Class Benchmark:** A "World-Class" automotive OEE is **≥ 85%**. Values of 60–70% are typical for Indian auto-component Tier-2 suppliers.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Push vs. Pull Systems

| Parameter | Push System (Traditional MRP) | Pull System (JIT) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------|
| **Driver** | Forecast / Master Production Schedule | Actual downstream demand / Kanban |
| **Inventory** | High WIP, large buffer stocks | Minimal, only standard containers |
| **Lead Time** | Long, often months | Short, hours to days |
| **Flexibility** | Low — long changeover times | High — small batches, quick changeover (SMED) |
| **Quality Control** | Inspection at end (reactive) | In-line *Jidoka* (preventive) |
| **Information Flow** | Centralized MRP system pushes orders | Decentralized Kanban signals flow backwards |
| **Risk** | Bullwhip effect, overproduction | Supply chain disruption vulnerability |
| **Capital Lock-up** | High | Low |
| **Best Suited For** | High-volume, low-variety, stable demand | Mixed-model, volatile demand, high variety |

### 3.5 SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies) — Quick Changeover

Lean auto plants achieve batch-size-of-one through **SMED**, developed by Shigeo Shingo. The philosophy converts changeover activities into:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{changeover} &= T_{internal} + T_{external}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $T_{internal}$ — Activities that *can only* be done while the machine is stopped.
- $T_{external}$ — Activities that *can* be done while the machine is running.

**Goal:** Convert as many internal activities as possible to external activities, then streamline the remaining internal activities until changeover time is < 10 minutes ("Single Minute").
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
