<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 KTU Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Just-In-Time (JIT) production system. List the seven types of waste (muda) addressed by Lean manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Just-In-Time (JIT) is a production strategy pioneered by Toyota that aims to produce and deliver the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time, thereby eliminating all forms of waste and reducing inventory levels to an absolute minimum. It relies on a pull-based system where each stage produces only what the downstream stage has consumed.

The seven wastes (muda) addressed by Lean are: **(1) Transport** – unnecessary movement of materials, **(2) Inventory** – excess raw materials, WIP, and finished goods, **(3) Motion** – unnecessary movement of people, **(4) Waiting** – idle time of machines or workers, **(5) Overproduction** – producing more than customer demand, **(6) Over-processing** – doing more work than the customer values, and **(7) Defects** – rework, scrap, and warranty returns. *(A commonly added 8th waste is "Unused Talent" — wasting worker creativity and skills.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023] What is a Kanban system? Differentiate between a Withdrawal Kanban and a Production Kanban.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Kanban** is a Japanese term meaning "signal card" or "visible record." It is a fundamental tool of the JIT pull system, used to authorize and control the production or movement of materials between workstations. Kanban can be physical cards, containers, or electronic (e-Kanban) signals.

**Withdrawal Kanban** authorizes the *movement* of parts from one workstation to the next. It tells the preceding process to deliver a specific quantity of parts to the succeeding process. **Production Kanban** authorizes the *manufacture* of parts. It tells the producing workstation to start manufacturing a specific quantity of parts to replenish the stock that has just been withdrawn. Together, these two types of cards create the closed loop that drives a pull-based production system.

---

### 5.2 KTU Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (1 × 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** for 14-mark questions. Students attempt either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the Just-In-Time (JIT) production system in detail. Discuss its key principles and the relationship between JIT and Lean manufacturing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of JIT (2 Marks):**
Just-In-Time (JIT) is a Japanese-origin pull-based production philosophy that aims to deliver the right quantity of the right item at the right time, minimizing all forms of waste. It was pioneered by Taiichi Ohno at Toyota Motor Corporation in the 1950s–1970s and is the operational core of the Toyota Production System (TPS).

**2. Key Principles of JIT (3 Marks):**
- **Pull, Not Push:** Production is triggered by actual downstream demand (via Kanban), not by forecast or master schedule.
- **Zero Inventory Philosophy:** Inventory is treated as a liability that hides problems; the goal is single-piece flow.
- **Small Lot Production:** Small batches reduce lead time, WIP, and the bullwhip effect.
- **Quick Changeover (SMED):** Enables economic small batches by reducing setup time below 10 minutes.
- **Supplier Partnership:** Long-term relationships, few reliable suppliers, frequent small deliveries.
- **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Every worker continuously identifies and eliminates waste.
- **In-line Quality (Jidoka):** Defects are stopped at the source, not passed downstream.

**3. Relationship Between JIT and Lean (2 Marks):**
Lean Manufacturing is the *broader management philosophy* that encompasses JIT. While JIT specifically addresses inventory and production flow, Lean extends these principles to the *entire value stream* — including design, supply chain, logistics, and administration. **JIT is a critical tool/subsystem within the Lean framework.** Other Lean tools include 5S, Value Stream Mapping (VSM), Total Productive Maintenance (TPM), Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing), and Kaizen.

---

**Q.A. (b)** An automotive component shop has a daily demand of **600 brake calipers**. The supplier's lead time is **3 days**. Each standard container holds **40 calipers**. The safety factor is **0.15**. Calculate the number of Kanban cards required. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Data (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 600\ \text{units/day} \\
L &= 3\ \text{days} \\
C &= 40\ \text{units/container} \\
S &= 0.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — State the Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_k &= \dfrac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_k &= \dfrac{600 \times 3 \times (1 + 0.15)}{40} \\
&= \dfrac{1800 \times 1.15}{40} \\
&= \dfrac{2070}{40} \\
&= 51.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Integer Round-Up Rule (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_k &= 52\ \text{Kanban cards}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (2 Marks):**
With 52 Kanban × 40 calipers = 2,080 units in circulation. The theoretical demand during lead time + safety = 2,070 units. The system is fully covered, with a small additional 10-unit (≈0.5%) buffer above the theoretical 15% safety stock. The 52 cards must circulate continuously between the customer station and the supplier to maintain seamless pull-flow.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q.B. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the Toyota Production System (House of Toyota). Explain the concept of "Jidoka" and its role in Lean manufacturing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram of the Toyota Production System (3 Marks):**
*(The student should draw the House of Toyota diagram — see SECTION 4.1 of these notes for the reference.)*

The House of Toyota is built on a foundation consisting of: (i) **Standardized Work**, (ii) **Heijunka (Level Scheduling)**, and (iii) **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)**. Rising from this foundation are the two main pillars: **(1) Just-In-Time (JIT)** — producing only what is needed, when needed, in the amount needed; and **(2) Jidoka (Autonomation)** — building in quality at the process, not at the inspection stage. At the apex of the house are the business goals: **Best Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time, Safety & High Morale, and Best Delivery**.

**2. Concept of Jidoka (2 Marks):**
Jidoka, sometimes translated as "Autonomation" (Automation with a Human Touch), is a principle that empowers machines and workers to *automatically detect defects and stop production* when an abnormality occurs. The philosophy was developed by Sakichi Toyoda for his automatic loom in 1896, where the machine would stop immediately upon thread breakage, preventing defective cloth.

**3. Role of Jidoka in Lean Manufacturing (2 Marks):**
- **In-line Quality Control:** Defects are detected and contained at the source, preventing the "one defective part becomes 100" problem.
- **Abnormality Visibility:** Line stoppage acts as a visual signal, forcing immediate investigation and root-cause analysis.
- **Prevents Overproduction of Defects:** If the line stops, defective goods are not produced en masse.
- **Empowers Workers:** Workers are authorized and expected to "stop the line" (Andon cord) when they see a problem.
- **Foundation of Pull System:** Because defects are prevented, the JIT system can confidently operate with minimal inventory buffers.

---

**Q.B. (b)** A passenger car assembly line operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each**, with a total of **1.5 hours** of planned breaks per day. The plant must produce **1,000 cars per day** to meet the monthly demand. Calculate the **Takt Time** and comment on the operational implication if a specific station has a cycle time of **45 seconds**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Available Production Time (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avail} &= (16 - 1.5)\ \text{hours/day} \times 3600\ \text{seconds/hour} \\
&= 14.5 \times 3600 \\
&= 52{,}200\ \text{seconds/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — State Takt Time Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t &= \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Takt Time (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t &= \dfrac{52{,}200\ \text{seconds/day}}{1{,}000\ \text{cars/day}} \\
&= 52.2\ \text{seconds/car}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare Takt Time with Station Cycle Time (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Takt Time} &= 52.2\ \text{sec/car} \\
\text{Station Cycle Time} &= 45\ \text{sec/car} \\
\text{Slack Time} &= 52.2 - 45 = 7.2\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Operational Comment (1 Mark):**
Since the station cycle time (**45 sec**) is *less than* the Takt Time (**52.2 sec**), the station has a **positive slack of 7.2 seconds per car**. This means the station is *not* a bottleneck and can comfortably meet customer demand. The 7.2-second buffer provides some flexibility to absorb minor variability (such as a worker briefly adjusting a tool) without slowing the line.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Takt Time with Cycle Time:** Takt Time is derived from *customer demand*; Cycle Time is the *actual time* a station takes. Mark deduction if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the Round-Up Rule in Kanban:** If calculation gives 17.6, writing "17.6 Kanban" loses 1 mark. Must be **18**.
> 3. **Ignoring Safety Stock:** Writing the formula without $(1 + S)$ loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Unit Mismatch:** Computing Takt Time in *minutes* but the formula demands *seconds* (or vice-versa) is a frequent error.
> 5. **Diagram Mistakes:** Drawing the "House of Toyota" with pillars swapped (JIT on Jidoka's side) loses at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Missing the 8th Waste:** Listing only 7 wastes when the question asks for "all wastes" — examiners may deduct marks for not mentioning "Unused Talent."
> 7. **Confusing Push with Pull:** Stating that JIT "pushes" parts through the system is a fundamental conceptual error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **JIT = Right Item + Right Quantity + Right Time.** Memorize this triple definition.
- **Lean = Broader philosophy; JIT = Tool within Lean.**
- **Takt Time** is calculated as $T_t = T_{avail} / D$ and is set by the *customer*, not the engineer.
- **Cycle Time** is determined by the *station* and should be ≤ Takt Time.
- **Kanban** = signal card that triggers production/withdrawal. Two types: **Withdrawal** (movement) and **Production** (manufacturing).
- **Number of Kanban formula:** $N_k = D \cdot L \cdot (1+S) / C$. Always **round up** to the next integer.
- **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD+S):** Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, Skills.
- **Three Pillars of TPS:** JIT, Jidoka, Kaizen — built on Standardized Work and Heijunka.
- **Push system** = forecast-driven (MRP). **Pull system** = demand-driven (JIT/Kanban).
- **SMED** = Single Minute Exchange of Dies; reduces changeover time to < 10 minutes.
- **Jidoka** = "Automation with a Human Touch" — stop the line on defect detection.
- **Heijunka** = Level Scheduling — smooths production volume and mix to avoid demand spikes.
- **OEE = A × P × Q**, World-class benchmark ≥ 85%.
- **Inventory Turnover ≥ 50–100** in world-class JIT auto plants; **DOI ≤ 5 days**.
- **Toyota Production System (House of Toyota)** must be drawn correctly: foundation → pillars → roof (goals).
- **Overproduction is the most dangerous waste** because it generates *all other* wastes.
- **JIT reduces working capital lock-up** but increases vulnerability to supply chain disruptions (e.g., COVID-19, semiconductor shortage of 2021–2023 exposed JIT fragility in the auto industry).
- **Poka-Yoke** = mistake-proofing devices that make it impossible to assemble a part incorrectly.
- **5S** = Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain — the foundation of any Lean transformation.
- **Kaizen** = small, daily, incremental improvements; the engine of long-term Lean transformation.
- **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** is the diagnostic tool used to identify waste in the entire material and information flow.
- **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** ensures that equipment is always available, reliable, and capable — a prerequisite for JIT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Quality Management (TQM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT524 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT IN AUTO INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Total Quality Management (TQM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving all members of the organization — from top management to frontline workers — to meet or exceed customer expectations. In the context of the **automotive industry**, TQM is a structured system that integrates quality planning, quality control, quality assurance, and quality improvement across the entire value chain, from supplier (Tier-1, Tier-2) to Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) to the end customer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Total Quality Management is a management approach centred on quality, based on the participation of all members of the organization and aiming at long-term success through customer satisfaction and benefits to all members of the organization and to society.*

### 1.2 ISO Standard Reference

The formal international anchor for TQM is captured in:
- **ISO 8402:1986** — Defines TQM terminology (now superseded by ISO 9000:2015).
- **ISO 9000:2015** — Defines quality as the *"degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements."*
- **ISO/TS 16949 (now IATF 16949:2016)** — The automotive-specific quality management standard that mandates TQM-aligned practices across the global automotive supply chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality (Q)** is formally defined as:
> $$Q = \frac{\text{Performance (Actual)}}{\text{Expectations (Customer Needs)}}$$
> When $Q > 1$, the product *exceeds* expectations; when $Q = 1$, the product *meets* expectations; when $Q < 1$, there is a *quality gap* (customer dissatisfaction).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Michelin-Star Kitchen"**
> Imagine a Michelin-star restaurant. TQM is **NOT** just the head chef (the quality inspector) tasting the food before it leaves the kitchen. TQM is the *entire system* — the farmer who grows the vegetables, the delivery driver who brings them on time, the line cook who plates the dish, the dishwasher who ensures spotless plates, the manager who takes customer feedback, and even the receptionist who greets guests. **Every single person in the chain owns quality.** If one link fails, the customer gets a bad experience. That is the *Total* in TQM, and that is the *Management* — leading the whole system.

In an **automobile manufacturing plant** like Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, or Toyota, TQM works exactly the same way:
- The **welding robot operator** must ensure every weld is defect-free.
- The **seat supplier (Tier-1)** must deliver foam density within ±0.5 kg/m³ tolerance.
- The **paint shop technician** must verify paint thickness (typically 80–120 µm).
- The **dealership service advisor** must capture customer Voice of Customer (VoC).
- The **CEO** must sponsor the quality policy and review QMS audits.

---

### 1.4 Historical Evolution of TQM (Timeline)

| Era | Stage | Key Milestone |
|---|---|---|
| **1920s–1930s** | Inspection Era | Walter Shewhart introduces Statistical Quality Control (SQC) at Bell Labs. |
| **1940s–1950s** | Quality Control (QC) Era | Post-WWII, Japan adopts QC; **W. Edwards Deming** and **Joseph Juran** train Japanese engineers. |
| **1960s–1970s** | Quality Assurance (QA) Era | **Kaoru Ishikawa** develops the *Quality Circles*; **Armand Feigenbaum** coins "Total Quality Control" (1956). |
| **1980s** | TQM Era | **Philip Crosby** publishes *Quality is Free* (1979); US firms adopt TQM to counter Japanese competition. |
| **1990s** | Six Sigma Era | **Motorola** (1986) and **GE** (1995, under Jack Welch) popularize Six Sigma. |
| **2000s–Present** | Lean + Six Sigma + IATF 16949 | Integrated *Lean Six Sigma*, digital QMS, **Industry 4.0** quality analytics. |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically asks students to know the contributions of the **"Quality Gurus"**: Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, and Feigenbaum. These will be detailed in Section 2.

---

### 1.5 Why TQM in the Auto Industry? — The Strategic Imperative

The automotive industry is one of the **most quality-intensive** manufacturing sectors globally because:

1. **Safety-Critical Products** — A defect in a brake caliper or airbag system can be fatal. TQM ensures zero-defect design.
2. **Regulatory Compliance** — IATF 16949, ISO 9001, AIS (Automotive Industry Standards in India), and ECE regulations mandate QMS.
3. **High Recall Costs** — The average automotive recall costs **$500 to $1,500 per vehicle**. A TQM-driven **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** system prevents recalls at the source.
4. **Brand Reputation** — J.D. Power Initial Quality Study (IQS) rankings directly influence sales; TQM is the only way to consistently top these rankings.
5. **Global Supply Chain Complexity** — A modern car has 20,000+ parts sourced from 300+ suppliers. TQM's *supplier quality management* is non-negotiable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Quality Frameworks Compared

| Framework | Full Form | Focus | Key Idea | Era |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **QC** | Quality Control | Product inspection | Detect defects via inspection | 1940s |
| **QA** | Quality Assurance | Process & system | Build quality *into* the process | 1960s |
| **TQM** | Total Quality Management | Entire organization | Quality is everyone's responsibility, customer-driven, continuous improvement | 1980s+ |

---

### 2.2 The TQM Gurus — Their Theories & Contribution

#### 2.2.1 W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993) — "The Father of Quality"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's 14 Points for Management** (Highly Examinable in KTU)

Deming believed that **94% of quality problems are attributable to the system (management), only 6% to workers**.

1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product and service.
2. Adopt the new philosophy.
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality (build quality in).
4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
5. Improve constantly every process for planning, production, and service.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

**Deming's PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle):**

$$\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act}$$

- **Plan:** Identify the problem, analyse data, develop a hypothesis.
- **Do:** Implement the plan on a small scale (pilot).
- **Check:** Measure results, compare against expected outcomes.
- **Act:** Standardize if successful, or revise and repeat.

#### 2.2.2 Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008) — "The Quality Planning Pioneer"

Juran's **Quality Trilogy** (Juran's Three Universal Processes):

| Step | Process | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quality Planning** | Identify customers, determine their needs, develop product features, establish quality goals, develop a process. |
| 2 | **Quality Control** | Evaluate actual performance vs. goals, act on differences. |
| 3 | **Quality Improvement** | Establish infrastructure, identify improvement projects, establish project teams, provide training, implement solutions. |

> [!NOTE]
> Juran defined quality as **"Fitness for Use"** — a product must serve the customer's intended purpose.

#### 2.2.3 Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001) — "The Zero-Defects Guru"

Crosby advanced **Four Absolutes of Quality** and the concept of **"Quality is Free"** (the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of correction).

| Absolute | Statement |
|---|---|
| 1 | **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** (not "goodness") |
| 2 | **Prevention, not Inspection** |
| 3 | **Zero Defects Performance Standard** |
| 4 | **Measure Quality by the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** |

Crosby's **5-Stage Quality Improvement Process:**

$$\text{Awareness} \rightarrow \text{Commitment} \rightarrow \text{Measurement} \rightarrow \text{Communication} \rightarrow \text{Action}$$

#### 2.2.4 Kaoru Ishikawa (1915–1989) — "Father of Quality Circles"

Ishikawa introduced:
- **The Ishikawa Diagram** (Fishbone / Cause-and-Effect Diagram) — categorizes causes of a problem into **6 Ms**: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).
- **Quality Circles** — Small groups of 6–12 frontline workers who meet regularly to identify and solve quality problems in their work area. Pioneered in Toyota and adopted globally in auto industry.

#### 2.2.5 Armand V. Feigenbaum (1922–2014) — Coined "Total Quality Control"

Feigenbaum's three-step approach to quality:
1. **Quality Leadership**
2. **Modern Quality Technology (SPC, reliability engineering)**
3. **Organizational Commitment** — including marketing, engineering, production, finance, and after-sales.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Difference:** Feigenbaum's "Total Quality Control" (1956) focused on *systems and cost of quality*. TQM as we know it today evolved from Feigenbaum's TQC, expanded by Deming and Juran's human-side principles.

---

### 2.3 The Eight Principles of TQM (ISO 9004 / TQM Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These principles are highly KTU-examinable — memorize the 8 principles verbatim.**

| # | Principle | Auto Industry Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | J.D. Power IQS analysis, Net Promoter Score (NPS) tracking post-delivery. |
| 2 | **Leadership** | CEO sponsorship of QMS; plant-level Quality Council chaired by Plant Head. |
| 3 | **Involvement of People** | Quality Circles, Suggestion Schemes (Toyota: avg. 30+ suggestions/employee/year). |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | APQP (Advanced Product Quality Planning), IATF 16949 process audits. |
| 5 | **System Approach to Management** | QMS as a network of interrelated processes; SIPOC diagrams. |
| 6 | **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** | PDCA, DMAIC, Kaizen events, Gemba walks. |
| 7 | **Factual Approach to Decision Making** | SPC, MSA, Pareto analysis, hypothesis testing. |
| 8 | **Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships** | Supplier partnership programs (e.g., Toyota's "Keiretsu"), Tier-1 scorecards. |

---

### 2.4 The Seven Quality Control (7 QC) Tools

These are the **foundation tools** every KTU student must know for TQM questions.

| # | Tool | Purpose | Visual Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Ishikawa/Fishbone) | Identify root causes of a problem | Fishbone structure |
| 2 | **Check Sheet** | Collect data systematically in real-time | Tally table |
| 3 | **Control Chart** | Monitor process variation over time | Time-series with UCL/LCL |
| 4 | **Histogram** | Show frequency distribution of data | Bar chart |
| 5 | **Pareto Chart** | Prioritize problems (80/20 rule) | Sorted bar + cumulative % curve |
| 6 | **Scatter Diagram** | Show correlation between two variables | XY plot |
| 7 | **Flowchart** | Map a process step-by-step | Process flow diagram |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule, Vilfredo Pareto, 1906):**
> *80% of problems come from 20% of causes.* In automotive defects, for example, ~80% of warranty claims may come from ~20% of part types (e.g., infotainment, sensors).

---

### 2.5 Cost of Quality (COQ) — A KTU High-Yield Topic

Cost of Quality is the total cost incurred to ensure quality, classified into **4 categories** forming a P-A-F model:

| Category | Type | Examples in Auto Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Costs** | Cost of preventing defects | Quality training, supplier audits, APQP, FMEA, design reviews |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Cost of detecting defects | Incoming inspection, in-process gauges, end-of-line testing, CMM measurement |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Cost of defects found before delivery | Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime due to defects |
| **External Failure Costs** | Cost of defects found after delivery | Warranty claims, recalls, legal liability, customer goodwill loss |

$$\boxed{\text{Total COQ} = P + A + IF + EF}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Prevention cost
- $A$ = Appraisal cost
- $IF$ = Internal Failure cost
- $EF$ = External Failure cost

> [!TIP]
> **Crosby's Principle:** The optimal strategy is to **increase P and A** (prevention & appraisal) to *drastically* reduce $IF$ and $EF$. The net COQ drops — this is the essence of "**Quality is Free**."

**Numerical example for KTU-style problem:**
- A car plant spends ₹2 crore on prevention, ₹1.5 crore on appraisal, ₹4 crore on internal failures, and ₹8 crore on external failures per year.
- **Total COQ = 2 + 1.5 + 4 + 8 = ₹15.5 crore/year.**
- If prevention is increased to ₹4 crore (doubled), IF and EF each drop by 60%:
  - New $IF$ = $4 \times 0.4 = 1.6$ crore
  - New $EF$ = $8 \times 0.4 = 3.2$ crore
  - New COQ = $4 + 1.5 + 1.6 + 3.2 = 10.3$ crore
  - **Savings = ₹5.2 crore/year** ✅.

---

### 2.6 Six Sigma — TQM's Quantitative Successor

Six Sigma was developed by **Motorola** in 1986 (Bill Smith) and popularized by **GE** under Jack Welch in 1995. It is a **data-driven TQM methodology** that aims to reduce process variation to **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**.

**Six Sigma DMAIC Framework:**

$$\text{Define} \rightarrow \text{Measure} \rightarrow \text{Analyze} \rightarrow \text{Improve} \rightarrow \text{Control}$$

| Phase | Purpose | Tools Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Define** | Identify the problem, customer (CTQ), project scope | Project charter, SIPOC, VOC |
| **Measure** | Quantify the current process capability | Process mapping, MSA, Cpk, baseline DPMO |
| **Analyze** | Identify root causes of variation | Pareto, fishbone, regression, hypothesis testing |
| **Improve** | Implement solutions, verify gains | DOE, pilot, FMEA, Kaizen |
| **Control** | Sustain improvements, standardize | Control plans, SPC, SOPs, training |

**Process Capability Indices (KPU Formula Sheet):**

$$\boxed{C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}}$$

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)}$$

- $C_p \geq 1.33$ is the typical automotive industry benchmark (IATF 16949).
- For Six Sigma quality, $C_p \geq 2.0$ (and $C_{pk} \geq 1.5$ minimum).

**DPMO Calculation:**

$$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Number of Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sigma Level vs DPMO Table** (Memorize for KTU):
> - 3σ = 66,807 DPMO (93.3% yield)
> - 4σ = 6210 DPMO (99.38% yield)
> - 5σ = 233 DPMO (99.977% yield)
> - 6σ = **3.4 DPMO** (99.99966% yield)

---

### 2.7 TQM Tools Used in Automotive Industry (Beyond 7 QC Tools)

| Tool | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **FMEA** | Failure Mode and Effects Analysis | Identify potential failures in design or process; rank by RPN (Risk Priority Number = $S \times O \times D$). |
| **QFD** | Quality Function Deployment (House of Quality) | Translate customer requirements (Whats) into engineering characteristics (Hows). |
| **APQP** | Advanced Product Quality Planning | 5-phase structured product launch process mandated by IATF 16949. |
| **PPAP** | Production Part Approval Process | Submit 18 documents to customer to approve a part for serial production. |
| **SPC** | Statistical Process Control | Use of control charts (X-bar, R, p, c) to monitor process stability. |
| **MSA** | Measurement System Analysis | Gage R&R, linearity, bias studies to ensure measurement accuracy. |
| **5S** | Workplace Organization | Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain. |
| **Poka-Yoke** | Mistake-Proofing | Design a process so a defect *cannot* be made (e.g., a connector that only fits one way). |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous Improvement | Small, incremental, daily improvements. |
| **Kanban** | Just-in-Time (JIT) pull system | Reduces overproduction, a key form of waste (Muda). |
| **Gemba Walks** | Go to the actual workplace | Leaders observe the process firsthand (Lean principle). |

---

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| Formula | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $Q = P / E$ | Quality = Performance / Expectations | Quality gap analysis |
| $\text{COQ} = P + A + IF + EF$ | Total Cost of Quality | Management accounting |
| $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million opportunities | Six Sigma level |
| $\sigma \text{ Level} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process sigma | Six Sigma |
| $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL}-\text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability | Process qualification |
| $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Centered process capability | Process control |
| $\text{Pareto: 80/20}$ | 80% of problems from 20% of causes | Prioritization |
| $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number (FMEA) | Risk assessment |
| $\text{Yield} = e^{-DPMO/10^6}$ | Rolled Throughput Yield (approx) | Sigma-to-yield conversion |
| $\text{AQL} = \text{Acceptable Quality Limit}$ | Sampling threshold (e.g., 1.0 AQL for safety parts) | Inspection planning |
| $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | Equipment reliability |
| $\text{DPMO-to-Sigma}$ lookup | 3.4 DPMO $\rightarrow$ 6σ | Six Sigma certification |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Process Capability Index ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$)

**Given:**
- A machining process produces a shaft diameter with specification LSL = 24.95 mm, USL = 25.05 mm.
- Process mean $\mu = 25.00$ mm.
- Standard deviation $\sigma = 0.012$ mm.

**Find:** $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and determine if the process is capable.

**Step 1: Write the $C_p$ formula.**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$C_p = \frac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.012}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the numerator (tolerance width).**

$$\text{USL} - \text{LSL} = 25.05 - 24.95 = 0.10 \ \text{mm}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the denominator (6-sigma spread).**

$$6\sigma = 6 \times 0.012 = 0.072 \ \text{mm}$$

**Step 5: Compute $C_p$.**

$$C_p = \frac{0.10}{0.072} = 1.389$$

**Step 6: Interpret $C_p$.**

Since $C_p = 1.389 > 1.33$ (the IATF 16949 minimum), the **process spread fits within the specification limits with margin**. ✅

**Step 7: Write the $C_{pk}$ formula.**

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$$

**Step 8: Calculate each component separately.**

$$\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.05 - 25.00}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.05}{0.036} = 1.389$$

$$\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.00 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.05}{0.036} = 1.389$$

**Step 9: Take the minimum.**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.389,\ 1.389) = 1.389$$

**Step 10: Interpret the result.**

Since $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.389$, the process is **perfectly centered**. A $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ confirms the process is **automotive-industry capable**. ✅

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: DPMO and Sigma Level Calculation

**Problem:** A transmission assembly line produces 5,000 units per day. Each unit has 25 assembly opportunities for defects. Over 30 days, 1,200 defects are recorded.

**Find:** DPMO and corresponding Sigma Level.

**Step 1: Total units produced in 30 days.**

$$U = 5{,}000 \times 30 = 150{,}000 \ \text{units}$$

**Step 2: Total defect opportunities.**

$$O_{\text{total}} = U \times \text{Opportunities per unit} = 150{,}000 \times 25 = 3{,}750{,}000$$

**Step 3: Apply the DPMO formula.**

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$$

**Step 4: Substitute values.**

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{1{,}200}{150{,}000 \times 25} \times 10^6$$

**Step 5: Compute the denominator.**

$$150{,}000 \times 25 = 3{,}750{,}000$$

**Step 6: Divide defects by opportunities.**

$$\frac{1{,}200}{3{,}750{,}000} = 0.00032$$

**Step 7: Multiply by $10^6$.**

$$\text{DPMO} = 0.00032 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 320 \ \text{DPMO}$$

**Step 8: Convert DPMO to Yield (%).**

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} = 1 - \frac{320}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.99968 = 99.968\%$$

**Step 9: Look up Sigma Level from the standard table.**

A DPMO of 320 corresponds to approximately **4.7σ** (interpolating between 4σ = 6,210 DPMO and 5σ = 233 DPMO).

**Step 10: Interpret.**

The line operates at roughly **4.7 sigma**, which is below the auto industry's Six Sigma target. A DMAIC project is recommended to reduce variation. ⚠️

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: FMEA Risk Priority Number (RPN) Reduction

**Scenario:** A brake-fluid leak. Before corrective action:
- Severity ($S$) = 9 (catastrophic, safety hazard)
- Occurrence ($O$) = 5 (moderate frequency)
- Detection ($D$) = 7 (low detection capability)

**Find:** Original RPN and the % reduction after installing a Poka-Yoke that reduces $D$ from 7 to 2 and $O$ from 5 to 3.

**Step 1: Original RPN formula.**

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{old}} = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2: Substitute original values.**

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{old}} = 9 \times 5 \times 7 = 315$$

**Step 3: New RPN after Poka-Yoke.**

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} = 9 \times 3 \times 2 = 54$$

**Step 4: Calculate the absolute reduction.**

$$\Delta \text{RPN} = 315 - 54 = 261$$

**Step 5: Calculate percentage reduction.**

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{261}{315} \times 100 = 82.86\%$$

**Step 6: Interpretation.**

The Poka-Yoke intervention reduced the FMEA risk score by **~83%**, which is well above the 50% threshold typically required by IATF 16949 PFMEA reviews. ✅

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Cost of Quality (COQ) Optimization

**Problem:** A Tier-1 auto component supplier has the following annual quality costs:
- Prevention (P) = ₹50 lakhs
- Appraisal (A) = ₹80 lakhs
- Internal Failure (IF) = ₹200 lakhs
- External Failure (EF) = ₹400 lakhs

The supplier invests an additional ₹100 lakhs in prevention, which is expected to:
- Reduce IF by 50%
- Reduce EF by 60%
- Reduce A by 20% (less inspection needed)

**Step 1: Original COQ.**

$$\text{COQ}_{\text{old}} = 50 + 80 + 200 + 400 = 730 \ \text{lakhs}$$

**Step 2: New P, A, IF, EF.**

$$P_{\text{new}} = 50 + 100 = 150 \ \text{lakhs}$$

$$A_{\text{new}} = 80 \times (1 - 0.20) = 80 \times 0.80 = 64 \ \text{lakhs}$$

$$IF_{\text{new}} = 200 \times (1 - 0.50) = 100 \ \text{lakhs}$$

$$EF_{\text{new}} = 400 \times (1 - 0.60) = 160 \ \text{lakhs}$$

**Step 3: New total COQ.**

$$\text{COQ}_{\text{new}} = 150 + 64 + 100 + 160 = 474 \ \text{lakhs}$$

**Step 4: Net savings.**

$$\Delta \text{COQ} = 730 - 474 = 256 \ \text{lakhs} \ (\text{₹} 2.56 \ \text{crore})$$

**Step 5: ROI on prevention investment.**

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Net Savings}}{\text{Additional Investment}} = \frac{256}{100} = 2.56 \ (\text{i.e., } 256\%)$$

**Step 6: Interpretation.**

For every ₹1 invested in prevention, the company saves ₹2.56 in total quality cost. **This validates Crosby's "Quality is Free" principle.** ✅

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: TQM Elements Mapped to Auto Industry Stakeholders

| TQM Element | OEM (e.g., Maruti) | Tier-1 Supplier | Dealer | Customer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Customer Focus | JD Power analysis | OEM scorecards | Feedback forms | Voice of Customer (VoC) |
| Leadership | CEO + Plant Heads | Managing Director | Dealer Principal | Influencer reviews |
| Process Approach | APQP, IATF 16949 | PPAP, FMEA | PDI inspection | Service process |
| Continuous Improvement | Kaizen, DMAIC | Lean Six Sigma | Service Kaizen | Loyalty programs |
| Factual Decision Making | SPC dashboards | MSA, DOE | Diagnostic data | J.D. Power surveys |
| Supplier Partnership | Long-term agreements | JIT, shared engineering | OEM-aligned parts | Brand trust |

---

### 3.6 Decision Logic: When to Use Which TQM Tool?

| If the Problem is... | Use This Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Identifying root causes | **Ishikawa (Fishbone)** | Categorizes causes systematically |
| Prioritizing many causes | **Pareto Chart** | Ranks by frequency, applies 80/20 |
| Monitoring a process over time | **Control Chart** | Detects special vs. common cause variation |
| Showing correlation | **Scatter Diagram** | Reveals strength and direction of relationship |
| Collecting data at the source | **Check Sheet** | Real-time tally, simple and visual |
| Mapping a process | **Flowchart / SIPOC** | Defines process boundaries |
| Ranking risks | **FMEA (RPN)** | Quantifies severity, occurrence, detection |
| Translating customer wants to specs | **QFD (House of Quality)** | Bridges marketing and engineering |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Deming PDCA Cycle — Closed-Loop Improvement

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["PLAN\nIdentify problem\nAnalyse data\nSet objectives"]
    node2["DO\nImplement pilot\nTrain team\nExecute small-scale trial"]
    node3["CHECK\nMeasure results\nCompare to target\nValidate with SPC"]
    node4["ACT\nStandardize if success\nOr revise plan\nDocument lessons learned"]
    node1 ==> node2
    node2 ==> node3
    node3 ==> node4
    node4 ==> node1
```

> **Interpretation:** Every TQM improvement in an auto plant (e.g., reducing paint defects from 5% to 0.5%) follows this cycle. Deming emphasized that the cycle is **never-ending** — quality improvement is a *continuous spiral*, not a one-time project.

---

### 4.2 Juran's Quality Trilogy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["JURAN QUALITY TRILOGY"]
    nodeB["QUALITY PLANNING\nIdentify customers\nDetermine needs\nDevelop product specs\nPlan the process"]
    nodeC["QUALITY CONTROL\nEvaluate performance\nCompare actual vs goal\nAct on difference"]
    nodeD["QUALITY IMPROVEMENT\nEstablish infrastructure\nIdentify projects\nForm teams\nImplement solutions"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeC -.feedback.-> nodeB
    nodeD -.insight.-> nodeB
```

---

### 4.3 Six Sigma DMAIC Roadmap (with TQM Overlap)

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1["DEFINE\nProject charter\nVOC and CTQ\nSIPOC map\nProblem statement"]
    step2["MEASURE\nData collection plan\nMSA and Gage R&R\nBaseline Cp, Cpk\nDPMO calculation"]
    step3["ANALYZE\nPareto chart\nFishbone diagram\nHypothesis testing\nRegression analysis"]
    step4["IMPROVE\nDOE pilot run\nPoka-Yoke design\nFMEA update\nKaizen event"]
    step5["CONTROL\nSPC control chart\nStandardized SOP\nControl plan\nTraining handover"]
    step1 ==> step2
    step2 ==> step3
    step3 ==> step4
    step4 ==> step5
    step5 -.monitor.-> step1
```

---

### 4.4 TQM House of Excellence — Integrated Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    foundation["FOUNDATION\n5S Workplace\nPoka-Yoke\nStandardized Work\nGemba Walks"]
    tools["TOOLS LAYER\n7 QC Tools\nSPC and MSA\nFMEA and APQP\nPPAP Control"]
    methods["METHODS LAYER\nPDCA Cycle\nDMAIC\nKaizen Events\nQuality Circles"]
    management["MANAGEMENT LAYER\nTop Management Commitment\nQMS Policy\nIATF 16949\nAnnual Quality Review"]
    customer["CUSTOMER SATISFACTION\nJ.D. Power IQS\nNet Promoter Score\nWarranty Reduction\nBrand Loyalty"]
    management ==> methods
    methods ==> tools
    tools ==> foundation
    foundation ==> customer
    customer -.feedback loop.-> management
```

> **Interpretation:** The "TQM House" is built from the **foundation up** — without 5S and standardized work, advanced tools fail; without management commitment, no amount of Kaizen sustains. The **roof** (customer satisfaction) is the ultimate goal; the **feedback loop** ensures the house continuously adapts.

---

### 4.5 Cost of Quality — P-A-I-E Optimization Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    boxA["PREVENTION COSTS\nTraining, FMEA, APQP\nSupplier audits\nQuality planning"]
    boxB["APPRAISAL COSTS\nIn-process inspection\nCMM measurement\nEnd-of-line testing"]
    boxC["INTERNAL FAILURE\nScrap and rework\nRe-inspection\nProduction downtime"]
    boxD["EXTERNAL FAILURE\nWarranty claims\nRecalls\nLegal liability\nLost goodwill"]
    goal["OPTIMAL COQ ZONE\nMinimum total cost\nMaximize P, minimize IF and EF"]
    boxA --> goal
    boxB --> goal
    boxC -.Reduced by P and A.-> boxA
    boxC -.Reduced by P and A.-> boxB
    boxD -.Reduced by P and A.-> boxA
    boxD -.Reduced by P and A.-> boxB
```

---

### 4.6 IATF 16949 Process Flow (Automotive QMS)

```mermaid
graph LR
    cust["Customer Requirements"]
    plan["APQP Phase 1 to 5\nPlan and Define"]
    design["Design and Development\nFMEA, DFM"]
    process["Process Design\nPFMEA, Control Plan"]
    produce["Production\nSPC, Poka-Yoke, 5S"]
    verify["Verification\nPPAP, MSA, Capability"]
    deliver["Customer Delivery\nService, Warranty"]
    improve["Continual Improvement\nDMAIC, Kaizen"]
    cust ==> plan
    plan ==> design
    design ==> process
    process ==> produce
    produce ==> verify
    verify ==> deliver
    deliver ==> improve
    improve -.feedback.-> plan
```

> **Interpretation:** IATF 16949 is the *operational embodiment* of TQM in the global automotive industry. Every OEM (Toyota, Tata, Hyundai) and every Tier-1 supplier must be certified to sell to major automakers.

### 4.7 Pareto 80/20 in Warranty Defect Analysis (Example Block Flow)
