KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Green Manufacturing**. List any **four** of the 6R principles with one automotive example each. **Model Answer (12 lines max):** > **Green Manufacturing** is the systematic design and operation of production processes that **minimize waste, energy use, and emissions** while maximizing resource productivity, ensuring compliance with environmental regulations, and protecting worker and community health. > > **6R Principles (any 4):** > 1. **Reduce** — Down-gauging steel sheet from 1.2 mm to 0.9 mm in car body panels, saving 8–10 % mass. > 2. **Reuse** — Returnable metal racks and dunnage used for shipping parts between OEM and Tier-1 suppliers. > 3. **Recycle** — Aluminium stamping scrap (chips and offcuts) returned to smelter to make secondary alloy ingots. > 4. **Redesign** — Modular dashboard designed with snap-fits instead of PU adhesives to enable easy disassembly. > 5. **Recover** — Auto Shredder Residue (ASR/fluff) sent to cement kilns as refuse-derived fuel (RDF). > 6. **Rethink** — Replacing mechanical AC compressor in EVs with an electric heat pump, eliminating an entire refrigerant loop. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the **four phases of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** as per **ISO 14040/14044** with one automotive application per phase. **Model Answer (10–12 lines):** > **Phase 1 — Goal & Scope Definition:** Defines the purpose (e.g., comparative LCA of a BEV vs. an ICEV), the **functional unit** (1 vehicle × 1 km), and **system boundary** (cradle-to-grave, cradle-to-gate, or gate-to-gate). > **Phase 2 — Life Cycle Inventory (LCI):** Compilation of all **inputs** (kg steel, kWh electricity, litres of paint) and **outputs** (CO₂, NOx, wastewater) across raw material extraction, manufacturing, use, and end-of-life. > **Phase 3 — Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA):** LCI data is converted into **impact categories** such as GWP (kg CO₂-eq), AP (kg SO₂-eq), EP (kg PO₄³⁻-eq), and ODP (kg CFC-11-eq), using characterization factors. > **Phase 4 — Interpretation:** Results are analyzed to identify **hotspots** (e.g., battery cell production = 48 % of BEV GWP), sensitivity-analyzed, and **recommendations** are made — e.g., "switch to renewable electricity in the gigafactory." --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **EU End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) Directive 2000/53/EC** in detail. Discuss the **recovery and recycling rate targets** and the role of **Design for Disassembly (DfD)** in achieving them. Describe the **take-back system** for ELVs. **(b) [7 Marks]** A vehicle manufacturer plans to install a new **Regenerative Thermal Oxidizer (RTO)** in the paint shop to control VOC emissions. Currently, VOC emissions are **1,200 kg/year**. The proposed RTO has a **destruction and removal efficiency (DRE) of 99 %**. VOC is composed of **toluene (C₇H₈)** with **GWP = 2.0** and **xylene (C₈H₁₀)** with **GWP = 1.8**, in equal mass fractions. Calculate: (i) The **mass of VOC emitted after RTO installation** per year. (ii) The **annual CO₂-equivalent emissions** from the residual VOC slip. (iii) Comment on whether the RTO upgrade is sufficient to comply with a hypothetical regulatory limit of **20 tonnes CO₂-eq/year** from paint VOC. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) EU ELV Directive 2000/53/EC — Detailed Explanation** The **ELV Directive** is the cornerstone European regulation governing end-of-life treatment of road vehicles. It was **adopted in 2000** and **last amended in 2018**. **Key Provisions:** 1. **Scope:** M1 and N1 category vehicles (passenger cars and light commercial vehicles up to 3.5 t). 2. **Quantitative Targets (per vehicle per year, averaged across the market):** - **Reuse + Recycling ≥ 85 %** of vehicle mass. - **Reuse + Recovery (incl. energy) ≥ 95 %** of vehicle mass. 3. **Material Restrictions (Article 4):** Prohibition of **lead, mercury, cadmium, and hexavalent chromium** above specified thresholds. 4. **Take-Back System (Article 5):** Economic operators (OEMs and importers) must establish **nationwide collection systems** for ELVs at no cost to the last owner. 5. **Treatment Requirements (Annex I):** Mandatory depollution steps: - Battery removal - Liquid extraction (fuel, oil, coolant, brake fluid, refrigerant) - Airbag and pretensioner neutralization - Component recovery (catalytic converter, tyres, glass, large plastic parts) 6. **Coding & Information (Article 8):** Vehicle manufacturers must provide **dismantling manuals** and **material/weight data** to authorized treatment facilities (ATFs) through a standardized **International Material Data System (IMDS)**. **Role of Design for Disassembly (DfD):** DfD is the **upstream preventive strategy** that enables downstream compliance. It involves: - **Standardized fasteners** (e.g., all M6 bolts in door assembly). - **Snap-fits and quick-release clips** instead of permanent adhesives. - **Single-material components** (mono-material bumpers) to enable clean recycling. - **Modular design** for parts that have predictable failure (alternator, starter motor). - **Tool-free access** to high-value recovery targets (catalytic converter, gearbox). > A **DfD Index (DFD-I)** can be computed as the fraction of total fasteners that are either standardized, snap-fit, or part of a single-material joint. OEMs with DFD-I > 0.7 typically achieve recycling rates above 88 % without retrofitting. **Take-Back System — Operational Model:** - **Last Owner** delivers ELV to an **Authorized Treatment Facility (ATF)**. - **ATF** issues a **Certificate of Destruction (CoD)** to the vehicle registration authority. - **OEM/Importer** bears the cost of treatment (or the cost above market value of the ELV if no treatment is requested). - This is operationalized in India via the **ELV Policy Draft 2024 (MoRTH)** and globally in EU member states. --- **(b) RTO Numerical Solution** **Given:** - Pre-RTO VOC = 1,200 kg/year - DRE = 99 % → **Slip = 1 %** - Toluene GWP = 2.0; Xylene GWP = 1.8 - Equal mass fractions: 600 kg toluene + 600 kg xylene per year **Step 1 — Post-RTO residual VOC mass** $$E_{slip} = 1{,}200 \times 0.01 = 12 \text{ kg/year}$$ **Step 2 — CO₂-eq from toluene slip** $$E_{tol} = 600 \times 0.01 \times 2.0 = 12.0 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/year}$$ **Step 3 — CO₂-eq from xylene slip** $$E_{xyl} = 600 \times 0.01 \times 1.8 = 10.8 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/year}$$ **Step 4 — Total annual CO₂-eq** $$E_{total} = 12.0 + 10.8 = 22.8 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/year}$$ > **Step 4 alternative (using aggregate GWP of equal-mix = 1.9):** > $E_{total} = 12 \times 1.9 = 22.8$ kg CO₂-eq/year ✓ (matches) **Step 5 — Compliance Check Against 20 tonnes/year limit** $$\boxed{22.8 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/year} = 0.0228 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{-eq/year} \ll 20 \text{ tonnes/year limit}}$$ **Conclusion:** The RTO with 99 % DRE is **vastly sufficient** to comply with the 20 tonnes/year limit. The residual emissions are only **0.114 %** of the regulatory ceiling. In fact, the plant could even tolerate a **DRE of just ~80 %** and still meet the 20-tonne limit (you may verify: 1,200 × 0.2 × 1.9 = 456 kg = 0.456 t, still well under 20 t). The **binding constraint** is therefore not the RTO, but the **VOC inventory source reduction** at the paint shop (e.g., switching to water-based paints). --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the **ISO 14001:2015 Environmental Management System (EMS)**. Describe the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle in detail and discuss the **benefits of ISO 14001 certification** for an automotive OEM. **(b) [7 Marks]** An automotive stamping plant produces **250,000 body panels/year**. The plant consumes **8,500 MWh of grid electricity per year** (India grid emission factor = **0.71 kg CO₂/kWh**) and **45,000 m³ of water per year**. The plant emits **1,800 kg of VOC/year** and generates **120 tonnes of hazardous waste/year**. Calculate: (i) **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** in kWh/panel. (ii) **Scope-2 CO₂ emissions** per year in tonnes. (iii) **Water Withdrawal Intensity (WWI)** in m³/panel. (iv) Comment on **two areas** where the plant should focus improvement using the **6R hierarchy**. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) ISO 14001:2015 EMS — Detailed Explanation** > **Refer to Section 4.4** for the PDCA flowchart. **Key Clauses of ISO 14001:2015 (High-Level Structure — HLS):** | Clause | Title | Auto Industry Application | |---|---|---| | **4** | Context of the Organization | Identify internal/external issues (e.g., EV policy, supply chain disruption) | | **5** | Leadership | Top management commitment, environmental policy | | **6** | Planning | Identify environmental aspects (paint VOC, foundry dust) and impacts | | **7** | Support | Competence, awareness, communication, documented information | | **8** | Operation | Operational planning and control, emergency preparedness | | **9** | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit | | **10** | Improvement | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement | **PDCA Cycle in Detail:** - **PLAN:** Establish environmental objectives, identify aspects/impacts, set KPIs (e.g., "reduce VOC by 10 % in FY25"). - **DO:** Implement training programs, install controls, run operations, maintain documentation. - **CHECK:** Monitor KPIs, conduct internal audits, evaluate compliance with legal requirements. - **ACT:** Conduct management review, address nonconformities, drive continual improvement. **Benefits of ISO 14001 for an Automotive OEM:** 1. **Regulatory compliance** — single framework to satisfy multiple regulations (ELV, REACH, RoHS). 2. **Cost savings** — energy, water, and waste reduction translate to **5–15 %** operational cost savings (typical auto industry data). 3. **Market access** — many global OEMs (Toyota, VW, Tata Motors) **mandate** ISO 14001 from their Tier-1 suppliers. 4. **Investor confidence** — supports **ESG** and **BRSR** disclosures required by SEBI. 5. **Risk mitigation** — proactive environmental management reduces legal/penalty exposure. 6. **Brand value** — ISO 14001-certified plants are preferred locations for **greenfield investments** and global supply. --- **(b) Stamping Plant Numerical Solution** **Given:** - N = 250,000 panels/year - E = 8,500 MWh/year = 8,500,000 kWh/year - EF = 0.71 kg CO₂/kWh - V = 45,000 m³/year - VOC = 1,800 kg/year - HW = 120 t/year **Step 1 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) — Formula F4** $$SEC = \dfrac{8{,}500{,}000}{250{,}000} = 34 \text{ kWh/panel}$$ **Step 2 — Scope-2 CO₂ emissions (Formula F8)** $$E_{S2} = 8{,}500{,}000 \times 0.71 = 6{,}035{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/year}$$ $$E_{S2} = 6{,}035 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{-eq/year}$$ **Step 3 — Water Withdrawal Intensity (Formula F9)** $$WWI = \dfrac{45{,}000}{250{,}000} = 0.18 \text{ m}^3/\text{panel} = 180 \text{ litres/panel}$$ **Step 4 — Improvement Recommendations (6R Hierarchy)** **Improvement Area 1 — Electricity (Scope-2 Emissions at 6,035 t CO₂/year):** - **R2 — Reduce:** Install **VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives)** on stamping press motors; expected saving = 15–20 % of 8,500 MWh = 1,275–1,700 MWh/year, equivalent to **905–1,208 tonnes CO₂/year** avoided. - **R5 — Redesign (Energy Source):** Install a **1.5 MW rooftop solar PV** system. Annual generation ≈ 2,200 MWh (India-specific yield ≈ 1,450 kWh/kWp), reducing grid draw by **26 %** and Scope-2 emissions by ~**1,560 tonnes CO₂/year**. **Improvement Area 2 — Hazardous Waste (120 t/year):** - **R1 — Rethink:** Substitute solvent-based cleaning with **aqueous-based degreasing agents** in stamping line; expected HW reduction = 50–70 %. - **R3 — Reuse:** Route used stamping lubricants through a **centrifuge + filtration** loop to extend service life from 1 to 4 cycles, reducing spent-oil HW by **75 %**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Students often report SEC as **MWh/panel** instead of kWh/panel. Always normalize: 1 MWh = 1,000 kWh. Full marks require **correct unit**. > 2. **Formula F2 Misuse:** In ELV recovery problems, **energy recovery (RDF)** is counted in *recovery* rate but **NOT** in *recycling* rate. Conflating these is a common **2–3 mark loss**. > 3. **LCA Boundary Confusion:** Cradle-to-gate ≠ cradle-to-grave. A **BEV may have higher cradle-to-gate GWP** than an ICEV due to battery, but lower cradle-to-grave. Always state the **system boundary** explicitly. > 4. **GWP vs. CO₂ Confusion:** CO₂ has GWP = 1. CH₄ has GWP = **28**, N₂O = **265**. Some students forget to multiply the inventory mass by the GWP factor and lose **3–4 marks**. > 5. **6R Priority Order:** "Reduce" precedes "Recycle" — students writing "first recycle, then reduce" lose **1–2 marks** on principle hierarchy questions. > 6. **PDCA Writing:** A 14-mark question often asks for **all four phases with auto-industry examples** — writing a generic PDCA without examples caps marks at **9–10 out of 14**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Cheat Sheet — Memorize Before the Exam:** - ☐ **Green Manufacturing** = eco-efficient production; **6R hierarchy** = Rethink > Reduce > Reuse > Redesign > Recycle > Recover. - ☐ **LCA Phases (ISO 14040/44)** = Goal & Scope → Inventory → Impact Assessment → Interpretation (4 phases, 100 % mandatory to mention in order). - ☐ **Five Mandatory Impact Categories** = GWP, AP, EP, ODP, POCP. - ☐ **GWP Multipliers** (IPCC AR5): CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265. - ☐ **ELV Directive Targets** = **95 %** recovery, **85 %** recycling (avg. per vehicle per year). - ☐ **EU Euro 6/7** = **80 mg/km** NOx for petrol cars; **Real Driving Emissions (RDE)** mandatory. - ☐ **CAFE (India)** = **113 g CO₂/km** by 2032; current = ~119 g CO₂/km. - ☐ **India grid EF** = **0.71 kg CO₂/kWh** (CEA 2022 baseline). - ☐ **ISO 14001:2015** uses the **PDCA** cycle; HLS-aligned 10-clause structure. - ☐ **Aluminium LCA warning** — primary aluminium = 11.5 kg CO₂/kg; recycled = 1.5 kg CO₂/kg (~ **7.6× gap**). - ☐ **VOC units** = kg VOC per kg of product, NOT kg per L; **RTO DRE** typically **95–99 %**. - ☐ **Scope 1, 2, 3** — Direct / Purchased Energy / Value Chain (memorize boundary). - ☐ **REACH** = Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation, Restriction of **Chemicals** (NOT ELV!). - ☐ **RoHS** = Restriction of Hazardous Substances in **electrical/electronic** auto components. - ☐ **DfD Index** = ratio of standardized/snap-fit/mono-material fasteners to total. - ☐ **VOC limit for paint shop** (typical EU Industrial Emissions Directive) = **50 mg C/Nm³** for RTO outlet. - ☐ **BS-VI** = India's equivalent of Euro 6, enforced April 2020 onwards. - ☐ **India ELV Policy 2024 (draft)** targets **85 %** recycling, **95 %** recovery by **2030** — directionally aligned with EU. - ☐ **Closed-loop material ratio** (Al) at top OEMs (Tesla, Audi) exceeds **75 %** for body sheet. - ☐ **Net-Zero targets** — Volvo (2040), Tata Motors (2045), Maruti Suzuki (2070), BMW (2050), GM (2035 light-duty). --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **End of Module 4 Notes — PEAUT524 Operations Management in Auto Industry** > **KTU 2024 Scheme | Topic: Environmental Sustainability: Green Manufacturing Practices and Regulations in the Auto Industry** > *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol — aligned with Course Outcomes, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy, and KTU Board Examiner valuation patterns.*
